modules.title: Advanced Therapeutic Bronchoscopy
modules.description: Techniques for cryo, ablative, and stent-based airway interventions.

questions.prompt: All of the following tissue types are considered cryosensitive except:

options.text: Granulation tissue
Mucosa
Cartilage
Tumor

questions.explanation: Tissues with higher water content are more sensitive to the destructive effects of the freeze thaw cycles that are characteristic of cryotherapy due to the formation of intracellular and extra cellular ice crystals which damage organelles and contribute to local vascular changes. Cartilage has a low water content is therefore relatively resistant to damage from freezing.
references: (1) Gage AA, Baust J. Mechanisms of tissue injury in cryosurgery. Cryobiology. 1998;37(3):171-186. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/9787063/
(2) Theodorescu D. Cancer cryotherapy: evolution and biology. Reviews in urology. 2004;6(Suppl 4):S9. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16985871/

(3) Vergnon J, Huber R, Moghissi K. Place of cryotherapy, brachytherapy and photodynamic therapy in therapeutic bronchoscopy of lung cancers. European Respiratory Journal. 2006;28(1):200-218. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16816349/

questions.prompt: A 46 years-old male with a chronic cuff-less tracheostomy and extensive small cell lung carcinoma currently on cycle 3 of carboplatin / etoposide presents with massive hemoptysis. CT chest shows a left hilar tumor encasing the main and left upper lobe pulmonary arteries, measuring 2.5 x 1.3cm. He is orally intubated in the ED, paralyzed and the interventional pulmonary team consulted. Bleeding continues and he is placed in left decubitus position. You are considering rigid bronchoscopy. What is an absolute contraindication for rigid bronchoscopy in this patient? Figure 1. Multidetector CT angiography axial view in in lung and mediastinal windows (Left and right respectively)

options.text: Need for general anesthesia to perform the procedure
Prior total laryngectomy (access via tracheal stoma)
Ongoing bleeding and need for lateral decubitus position
Presence of cervical spine metastasis
None of the above

questions.explanation: Contraindications for rigid bronchoscopy (RB) are few. RB is commonly performed under general anesthesia using usually propofol and an opioid, combined sometimes with a short-acting muscle relaxant. But it can also be performed in a non-paralyzed, deeply sedated patient.2,3 General anesthesia allows a motionless operating field.1,2 RB can be performed through a tracheal stoma, without the need for much, if any, neck manipulation. Therefore, limitations on cervical spine mobility are not precluding factors for such patients.2 For standard RB via the transoral route, however, the inability to achieve neck extension - for example due to cervical metastasis, recent trauma, or ankylosing spondylitis- are usually contraindications to RB.2 Other limiting factors include unfavorable oral anatomy (small mouth opening, short inter-incisor distance, overbite, etc.) and bronchoscopist inexperience.
references: (1) Schuhmann M. Rigid bronchoscopy. In: Herth FJF, Shah PL, Gompelmann D, eds. Interventional Pulmonology (ERS Monograph). Sheffield, European Respiratory Society, 2017; pp. 19-28 10.1183/2312508X.10002417]. https://doi.org/

(2) Brodsky J, "Bronchoscopic procedures for central airway obstruction," Journal of Cardiothoracic and Vascular Anesthesia, vol. 17, no. 5, pp. 638-646, 2003. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/14579222/
(3) Sarkiss M. Anesthesia for bronchoscopy and interventional pulmonology: from moderate sedation to jet ventilation. Curr Opin Pulm Med 2011;17(4):274-278 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21519266/
(4) Alraiyes AB, Machuzak MS. Rigid Bronchoscopy. Semin Respir Crit Care Med 2014;35:671-680. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25463158/

questions.prompt: An otherwise healthy young patient is referred to you for wheezing, recurrent pneumonias and intermittent mild hemoptysis over the last year. After routine testing is mostly unrevealing, a CT scan is obtained and the only abnormality found is shown below. Bronchoscopy is performed, showing the lesion seen, which has a base of about 15mm. Forceps biopsy demonstrates cells with granular cytoplasm organized in nests that stain positive for chromogranin. No mitotic figures are visible per 2mm2, Ki-67 stain is <5%, and there is no evidence of necrosis. EBUS-TBNA of tissue in the subcarinal region distinct from the lesion reveals lymph tissue. Assuming further work-up is unrevealing, what is the most appropriate definitive management for this patient?

options.text: Repeat bronchoscopy with electrocautery loop snare of the lesion, then laser ablation of its mucosal base
Repeat bronchoscopy with electrocautery loop snare of the lesion, then photodynamic therapy of its mucosal base
Repeat bronchoscopy with cryodebulking of the lesion, followed by cryoablation of its mucosal base
Surgical resection
Concurrent chemotherapy and radiation

questions.explanation: The case presented is one of a bronchial low-grade neuroendocrine tumor (NET), also known as "carcinoid." The endoscopic features of the depicted tumor above are typical of carcinoid: a fairly smooth and round lesion, with a pearly-reddish surface resembling hypervascular mucosa. Classification of carcinoid is primarily guided by its histologic characteristics (presence of necrosis and level of mitotic activity) and evidence of metastatic disease. Lack of all of these features, as in this case, diagnoses a low-grade NET, or 'typical carcinoid.'1 Management of localized pulmonary NETs is typically by resection, preferably by a surgical lung-sparing approach. However, endobronchial therapy using various bronchoscopic modalities are an emerging acceptable alternative, especially for small, centrally located NETs and/or in patients who are suboptimal surgical candidates. There is no single accepted or recommended modality for bronchoscopic resection of carcinoid, but the approaches listed above have all been described.2,3 Regardless of method chosen, the technique should aim to eliminate gross evidence of endoluminal tumor and thoroughly ablate the residual mucosal base. Close follow-up thereafter is mandatory using a combination of CT and endoscopic surveillance. The American College of Chest Physician (ACCP) 2013 Guidelines recommend bronchoscopy within 6 weeks after endobronchial resection, every 6 months for 2 years, and annually thereafter.4 Tumor features that maximize chance of endobronchial cure are a narrow base and purely intraluminal growth as characterized by both CT scan and bronchoscopy. One study found a very high endobronchial therapy failure rate for any carcinoid over 20 mm in size.2 These should be referred for resection. In this patient, the luminal tumor base is about 15 mm. However, the tumor is not purely endoluminal as suggested by apparent invasion beyond the submucosa on CT. Therefore, this patient should be referred for surgery (choice D correct, choices A, B, C incorrect). Chemotherapy and radiation have minimal to no role in managing low-grade NET (choice E incorrect).
references: (1) Colt HG, Murgu SD, Korst RJ, Slatore CG, Unger M, Quadrelli S. Follow-up and surveillance of the patient with lung cancer after curative-intent therapy: Diagnosis and management of lung cancer, 3rd ed: American College of Chest Physicians evidence-based clinical practice guidelines. Chest. 2013;143(5 Suppl):e437S‐e454S. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23649451/

(2) Wolin EM. Advances in the Diagnosis and Management of Well-Differentiated and Intermediate-Differentiated Neuroendocrine Tumors of the Lung. Chest 2017;151:1141-6. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27373769/

(3) Reuling E, Dickhoff C, Plaisier PW, et al. Endobronchial Treatment for Bronchial Carcinoid: Patient Selection and Predictors of Outcome. Respiration 2018;95:220-7. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29433123/

(4) Reuling E, Dickhoff C, Plaisier PW, Bonjer HJ, Daniels JMA. Endobronchial and surgical treatment of pulmonary carcinoid tumors: A systematic literature review. Lung Cancer 2019;134:85-95. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31320001/

questions.prompt: A patient presents with rapidly worsening dyspnea and malaise. CXR obtained 1 month prior was read as normal. A flexible bronchoscopy is performed, demonstrating a completely obstructed LMS. TBNA of the lesion tumor is consistent with small cell carcinoma. The lesion is traversed with an ultrathin scope, and mixed tumor found to extend from carina to mid-LMS, with patent distal airway. Which of the following statements regarding the management of this patient's malignant airway obstruction is correct?

options.text: Based on his clinical status and bronchoscopic findings, photodynamic therapy or brachytherapy would be adequate initial choices for tumor ablation
Microstomia and C-Spine fusion may indicate a difficult intubation with rigid bronchoscope
Jet ventilation is required to perform rigid bronchoscopy
In comparison with rigid bronchoscopy, flexible bronchoscopy allows for better bleeding control

questions.explanation: In a patient with significant symptomatic central airway obstruction bronchoscopic interventions which provide immediate recanalization such as rigid coring and thermal ablative interventions are preferred. Interventions which provide late benefit such as photodynamic therapy and brachytherapy would not be appropriate. Microstomia, limited neck mobility (such as with C-spine fusion), and possibly morbid obesity are all indicators of potential difficult rigid bronchoscopy and intubation. Rigid bronchoscopy can be performed with conventional ventilation performed with packing of the mouth and nose to avoid leak or jet ventilation. There have not been any randomized controlled trials comparing one technique to another and typically choice of anesthesia modality is based on institutional preference and experience. Bleeding can be managed better with rigid bronchoscopy for several reasons. It allows for the concomitant passage of a flexible bronchoscope and a separate suction catheter, which enhances ability to clear blood from the airway. It also allows for selective intubation of the main bronchi ipsilateral to the bleeding source for better localization of the bleed and protection from soiling of the normal airway.
references: (1) Ernst A, Feller-Kopman D, Becker HD, Mehta AC. Central airway obstruction. American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine. 2004;169(12):1278-1297. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15187010/

questions.prompt: Which of the following is NOT a contraindication to using Bronchial thermoplasty?

options.text: Presence of a pacemaker, internal defibrillator, or other implantable electronic devices
Active respiratory infection
Asthma exacerbation 3 months prior
Known coagulopathy

questions.explanation: Bronchial thermoplasty is indicated for the treatment of severe persistent asthma in patients 18 years and older whose asthma is not well controlled with inhaled corticosteroids and long-acting beta-agonists. Caution should be taken for patient demographic in whom limited data is available in regards to efficacy and safety. This includes patients with a post-bronchodilator FEV1 < 65% predicted, emphysema, vocal cord dysfunction, mechanical upper airway obstruction, cystic fibrosis, or uncontrolled obstructive sleep apnea. Additionally, patients in which caution should be used include individuals consuming greater than 10 milligrams of oral prednisone per day for asthma, history of intubation or ICU admission for asthma within the prior 24 months, or in the presence of >3 or more lower respiratory tract infections >2 hospitalizations for respiratory symptoms or >3 or more oral corticosteroid pulses for asthma exacerbations in the previous 12 months. Strong contraindications to bronchial thermoplasty include known coagulopathy, the presence of implantable electronic devices such as pacemakers of internal defibrillators, active respiratory infections, as well as asthma exacerbation of changing of dose of systemic corticosteroids in the fourteen days prior to treatment (not 3 months; choice C correct).
references: (1) Castro M, Rubin AS, Laviolette M, et al. Effectiveness and safety of bronchial thermoplasty in the treatment of severe asthma: a multicenter, randomized, double-blind, sham-controlled clinical trial. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2010;181(2):116-124. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19815809/

questions.prompt: You are called urgently to the ER to evaluate one of your colleague's patients for acute dyspnea and stridor. The patient is a 49 years-old obese woman with type 2 diabetes who developed a complex post-intubation tracheal stenosis after a prolonged hospitalization following a complicated cholecystectomy. Because she was not a candidate for tracheal resection, your colleague placed a straight silicone stent in the upper trachea approximately 3 weeks ago. The patient immediately felt better, but over the last day she has become increasingly dyspneic, which is now present at rest. She denies fever or increase in mucous production and reports compliance with her pulmonary clearance regimen. On exam, the patient is moderately uncomfortable with some increase in work of breathing and mild inspiratory stridor. She states it is "harder to breathe" since being placed on BiPAP in the ED. Otherwise, aside from tachycardia and moderate obesity, the exam is unremarkable. An ABG shows ph 7.46, pco2 28, pao2 189 on IPAP of 10 and EPAP of 5cm h2o. What is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.text: Endotracheally intubate with direct laryngoscopy to secure the airway
Discontinue BiPAP and prepare for bronchoscopy via trans-oral approach and under moderate sedation, with plans to retrieve the stent using flexible forceps in case of distal migration
Increase IPAP to 15cm h2o and EPAP to 8cm h2o
Discontinue BiPAP and initiate Heliox 70/30 mixture
Continue BiPAP and administer nebulized bronchodilators and racemic epinephrine

questions.explanation: Flow dynamics are dictated by multiple factors. Airway resistance to flow is governed by the cross-sectional area, length, and configuration of the flow-tube. Turbulence, which occurs at high velocities, along the walls of a flow-tube, at branch points, or across stenoses, increases resistance to air flow and results in a larger pressure drop required to maintain a given flow. A flow medium with a high density and/or low viscosity also increases turbulence. Dyspnea related to large airway obstruction occurs due to the increased work required to generate a higher pressure drop across the stenosis needed to maintain flow. Flow dynamic studies show that this pressure drop reaches a significant threshold at about 50% reduction in the cross-sectional area of the tube, leading to dyspnea during higher flow velocities, such as with exertion.1 At about 75% obstruction, the pressure drop is enough to result in dyspnea at rest. Patients with tracheal pathology, including those with stents, who present with acute dyspnea should be considered to have a compromised airway. All stent types can migrate, though hybrid and straight silicone stents may have a higher risk, depending on multiple factors.2,3 Furthermore, granulation tissue can form at the ends of the stents, which can be particularly dangerous in the subglottis. An assessment of the airway by an experienced bronchoscopist will be required as this patient clinically has a severe obstruction (>75% given stridor and dyspnea with rest) and a stent-related complication is likely. However, because there isn't impending respiratory collapse, intervention should be delayed until it can be performed under controlled conditions, preferably utilizing rigid tracheobronchoscopy under coordinated anesthesia (choice B incorrect). This will allow optimal access to and control over the airway to more efficiently and safely manage all possible complications. Uncontrolled manipulation of the airway (for example by routine flexible bronchoscopy without proper instruments and under inadequate sedation) may prove imprecise and incomplete, while risking airway trauma and worsening obstruction. A distally migrated stent past a stenosis should not be retrieved with simple forceps in uncontrolled conditions, as the folded stent may become lodged within the stenotic airway. In the trachea, this would lead to complete airway obstruction. The initial goal in this case is to temporize the situation while enhancing patient comfort by optimizing flow dynamics. The safest and quickest way to achieve this is to minimize flow turbulence by decreasing inspiratory velocities and replacing air with a lower density gas. These two goals are best accomplished here by substituting air (nitrogen-oxygen) with a helium-oxygen gas mixture (Heliox; choice D correct). Because helium is about one-tenth the density of air, it enhances laminar flow (less turbulence), thereby decreasing the pressure drop required to maintain flow, and hence reduces the work required to breathe. BiPAP delivers gas at inconsistent flows and the tight-fitting facemask may enhance patient anxiety and increase flow velocities, making airflow turbulence worse (choice C incorrect). A patient with a suspected upper tracheal lesion (or stent) should not be endotracheally intubated without fiberoptic guidance except in an absolute emergency (choice A incorrect). Bronchodilators and racemic epinephrine have little or no role in directly managing tracheal stenosis (choice E incorrect).
references: (1) Lee HJ, Labaki W, Yu DH, et al. Airway stent complications: the role of follow-up bronchoscopy as a surveillance method. J Thorac Dis. 2017;9(11):4651‐4659. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29268534/
(2) Brouns M, Jayaraju ST, Lacor C, et al. Tracheal stenosis: a flow dynamics study. J Appl Physiol (1985) 2007;102:1178-84. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17138831/

(3) Murgu SD, Egressy K, Laxmanan B, Doblare G, Ortiz-Comino R, Hogarth DK. Central Airway Obstruction: Benign Strictures, Tracheobronchomalacia, and Malignancy-related Obstruction. Chest 2016;150:426-41. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26874192/

questions.prompt: A 70 years old male with dyspnea and productive cough is seen to have complete occlusion of the left lower lobe bronchus and left lower lobe collapse on a CT chest. His history includes active tobacco abuse, severe coronary artery disease, atrial fibrillation with compensated systolic heart failure, residual hemiparesis from a prior CVA and obesity. A flexible bronchoscopy is performed and the following findings were seen: a distal tracheal sessile polyp and a pedunculated polyp almost completely occluding the left lower lobe take off. He undergoes a rigid bronchoscopy and the lesions are removed completely. The pathology is consistent with a squamous papilloma with severe dysplasia. P16 staining is diffusely positive. Follow up is scheduled in six months with an airway inspection which shows slight growth of the papilloma at the left lower lobe take off, and a CT which shows partial re-expansion of the left lower lobe. However, 6 months later, the patient has recurrence of symptoms and is seen to have a recurrence with worsening left lower lobe collapse. Which of the following would be the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Surgery for left lower lobectomy
Start IV acyclovir
Repeat debulking and consider intralesional cidofovir
Continue surveillance without any airway interventions

questions.explanation: Recurrent respiratory papillomatosis (RRP) is a rare disease of the airways characterized by the growth of mucosal papillomas and is mediated by a viral infection with human papilloma virus (strains 6 and 11). There are three different age distributions: childhood (juvenile), early adulthood (30s) and late adulthood (60s). Although this disease commonly affects the upper airways (larynx and vocal cords), it has been reported to affect the trachea, bronchi and, very rarely, the lungs. Symptoms depend upon site of disease: problems with phonation, increased cough and mucous secretions, symptoms of bronchitis, hemoptysis and atelectasis/pneumonia from obstruction of segmental bronchi. There is no curative treatment for this disease. Treatment options for adult RRP include surgical/endoscopic resection/ablation and intralesional applications to reduce recurrence (cidofovir and bevacizumab).1 Currently, there are no guidelines to recommend when to initiate intralesional therapies although data from laryngeal RRP support their use when there is rapid recurrence leading to airway compromise, distal multisite spread and if more than four surgical procedures are required annually.2 In this patient's case, the recurrence of the papilloma was associated with worsening collapse of the left lower lobe and manual debulking alone was unsuccessful: intralesional therapy is reasonable. Lobectomy is not indicated since this is endobronchial and there are multiple lesions. Acyclovir is not indicated for treatment of HPV, and surveillance is not an option since he has a left lower lobe collapse.
references: (1) A Systematic Review..Drejet S, Halum S, Brigger M, Skopelja E, Parker NP. Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg. 2017 Mar;156(3):435-441. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28072562/

questions.prompt: The following comparisons between standard silicone and self-expandable metal stents (SEMS) are true EXCEPT:

options.text: SEMS are more likely to erode through the airway wall
SEMS have a lower inner/outer diameter ratio
Silicone stents are easier to remove/adjust, but they are also more prone to migrate
Silicone are more prone to mucostasis

questions.explanation: Self-expandable metallic stents have a high inner to outer ratio which can result in an increased luminal diameter when compared to silicone stents. Erosion through airway wall is rare but has been reported more often in metallic stents. Silicone stents are associated with a high rate of mucostasis.
references: (1) Interventional Pulmonology. European Respiratory Society Journals Ltd; 2010.

(2) Ost DE, Shah AM, Lei X, et al. Respiratory infections increase the risk of granulation tissue formation following airway stenting in patients with malignant airway obstruction. CHEST Journal. 2012;141(6):1473-1481. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22194585/

questions.prompt: A 62-year-old gentleman with a prior history of smoking is newly diagnosed with a non-small cell stage III A lung cancer at an outside facility. He complains of mild to moderate dyspnea with exertion. The tumor is located at the main carina and partially obstructs (40-60%) the entrance to both main stem bronchi with its bulky endoluminal component. Distal main stem bronchial airways are patent. He is medically fit for bronchoscopy. You are asked to assist with management of his dyspnea. What would be the most appropriate initial approach?

options.text: Bronchoscopy with "Y"- silicone stent placement, no tumor debulking
Chemo-radiation, if no response, bronchoscopic tumor debulking
Bronchoscopic tumor debulking followed by Chemoradiation
Bronchoscopic tumor debulking to near complete airway patency, followed by immediate "Y" - silicone stent insertion

questions.explanation: In this case the goal is to provide symptom relief and optimize tolerance of definitive therapies. The mass has a large endouminal component, which is amenable to debulking. Goal of airway recanalization is to provide at least a 50% patent airway. Stenting is reserved for persistent airway obstruction despite attempts at debulking, for example in mixed disease. Given that non-small cell lung cancer is likely to respond to chemotherapy and radiation, especially in a 'treatment-naïve' individual, this would be the first line management. If adequate endoluminal recanalization is achieved through tumor debulking, stent placement would be reserved for recurrence/progression after definitive therapy.
references: (1) Mudambi L, Miller R and Eapen G. Malignant central airway obstruction. Journal of Thoracic Disease 2017; 9(Suppl 10):S1087-S1110 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29214067/

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about stent-related complications is true?

options.text: Straight silicone stents have a higher chance of migration than Y-silicone stents
Uncovered metal stents have a lower chance of granulation than straight silicone stents
Uncovered metal stents have a higher chance of mucostasis than silicone Y-stents
Stents are associated with infection when placed for non-malignant rather than for malignant disease

questions.explanation: Straight silicone stents have a modest risk for migration (between 5% and 10%), depending on location and indication. Silicone Y-stents, however, because of anchoring over the carina have a relatively rare migration rate. Generally, metallic stents will granulate easier than silicone stents, especially the uncovered type, but will have a lower chance for mucostasis given higher inner to outer diameter ratio. Stents placed for malignant airway obstruction have been shown to be associated with lower respiratory tract infections.
references: (1) Gildea TR, Downie G, Eapen G, Herth F, Jantz M, Freitag L. A prospective multicenter trial of a self-expanding hybrid stent in malignant airway obstruction. Journal of Bronchology & Interventional Pulmonology. 2008;15(4):221-224. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17099019/

(2) Ost DE, Shah AM, Lei X, et al. Respiratory infections increase the risk of granulation tissue formation following airway stenting in patients with malignant airway obstruction. CHEST Journal. 2012;141(6):1473-1481. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22194585/

(3) Wood DE, Liu YH, Vallieres E, Karmy-Jones R, Mulligan MS. Airway stenting for malignant and benign tracheobronchial stenosis. Ann Thorac Surg. 2003;76(1):167-172; discussion 173-164 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/12842534/

(4) Dumon J-F, Cavaliere S, Diaz-Jimenez JP, et al. Seven-Year Experience with the Dumon Prosthesis. Journal of Bronchology & Interventional Pulmonology. 1996;3(1):6-10. https://journals.lww.com/bronchology/Abstract/1996/01000/Seven_Year_Experience_with_the_Dumon_Prosthesis.3.aspx/
(5) Interventional Pulmonology. European Respiratory Society Journals Ltd; 2010.

(6) Miyazawa T, Yamakido M, Ikeda S, et al. Implantation of ultraflex nitinol stents in malignant tracheobronchial stenoses. CHEST Journal. 2000;118(4):959-965. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11035663/

(7) Mitsuoka M, Sakuragi T, Itoh T. Clinical benefits and complications of Dumon stent insertion for the treatment of severe central airway stenosis or airway fistula. General thoracic and cardiovascular surgery. 2007;55(7):275-280. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17679254/

(8) Lemaire A, Burfeind WR, Toloza E, et al. Outcomes of tracheobronchial stents in patients with malignant airway disease. The Annals of thoracic surgery. 2005;80(2):434-438. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16039180/

(9) Saad CP, Murthy S, Krizmanich G, Mehta AC. Self-expandable metallic airway stents and flexible bronchoscopy: long-term outcomes analysis. CHEST Journal. 2003;124(5):1993-1999. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/14605078/
(10) Gildea TR, Murthy SC, Sahoo D, Mason DP, Mehta AC. Performance of a self-expanding silicone stent in palliation of benign airway conditions. CHEST Journal. 2006;130(5):1419-1423.

questions.prompt: A 57-year-old gentleman with prior history of smoking volunteers for bronchoscopy as part of a research trial for COPD. He is incidentally found to have a 5-mm raised lesion in the distal trachea, with "dotted vessel" appearance as seen with narrow band imaging, and absence of cartilage invasion by RP-EBUS. Biopsy reveals a squamous cell carcinoma in situ. Patient is deemed medically inoperable. Please choose the correct statement:

options.text: Brachytherapy and photodynamic therapy are likely to achieve a complete response, with the main concern a potential for recurrence
Brachytherapy is NOT an option due to the lesion's size
Only surveillance bronchoscopy is indicated since it is an "in situ" lesion
This lesion cannot be treated with photodynamic therapy due to its location

questions.explanation: Early stage central airway cancer is defined as radiographically occult squamous cell carcinoma less than 2 cm in surface area, with clear visible margins, appearing superficial endoscopically and without invasion beyond the bronchial cartilage assessed either by pathologic assessment or by imaging such as radial probe EBUS. Photodynamic therapy (PDT), brachytherapy, electrocautery, cryotherapy, and Nd-YAG laser therapy have all been used as primary treatment of early radiographically occult cancers. All of these modalities are considered to be similar in efficacy when used for curative intent. However, Nd-YAG laser has a higher risk of airway perforation. In patents who are treated with one of these modalities for occult early stage occult malignancies, close monitoring for recurrent disease and development of metachronous lesions is warranted and these patients should undergo bronchoscopic examination every 3 to 6 months with both white light and fluorescence bronchoscopy if available.
references: (1) Mathur PN, Edell E, Sutedja T, Vergnon J-M. Treatment of early stage non-small cell lung cancer. Chest Journal. 2003;123(1_suppl):176S-180S. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/12527577/

(2) Marsiglia H, Baldeyrou P, Lartigau E, et al. High-dose-rate brachytherapy as sole modality for early-stage endobronchial carcinoma. International Journal of Radiation Oncology* Biology* Physics. 2000;47(3):665-672. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/10837950/

(3) Cortese DA, Edell ES, Kinsey JH. Photodynamic therapy for early stage squamous cell carcinoma of the lung. Paper presented at: Mayo Clinic Proceedings1997. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/9212759/

questions.prompt: A 77-year-old gentleman with history of severe COPD, severe CHF, and a prior disabling stroke, is incidentally found to have a left hilar and subcarinal mass with severe extrinsic compression of the proximal-left main stem bronchus. His ECOG performance status is 4 (baseline) and he has no dyspnea. He is home-oxygen dependent. A PET-CT shows multiple liver and bone metastases. A diagnosis of lung cancer is obtained from biopsy of a bone metastases. Due to his performance status he is not deemed a candidate for chemotherapy and he is scheduled for palliative XRT to the subcarinal mass. You are requested to evaluate the patient for his LMS obstruction. Which of the following recommendations would be most appropriate in this setting?

options.text: Rigid bronchoscopy and Y stent placement
Rigid/flexible bronchoscopy and self-expandable metal stent placement in LMS
No airway intervention
Balloon dilation of LMS, temporizing straight silicone stent placement, followed by photodynamic therapy

questions.explanation: Proper patient selection is essential in optimizing outcomes. Airway intervention and recanalization is performed with palliative intent. If extent of palliation is minimal, a careful risk-benefit analysis should be performed before proceeding. In this case, the patient has a poor performance status at baseline, poor expected survival, and lack of symptoms to palliate. Airway intervention would only offer risk. Palliative care and end-of-life planning is more appropriate in this setting.
references: (1) Chhajed PN, Baty F, Pless M, Somandin S, Tamm M, Brutsche MH. Outcome of treated advanced non-small cell lung cancer with and without central airway obstruction. CHEST Journal. 2006;130(6):1803-1807. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17167000/

(2) Ost DE, Ernst A, Grosu HB, et al. Complications following therapeutic bronchoscopy for malignant central airway obstruction: Results of the aquire registry. Chest. 2015;148(2):450-471. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25741903/

questions.prompt: A 60-year-old gentleman with prior history of smoking is referred for the consideration of bronchoscopic lung volume reduction (BLVR) using endobronchial valves for COPD. He quit smoking 6 years ago and currently is on room air. His FEV1 is 35%, DLCO 40% and RV is 180% of predicted. Six-minute walk distance is 250 meters. ABG showed PaO2 of 60 mm Hg and PaCO2 of 44 mm Hg on room air. Chest CT showed heterogeneous emphysema with predominance in the upper lobes. Based on a review of density (emphysematous destruction) and fissure analysis, you are planning to place duck bill (Zephyr) endobronchial valves for BLVR. The patient asks you about the benefits and risks of the endobronchial valve. Which of the following is true?

options.text: Placement of duck bill valve results in mean improvement of FEV1 by 10% in heterogeneous emphysema.
The relative risk of pneumothorax after duck bill valve placement is 6.3.
Pneumothorax rates are higher for homogenous emphysema as compared to heterogeneous emphysema.
On average, patients who underwent duck bill valve placement for emphysema had 15 meters improvement in their six-minute walk distance.

questions.explanation: Duck bill valves (Zephyr) and Umbrella shaped valves (Spiration) have been approved by FDA for endoscopic lung volume reduction in COPD patients refractory to medical therapy. This modality is less invasive as compared to surgical lung volume reduction. Labarca et al. performed meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials comparing duck bill valve placement with either sham EBV or standard of care in patients with stage III or IV COPD without collateral ventilation. In this meta-analysis, patients with heterogenous emphysema had mean FEV1 improvement of 21.78% as compared to 16% in homogenous emphysema patients after EBV placement. Overall relative risk of pneumothorax was 6.32 after EBV placement. There was no difference in the relative risk of pneumothorax in homogenous emphysema patients as compared to heterogeneous emphysema patients. On average, 6MWT distance improved by 50 meters.
references: (1) Labarca G, Uribe JP, Pacheco C, et al. Bronchoscopic Lung Volume Reduction with Endobronchial Zephyr Valves for Severe Emphysema: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis. Respiration. 2019;98(3):268‐278. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31117102/

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about APC (argon-plasma coagulation) is NOT true?

options.text: Ionized argon gas conducts electric current between probe and tissue.
The gas travels in a straight direction from the catheter tip, parallel to the axis of the tip of the probe.
It is a non-contact mode of electrosurgery
Its superficial action reduces risk of airway perforation, but it is less effective than standard monopolar electrosurgery and Nd:YAG laser for in depth tissue destruction of bulky tumors.

questions.explanation: Argon plasma coagulation is a form of noncontact electrocoagulation. The noncontact feature of APC allows rapid coagulation however with a depth of penetration of only about 1-2mm. Argon plasma is used as an electrical conducting medium to deliver high-frequency current via a flexible probe. Because the current follows the path of least resistance, holding the tip of the probe in proximity to the side of the target will allow current to flow laterally rather than on a straight path.
references: (1) Morice RC, Ece T, Ece F, Keus L. Endobronchial argon plasma coagulation for treatment of hemoptysis and neoplastic airway obstruction. CHEST Journal. 2001;119(3):781-787. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11243957/

questions.prompt: A 44-year-old woman undergoes rigid bronchoscopy for tumor debulking of an adenoid cystic carcinoma of her mid-trachea. Mechanical coring out of the tumor is complicated with profound bleeding, which is controlled after multiple rounds of argon-plasma coagulation (APC), about 20 minutes total. The patient's pulse oximetry remained above 90% despite the bleeding, ventilation seemed adequate, and she was hemodynamically stable throughout the procedure. When anesthesia is reversed, the patient fails to arouse. What is a suspected explanation to the clinical scenario?

options.text: Electric current-induced seizure disorder
Anoxic encephalopathy from prolonged bleeding
Severe hypercapnia
Gas embolism

questions.explanation: APC utilizes argon flow to deliver an electric current to the target tissue. Gas flowing from the probe under pressure can cross mucosal membranes into the microvasculature resulting in systemic gas embolism. Bronchoscopists should be cognizant to minimize APC in small airways or distal to fixed obstruction and use the lowest gas flow rate possible. Diagnosis of gas embolism should be considered in patients with acute post procedural neurological changes consistent with cerebral infarct or acute ECG changes and chest discomfort. When suspicion is high, TEE can be useful in confirming the diagnosis.
references: (1) Reddy C, Majid A, Michaud G, et al. Gas embolism following bronchoscopic argon plasma coagulation: a case series. Chest. 2008;134(5):1066-1069. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18988782/

questions.prompt: You are evaluating a patient referred to you for Recurrent Respiratory Papillomatosis (RRP). Which of the following is true regarding the management of these patients?

options.text: Laser resection is the preferred option over a microdebrider for lesional excision.
Application of photodynamic therapy is more likely to aerosolize viral particles during bronchoscopy compared with laser therapy.
Lesions are exclusively found in the airways and do not involve lung parenchyma.
Tracheostomy may increase chance of local and distal disease spread in the airway
Systemic, rather than intralesional, antiviral injection is the preferred adjunctive therapy

questions.explanation: There is no treatment currently available which can completely eradicate recurrent respiratory papillomatosis. Bronchoscopic interventions to manage lesions and maintain airway patency include laser resection, electrocautery, microdebrider, brachytherapy, photodynamic therapy, and intralesional injection of antiviral agents such as cidofivir. All have the potential to aerosolize viral particles, whether during direct application or follow-up bronchoscopy for clearance of necrotic debris. Placement of tracheostomy has been shown to enhance the local and distal spread of RRP in the airway (choice D correct). Adjunctive antiviral therapies have shown mixed results in the literature and unfortunately these patients still require frequent repeat procedures.
references: (1) Cole RR, Myer CM III, Cotton RT. Tracheotomy in children with recurrent respiratory papillomatosis. Head and Neck. 1989;11(3):226-230. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/2722498/
(2) Chadha N, James A. Antivirals for recurrent respiratory papillomatosis. Health. 2012. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23235619/

(3) Schraff S, Derkay CS, Burke B, Lawson L. American Society of Pediatric Otolaryngology members' experience with recurrent respiratory papillomatosis and the use of adjuvant therapy. Archives of Otolaryngology-Head & Neck Surgery. 2004;130(9):1039-1042. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15381589/

questions.prompt: Treating tissue with electrocautery will produce tissue coagulation when the tissue reaches what approximate temperature?
questions.explanation: The effect of electrosurgery on tissue is affected by the voltage, the duration, the area of contact, the tissue density and watering content. In general, temperatures below 40° Celsius would not produce significant tissue injury. About 40° Celsius a transition to irreversible tissue injury begins. Above 70° Celsius tissue coagulation starts and water vaporization begins at temperatures around 100° Celsius. Carbonization and vaporization of tissue occur at temperature is above 200° Celsius.
references: (1) Van Boxem T, Venmans B, Schramel F, et al. Radiographically occult lung cancer treated with fibreoptic bronchoscopic electrocautery: a pilot study of a simple and inexpensive technique. European Respiratory Journal. 1998;11(1):169-172. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/9543288/

questions.prompt: A 34-year-old woman with epilepsy and Tetralogy of Fallot, presents to the emergency department with status epilepticus. She is intubated on arrival and transferred to the intensive care unit. You are consulted urgently because she develops severe subcutaneous emphysema in her upper hemithorax, neck, and face. A bedside diagnostic bronchoscopy is performed showing a large posterior tracheal wall tear. There is no evidence of mediastinitis, esophageal perforation, or pneumothorax. Your thoracic surgery colleagues think that she is at high-risk for complications and defer any surgical intervention at this time. What would be the most appropriate next step? Figure 1. Endoscopic view of trachea revealing a longitudinal post-intubation tracheal laceration that extends from the proximal to the mid trachea without herniation on expiration.

options.text: Medical management. Start antibiotics, decrease airway pressures as much as possible, and increase FiO2 to 100% to stimulate the resolution rate of the subcutaneous emphysema
Bronchoscopy with fibrin glue application
Bronchoscopy with uncovered self-expanding metallic stent
Bronchoscopy with covered self-expanding metallic stent

questions.explanation: Tracheobronchial injuries (TBI) are life-threating and can be caused by blunt, penetrating injuries or by iatrogenic procedures (endotracheal intubation, rigid/flexible bronchoscopy, balloon dilation, tracheostomy, and other surgical thoracic interventions).1,2 Bronchoscopy remains the gold standard diagnostic tool for the diagnosis of TBI. A morphologic classification for tracheal wall injuries post endotracheal intubation was proposed by Cardillo et al.:3 Level I: Mucosal or submucosal injury without mediastinal emphysema and esophageal injury Level II: Lesion extending to the muscular wall with subcutaneous or mediastinal emphysema without esophageal injury or mediastinitis Level IIIa: Complete laceration with esophageal or mediastinal soft-tissue herniation without esophageal injury or mediastinitis Level IIIb: Any laceration with esophageal injury or mediastinitis The figure reveals a tracheal injury extending to the muscular wall. The clinical vignette describes the presence of subcutaneous emphysema but no esophageal injury, herniation or mediastinitis. Therefore, it would be classified as level II. The management of TBI depends on multiple variables such as clinical presentation, patient comorbidities, and anatomy of the injury (depth, length, and location).1,2 In general, operative surgical reconstruction is the treatment of choice in cases with penetrating injuries, extension to the right mainstem, significant air leak, mediastinal herniation, associated esophageal injuries, and/or mediastinitis. A more conservative approach might be considered in high-risk surgical candidates, small or superficial injuries, and cases when long-term ventilation is required.1 Pre-requisites for conservative management of tracheal lacerations include small size (< 2 cm), ability to inflate the endotracheal tube cuff distal to the injury site, absence of or successful management of pneumothorax, stable or absent subcutaneous emphysema, absence of esophageal injury, and no evidence of an ongoing infection. The classification proposed by Cardillo et al. is a valuable tool as it serves as guidance for the management of TBI. While, level I injuries can be managed conservatively, level III injuries need multidisciplinary evaluation (thoracic surgery and interventional pulmonology) for consideration of surgical or interventional management. Level II injuries should be assessed on a case-by-case basis.2,3 In this case, the patient is a high-risk surgical candidate and has a level II injury with no immediate indication for surgery (mediastinitis, esophageal injury or herniation). However, she has developed severe subcutaneous emphysema and is on mechanical ventilation. Observation and optimization of the ventilator pressures would not be enough (Choice A incorret). An endoscopic approach might be best suited in this clinical scenario. If a stent is placed, it would need to meet the following requirements: a) seal the tracheal defect, b) stimulate granulation tissue, and c) conform properly to a distorted anatomy. The short-term placement of a self-expanding covered metallic stent will meet all these criteria (choice D correct).
references: (1) Grewal HS, Dangayach NS, Ahmad U, Ghosh S, Gildea T, Mehta AC. Treatment of Tracheobronchial Injuries: A Contemporary Review. Chest. 2019;155(3):595‐604. doi:10.1016/j.chest.2018.07.018 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30059680/
(2) Welter S. Repair of tracheobronchial injuries. Thorac Surg Clin. 2014;24(1):41‐50. doi:10.1016/j.thorsurg.2013.10.006 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24295658/

(3) Cardillo G, Carbone L, Carleo F, et al. Tracheal lacerations after endotracheal intubation: a proposed morphological classification to guide non-surgical treatment. Eur J Cardiothorac Surg. 2010;37(3):581‐587. doi:10.1016/j.ejcts.2009.07.034 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19748275/

questions.prompt: A 36-year-old woman underwent tracheal reconstructive surgery for a subglottic stenosis that she developed after an elective intubation for a cholecystectomy. Within 6 months of her tracheal surgery, she developed severe focal stenosis at the level of the anastomosis (2.5cm from the glottis), which required several bronchoscopic dilations in just one year. The patient adamantly refuses a tracheostomy tube, and she is not a candidate for any further reconstructive surgery. Which of the following should NOT be offered as a bronchoscopic alternative?

options.text: Repeat dilation followed by self-expandable metal stent placement
Repeat dilation followed by topical mitomycin C
Repeat dilation followed by tracheal hour-glass shaped silicone stent
Repeat dilation followed by tracheal silicone stent with external fixation (anchoring suture)

questions.explanation: In recurrent non-malignant central airway obstruction following surgery, or when there are no surgical options, mucosal sparing airway dilatation is the preferred therapy and there may be some benefit with topical mitomycin application. Stenting may be required after failure of repeated airway dilatations or for a stenosis with complex features (not specified in this question stem). Silicone stents are preferred. Due to high risk of complications, use of metallic stents in non-malignant disease should be a last resort or avoided altogether, especially in individuals with an expected long lifespan.
references: (1) Brichet A, Verkindre C, Dupont J, et al. Multidisciplinary approach to management of postintubation tracheal stenoses. The European respiratory journal. 1999;13(4):888-893. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/10362058/

(2) Viveiros F, Gomes J, Oliveira A, Neves S, Almeida J, e Sá JM. Topical application of mitomycin-C as an adjuvant treatment to bronchoscopic procedures in post-intubation tracheal stenosis. Revista Portuguesa de Pneumologia (English Edition). 2013;19(6):276-280. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24119923/

questions.prompt: A 30-year-old woman with end-stage cystic fibrosis undergoes bilateral lung transplant. Her post-transplant course is complicated with aspergillus infection. Three weeks later, she develops dyspnea on exertion, low grade fevers, and a severe decline in her pulmonary function testing. A picture of the surveillance bronchoscopy performed by the lung transplant team is shown in Figure 1. The interventional pulmonology team is consulted. What would be the best next step? Figure 1. Bronchoscopic view of the right main stem anastomosis.

options.text: Flexible bronchoscopy + balloon dilation
Flexible bronchoscope + covered self-expanding metallic stent
Flexible bronchoscopy + endobronchial cryodebridement
Flexible bronchoscopy + silicone stent placement
Flexible bronchoscopy + uncovered self-expanding metallic stent

questions.explanation: This case describes a patient with partial dehiscence (6 o'clock position) of her right main stem anastomosis. Post lung transplant airway complications have a significant impact in the morbidity and mortality of this population, as they lead to impaired functional capacity, poor quality of life and both increased hospital readmissions and use of healthcare resources.1 The management of airway complications following lung transplant is multidisciplinary and based on the type of airway complication, timing, severity and its location.1 As this case depicts a dehiscence, bronchial balloon dilation would not be indicated except in cases of pure bronchial stenosis (choice A incorrect). Cryodebridement is indicated in cases of exophytic granulation tissue following lung transplant, and it must be used carefully to avoid creating a bronchomediastinal fistula.2 (choice C incorrect) Bronchial necrosis and subsequent dehiscence can be seen as a continuum process. 3 Ischemia and infection play major roles in most severe cases of dehiscence. Dehiscence is associated with significant mortality and morbidity,1 leading to prolonged air leak, pneumothorax, pneumomediastinum, infections or peri-bronchial abscess formation.1 The management of dehiscence depends on the patient symptoms and its severity. Open surgical repair of anastomosis, flap bronchoplasty or re-transplantation should be discussed with the surgical team in severe cases, taking into account that these procedures are not exempt of risks.2 Some experts recommend that non-surgical candidates with bronchopleural fistulas (BPF) measuring 3-5mm should be treated endoscopically with fibrin glue, or self-expanding metallic (SEM) stents in cases of larger defects.4 In cases of concomitant mediastinal infection, either debridement or percutaneous drainage will need to be considered, along with prolonged antibiotic administration. 4 Deployment of stents should be used always as last resort after a careful discussion with the lung transplant and surgical teams, and they should be removed as soon adequate healing of the dehiscence and before epithelization of the stent has occurred.5 In cases of severe dehiscence, the use of a silicone stent is not widely accepted as the vigorous force required for stent placement can increase the defect size (choice D incorrect). 1 An uncovered SEM is preferred in those cases to avoid bacterial colonization, avoid endobronchial blockage of the lobes involved and allow drainage of mediastinal and bronchial secretions. These metallic stents should be removed in 4-6 weeks to avoid excessive granulation tissue (choice E correct, B incorrect).
references: (1) Kshettry VR, Kroshus TJ, Hertz MI, et al. Early and late airway complications after lung transplantation: incidence and management. Ann Thorac Surg 1997;63:1576- 1583. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/9205151/

(2) Mughal MM, Gildea TR, Murthy Set al. Short-term deployment of self-expanding metallic stents facilitates healing of bronchial dehiscence. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2005 Sep 15;172(6):768-71. Epub 2005 Jun 3. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15937290/
(3) Murthy SC, Blackstone EH, Gildea TR, et al. Impact of anastomotic airway complications after lung transplantation. Ann Thorac Surg 2007;84:401-409. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17643606/

(4) Mahajan AK, Folch E, Khandhar SJ et al. The Diagnosis and Management of Airway Complications Following Lung Transplantation. Chest. 2017 Sep;152(3):627-638. doi: 10.1016/j.chest.2017.02.021. Epub 2017 Mar 6. Review. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28274791/

(5) Santacruz JF, Mehta AC. Airway complications and management after lung transplantation: ischemia, dehiscence, and stenosis. Proc Am Thorac Soc. 2009 Jan 15;6(1):79-93. doi: 10.1513/pats.200808-094GO. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19131533/

questions.prompt: A 59 years-old male with recurrent renal cell carcinoma after 2nd line treatment, is admitted with dyspnea, mild hemoptysis, and complete left lung collapse. Patient has opted to receive no further systemic therapy. He is now in moderate respiratory distress. CT chest shows a left main stem bronchial tumor with complete obstruction of the airway. A bronchoscopy is performed revealing a large friable intraluminal mass. An ultra-thin bronchoscope can bypass the mass and it shows uninvolved patent distal airway. A biopsy is positive for renal cell carcinoma. What would be the most appropriate approach?

options.text: Referral for external beam radiation (EBRT) for palliation of obstruction
Medical palliative care only
Bronchoscopic photodynamic therapy (PDT)
Bronchoscopic tumor debulking and stent placement

questions.explanation: The patient requires urgent management of his airway and an immediate-, rather than delayed-effect therapy is required. Therefore, PDT and EBRT are not ideal choices in this scenario. In addition, renal cell carcinoma is poorly responsive to radiotherapy. Stents are primarily indicated for relief of extrinsic airway compression and mixed airway obstruction. In this case the endoluminal component should be debulked and residual obstruction assessed prior to stent placement. However, since this patient has opted to receive no further systemic treatment and his renal cell carcinoma has recurred twice, stenting is a reasonable option regardless.
references: (1) Mudambi L, Miller R and Eapen G. Malignant central airway obstruction. Journal of Thoracic Disease 2017; 9(Suppl 10):S1087-S1110 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29214067/

questions.prompt: All of the following are mechanisms of tissue injury from photodynamic therapy (PDT) except:

options.text: Damage to cell membranes
Direct cellular damage to mitochondria
Apoptosis
Thermal cytotoxic injury

questions.explanation: As with brachytherapy, photodynamic therapy leads to apoptosis in a delayed fashion. Additionally, PDT also directly damages the cellular membrane and mitochondria via reactive oxygen species when the photosensitizing agent ( porfimer sodium most commonly used) is activated by light of the proper wavelength. PDT is not a thermal modality of tissue destruction.
references: (1) Vergnon J, Huber R, Moghissi K. Place of cryotherapy, brachytherapy and photodynamic therapy in therapeutic bronchoscopy of lung cancers. European Respiratory Journal. 2006;28(1):200-218. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16816349/

questions.prompt: A 68-year-old female is diagnosed with a large polypoid tumor in the subglottic area resulting in severe central airway obstruction (approximately 80-90%). A rigid bronchoscopy is planned. Upon induction of anesthesia, her oxygenation rapidly worsens. The rigid bronchoscope is swiftly introduced past the vocal cords, and jet ventilation is started, but oxygenation only marginally recovers to 84% saturation on FiO2 1.0. The tumor appears to be producing a ball-valve mechanism, with progressive hyperexpansion of the chest noted. The tumor cannot be by-passed with the rigid bronchoscope. Which of the following techniques should you immediately consider in this case?

options.text: Nd-YAG LASER
Mechanical coring out with rigid bronchoscope
Argon Plasma Coagulation
Cryospray therapy

questions.explanation: In this emergent situation related to intrinsic airway obstruction by tumor, the therapeutic modality which would provide the most rapid action should be utilized. Mechanical coring with the bevel of the rigid bronchoscope provides such an approach. Cryospray therapy, as with other modalities that provide delayed cytotoxic effects (PDT and brachytherapy), should not be used when rapid recanalization of the airway is required. Argon plasma coagulation is an excellent tool for hemostasis but has a very minimal depth of penetration and would provide slow and suboptimal debulking. YAG laser provides coagulation with tissue destruction and is an effective tool for airway recanalization. However, as with other thermal modalities, a reduction in FiO2 is required to mitigate risk of airway fire and this is not possible in this patient with already compromised oxygenation.
references: (1) Ernst A, Feller-Kopman D, Becker HD, Mehta AC. Central airway obstruction. American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine. 2004;169(12):1278-1297. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15187010/

questions.prompt: Which of the following patients could be a candidate for endobronchial photodynamic therapy (PDT)?

options.text: 65 y/o female with moderate COPD (FEV1 2L and DLCO 55%) with an endobronchial squamous cell Ca in situ in RUL and RLL.
50 y/o male post left pneumectomy for squamous cell carcinoma and recent recurrence in the stump. PET scan shows no mediastinal adenopathy.
48 y/o female with a metastatic IV colon cancer and partial obstruction of the right main stem and right bronchus intermedius.
All of the above

questions.explanation: PDT is based on the interaction of tumor-selective photosensitizer and laser light. This interaction causes selective death of tumor cells. The majority of clinical data using PDT in early lung cancer has been for treatment of patients who were deemed nonsurgical candidates. In palliative settings, PDT effectively reduces airway obstruction and improves respiratory function, although its effect is delayed and requires repeat bronchoscopic intervention.
references: (1) Shafirstein G, Battoo A, Harris K, Baumann H, Gollnick SO, Lindenmann J, Nwogu CE. Photodynamic Therapy of Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer. Narrative Review and Future Directions. Ann Am Thorac Soc. 2016 Feb;13(2):265-75. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26646726/

questions.prompt: A 35-year-old man with no significant medical problems was involved in a motor vehicle accident six months ago resulting in pulmonary contusion and head trauma requiring tracheostomy and prolonged ventilator support for three months. He was then decannulated and underwent rehabilitation. He is referred for evaluation of worsening dyspnea on exertion and wheezing. His imaging and bronchoscopy revealed 50% stenosis of the trachea at the prior tracheostomy site extending about 2 cm in length with collapse of the anterior wall with expiration to greater than 75%. What would be the best treatment approach at the present time?

options.text: Flexible/rigid bronchoscopy with balloon dilation and airway stenting.
Referral for surgical evaluation for resection.
Continue clinical monitoring with PFTs and bronchoscopy at regular intervals
Dynamic bronchoscopy with CPAP titration.

questions.explanation: This patient is young with no significant medical comorbidities with a complex post-intubation tracheal stenosis. He should be evaluated for surgical resection and anastomosis which offers the most definitive and successful therapy. Balloon dilation of stenosis with airway stenting would be an appropriate option if immediate stabilization of the airway is needed or if the patient is deemed not a surgical candidate. He has significant airway obstruction with worsening respiratory symptoms, and so continued clinical monitoring would not be appropriate. Dynamic bronchoscopy with CPAP titration is a reasonable first step in tracheobronchomalacia/excessive dynamic airway collapse. However, in this case, the patient has a complex tracheal stenosis.
references: (1) A. Ernst and F.J.F. Herth, Principles and Practice of Interventional Pulmonology, ISBN 978-1-4614-4291-2; p 269
(2) Tracheal Resection with End-to-End Anastomosis for Benign Tracheal Stenosis. Ann Otol Rhinal Laryngol 113:2004 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15330139/
(3) Central Airway Obstruction Benign Strictures, Tracheobronchomalacia, and Malignancy-related Obstruction. CHEST 2016; 150(2):426-441 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26874192/

questions.prompt: A 75-year-old man from India who has been living in the US for the past 20 years presented with shortness of breath that was gradually worsening in the past 2-3 years. He underwent evaluation and on the CT scan it was found that he had the following lesion. All the following are an acceptable approach to address this lesion, except?

options.text: Rigid bronchoscopy with microdebrider
VV-ECMO, then flexible bronchoscopy with electrocautery snare
Flexible bronchoscopy with photodynamic therapy
Rigid bronchoscopy with coring
Rigid bronchoscopy with cryodebridment

questions.explanation: This patient has central airway obstruction. Given his presentation and impressive CT findings, he should be offered an approach that will provide immediate relief of his central airway obstruction. All the above choices provide an immediate effect except for photodynamic therapy. Photodynamic therapy also risks worsening airway obstruction as it can result in extensive necrosis post treatment.
references: (1) Mahmood K, Wahidi MM. Ablative therapies for central airway obstruction. Semin Respir Crit Care Med. 2014 Dec;35(6):681-92. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25463159/

questions.prompt: A 33-year-old woman presents with increasing dyspnea. She had a tracheostomy performed 3 years ago after a 2-week intubation for pneumonia, and currently has a #6 distal XLT trach tube. She weighs 449 lbs. ht 5' 7" (BMI 70), and is a Mallampati Class 4, with neck circumference 25 inches. During flexible bronchoscopy through the trach-tube you noticed stenosis at the distal end of the trach tube about 3cm proximal to the main carina. You are contemplating rigid bronchoscopy for dilation and possible stent placement under general anesthesia with jet ventilation. Which of the following about this approach is correct?

options.text: Rigid Bronchoscopy is contraindicated in patients with morbid obesity (BMI>40)
If oral intubation with rigid bronchoscope is difficult, her tracheostomy stoma can be used to insert the rigid bronchoscope
During manual (Sanders) jet ventilation, all ports of the rigid bronchoscope and oral cavity should be kept closed to prevent any air leak
Use of LMA compared to face mask shortens recovery time after rigid bronchoscopy
Balloon dilation has longer lasting effect compared to rigid bronchoscopic dilation

questions.explanation: Morbid obesity is not a contraindication to rigid bronchoscopy, which can also be performed through a tracheostomy stoma. When manual jet ventilation is used for rigid bronchoscopy all side ports should remain open to avoid barotrauma. There is no difference in recovery time following rigid bronchoscopy between LMA and facemask. Rigid bronchoscopic dilatation and balloon dilatation have similar effects for dilatation of simple tracheal stenosis.
references: (1) Nicastri DG, Weiser TS. Rigid bronchoscopy: Indications and techniques. Operative Techniques in Thoracic and Cardiovascular Surgery. 2012;17(1):44-51. https://www.optechtcs.com/article/S1522-2942(12)00052-9/pdf/

questions.prompt: A 56-year-old man is brought to the emergency center by EMS after he was found "blue and unresponsive" by family. Prior to presentation, the patient had reported dyspnea and cough for one week and had been sleeping in a recliner because of dyspnea. Past medical history was significant for squamous cell carcinoma of the tonsil diagnosed nine years prior and treated with XRT to neck. Additionally, he was diagnosed with Stage IIIB NSCLC squamous cell carcinoma of the lung three months ago and treated with cisplatin-etoposide, last dose three weeks prior. On initial presentation, the patient was speaking complete sentences, not tachypneic but wheezing and noted to have limited neck mobility. At the time of your physical exam, BP was 135/64, HR was 91, RR was 16, and oxygen saturations 93% on 2LNC. The patient was admitted to MICU and a few hours later was unable to lay flat with increasing dyspnea. Oxygen saturation is now 94% on FiO2 0.4 by mask. ABG was obtained with pH of 7.38; PCO2 of 52; PO2 of 92. Which of the following about this patient's management is INCORRECT?

options.text: After successful bronchoscopic intervention, he will have comparable survival to a patient with similar stage NSCLC without central airway obstruction
Overall complication rate from bronchoscopic intervention in malignant central airway obstruction is <5%.
His ECOG/Zubrod score is a predictor of 30-day post procedure mortality
Rigid bronchoscopy is absolutely contraindicated due to the inability to lie flat and extend the neck

questions.explanation: Based on imaging and clinical presentation, this patient has a severe central airway obstruction, likely malignant. In patients with advanced non-small cell lung cancer, bronchoscopically treated central airway obstruction results in similar survival to those without central airway obstruction. In the AQUIRE registry 1115 procedures were performed in 947 patients at 15 centers for treatment of malignant central airway obstruction with an overall complication rate of 3.9% with a procedural mortality rate of 0.5%. ECOG score > 1 or ASA > 3 are associated with increased 30-day mortality rate after initial bronchoscopy. Slight extension of the neck is helpful in rigid bronchoscopic intubation but not an absolute requirement. In a patient who is unable to lie flat rigid intubation can be performed in the semi-recumbent position and with sequential induction of anesthesia. Therefore an attempt at rigid bronchoscopy in this case is reasonable.
references: (1) Ost DE, Ernst A, Grosu HB, et al. Complications following therapeutic bronchoscopy for malignant central airway obstruction: Results of the aquire registry. Chest. 2015;148(2):450-471. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17167000/
(2) McGarvey JM, Pollack CV. Heliox in Airway Management. Emergency Medicine Clinics.26(4):905-920. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19059090/

(3) Chhajed PN, Baty F, Pless M, Somandin S, Tamm M, Brutsche MH. Outcome of treated advanced non-small cell lung cancer with and without central airway obstruction. CHEST Journal. 2006;130(6):1803-1807. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17167000/

questions.prompt: A 40-year-old woman with granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA) presents for cough and dyspnea on exertion. Work-up, including a CT scan, reveals mid-tracheal stenosis along with other areas of mild bronchial stenosis. During bronchoscopy you notice a 1 cm complex tracheal stenosis segment in the distal trachea, with a stenotic index of ~60%. You decide to treat this with spray cryotherapy. Which of the following used during spray cryotherapy activation will minimize the risk associated with this procedure?

options.text: deflating the endotracheal tube cuff
disconnecting the endotracheal tube bronchoscope adapter
disconnecting the rigid bronchoscope from the ventilator
use of low-flow settings (12.5 W vs. 25 W)
All of the above

questions.explanation: Spray cryotherapy (i.e. cryospray) utilizes rapid expansion of liquid nitrogen in the airway causing flash freezing of tissue at −196°C. This rapid expansion though, can result in barotrauma resulting in pneumothorax, as well as hypoxemia from displacement of oxygen in up to 5% of patients. Finley et al. reported a 19% intraoperative complication rate and 3.75% pneumothorax risk. However, more recent reports have not described any major complications. All the maneuvers described in choices A, B and C allow for egress of nitrogen gas and prevent the building up of high airway pressures that may result in a pneumothorax. In addition, use of low-flow settings can also be used to mitigate the pneumothorax risk.
references: (1) Browning R, Parrish S, Sarkar S, Turner JF Jr. First report of a novel liquid nitrogen adjustable flow spray cryotherapy (SCT) device in the bronchoscopic treatment of disease of the central tracheo-bronchial airways. J Thorac Dis 2013;5:E103-E106. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23825781/

(2) Finley DJ, Dycoco J, Sarkar S, Krimsky WS, Sherwood JT, Dekeratry D, et al. Airway spray cryotherapy: initial outcomes from a multiinstitutional registry. Ann Thorac Surg 2012;94:199-203, discussion 203-204. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22516831/

(3) Browning R, Turner JF Jr, Parrish S. Spray cryotherapy (SCT): institutional evolution of techniques and clinical practice from early experience in the treatment of malignant airway disease. J Thorac Dis 2015;7(Suppl 4):S405-S414. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26807288/

(4) Bhora FY, Ayub A, Forleiter CM, Huang CY, Alshehri K, Rehmani S, et al. Treatment of benign tracheal stenosis using endoluminal spray cryotherapy. JAMA Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg 2016;142:1082-1087. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27532803/

questions.prompt: A 65-year-old man with a history of T3N2M0 squamous cell carcinoma underwent a successful palliative airway stent placement for extrinsic airway obstruction. He was discharged on a nebulized regimen with albuterol every 4 hours and 3% saline twice a day. Approximately six weeks later, he presents to ED with an acute onset of dyspnea and oxygen saturation 80%. He is intubated and transferred to the ICU. Bronchoscopy is performed and shows severe mucous impaction and slight proximal migration of the stent. Aggressive therapeutic aspiration and repositioning of the stent allows for successful extubation a few hours later. What is the most likely location and type of this patient's airway stent?

options.text: Uncovered metal stent in the left main stem bronchus
Silicone tubular stent in the left main stem bronchus
Fully covered metal stent in the right main stem bronchus
Partially covered metal stent in the right main stem bronchus

questions.explanation: All stents have associated long-term complications, with type and frequency depending on type of stent, location of placement, and underlying disease. Tubular silicone stents have the highest mucostasis rate. Both silicone stents and fully covered metal stents have higher migration rates than uncovered metal stents. Metal stents are more likely to promote formation of granulation tissue. In an analysis of 195 stents procedures, having a left-sided or silicone stent increased risk of mucus impaction, but older age or chemotherapy post-stent placement was protective.
references: (1) CHEST 2012; 141(6):1473-1481 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22194585/
(2) J Thorac Dis. 2017 Nov;9(11):4651-4659 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29268534/

questions.prompt: You are seeing a patient for evaluation of bronchoscopic lung volume reduction (BLVR) using endobronchial valves (EBV). The patient would like to learn more about the risk of pneumothorax with the procedure. You inform him that:

options.text: Up to one third of patients may develop a pneumothorax following valve placement.
The risk of pneumothorax is the highest in the first 3 days post procedure.
The occurrence of pneumothorax doesn't alter the outcomes of EBV.
All of the above.

questions.explanation: Pneumothorax is the most common complication reported following BLVR using EBV, with the incidence ranging between 18-34%. The risk is higher in the first 3 days following the procedure, although it may still occur beyond that period. Based on prior studies, patients who developed a pneumothorax experienced similar benefits at 12 months (six-minute walk distance, change in SGRQ, change in FEV1) compared to subjects who did not have a pneumothorax.
references: (1) Klooster K, ten Hacken NH, Hartman JE, et al. Endobronchial Valves for Emphysema without Interlobar Collateral Ventilation. N Engl J Med. 2015 Dec 10;373(24):2325-35 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26650153/

(2) Criner GJ, Sue R, Wright S, et al. A Multicenter Randomized Controlled Trial of Zephyr Endobronchial Valve Treatment in Heterogenous Emphysema (LIBERATE). Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2018 Nov 1;198(9):1151-1164 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29787288/
(3) Herth FJF, Slebos DJ, Criner GJ, et al. Endoscopic lung volume reduction: an expert panel recommendation - update 2019. Respiration. 2019:1-10. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30836374/

questions.prompt: A 63-year-old man with prior history of smoking, COPD with baseline FEV1 1.2 L, presents with respiratory failure requiring non-rebreathing mask. His ECOG performance status was 0 prior to a rapid decline in respiratory status during the past 2 weeks. Past medical history is significant for COPD, coronary artery disease on aspirin 325mg, C1 to T2 spinal fusion. Vitals signs are notable for BP 153/89, HR 115, RR 28, O2 sat 93% on non-rebreather mask. On physical exam, the patient is in respiratory distress with mild use of accessory muscles, microstomia is noted, and breath sounds are absent in the left hemithorax. Representative images from the patient's CT scan are shown here: You are concerned about central airway obstruction and are considering therapeutic bronchoscopy. Assuming a malignant etiology, which statement is "true" regarding this patient's airway obstruction?

options.text: The lack of obvious "patent" distal airway in CT-chest rules out the possibility of a successful therapeutic bronchoscopy
Therapeutic bronchoscopy could potentially recanalize this patient's airway, improve his dyspnea, and act as a bridge to systemic therapy for his underlying malignancy
The patient's baseline performance status is irrelevant when making a decision to proceed with therapeutic bronchoscopy
If the patient is intubated and requires mechanical ventilation, a bronchoscopic intervention would be futile

questions.explanation: Although the patient does not appear to have patent distal airways on CT, a central obstruction related to focal tumor or mucous plugging can result in post-obstructive atelectasis and radiographic findings which could mimic non-viable lung. Clues on CT scan to presence of viable, atelectatic lung rather than tumor mass include homogenously enhancing and vascularized lung, with ipsilateral volume loss, as with this patient. Therapeutic bronchoscopy to recanalize the airway in patients with central airway obstruction secondary to NSCLC has been shown to result in survival equivalent to similarly staged patients without central airway obstruction. Therefore, in conjunction with recent good functional status, attempt at airway recanalization should be made. Baseline ECOG of > 1 has been shown to significantly reduce 30-day survival in patients undergoing therapeutic bronchoscopy for malignant central airway obstruction. Bronchoscopic interventions for malignant central airway obstruction in patients intubated on mechanical ventilation has been shown to result in a significant proportion of patients being liberated from mechanical ventilation with subsequent decrease in required level of care.
references: (1) Chhajed PN, Baty F, Pless M, Somandin S, Tamm M, Brutsche MH. Outcome of treated advanced non-small cell lung cancer with and without central airway obstruction. CHEST Journal. 2006;130(6):1803-1807. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17167000/

(2) Louie AV, Lane S, Palma DA, Warner A, Cao JQ, Rodrigues GB. Radiotherapy for Intubated Patients with Malignant Airway Obstruction: Futile or Facilitating Extubation? Journal of Thoracic Oncology.8(11):1365-1370. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24077459/

(3) Guibert N, Mazieres J, Lepage B, Plat G, Didier A, Hermant C. Prognostic factors associated with interventional bronchoscopy in lung cancer. The Annals of thoracic surgery. 2014;97(1):253-259. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24094520/

(4) Amjadi K, Voduc N, Cruysberghs Y, et al. Impact of interventional bronchoscopy on quality of life in malignant airway obstruction. Respiration. 2008;76(4):421-428. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18758153/

(5) Stratakos G, Gerovasili V, Dimitropoulos C, et al. Survival and Quality of Life Benefit after Endoscopic Management of Malignant Central Airway Obstruction. Journal of Cancer. 2016;7(7):794-802. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27162537/

(6) Jeon K, Kim H, Yu C-M, et al. Rigid bronchoscopic intervention in patients with respiratory failure caused by malignant central airway obstruction. Journal of Thoracic Oncology. 2006;1(4):319-323.

(7) Miyazawa T, Miyazu Y, Iwamoto Y, et al. Stenting at the flow-limiting segment in tracheobronchial stenosis due to lung cancer. American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine. 2004;169(10):1096-1102. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15132959/

(8) Razi SS, Lebovics RS, Schwartz G, et al. Timely airway stenting improves survival in patients with malignant central airway obstruction. The Annals of thoracic surgery. 2010;90(4):1088-1093. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20868793/

questions.prompt: A 65-year-old woman is referred to you for consideration of bronchoscopic lung volume reduction (BLVR) using endobronchial valves (EBV). She has an FEV1 of 25% predicted, TLC of 135% and an RV of 200%. Her CT shows heterogenous emphysema bilaterally. Her StratX report is shown below. You proceed with bronchoscopy. Chartis information from the right upper and lower lobes are shown below. Based on the available information you determine that:

options.text: The patient is not a candidate for EBVs
She is a candidate for BLVR to the RUL
She is a candidate for BLVR to the RUL + RML
She is a candidate for BLVR to the Left upper lobe

questions.explanation: The StratX report demonstrates more emphysema destruction in the right upper lobe and right middle lobe combination compared to the rest of the lobes. Based on the StratX information, the target lobe would be either the right upper lobe or the right upper and middle lobes, depending on whether there is collateral ventilation across the minor fissure. The Chartis system is used during bronchoscopy to help with target lobe selection by confirming whether collateral ventilation between the lobes is present. This is performed by occluding the bronchus of the target lobe using the Chartis balloon while measuring the air flow distal to the balloon. Collateral ventilation is considered present if flow persists despite occlusion of the airway. As shown in the question when the right lower lobe was occluded there was no evidence of collateral ventilation (confirmed by the downward trend of airflow). On the other hand, collateral ventilation was present when the right upper lobe was occluded (confirmed by the persistence of airflow throughout the study). This suggests that there is collateral ventilation across the minor fissure while the major fissure is intact. Based on this information, BLVR to the right upper lobe and right middle lobe should be performed. In addition, there is heterogeneity in destruction between these lobes and the ipsilateral non-target lobe (i.e. the RLL).
references: (1) Mets OM, Smith EJ, Mohamed Hoesein FA, et al. Visual versus automated evaluation of chest computed tomography for the presence of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. PLoS ONE. 2012;7(7):e42227. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22848747/
(2) Herth FJF, Slebos DJ, Criner GJ, et al. Endoscopic lung volume reduction: an expert panel recommendation - update 2019. Respiration. 2019:1-10. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30836374/

questions.prompt: You perform a bronchoscopy on a 75-year-old man with COPD on baseline 4 lpm O2. There is an endoluminal tumor causing near complete obstruction of the right main bronchus. It is difficult to use sustained periods of FiO2 of less than 0.4 so you decide to use cryotherapy for tumor debulking and airway recanalization. Which of the following is true regarding cryotherapy:

options.text: Amongst therapeutic procedures recorded in the AQuIRE Registry, cryotherapy was the most commonly used modality
Heat based therapy use results in successful airway patency restoration more often than with cryotherapy use
