Cryodebulking (or cryorecanalization) refers to the use of 10-30 second freeze-thaw cycles to devascularize tumor
Cryodebulking has a significant risk of moderate bleeding (4-25%), defined as requiring ablative therapy or cold saline to achieve hemostasis

questions.explanation: Cryotherapy can be utilized as contact probe based therapy for delayed effect, or cryodebulking/ cryorecanalization for immediate effect. Contact probe cryotherapy is performed by applying the probe for multiple 10-30-second freeze-thaw cycles. Successive applications are to adjacent zones (Choice C incorrect). With cryodebulking, instead of freeze-thaw cycles, the cryoprobe is activated for 3-15 seconds to adhere to target tissue and then is firmly and briskly pulled away from the lesion with the intent of debulking pieces. With this technique, hemostasis is not always achieved at the junction of the piece debulked and the remaining lesion, resulting in bleeding requiring cold saline or ablative therapies, such as Argon Plasma Coagulation (APC), in upto 25% of cases (Choice D correct). APC was the most commonly used therapeutic modality in the AQuIRE Registry; cryotherapy was used in 8% of cases (Choice A incorrect).
references: (1) Ost DE, Ernst A, Grosu HB, Lei X, Diaz-Mendoza J, Slade M, et al.; AQuIRE Bronchoscopy Registry. Complications following therapeutic bronchoscopy for malignant central airway obstruction: results of the AQuIRE Registry. Chest 2015;148:450-471. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25741903/

(2) Schumann C, Hetzel M, Babiak AJ, Hetzel J, Merk T, Wibmer T, et al. Endobronchial tumor debulking with a flexible cryoprobe for immediate treatment of malignant stenosis. J Thorac Cardiovasc Surg 2010;139:997-1000. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19716140/

(3) Inaty H, Folch E, Berger R, Fernandez-Bussy S, Chatterji S, Alape D, et al. Unimodality and multimodality cryodebridement for airway obstruction: a single-center experience with safety and efficacy. Ann Am Thorac Soc 2016;13:856-861. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26999041/

questions.prompt: A 75 years old man with 70 pack year smoking history transferred from an outside facility with shortness of breath and hypoxemia for higher level of care. His CT chest showed severe obstruction of mid-left mainstem bronchus, but with apparent patent distal airway likely impacted with mucous. He is requiring high flow nasal cannula at 15 liters per minute at 60% FiO2. Rigid bronchoscopy is performed and despite 100% FiO2 and high respiratory rate, patient's oxygen saturation is marginal. The obstruction appears mostly endoluminal with suspicion for endobronchial tumor. Which of the following would be the most appropriate management option?

options.text: Endobronchial biopsy followed by external beam radiation
Tumor destruction using laser
Tumor debulking with cryotherapy
Tumor destruction using photodynamic therapy

questions.explanation: This is a patient with newly diagnosed lung mass and secondary left-sided endoluminal obstruction with acute airway compromise. Obtaining a tissue diagnosis as well as urgent bronchoscopic airway stabilization is indicated. Tumor destruction can be achieved with the use of heat or cold therapies. However, because the patient is currently requiring high FiO2, thermal application would risk airway fire, and hence laser therapy is contraindicated. Debulking with a cryoprobe can be safely performed to obtain tissue and recanalize the airway without the need to decrease the inspired oxygen concentration. External beam radiation and photodynamic therapy are effective means of controlling tumor burden but have delayed effects, making their use for an acute unstable airway inappropriate.
references: (1) Cryorecanalization: A new approach for the immediate management of acute airway obstruction. J Thorac Cardiovasc Surg 2004;127: 1427-31 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15116003/
(2) The application of cryosurgery in the treatment of lung cancer M.O. Maiwand et al. / Cryobiology 48 (2004) 55-61 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/14969682/
(3) British Thoracic Society guideline for advanced diagnostic and therapeutic flexible bronchoscopy in adults. Thorax 2011;66:iii1eiii21. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21987439/

questions.prompt: A 59-year-old woman with a history of right upper lobe non-small cell lung cancer Stage IIIA, post chemotherapy and radiation, presents with hemoptysis of one-half cup per day for the past five days. CT chest shows endobronchial tumor obstructing the RUL and RMS bronchi. There is mild dyspnea but no respiratory distress. Oxygen requirement has increased. A pulmonologist has performed a flexible bronchoscopy confirming the above tumor as an active source of bleeding and requests your help. Which of the following options would be the most appropriate management approach?

options.text: Radiation oncology consultation for palliative radiotherapy
Place patient in the left lateral decubitus (left side down) and consultation to interventional radiologist for bronchial artery embolization
Perform rigid bronchoscopy, cold saline for cleaning and bleeding control, followed by electroablation for hemostasis
Rigid bronchoscopy, cold saline for cleaning and bleeding control, followed by cryotherapy for hemostasis

questions.explanation: Of the choices listed, performing bronchoscopy for immediate bleeding control is most reasonable given suggestion of worsening respiratory status. Electroablation (via laser, electrocautery or argon-plasma coagulation) provides a more immediate and reliable hemostatic effect than cryotherapy. Radiation therapy, while useful for palliation of hemoptysis, has a delayed effect and is better reserved for less urgent cases. Bronchial artery embolization may be required if endobronchial therapies fail. Positioning the patient with the active bleeding side dependent is important to minimize spillage of blood into the more normal airway. In this case, the patient should be placed in the right lateral rather than left lateral decubitus.
references: (1) Maguire MF, Berry CB, Gellett L, Berrisford RG. Catastrophic haemoptysis during rigid bronchoscopy: a discussion of treatment options to salvage patients during catastrophic haemoptysis at rigid bronchoscopy. Interactive cardiovascular and thoracic surgery. 2004;3(2):222-225. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17670219/
(2) Karmy-Jones R, Cuschieri J, Vallieres E. Role of bronchoscopy in massive hemoptysis. Chest surgery clinics of North America. 2001;11(4):873-9 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11780301/

questions.prompt: A 30 years-old Caucasian woman with history of cystic fibrosis-related lung disease, chronic sinusitis, and diabetes mellitus underwent bilateral sequential lung transplantation two years prior. She is noted to have declining lung function and complains of dyspnea with exertion. Bronchoscopy demonstrates the bronchus intermedius as seen below. You proceed with balloon dilation, but the benefit is short-lived. After two more dilations in the next two months, PFTs continue to show steady decline in FEV1. Most recent surveillance lung biopsy did not show any rejection. Her vitals are within normal limits and she is on room air. What would be the next step in management?

options.text: Increase immunosuppression
Repeat airway balloon dilation, with steroid injection
Repeat airway balloon dilation, and place silicone stent
Evaluation for re-transplantation

questions.explanation: This patient is having recurrent bronchial stenosis with significant stenosis of the bronchus intermedius requiring frequent balloon dilation. Given the short-lived effect of the dilation alone, she would benefit from silicone stent placement to maintain airway patency, improve FEV1 and avoid frequent bronchoscopic interventions. Because the most recent lung biopsy did not show any evidence of rejection, the decline in lung function is likely related to the bronchial stenosis, and there is no indication to increase immunosuppression. There is no evidence supporting local steroid injection in benign airway stenosis in the lung transplant recipient. The bronchial stenosis can be managed with bronchoscopic intervention. There is no indication for re-transplantation at this time.
references: (1) A. Ernst and F.J.F. Herth, Principles and Practice of Interventional Pulmonology, ISBN 978-1-4614-4291-2; p 269
(2) Airway stenoses after lung transplantation: Incidence, management and outcome. J Thorac Cardiovasc Surg 2008;136:1569-75 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19114208/
(3) Airway complications and management after lung transplantation: ischemia, dehiscence, and stenosis. Proc Am Thorac Soc. 2009;6(1):79-93 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19131533/

questions.prompt: A 60-year-old man with emphysema is referred to you for consideration of bronchoscopic lung volume reduction (BLVR) using endobronchial valves (EBV). His FEV1 is 30% and TLC is 210%. On chest CT his emphysema appears heterogenous and his fissures appear complete. You see the patient with this StratX report in clinic. Based on the report what do you tell the patient?

options.text: He is not a candidate for BLVR as his FEV1 is too low
He is a candidate for BLVR using EBV to the RUL
He is a candidate for BLVR using EBV to the LUL
Fissure completeness must be confirmed intra-procedurally. Until then you cannot comment.

questions.explanation: Based on an expert panel recommendation, patients considered for BLVR using EBV must have severe or very severe airflow limitation (i.e. FEV1 20-50%), have hyperinflation (RV ≥ 175% or RV/TLC ≥ 0.58) and be symptomatic (grades C and D; CAT scores ≥ 10, mMRC ≥ 2; reduced 6MWD (100-450 m) (Choice A incorrect). High resolution CT helps characterize emphysema heterogeneity and determine fissure integrity. Visual assessment provides only a moderate accuracy in assessment and suffers from inter-observer variability. This is done better on a StratX report (from PulmonX) that provides the percentage of emphysematous destruction in different lobes (< − 910 HU and < − 950 HU), in addition to information on fissure completeness. In most trials, a cut-off of >10-20% difference in the proportion of pixels of < − 910 HU between the target and the ipsilateral non-target lobe was used to define emphysema as heterogenous. In this patient, the left major fissure is complete and the LUL with the highest degree of destruction and volume, suggesting it should be targeted with EBV (choice C correct, B incorrect). Intraprocedural fissure integrity assessment using Chartis system is optional for patients who have >95% fissure integrity (Choice D incorrect).
references: (1) Herth FJF, Slebos DJ, Criner GJ, Valipour A, Sciurba F, Shah PL. Endoscopic lung volume reduction: an expert panel recommendation - update 2019. Respiration. 2019: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30836374/

(2) Mets OM, Smit EJ, Mohamed hoesein FA, et al. Visual versus automated evaluation of chest computed tomography for the presence of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. PLoS ONE. 2012;7(7):e42227. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22848747/

questions.prompt: A 21-year-old woman with refractory asthma but otherwise healthy required intubation and mechanical ventilation. After 10 days on the ventilator, she was extubated and went home. However, her symptoms returned, prompting a visit to an ENT physician who diagnosed the lesion seen below. Which of the following conditions is most likely?

options.text: Idiopathic
Granulomatosis with polyangiitis
Post intubation
Relapsing polychondritis

questions.explanation: This mid tracheal lesion is characteristic of simple web-like cicatricial post-intubation tracheal stenosis. Idiopathic subglottic stenosis occurs most commonly in the subglottic area in middle age women. Granulomatosis with polyangiitis is possible, as lesions can be located anywhere in the airway and be single or multiple. However, such a lesion in this location and without other signs of systemic disease is statistically unlikely to be related to GPA. Relapsing polychondritis stenosis is usually associated with cartilage destruction and associated malacia.
references: (1) Poetker DM, Ettema SL, Blumin JH, Toohill RJ, Merati AL. Association of airway abnormalities and risk factors in 37 subglottic stenosis patients. Otolaryngology--Head and Neck Surgery. 2006;135(3):434-437. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16949978/

questions.prompt: Which of the following was NOT a finding of the landmark AIR-2 trial studying the efficacy of bronchial thermoplasty?

options.text: Improvement in morning peak flow, rescue medication use & FEV1
Significant reductions in severe asthma exacerbations
Significant reductions in emergency department visits
Significant reductions in days or work or school missed

questions.explanation: "The Effectiveness and Safety of Bronchial Thermoplasty in the Treatment of Severe Asthma (AIR2)" trial showed a significant reduction in severe asthma exacerbations in the 12 months following BT in the treated subjects as well as a reduction in days lost from work, school, or other activities due to asthma. Pre-bronchodilator FEV1, morning peak expiratory flow rate, and rescue medication use, were not significantly improved in BT treated subjects compared to sham controls.
references: (1) Castro M, Rubin AS, Laviolette M, et al. Effectiveness and safety of bronchial thermoplasty in the treatment of severe asthma: a multicenter, randomized, double-blind, sham-controlled clinical trial. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2010;181(2):116-124. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19815809/

questions.prompt: A 34-year-old man with a history of a right femur osteosarcoma presents with multiple rapidly growing large lung metastases. After several rounds of chemotherapy and radiation, there is again progression. The patient is referred to you for fairly abrupt onset dyspnea. A CT scan is shown below. You proceed with bronchoscopy and find a lesion originating from the right upper lobe and invading into and causing near-complete airway obstruction from the distal mainstem to mid-bronchus intermedius. Using a combination of ablative and debulking techniques, the tumor is excised, the airway recanalized, and distal purulent secretions cleared. Given the aggressive and recurrent nature of the disease, you decide on placing a partially covered self-expanding metal stent, which terminates in the distal bronchus intermedius as shown. Histology of the resected tumor is consistent with the patient's high-grade osteosarcoma. The patient feels much better after the procedure and is discharged home. Three weeks later, he calls you for new cough and recurrent dyspnea and you proceed with surveillance bronchoscopy which reveals the findings below at the level of the distal bronchus intermedius. The proximal aspect of the stent has similar, albeit milder changes. Which of the following best describes the clinical scenario at this time?

options.text: Biopsy of the new lesion seen will reveal osteosarcoma
The development of this finding increases the chance of a complicated future course
The stent should be immediately removed by grasping its proximal end using flexible forceps
A second, reinforcing stent is indicated
This stent has an increased risk of future migration

questions.explanation: The new lesion depicted in the figure is exuberant circumferential granulation over the distal, uncovered portion of the partially covered metal stent, resulting in clinically significant airway narrowing. Both silicone and metal stents can induce granulation.1,2 Factors contributing to risk of stent-associated granulation or fibrosis include oversizing the stent and presence of infectious bronchitis.3 It is very unlikely the lesion is an osteosarcoma given its location and appearance, as well as time to development after a completely clear distal bronchus intermedius just a few weeks earlier (choice A incorrect). The development of granulation may portend a more complicated future course, especially when associated with airway compromise (choice B correct).3 This includes an increased association with respiratory infections and need for further interventions to manage the granulation tissue. The most effective treatment is removal of the inciting agent, in this case the stent. However, removal of a partially embedded stent may be difficult (choice E incorrect), and should not be performed without ensuring the stent can be easily freed from the airway wall. Using flexible forceps alone is unlikely to be successful in this case, and risks unnecessary airway trauma and / or stent fracture (choice C incorrect). Rigid forceps or the bevel of the rigid scope itself may be needed to free the stent from the airway wall.4 Supplementary methods of managing stent-associated granulation and/or facilitating stent removal include one or a combination of simple debulking, thermal energy, cryotherapy, and photodynamic therapy.4,5 A second reinforcing stent is not needed, as the original stent is functional (choice D incorrect).
references: (1) Ost DE, Shah AM, Lei X, et al. Respiratory infections increase the risk of granulation tissue formation following airway stenting in patients with malignant airway obstruction. Chest. 2012;141(6):1473‐1481. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22194585/
(2) Lunn W, Feller-Kopman D, Wahidi M, Ashiku S, Thurer R, Ernst A. Endoscopic removal of metallic airway stents. Chest. 2005;127(6):2106‐2112. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15947327/
(3) Chung FT, Chen GY, Chou CL, et al. Remove airway ultraflex stents by flexible bronchoscope. Am J Med Sci. 2012;343(4):267‐272. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21825965/

(4) Lee HJ, Labaki W, Yu DH, et al. Airway stent complications: the role of follow-up bronchoscopy as a surveillance method. J Thorac Dis. 2017;9(11):4651‐4659. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29268534/
(5) Sabath BF, Ost DE. Update on airway stents. Curr Opin Pulm Med. 2018;24(4):343‐349. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29538079/

questions.prompt: You are asked to see a smoking 55 years-old man admitted to the MICU with acute respiratory failure. He had been complaining of mild weight loss and worsening orthopnea over the last few months, which had progressed to continuous dyspnea at rest over the last few days. On exam, the patient is intubated and sedated on volume controlled mechanical ventilation. Airway pressures are elevated, with peak pressures in the 50s-60s, with a set maximum inspiratory flow rate of 40L/m. The exam is otherwise non-contributory. ABG is unremarkable on basic ventilator settings. CT scan shows a large mediastinal mass with effect on central airways. You perform flexible bronchoscopy through a 8.5 ETT, and find significant, mostly extrinsic airway compression from mid-distal trachea to the proximal bilateral main bronchi, right involved greater than left. Bronchial mucosa is slightly hyperemic and irregular, but no discrete endobronchial mass is identified. Airways distal to the mainstem bronchi are all normal and patent, but you are barely able to pass the 4.8mm OD bronchoscope past the distal trachea without risking trauma. You proceed with EBUS-guided TBNA of the superior aspect of the mediastinal mass which is clear of the stenotic airway. Bedside (intraoperative) cytology is described as "sheets of cells with large nuclei, scant cytoplasm." You send the rest of the samples for further analysis including for immunohistochemistry and flow cytometry, and are told they will be available in 2-3 days. Of the following, which is the most appropriate management for this patient?

options.text: Endobronchial balloon dilation
Stent placement
Emergent chemoradiation
Tracheostomy tube placement
Await final histopathology results

questions.explanation: This patient has acute and unstable respiratory failure related to malignant central airway obstruction (MCAO). Sampling of the lesion preliminarily suggests small cell carcinoma, though lymphoma or a poorly differentiated tumor remain on the differential diagnosis until further processing of tissue is completed. Given the unstable respiratory status, intervention is required urgently, with a goal of liberating the patient from mechanical ventilation in anticipation of further definitive therapy (choice E incorrect).1,2 Since the obstruction is mostly extrinsic in nature, there is no target for tissue debulking. Balloon dilation is likely to have only a temporary effect, and so stent placement is the most likely modality to benefit this patient (choice B correct, choice A incorrect).3 The type of stent utilized is dictated by the location and extent of the lesion, as well as availability and local expertise. In case stent placement is not feasible, emergent chemotherapy and/or radiation may be attempted. However, if possible this should be deferred until the specific diagnosis is secured (choice C incorrect). Tracheostomy tube placement would not bypass this obstruction (choice D incorrect).
references: (1) Murgu SD, Egressy K, Laxmanan B, Doblare G, Ortiz-Comino R, Hogarth DK. Central Airway Obstruction: Benign Strictures, Tracheobronchomalacia, and Malignancy-related Obstruction. Chest 2016;150:426-41. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26874192/

(2) Ost DE, Ernst A, Grosu HB, et al. Therapeutic bronchoscopy for malignant central airway obstruction: success rates and impact on dyspnea and quality of life. Chest. 2015;147(5):1282‐1298. doi:10.1378/chest.14-1526 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25358019/

(3) Aboudara M, Rickman O, Maldonado F. Therapeutic Bronchoscopic Techniques Available to the Pulmonologist: Emerging Therapies in the Treatment of Peripheral Lung Lesions and Endobronchial Tumors. Clin Chest Med. 2020;41(1):145‐160. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32008626/

questions.prompt: A 25 years-old Caucasian woman with cystic fibrosis related lung disease, DM, and allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis underwent bilateral sequential lung transplantation three months prior. Her surveillance bronchoscopy revealed grade III bronchial wall necrosis with dehiscence of right bronchial anastomosis. Her CT chest showed peribronchial air, no pneumothorax. In addition to starting broad spectrum antibiotics, what would be the most appropriate management?

options.text: Bronchoscopic debridement of necrotic tissue and application of cyanoacrylate glue
Placement of uncovered self-expanding metal stent with plans to replace in two weeks
Placement of silicone stent to cover the anastomosis
Surgical correction of dehiscence

questions.explanation: This patient has grade III bronchial wall dehiscence as evident from bronchoscopy and CT chest. Bronchial dehiscence occurs as a continuum of tissue necrosis at the anastomotic site. The management focuses on interventions to promote granulation tissue formation. Granulation tissue formation is one of the known complications of self-expanding metal stent and this plays a beneficial role in management of bronchial dehiscence. An uncovered self-expanding metal stent is placed first and then replaced when granulation tissue formation starts, typically within 1-3 weeks. This cycle is repeated until the airway dehiscence heals (choice B correct). Use of cyanoacrylate glue has been reported but it does not promote granulation tissue formation and success is variable. Placement of a silicone stent would cover the area of dehiscence and prevent air leak. However, it does not promote granulation tissue formation at the anastomosis site. Also, this patient does not have any significant air leak. Surgical correction of dehiscence is an option but it carries the risk of high mortality and morbidity. A non-surgical approach is more appropriate in this case.
references: (1) Airway complications and management after lung transplantation: ischemia, dehiscence, and stenosis. Proc Am Thorac Soc. 2009;6(1):79-93 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19131533/
(2) A. Ernst and F.J.F. Herth, Principles and Practice of Interventional Pulmonology, ISBN 978-1-4614-4291-2; p 463
(3) The role of self-expandable metallic stents for the treatment of airway complications after lung transplantation. Transplantation. 2003;75(9):1532-8. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/12792510/

questions.prompt: A 68-year-old man with history of frequent hospitalizations and multiple intubations for COPD exacerbation who was admitted to the hospital with progressive worsening shortness of breath over the past few months. He is compliant with his medications and denies any recent respiratory infections. Patient is awake and alert, hemodynamically stable with oxygen saturation in low 90s on RA. Chest x-ray was suggestive of narrowing of trachea. CT of neck and chest revealed tracheal stenosis. Flexible bronchoscopy showed an isolated circumferential cicatricial tracheal stenosis about 3 cm below the cricoid cartilage. What is the definitive treatment for this patient with post-intubation tracheal stenosis (PITS)?

options.text: Surgery
Flexible bronchoscopy and balloon dilation
Rigid bronchoscopy and serial dilation with or without silicon stent placement
Rigid bronchoscopy, serial dilation and Nd-YAG laser therapy

questions.explanation: The subglottic space at the level of cricoid is the narrowest part of the airway. Post intubation tracheal stenosis is a rare but a serious problem.1 The first symptoms could be extubation failure or dysphonia. The role of bronchoscopy is mainly the diagnosis and exclusion of other diseases. Interventional bronchoscopic treatment such as mechanical dilation and stenting, laser ablation and balloon dilation can be used in emergency situations however these techniques have limited success in the definite treatment of complex subglottic stenosis, and are often reserved for non-surgical patients. Brichert et al. reported mechanical dilation and laser ablation was only curative in 66% of web-like stenosis.2 Tracheal resection and end to end anastomosis is the definitive treatment.
references: (1) Brichet A, et al. Multidisciplinary approach to management of postintubation tracheal stenoses. European Respiratory Journal. 1999; 13(4):888-93. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/10362058/
(2) D'Andrilli A., et al. Subglottic tracheal stenosis. Journal of Thoracic Disease. 2016; 8 (Suppl 2): S140-S147. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26981264/

questions.prompt: A 75 year-old man is admitted with hemoptysis (100cc in last 24 hours) arising from a recently diagnosed NSCLC. Patient has severe COPD (home oxygen dependent), and he is pace-maker dependent. During rigid bronchoscopy, a right mainstem tumor is seen. The lesion looks friable, is covered with fresh blood and obstructing 70% of the proximal RMS, with patent distal airway. Patient is being ventilated with 100% FiO2, and his oxygen saturation is 96%. Whoch of the following statements is correct regarding the management of this scenario:

options.text: LASER ablation does not require a decrease in the FiO2 to 40% or lower since it does not utilize thermal energy
CO2 LASER has high coagulative/hemostatic properties and would be the preferred choice in this case compared to Nd-YAG LASER
The depth of penetration of Nd-YAG LASER is influenced, among other things, by the color of the target lesion
LASERs should not be used in patients with pace-makers/AICD since it can interfere with their proper function

questions.explanation: Lasers deliver energy through photons which are coherent, collimated and monochromatic. Tissue effects depend on tissue and laser characteristics, as well as on power and pulse settings and time and distance of application. Laser energy is absorbed and converted to heat energy which leads to protein denaturing, coagulation and direct cell death, while microcoagulation and devascularization leads to ischemic cell death. Vaporization of water content and pressure wave formation results in tissue separation providing the cutting effects of lasers. Lasers, like all thermal therapies require FiO2 to be lowered, typically to less than 40%, to alleviate the risk of airway fire. CO2 lasers have excellent cutting properties however they provide poor coagulative and hemostatic properties. The depth of penetration of all lasers is influence by the color of the target lesion which affects the depth of penetration. Lasers provide energy through photons and not current and therefore they are considered safe with pacemakers/AICDs and should not interfere with function.
references: (1) Miller RJ, Murgu SD. Bronchoscopic Resection of an Exophytic Endoluminal Tracheal Mass. Annals of the American Thoracic Society. 2013;10(6):697-700. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24364776/

(2) Walsh JT, Flotte TJ, Deutsch TF. Er: YAG laser ablation of tissue: effect of pulse duration and tissue type on thermal damage. Lasers in surgery and medicine. 1989;9(4):314-326. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/2761327/

questions.prompt: A 59-year-old woman with unremarkable past medical history was admitted to your institution for altered mental status and acute hypoxemic respiratory failure. CT head upon ED assessment revealed a large left middle cerebral artery (MCA) stroke. While admitted to the Neurosurgical Intensive Care Unit patient acutely decompensates prompting a quick airway inspection through her endotracheal tube (ETT) on admission day 09. You get paged for evaluation of excessive dynamic airway collapse (EDAC). Neuro-ICU team has not been able to extubate due to altered mental status. Patient remains on propofol and fentanyl infusion, with permissive hypertension protocol. You confirm excessive bowing of the posterior membrane of the trachea with increasing levels of PEEP on the ventilator. Per the primary team, neurologic potential remains but the patient will likely require prolonged rehabilitation. What is the best course of action?

options.text: and
Tracheostomy with adjustable tube
Palliative care/hospice care referral

questions.explanation: Excessive dynamic airway collapse (EDAC) describes the excessive bowing of the posterior membranous portion of the trachea on exhalation. The diagnosis is confirmed utilizing dynamic CT chest protocol ( malacia protocol) and/or by direct visualization of the trachea during flexible bronchoscopy with minimal sedation, which allows the patient to follow simple commands and perform expiratory maneuvers. In this patient, while you can see excessive bowing of the membranous portion of the trachea, the procedure was performed under deep sedation (with propofol and fentanyl infusions) and an endotracheal tube. While this is suggestive of the diagnosis, formal comprehensive bronchoscopic evaluation is precluded due to the patient's neurological status [1, 2]. In the absence of a formal EDAC diagnosis and an unclear neurologic recovery potential, options (a), (b) and (c) are inappropriate at present. In addition, patient has been unable to be extubated due to decreased level of consciousness. The tracheostomy with an adjustable trach tube (ie. Bivona) offers the best treatment option for suspected EDAC as it will aid long term weaning and splint the airway open. A more formal evaluation and reassessment of therapeutic options may be completed if and when there is neurologic recovery.
references: (1) Murgu SD and Colt HG. Tracheobronchomalacia and excessive dynamic airway collapse. Clin Chest Med. 2013 Sep;34(3):527-55https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23993822/
(2) Murakami S, Tsuruta S, Ishida K, et al. Excessive dynamic airway collapse during general anesthesia: a case report. JA Clin Rep 2020;6(1):73.https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32989528/

questions.prompt: A 34-year-old female with epilepsy and Tetralogy of Fallot, presents to the emergency department with status epilepticus. She is intubated on arrival and transferred to the intensive care unit. You are consulted urgently because she develops severe subcutaneous emphysema in her upper hemithorax, neck, and face. A bedside diagnostic bronchoscopy is performed showing a large posterior tracheal wall tear. There is no evidence of mediastinitis, esophageal perforation, or pneumothorax. Your thoracic surgery colleagues think that she is at high-risk for complications and defer any surgical intervention at this time. How would you classify this tracheal injury? Figure 1. Endoscopic view of trachea revealing a longitudinal post-intubation tracheal laceration that extends from the proximal to the mid trachea without herniation on expiration.

options.text: Level I
Level II
Level IIIa
Level IIIb

questions.explanation: Tracheobronchial injuries (TBI) are life-threating and can be caused by blunt, penetrating injuries or by iatrogenic procedures (endotracheal intubation, rigid/flexible bronchoscopy, balloon dilation, tracheostomy, and other surgical thoracic interventions).1,2 Bronchoscopy remains the gold standard diagnostic tool for the diagnosis of TBI. A morphologic classification for tracheal wall injuries post endotracheal intubation was proposed by Cardillo et al.:3 Level I: Mucosal or submucosal injury without mediastinal emphysema and esophageal injury Level II: Lesion extending to the muscular wall with subcutaneous or mediastinal emphysema without esophageal injury or mediastinitis Level IIIa: Complete laceration with esophageal or mediastinal soft-tissue herniation without esophageal injury or mediastinitis Level IIIb: Any laceration with esophageal injury or mediastinitis The figure reveals a tracheal injury extending to the muscular wall. The clinical vignette describes the presence of subcutaneous emphysema but no esophageal injury, herniation or mediastinitis. Therefore, it would be classified as level II (choice B correct).
references: (1) Grewal HS, Dangayach NS, Ahmad U, Ghosh S, Gildea T, Mehta AC. Treatment of Tracheobronchial Injuries: A Contemporary Review. Chest. 2019;155(3):595‐604. doi:10.1016/j.chest.2018.07.018 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30059680/
(2) Welter S. Repair of tracheobronchial injuries. Thorac Surg Clin. 2014;24(1):41‐50. doi:10.1016/j.thorsurg.2013.10.006 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24295658/

(3) Cardillo G, Carbone L, Carleo F, et al. Tracheal lacerations after endotracheal intubation: a proposed morphological classification to guide non-surgical treatment. Eur J Cardiothorac Surg. 2010;37(3):581‐587. doi:10.1016/j.ejcts.2009.07.034 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19748275/

questions.prompt: A 70-year-old female is referred for evaluation of a possible right lower lobe endobronchial lesion seen on chest CT obtained for cough. PFT showed moderate obstruction with positive bronchodilator response and mildly reduced DLCO. Bronchoscopy revealed a small, polypoid endobronchial lesion just proximal to the bifurcation of the superior subsegment and basal subsegments of the right lower lobe as seen below. Pathology was consistent with typical carcinoid. EBUS staging was negative and Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT did not reveal any evidence of distant metastatic disease. She is referred for surgical resection but was told she will require sleeve resection with lobectomy and is concerned about undergoing such a procedure. She wants to discuss bronchoscopic resection. If surgical resection is not pursued, which of the following is the most appropriate bronchoscopy surveillance timeline after initial bronchoscopic ablation?

options.text: Repeat bronchoscopy at 6 weeks, then every 6 months for 2 years and then annually thereafter.
Repeat bronchoscopy at 1, 2 and 3 months then every 6 months for 2 years.
Repeat bronchoscopy at 6 weeks, then annually thereafter.
Repeat bronchoscopy at 3 months, then every 6 months thereafter for 5 years.
No repeat bronchoscopy is necessary if complete bronchoscopic resection is achieved.

questions.explanation: Surgical resection with lymph node dissection is considered the standard of care for patients with localized low-or intermediate-grade pulmonary neuroendocrine tumors (NETs). The prognosis for patients with low-grade pulmonary NETs (typical carcinoid) after surgical resection is very good with a reported five-year survival rate close to 90%.(1) Patients who cannot tolerate or decline surgical resection for endobronchial typical carcinoid tumors may be candidates for bronchoscopic resection for palliation or curative intent. Techniques such as electrocautery, APC, laser and cryoablation have been reported.(2,3) It is recommended that patients who opt for this approach undergo surveillance bronchoscopy procedures after initial bronchoscopic resection. ACCP 2013 guidelines recommend surveillance bronchoscopy at 6 weeks post-bronchoscopic resection followed by every 6 months for 2 years and then annually thereafter.(4) B-D are incorrect surveillance bronchoscopy timelines for typical carcinoid tumors with no lymph node metastases post-bronchoscopic resection. E is incorrect as patients with endobronchial carcinoid tumors treated with bronchoscopic resection should be followed regularly to evaluate for any evidence of recurrence or metastatic disease warranting additional treatments.
references: (1) Ferguson MK, Landreneau RJ, Hazelrigg SR, Altorki NK, Naunheim KS, Zwischenberger JB, Kent M, Yim AP. Long-term outcome after resection for bronchial carcinoid tumors. Eur J Cardiothorac Surg. 2000 Aug;18(2):156-61. doi: 10.1016/s1010-7940(00)00493-0. PMID: 10925223. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/10925223/

(2) Bertoletti L, Elleuch R, Kaczmarek D, Jean-François R, Vergnon JM. Bronchoscopic cryotherapy treatment of isolated endoluminal typical carcinoid tumor. Chest. 2006 Nov;130(5):1405-11. doi: 10.1378/chest.130.5.1405. PMID: 17099017. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17099017/

(3) Brokx HA, Paul MA, Postmus PE, Sutedja TG. Long-term follow-up after first-line bronchoscopic therapy in patients with bronchial carcinoids. Thorax. 2015 May;70(5):468-72. doi: 10.1136/thoraxjnl-2014-206753. Epub 2015 Mar 16. PMID: 25777586. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25777586/

(4) Detterbeck FC, Lewis SZ, Diekemper R, Addrizzo-Harris D, Alberts WM. Executive Summary: Diagnosis and management of lung cancer, 3rd ed: American College of Chest Physicians evidence-based clinical practice guidelines. Chest. 2013 May;143(5 Suppl):7S-37S. doi: 10.1378/chest.12-2377. PMID: 23649434. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23649434/

questions.prompt: Which of the following is a reasonable candidate for bronchoscopic lung volume reduction?

options.text: An 87-year-old female with a FEV1 62% predicted, RV 120% predicted, TLC 95% predicted and centrilobular emphysema with a 7mm right upper lobe lung nodule seen on chest CT.
A 62-year-old male with a FEV1 35% predicted, RV 195% predicted, TLC 125% predicted, DLCO 30% predicted and heterogeneous emphysema with complete fissures between all lobes.
A 57-year-old female with a FEV1 32% predicted, RV 180% predicted, TLC 104% predicted, DLCO 35% predicted and chronic hypercapnia requiring non-invasive ventilation.
A 67-year-old male with a FEV1 42% predicted, RV 140% predicted, TLC 92% predicted, DLCO 45% predicted and a chronic productive cough with evidence of bilateral lower lobe bronchiectasis on chest CT.

questions.explanation: Endobronchial valves are increasingly being placed for management of severe emphysema, either as primary treatment after maximizing medical therapy or as a bridge to transplant. They are FDA approved for patients meeting specific criteria with hyperinflation and severe or very severe obstruction. These criteria include: FEV1 >=15<=45% predicted, RV >=175% predicted, TLC >=100% predicted, 6 minute walk distance >150 m and minimal to no collateral ventilation in the targeted treatment lobe. Exclusion criteria include: repeated infections, severe bronchiectasis, coexistent pulmonary pathology on chest CT (such as concerning lung nodules or cavities), unstable cardiovascular co-morbidities, severe hypercapnia or hypoxemia, large bullae and current tobacco use. Careful selection of patients is crucial to optimal patient outcomes.
references: (1) Criner GJ, Sue R, Wright S, Dransfield M, Rivas-Perez H, Wiese T, Sciurba FC, Shah PL, Wahidi MM, de Oliveira HG, Morrissey B, Cardoso PFG, Hays S, Majid A, Pastis N Jr, Kopas L, Vollenweider M, McFadden PM, Machuzak M, Hsia DW, Sung A, Jarad N, Kornaszewska M, Hazelrigg S, Krishna G, Armstrong B, Shargill NS, Slebos DJ; LIBERATE Study Group. A Multicenter Randomized Controlled Trial of Zephyr Endobronchial Valve Treatment in Heterogeneous Emphysema (LIBERATE). Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2018 Nov 1;198(9):1151-1164. doi: 10.1164/rccm.201803-0590OC. PMID: 29787288. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29787288/

(2) Criner GJ, Delage A, Voelker K, Hogarth DK, Majid A, Zgoda M, Lazarus DR, Casal R, Benzaquen SB, Holladay RC, Wellikoff A, Calero K, Rumbak MJ, Branca PR, Abu-Hijleh M, Mallea JM, Kalhan R, Sachdeva A, Kinsey CM, Lamb CR, Reed MF, Abouzgheib WB, Kaplan PV, Marrujo GX, Johnstone DW, Gasparri MG, Meade AA, Hergott CA, Reddy C, Mularski RA, Case AH, Makani SS, Shepherd RW, Chen B, Holt GE, Martel S. Improving Lung Function in Severe Heterogenous Emphysema with the Spiration Valve System (EMPROVE). A Multicenter, Open-Label Randomized Controlled Clinical Trial. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2019 Dec 1;200(11):1354-1362. doi: 10.1164/rccm.201902-0383OC. PMID: 31365298; PMCID: PMC6884033. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31365298/

questions.prompt: A 37-year-old woman with cystic fibrosis underwent lung transplantation 8 weeks ago and is seen in follow-up clinic. You reviewed the hospital record and noted that she was weaned off vasopressors on POD2 and was subsequently extubated on POD3. She was ultimately discharged after 14 days in the hospital. Examination in the clinic showed expiratory wheezing on the left side. Spirometry reveals the following: The patient was scheduled for an urgent bronchoscopy, and the left anastomosis was noted to be narrowed about 75%. The anastomosis was noted to be about 3 cm from the left main carina. The patient underwent balloon dilation with acceptable results, showing about 20% residual narrowing. Bronchoalveolar lavage obtained during the bronchoscopy was positive for Aspergillus fumigatus, and transbronchial biopsy did not reveal evidence of rejection. She was subsequently started on Voriconazole for treatment of her Aspergillus infection. The following week, her spirometry had improved. Which of the following is true regarding this patient's risk factors for developing airway abnormalities after lung transplantation:

options.text: Her fungal infection had no impact on the risk for developing this complication.
If the anastomosis line on the left was divided closer to the donor left main carina, the risk for anastomotic complications could have been reduced.
Bronchial artery revascularization (BAR) is a commonly performed procedure in lung transplantation because it reduces the risk of airway complications.
If the donor and recipient bronchial size are mismatched, "telescoping" anastomosis may need to be performed, which does not impact the risk of developing an airway complication.

questions.explanation: Overall incidence of anastomotic airway complications following lung transplantation ranges from 2% to 33%. Several risk factors have been identified in the literature that increase risk for anastomotic complications. Post-operative fungal infections have been shown to have strong association with airway complications especially when identified in the first 30 days of transplantation (A is incorrect). Surgical strategies during the time of transplantation have been modified in the recent years. "Telescoping" anastomosis (as opposed to end-to-end anastomosis) has been shown to increase risk (D is incorrect). Dividing the donor bronchus closer to the lobar carina (shorter donor bronchus) resulted in reduction in the risk of airway complications (B is correct). Due to the added complexity of the procedure, BAR is not routinely performed in lung transplantation (C is incorrect).
references: (1) Mahajan, A. K., Folch, E., Khandhar, S. J., Channick, C. L., Santacruz, J. F., Mehta, A. C. et al. (2017). The Diagnosis and Management of Airway Complications Following Lung Transplantation. Chest, 152(3), 627-638. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28274791/

questions.prompt: A 65F with severe emphysema and hyperinflation underwent bronchoscopic lung volume reduction (BLVR) with placement of endobronchial valves in the left upper lobe. She was then subsequently admitted as an inpatient for observation. On the second post operative day, she developed severe shortness of breath and left sided chest pain. Chest X-ray is as shown. A 14-French pigtail chest tube was placed and a large air leak is noted. Which of the following is true regarding post-BLVR pneumothorax?

options.text: The patient should undergo another bronchoscopy for removal of all valves implanted for the pneumothorax to resolve.
The source of the air leak in this patient is in the left upper lobe where the valves were implanted.
If the air leak is persistent, valves may need to be placed in the left lower lobe to help resolve the air leak.
Some valves may need to be removed to help resolve the air leak.

questions.explanation: Pneumothorax is common after bronchoscopic lung volume reduction with prevalence of up to > 34% of treated patients. The pathophysiologic mechanisms thought to contribute to its occurrence include rupture of blebs or bullae in the ipsilateral untreated lobe due to sudden shifting of lung volumes (B is incorrect) or pneumothorax ex vacuo, due to a sudden increase in negative intra pleural pressure striving the air from the surrounding extra pleural tissue. Therefore, placing valves in the untreated lobe would not be appropriate to treat the persistent air leak (C is incorrect). Removal of one or 2 of the proximal valves can be considered in the case of persistent air leak (>= 5 days) or if the patient clinically deteriorates (d is correct) to return the target lobe to its pre-treatment state and promote resolution of the persistent air leak (A is incorrect).
references: (1) van Dijk, M., Sue, R., Criner, G. J., Gompelmann, D., Herth, F. J. F., Hogarth, D. K. et al. (2021). Expert Statement: Pneumothorax Associated with One-Way Valve Therapy for Emphysema: 2020 Update. Respiration, 1-10.

(2) Gompelmann D, Lim HJ, Eberhardt R, Gerovasili V, Herth FJ, Heussel CP, Eichinger M. Predictors of pneumothorax following endoscopic valve therapy in patients with severe emphysema. Int J Chron Obstruct Pulmon Dis. 2016 Aug 1;11:1767-73. doi: 10.2147/COPD.S106439. PMID: 27536088; PMCID: PMC4976918.

(3) Valipour A, Slebos DJ, de Oliveira HG, Eberhardt R, Freitag L, Criner GJ, Herth FJ. Expert statement: pneumothorax associated with endoscopic valve therapy for emphysema--potential mechanisms, treatment algorithm, and case examples. Respiration. 2014;87(6):513-21. doi: 10.1159/000360642. Epub 2014 Apr 24. PMID: 24777292.

questions.prompt: A 35 year-old female with severe persistent asthma and on maximal medical therapy presents to your office for evaluation for bronchial thermoplasty. She inquires about the long-term effects of the procedure. Based on published evidence, which of the following statements is true regarding the effects of bronchial thermoplasty?

options.text: Patients that received bronchial thermoplasty had increased hospitalizations and ER visits 10 years after receiving the procedure.
The majority of patients who received bronchial thermoplasty had evidence of bronchiectasis on follow up CT scans.
Quality of life was significantly improved 10 years after bronchial thermoplasty.
The proportion of severe exacerbations 10 years after the procedure was similar to the proportion of severe exacerbations 1 year after the procedure.

questions.explanation: The BT10+ is a multicenter, greater than 10 year follow up study on subjects that were previously enrolled in the AIR, AIR2 and RISA trials which were randomized controlled trials evaluating the efficacy of bronchial thermoplasty. Results of the BT10+ study showed that the effects of bronchial themroplasty are sustained over 10 years. There were no increases in hospitalizations or ER visits (Choice A is incorrect) and the proportion of severe exacerbations were stable compared to a year after the procedure (Choice D is correct). Quality of life results were comparable between year 1, 5 and 10+ (Choice C is incorrect). Only a small percentage of patients (7%) developed and had evidence of bronchiectasis on CT chest after the procedure (Choice B is incorrect).
references: (1) Chaudhuri R et al. Safety and effectiveness of bronchial thermoplasty after 10 years in patients with persistent asthma (BT10+): A follow-up of three randomised controlled trials. Lancet Respir Med 2021 May; 9:457. https://doi.org/10.1016/S2213-2600(20)30408-2 https://erj.ersjournals.com/content/54/suppl_63/RCT3782

(2) Castro M, Rubin AS, Laviolette M, et al. Effectiveness and safety of bronchial thermoplasty in the treatment of severe asthma: a multicenter, randomized, double-blind, sham-controlled clinical trial. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2010;181(2):116-124. doi:10.1164/rccm.200903-0354OC https://www.atsjournals.org/doi/full/10.1164/rccm.200903-0354OC

(3) Cox G, Thomson N, Rubin A, Niven R, Corris P, Siersted H, Olivenstein R, Pavord I, McCormack D, Chaudhuri R, et al., for the AIR Trial Study Group. Asthma control during the year after bronchial thermoplasty. N Engl J Med 2007;356:1327-1337. https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/nejmoa064707

(4) Pavord I, Cox G, Thomson N, Rubin A, Corris P, Niven R, Chung K, Laviolette M, for the RISA Trial Study Group. Safety and efficacy of bronchial thermoplasty in symptomatic, severe asthma. Am J Respir Crit Care Med 2007;176:1185-1191. https://www.atsjournals.org/doi/pdf/10.1164/rccm.200704-571OC

questions.prompt: A 63-year-old man with minimal prior medical history, remote prior tobacco (he quit a 5-year pack history over 30 years ago) and occasional alcohol intake on imaging was found to have a large mediastinal mass ultimately diagnosed as esophageal squamous cell carcinoma. Five weeks after initiating concomitant FOLFOX and local radiation he presents to the emergency room for acute onset of dyspnea over the last 24 hours, for which you are called. Repeat imaging in the emergency room is demonstrated in figure 1. What should your next step be?

options.text: Start Amoxicllin-clavulanate
Tracheostomy and percutaneous gastrostomy tube placement
Urgent bronchoscopy with stent placement
Consult with advanced gastro-endoscopists
Urgent bronchoscopy for a cardiac septal occlusive device

questions.explanation: This is an acquired malignant tracheoesophageal fistula (TEF) in the setting of esophageal cancer recently treated with first line concomitant chemo-radiation. Esophageal cancers are complicated by TEF in 4.5-12.5% of cases and in 0.3% of lung cancer. Though uncommon, TEF carries a high mortality rate with significant morbidity. TEF related symptoms include shortness of breath, infectious like symptoms from aspiration, halitosis and bleeding. In as often as 24% of patients therapy can lead to TEF formation by regression of tumor. Risk factors associated with TEF formation has been advanced local and lymph node staging, diminished esophageal elastance and extended patient longevity. Early case control studies have demonstrated the potential role of early intervention to extend survival. Definitive therapy includes surgical correction; however, this is often limited by poor nutritional status of the patient. Current guidelines recommend endoscopic interventions as the mainstay of therapy with esophageal stenting being the first line of therapy. However esophageal stenting is limited for higher lesions and is best used for the distal mid to 2/3 thirds esophagus. Endobronchial stenting is an additional option for TEF, especially in higher lesions and when there is concern for airway impingement from esophageal stenting. Alternative endoscopic approaches such as endoscopic glue instillation can have short lived effect. Placement of cardiac septal occlusive device is supported by anecdotal data, though its use may be limited given concern for airway erosion, especially when radiation therapy effect may persist for weeks after completion, leading to further propagation of the TEF defect (Choice E is incorrect). In this patient GI endoscopists were consulted but deemed the lesion too high, therefore a silicone tracheal stent was placed with good defect coverage. An interdisciplinary consultation is the most appropriate prior to proceeding (Choice D is correct and Choice C is incorrect). Additional therapeutic considerations include early percutaneous alimentation, which has been shown to stabilize weight loss when used early, however this would not be the next best step. Antibiotics are reasonable to use with evidence of clinical pneumonia, which this patient does not demonstrate (Choice A is incorrect). Tracheostomy has not been shown to benefit TEF management unless there is concomitant vocal cord paralysis (which can occur with TEF due to recurrent laryngeal nerve paralysis, Choice B is incorrect).
references: (1) Burt M Ann Thorac Surg 1991 - Ann Thorac Surg 1991;52:1222-9 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/1755674/
(2) Balazs A Eur Jour Cardio Thorac Surg 2008 - European Journal of Cardio-thoracic Surgery 34 (2008) 1103
(3) Takahiro T Medicine 2016 - doi: 10.1097/MD.0000000000003699 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27196482/
(4) Xu Y Radiation Oncology 2019 - Radiation Oncology (2019) 14:181 https://doi.org/10.1186/s13014-019-1385-y
(5) Sharma P, Am J Gastroenterol 2010 - Am J Gastroenterol. 2010 Feb;105(2):258-73; quiz 274. doi: 10.1038/ajg.2009.684. Epub 2009 Dec 22. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20029413/
(6) Spaander MCW, Endoscopy, 2016 - Endoscopy. 2016 Oct;48(10):939-48. doi: 10.1055/s-0042-114210. Epub 2016 Sep 14. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27626318/
(7) Kvale PA, Chest, Chest. 2007 Sep;132(3 Suppl):368S-403S. doi: 10.1378/chest.07-1391. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17873181/
(8) Miller PE Annals ATS 2014 https://doi.org/10.1513/AnnalsATS.201408-352LE
(9) De Moura DTH Ann Clin Endosc 2020 - Clin Endosc 2020;53:37-48 https://doi.org/10.5946/ce.2019.030 https://www.e-ce.org/journal/view.php?doi=10.5946/ce.2019.030

questions.prompt: The COPD expert physician in your division approaches you to discuss one of his clinic patients. This is a 63 years-old male with chronic medical conditions including COPD with chronic bronchitis (CB) phenotype, complete heart block s/p permanent pacemaker placement, and diabetes mellitus complicated with gastroparesis. He is on maximal medical therapy for CB and remains symptomatic. Your colleague has read there are promising bronchoscopic treatment options and would like your opinion. He recently performed a flexible bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage under general anesthesia and his patient tolerated very well. Based on the most recent literature suggesting efficacy, and if and when FDA approved, which treatment would you recommend:

options.text: Referral to center of excellence for bronchial rheoplasty
Referral to center of excellence for targeted lung denervation
Referral to center of excellence for metered cryospray
A, B and C are correct, therefore refer to any center
There is no evidence supporting the efficacy of any of the above treatments

questions.explanation: There are significant developments in the treatment of chronic bronchitis. Currently, there are 3 innovative bronchoscopic options under investigation (not approved by the FDA yet): bronchial rheoplasty, targeted lung denervation and metered cryospray [1]. Based on exclusion criteria, patient would not be a candidate for bronchial rheoplasty due to his permanent pacemaker (option A is incorrect). He would not be an ideal candidate for targeted lung denervation as he has gastroparesis (option B is incorrect). Metered cryospray would benefit him (Choice C is correct), particularly if his Saint George Respiratory Questionnaire score is > 50 [2]. Option E is incorrect as there is promising data for these bronchoscopic treatment modalities already.
references: (1) Hartman JE, Garner JL, Shah PL, et al. New bronchoscopic treatment modalities for patients with chronic bronchitis. Eur Respir Rev 2021;30:200281 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33472961/

(2) Garner JL, Shaipanich T, Hartman JE, et al. A prospective safety and feasibility study of metered cryospray for patients with chronic bronchitis in COPD. Eur Respir J 2020;56:2000556 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32586881/

questions.prompt: A 57 year old female patient with severe persistent asthma on maximal medical therapy including biologic agents is referred to you for further assessment. Prior esophagram and pH manometry revealed uncontrolled reflux disease, which significantly improved after Nissen fundoplication surgery. Bronchoscopy does not find any 'asthma mimickers' such as vocal cord dysfunction, severe excessive dynamic airway collapse, or unsuspected airway stenosis or tumors. You and the patient's asthma specialist confer about offering bronchial thermoplasty. Considering the most recent literature, you would advise the patient as follows:

options.text: Bronchial thermoplasty is safe and effective 5 - 10 years after treatment
Bronchial thermoplasty works better for certain asthma phenotypes
Bronchial thermoplasty has prohibitive procedure-related serious adverse events
Bronchial thermoplasty has not been used in patients with more severe disease
Bronchial thermoplasty does not reduce use of oral corticosteroids

questions.explanation: Bronchial thermoplasty (BT) is safe and effective on long term follow-up studies [1, 2]. There is no definite evidence BT works better in certain asthma phenotypes (choice B is incorrect), though it could be more useful in patients with high eosinophil count, atopy, allergy and without smoke exposure (T2 high phenotype) [3]. It is well known that asthma exacerbations can occur after bronchial thermoplasty. Chupp et al [1] describe 4 out of 284 patients requiring intubation and mechanical ventilation (1.4%), 2 of them for hemoptysis (choice C is incorrect). Langton et al [4] describe their experience with patients with severe persistent asthma with FEV1 < 50%, with good outcomes (choice D is incorrect). In Chupp's study [1], the proportion of subjects on maintenance oral corticosteroids decreased from 19.4% at baseline to 9.7% at 5 years (choice E is incorrect).
references: (1) Chupp G, Kline JN, Khatri SB, et al. Bronchial Thermoplasty in Patients with Severe Asthma at 5 years. The Post-FDA Approval Clinical Trial Evaluating Bronchial Thermoplasty in Severe Persistent Asthma Study. Chest 2022;1161(3):614-628 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34774528/

(2) Chaudhuri R, Rubin A, Sumino K, et al. Safety and effectiveness of bronchial thermoplasty after 10 years in patients with persistent asthma (BT10+): a follow-up of three randomized controlled trials. Lancet Respir Med 2021;9:457-66 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33524320/

(3) Papakonstantinou E, Koletsa T, Zhou L, et al. Bronchial thermoplasty in asthma: an exploratory histopathological evaluation in distinct asthma endotypes/phenotypes. Respir Res 2021;22:186 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34183014/
(4) Langton D, Ing A, Fielding D, et al. Safety and Effectiveness of Bronchial Thermoplasty When FEV1 is Less than 50%. Chest 2020;157(3):509-515 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31525358/

questions.prompt: A 75-year-old woman with history of right lung carcinoid tumor presented with hemoptysis. During bronchoscopy, a right middle lobe endobronchial lesion was confirmed as the source of hemoptysis. APC was performed with argon flow rate of 2 L/min and power of 40 W, and hemostasis was achieved. Moments following termination of APC, the patient became bradycardic and hypotensive, followed by Ventricular fibrillation and arrest. CPR was initiated, and when return of spontaneous circulation was not achieved, an emergent TEE showed presence of a large gas bubble in the left ventricle. What are some ways to minimize incidence of gas embolism during airway debulking using APC.

options.text: Minimize gas flow rates
Using power less than 20W
Decreasing supplied FiO2 to less than 30%
Position patient in reverse Trendelenburg position

questions.explanation: Cardiovascular complications or changes are not uncommon during therapeutic bronchoscopy and are often attributed to the high-risk patient population who may have not just tobacco-induced lung disease but also coronary artery disease with increased operative risk. The risk of systemic gas embolism may be influenced by the rate of gas flow exiting the tip of the probe. Systemic air embolism can occur with gas flow rates of 2, 1.5, and 1 L/min. The ideal gas flow rate for APC use in airways is unknown, and the current recommendations are to set the flow to lowest possible rate to prevent gas embolism (Choice A is correct). Using less than 20W of power or lower FiO2 are not known to be associated with decreased rates of gas embolism (Choices B and C are incorrect). One of the treatments for patients with gas embolism includes hyperbaric oxygen treatment. Reverse Trendelenburg position is suggested as a strategy to prevent of air embolism during central line insertion or removal from internal jugular and subclavian veins. This does not apply to gas embolism resulting from endobronchial ablation (Choice D is incorrect).
references: (1) Reddy C, Majid A, Michaud G, Feller-Kopman D, Eberhardt R, Herth F, Ernst A. Gas embolism following bronchoscopic argon plasma coagulation: a case series. Chest. 2008 Nov;134(5):1066-1069. doi: 10.1378/chest.08-0474. PMID: 18988782. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18988782/

(2) Feller-Kopman D, Lukanich JM, Shapira G, Kolodny U, Schori B, Edenfield H, Temelkuran B, Ernst A, Schindel Y, Fink Y, Fox J, Bueno R. Gas flow during bronchoscopic ablation therapy causes gas emboli to the heart: a comparative animal study. Chest. 2008 Apr;133(4):892-6. doi: 10.1378/chest.07-2266. Epub 2008 Jan 15. PMID: 18198247. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18198247/

questions.prompt: As you start your interventional pulmonology (IP) career after fellowship training, you are tasked to establish the advanced emphysema clinic in your institution, with your focus on bronchoscopic lung volume reduction (BLVR). Your institution already has Medicare and Medicaid designation for surgical lung volume reduction (SLVR). When going through inclusion/exclusion criteria with the Value Analysis committee, a member asks you: 'Imagine you have a patient that qualifies both for bronchoscopic and surgical lung volume reduction. Which one would you recommend? Do you have any head-to-head data on health outcomes from both procedures? Based on the most recent literature, you answer:

options.text: There is no head-to-head comparison data between BLVR and SLVR
BLVR has superior health outcomes when compared to SLVR
SLVR has superior health outcomes when compared to SLVR
BLVR and SLVR may have similar health outcomes

questions.explanation: BLVR inclusion/exclusion criteria are derived from the NETT trial [1]. There is medical literature describing the effectiveness of BLVR for patients with severe emphysema. Until recently, there was a dearth of literature comparing health outcomes in patients that otherwise would have qualified both for BLVR and SLVR. The 'CELEB' trial was a randomized controlled single-blind superiority study [2] that allocated 88 patients to either BLVR (n=47) or SLVR (n=41). It compared outcomes at year one post procedure using the I-BODE score. At 12 months, there was no significant difference in I-BODE scores or per individual components. Careful discussion in the outpatient clinic setting is necessary to determine which procedure a patient would prefer if they qualify for both BLVR and SLVR.
references: (1) Fishman A, Martinez F, Naunheim K, et al (National Emphysema Treatment Trial Research Group. A Randomized Trial Comparing Lung-Volume-Reduction Surgery with Medical Therapy for Severe Emphysema. N Engl J Med 2003;348:2059-73.

(2) Buttery SC, Banya W, Bilancia R, et al. Lung Volume Reduction Surgery Versus Endobronchial Valves: A Randomised Controlled Trial. Eur Respir J 2023; in press (https://doi.org/10.1183/13993003.02063-2022).

questions.prompt: A 43-year-old gentleman with advanced stage lung cancer is undergoing a rigid bronchoscopy procedure for tumor debulking. Rigid bronchoscopic intubation was uneventful. After 10-minutes into general anesthesia and jet ventilation, the high-pressure alarm has triggered. The jet ventilator and tubing were examined. What is the next step in management of the high-pressure alarm? (ADJUSTED)

options.text: Place a chest tube for tension pneumothorax
Reduce the driving pressure of the jet ventilator
Reduce frequency rate of the jet ventilator
Increase frequency rate of the jet ventilator
Correct the kinked ventilator tubing and reassess

questions.explanation: When a high-pressure alarm is faced during jet ventilation a thorough and methodological assessment of the causes of such an alarm should be performed. This starts with evaluating the jet ventilation machine and the connection to the rigid bronchoscope. In this case the ventilator tubing is kinked and is possibly the cause of the high-pressure alarm (Choice E is correct). While pneumothorax is still a possibility, especially with using jet ventilation, the possibility of pneumothorax should be assessed if the machine continues to show a high-pressure alarm despite correcting the kinked tubing (Choice A is incorrect). Adjusting the driving pressure or frequency would not be helpful in this situation (Choices B, C, and D are incorrect)

questions.prompt: What is the most efficient method for monitoring progression of laryngotracheal stenosis?

options.text: peak flow meter (PFM)
computed tomography of the neck without contrast
Surveillance bronchoscopy
spirometry
6MWT

questions.explanation: Spirometry, 6MWT and pulmonary function testing was first described in 1960 to follow gradual change in the degree of stenosis over time. Recent studies from the same group have found that changes in peak flow measurements was highly sensitive and specific for following the degree of stenosis. Moreover, peak flow measures help differentiate laryngotracheal stenosis from other illnesses that present with similar symptoms (i.e. COPD, asthma, and pulmonary fibrosis). Answers b and c are accurate, yet less efficient methods to evaluate for LTS.
references: (1) Carpenter DJ, Ferrante S, Bakos SR, Clary MS, et al. Utility of routine spirometry for surveillance of idiopathic subglottic stenosis. JAMA Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg. 2019;145(1) https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6439814/

questions.prompt: A 60-year-old male with history of 40 pack year smoking who quit smoking 6 months prior came for bronchoscopic lung volume reduction evaluation. The patient is currently on optimal medical therapy and has received Pulmonary rehabilitation. His 6-minute walk distance (6MWD) is 180 meters. He fulfills other criteria for lung volume reduction. You reviewed CT chest and noted that patient has homogenous emphysema. The patient asks about long-term benefits of endobronchial valve (EBV) placement. What insight will you provide the patient about the effect of EBV placement in the setting of homogenous emphysema?

options.text: At 3 months, the 6MWD will increase by 40 meters as compared to baseline prior to EBV placement.
On average, the patient should expect that at 6 months his 6MWD will be 28 meters more than if he did not have valve placed.
As compared to 6 months after EBV placement, at 12 months his 6MWD will remain same.
At 12 months, mean improvement in FEV1 is likely to be 13.7% as compared to baseline.

questions.explanation: Dyspnea on exertion is one of the limiting symptoms in COPD. Bronchodilators can be useful to alleviate some of the symptoms. But as COPD progresses, those medication can have limited use, particularly in those with hyperinflation and air trapping. Lung volume reduction surgery and EBV are well studied in heterogenous emphysema. The IMPACT study evaluated use of EBV in homogenous emphysema, with short term outcomes evaluated up to 3 months. At 3 months, patients were able to walk 22 meters more than prior to EBV placement (patient's baseline) and 40 meters more than patients without EBV placement (Choice A is incorrect). Eberhardt et al. reported the one year outcomes of the IMPACT trial. As compared to a patient without EBV therapy, six-minute walk distance remained higher by 28 meters (Choice B is correct). Improvement in six-minute walk test was similar to that at 3 months (21 meters as compared to baseline). However, at 12 months, six-minute distance was only 4 meters higher than baseline (Choice C is incorrect). As compared to baseline, mean FEV1 improvement at 3 month was 13.7%. But at 12 months, mean FEV1 improvement declined to 8.29% (Choice D is incorrect).
references: (1) Valipour A, Slebos DJ, Herth F, Darwiche K, Wagner M, Ficker JH, Petermann C, Hubner RH, Stanzel F, Eberhardt R; IMPACT Study Team. Endobronchial Valve Therapy in Patients with Homogeneous Emphysema. Results from the IMPACT Study. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2016 Nov 1;194(9):1073-1082. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27580428/

(2) Eberhardt R, Slebos DJ, Herth FJF, Darwiche K, Wagner M, Ficker JH, Petermann C, Hübner RH, Stanzel F, Shargill NS, Valipour A; IMPACT Study Team. Endobronchial Valve (Zephyr) Treatment in Homogeneous Emphysema: One-Year Results from the IMPACT Randomized Clinical Trial. Respiration. 2021;100(12):1174-1185.https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34350884/

modules.title: Airway Disease
modules.description: Diagnostic and therapeutic pathways for central and peripheral airway disease.

questions.prompt: You are evaluating a patient with a high likelihood for a right-sided lung cancer, with asymptomatic cerebral and bone lesions that are highly suspicious for metastases. The primary lesion is not easily accessible bronchoscopically, but there are several suspicious thoracic lymph nodes, as follows: station 4R=9mm, station 11R=18mm, station 7=24mm, station 4L=11mm. Your plan is to sample the nodes with EBUS guidance, but just as your patient has been sedated your technician informs you that the EBUS scope seems to be damaged and is non-functional. Your reserve EBUS scope is being used by your colleague. How should you proceed?

options.text: Abort the procedure and reschedule.
Proceed with the procedure and plan to sample all nodal stations listed above, starting with 4R, using conventional TBNA and supported by rapid on-site evaluation
Proceed with the procedure and sample the lymph nodes using conventional TBNA, starting with station 7, and supported by rapid on-site evaluation
Proceed with the procedure and plan to sample all nodal stations listed above, starting with 4L, using conventional TBNA and without rapid on-site evaluation
Abort the procedure and schedule the patient for sampling of suspected extrathoracic metastases

questions.explanation: A key point of this question is recognizing that diagnosis is the primary goal. This patient with likely right-sided lung cancer has multifocal distant lesions that almost certainly represent metastases, and so clinical stage IV disease is already established. Only tumor characterization is required. In this situation, options include proceeding with TBNA of the thoracic nodes without EBUS (conventional or 'blind' TBNA), wait for another EBUS scope or the broken scope to be repaired, or use another approach for sampling. The latter includes transthoracic biopsy of the primary lung lesion (not listed in the answer choices) or biopsy of the suspected metastatic lesions. Sampling extrathoracic metastases, especially when easily accessible, is a very reasonable alternative to bronchoscopy when the likelihood of malignancy is high, as it avoids the need for sedation or anesthesia.1 However, depending on the organ involved, this approach can also have disadvantages. A cerebral biopsy is more invasive than bronchoscopy, and bone lesions may be somewhat inaccessible or offer a lower yield (sample processing may compromise integrity of tumor tissue for genetic analysis). As the patient is already sedated it is reasonable to proceed with bronchoscopy in this case (choice A and E incorrect). Choices B, C, and D all involve using bronchoscopic conventional TBNA (cTBNA) of the nodes. cTBNA is inferior to EBUS-TBNA performance for mediastinal assessment at all nodal stations except station 7.2 Furthermore, cTBNA data suggest yields are maximized when the nodes are large (as in this case) and rapid on-site evaluation (ROSE) is used.3,4 Since diagnosis is the main goal for this patient and the largest, most suspicious node is station 7, starting at this site with cTBNA supported by ROSE is a very reasonable and efficient alternative to EBUS-TBNA of the same lymph node (choice C correct). Choice D describes sequence of nodal sampling if mediastinal staging was the goal, which it isn't in this case. Furthermore, the yield of cTBNA at all other nodal stations besides 7 may be suboptimal, especially without ROSE. There is also a risk of major vessel puncture (particularly at stations 4L and 11R). Therefore, waiting for a functioning EBUS scope in this circumstance may be prudent. Choice B describes an incorrect order of mediastinal staging for this patient, and starting with a node (4R) that isn't the most likely to yield a diagnosis with cTBNA.
references: (1) Silvestri GA, Gonzalez AV, Jantz MA, et al. Methods for staging non-small cell lung cancer: Diagnosis and management of lung cancer, 3rd ed: American College of Chest Physicians evidence-based clinical practice guidelines. Chest 2013;143:e211S-e50S. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23649440/

(2) Madan K, Dhungana A, Mohan A, et al. Conventional Transbronchial Needle Aspiration Versus Endobronchial Ultrasound-guided Transbronchial Needle Aspiration, With or Without Rapid On-Site Evaluation, for the Diagnosis of Sarcoidosis: A Randomized Controlled Trial. J Bronchology Interv Pulmonol. 2017;24(1):48‐58. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27984385/

(3) Madan NK, Madan K, Jain D, et al. Utility of conventional transbronchial needle aspiration with rapid on-site evaluation (c-TBNA-ROSE) at a tertiary care center with endobronchial ultrasound (EBUS) facility. J Cytol. 2016;33(1):22‐26. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27011437/

(4) Rivera MP, Mehta AC, Wahidi MM. Establishing the diagnosis of lung cancer: Diagnosis and management of lung cancer, 3rd ed: American College of Chest Physicians evidence-based clinical practice guidelines. Chest 2013;143:e142S-e65S. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23649436/

questions.prompt: For which of the following clinical scenarios is diagnostic bronchoscopy MOST appropriate as a next step in evaluation?

options.text: A patient from the Mississippi river valley in the USA with a history of histoplasmosis and an indistinct conglomerate subcarinal mass
A middle lobe calcified nodule with calcified station 11R lymph node in a smoking individual
A patient with chronically elevated brain naturietic peptide and bilateral interstitial-dominant lung infiltrates with PET-negative mediastinal adenopathy
Segmental atelectasis in a patient requiring mechanical ventilation for COVID-19
Massive hemoptysis in a patient with wheezing and progressing dyspnea over 4 weeks

questions.explanation: Choice A represents a patient with fibrosing mediastinitis (FM). FM results from an exaggerated inflammatory response to infections or neoplasms, leading to either exuberant proliferation of fibrous tissue beyond the limits of lymph nodes, or an encapsulated mass of granulomatous lymph nodes that could compress or adhere to other adjacent thoracic structures.1 Although bronchoscopy could be attempted for diagnostic purposes, its yield is low, and the diagnosis is usually made primarily using CT chest imaging.2 Airway interventions in asymptomatic patients with fibrosing mediastinitis should be avoided as there is higher risk of bleeding due to concomitant collateral engorged vessels and increased granulation tissue formation when airway stents are used (choice A incorrect). Calcified lymph nodes can result from tuberculosis, recurrent nodal inflammation subsequent to pulmonary fungus infection, chronic bronchitis, bronchiectasis, and chronic obstructive pulmonary diseases.3 Sampling of calcified lymph nodes via EBUS-TBNA has been associated with inadequate specimen collection and false negative results (choice B incorrect).4 Patients with acute fluid overload might develop "congestive lymphadenopathy."5 This abnormal finding, in most cases, resolves with adequate diuresis.2,5 Therefore, follow up imaging, rather than an invasive procedure, should be considered in the appropriate clinical context (i.e. in patients with acute fluid overload and low risk for malignancy, choice C incorrect). Atelectasis in dependent basilar pulmonary areas is not uncommon in ICU patients requiring mechanical ventilation of any cause. The recommended treatment for this is chest (kinetic and percussion) physiotherapy, positive end-expiratory pressure support, dornase-alfa instillation, and bronchodilators.2 Only in cases where there is evidence of distal bronchial obstruction leading to atelectasis, intolerance to, or failure of initial interventions, bronchoscopy could be considered (choice D incorrect). Massive hemoptysis is a life-threatening airway emergency.6 Bronchoscopy can be lifesaving when the etiology is an actively bleeding endotracheal or bronchial tumor via isolating the "good lung", identifying the source of bleeding with simultaneous hemostatic interventions, and securing the airway for a more definitive therapy (bronchial arterial embolization or surgery, choice E correct).
references: (1) Deshwal H, Avasarala SK, Ghosh S, Mehta AC. Forbearance With Bronchoscopy: A Review of Gratuitous Indications. Chest. 2019;155(4):834‐847. doi:10.1016/j.chest.2018.08.1035 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30171862/

(2) Cardenas-Garcia J, Feller-Kopman D. POINT: Should All Initial Episodes of Hemoptysis Be Evaluated by Bronchoscopy? Yes. Chest. 2018 Feb;153(2):302-305. doi: 10.1016/j.chest.2017.09.036. Epub 2017 Oct 10. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29029994/

(3) Li Z, Li Y, Wang L, et al. Management of calcified hilar lymph nodes during thoracoscopic lobectomies: avoidance of conversions. J Thorac Dis. 2019 Mar;11(3):657-663. doi: 10.21037/jtd.2019.02.71. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31019752/

(4) Goodwin RA, Nickell JA, Des Prez RM. Mediastinal fibrosis complicating healed primary histoplasmosis and tuberculosis. Medicine (Baltimore). 1972;51(3):227‐246. doi:10.1097/00005792-197205000-00008 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/4623262/

(5) Khalil A, Fedida B, Parrot A, Haddad S, Fartoukh M, Carette MF. Severe hemoptysis: from diagnosis to embolization. Diagn Interv Imaging. 2015;96(7-8):775-788. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26141487/

(6) Kennedy, M; Jimenez, CA. Morice, RC, et al. Factors Influencing the Diagnostic Yield of Endobronchial Ultrasound-guided Transbronchial Needle Aspiration, J Bronchology Interv Pulmonol. 2010 Jul;17(3):202-8. doi: 10.1097/LBR.0b013e3181e70007. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23168884/

(7) Chabbert, V., Canevet, G., Baixas, C. et al. Mediastinal lymphadenopathy in congestive heart failure: a sequential CT evaluation with clinical and echocardiographic correlations. Eur Radiol 14, 881-889 (2004). https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/14689226/
