questions.prompt: Which of the following ultrasound characteristics can obviate the need for biopsy of lymph nodes > 5mm in short axis diameter on EBUS examination?

options.text: Round shape
Indistinct borders
Heterogeneous echogenicity
Presence of central hilar structure
None of the above

questions.explanation: One large study found that echo characteristics of lymph nodes including round shape, distinct margins, heterogeneous echogenicity, absence of central hilar structure, and presence of coagulation necrosis sign were independently predictive for malignancy. However, none of these features obviates the need for biopsy. The 2013 ACCP recommendations are that for mediastinal staging all lymph node stations >5mm in short axis diameter on CT scan should be sampled. Rather, the value of these echo characteristics include guiding the bronchoscopist to the most suspicious node at a station with multiple nodes or when deciding to target a small node.
references: (1) Silvestri GA, Gonzalez AV, Jantz MA, et al. Methods for staging non-small cell lung cancer: Diagnosis and management of lung cancer, 3rd ed: American College of Chest Physicians evidence-based clinical practice guidelines. Chest 2013;143:e211S-e50S. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23649440/

(2) De Leyn P, Dooms C, Kuzdzal J, et al. Revised ESTS guidelines for preoperative mediastinal lymph node staging for non-small-cell lung cancer. European Journal of Cardio-Thoracic Surgery. 2014;45(5):787-798. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24578407/

(3) Fujiwara T, Yasufuku K, Nakajima T, et al. The utility of sonographic features during endobronchial ultrasound-guided transbronchial needle aspiration for lymph node staging in patients with lung cancer: a standard endobronchial ultrasound image classification system. Chest. 2010;138(3):641-647. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20382710/

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements regarding the practice of EBUS-TBNA for the diagnosis and mediastinal staging of suspected lung cancer is correct?

options.text: A minimum of 5 passes (samples) per lymph node is needed in the absence of ROSE
The use of ROSE has not been associated with a greater diagnostic yield
A larger needle (i.e. 21G vs 22G) will increase the yield for malignancy
Suction has been associated with a higher diagnostic yield when compared with the no-suction or "capillary" technique

questions.explanation: A 2008 study found that in the absence of ROSE diagnostic yield from EBUS-TBNA plateaus after 3 passes and there has been no convincing data supporting an increased diagnostic yield with the use of ROSE, although ROSE has been associated with a reduction in peripheral biopsies and hence a decrease in pneumothorax rate. Randomized trials comparing a 21-gauge to 22-gauge EBUS needle have not demonstrated differences in diagnostic yield or sample adequacy. Finally, Casal et al. compared suction vs no-suction or "capillary" technique and did not demonstrate a difference in diagnostic yield.
references: (1) Casal RF, Staerkel GA, Ost D, et al. Randomized clinical trial of endobronchial ultrasound needle biopsy with and without aspiration. Chest. 2012;142(3):568-573. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22156610/

(2) Oki M, Saka H, Kitagawa C, et al. Rapid on-site cytologic evaluation during endobronchial ultrasound-guided transbronchial needle aspiration for diagnosing lung cancer: a randomized study. Respiration. 2013;85(6):486-492. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23208623/

(3) van der Heijden EH, Casal RF, Trisolini R, et al. Guideline for the acquisition and preparation of conventional and endobronchial ultrasound-guided transbronchial needle aspiration specimens for the diagnosis and molecular testing of patients with known or suspected lung cancer. Respiration. 2014;88(6):500-517. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25377908/

(4) Lee HS, Lee GK, Lee H-S, et al. Real-time endobronchial ultrasound-guided transbronchial needle aspiration in mediastinal staging of non-small cell lung cancer: how many aspirations per target lymph node station? CHEST Journal. 2008;134(2):368-374. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18263688/

(5) Oki M, Saka H, Kitagawa C, et al. Randomized study of 21-gauge versus 22-gauge endobronchial ultrasound-guided transbronchial needle aspiration needles for sampling histology specimens. Journal of bronchology & interventional pulmonology. 2011;18(4):306-310. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23571718/

(6) Yarmus LB, Akulian J, Lechtzin N, et al. Comparison of 21-gauge and 22-gauge aspiration needle in endobronchial ultrasound-guided transbronchial needle aspiration: results of the American College of Chest Physicians Quality Improvement Registry, Education, and Evaluation Registry. CHEST Journal. 2013;143(4):1036-1043. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23632441/

questions.prompt: For which of the following lesions is bronchoscopic needle aspiration LEAST likely to offer a benefit over forceps biopsy?

options.text: A peripheral spiculated lung lesion, thought to be an adenocarcinoma, with no central bronchus sign on planning CT for navigational bronchoscopy
A visibly friable left mainstem endobronchial tumor thought to be a metastatic focus of existing primary renal cell carcinoma
A smooth, round lesion at the left upper/lower lobe carina in a non-smoking young individual with wheezing and 'frequent pneumonias,' clinically representing carcinoid tumor.
A central subcarinal mass with central airway submucosal infiltration in a heavy smoker, suspected to be small cell lung carcinoma
A polypoid airway tumor, thought to be squamous cell carcinoma, with visibly necrotic surface

questions.explanation: Needle aspiration (NA) is one of the cornerstones for effective diagnostic bronchoscopy. Usual needle sizes are 21 and 22G. NA secures cells for cytologic processing as well as for preparation in formalin fixation (via a 'cell block'). Diagnostic yields are typically excellent for most malignancies, and NA also provides a high yield for tumor genetic assessment. NA offers particular advantages in certain situations.1 The needle is often able to penetrate deeper into a target than forceps and is therefore useful for sampling peribronchial and submucosal lesions, as well as endobronchial lesions with surface necrosis which may otherwise be non-diagnostic with only superficial sampling (choices D, E). In cases of peripheral sampling, the needle is able to target lesions eccentric or even adjacent to the airway, enhancing diagnostic yield (choice A).2 The needle is typically less traumatizing than forceps biopsy. It thus may be helpful in sampling vascular lesions (choice B). The cells obtained with aspiration are less subject to crush artifact when compared to forceps biopsy. This is a known histologic consequence seen with forceps biopsy of small cell lung carcinoma (choice D).3 Forceps (or excisional) biopsy is the method of choice for diagnosing low grade neuroendocrine tumors ('carcinoids') to allow for thorough histologic characterization (typical vs atypical), which has implications on prognosis and management.4 Needle aspiration for the lesion in choice C would not offer much, if any additional benefit.
references: (1) Churg AM, Myers JL, Tazelaar HD, Wright JL. Thurlbeck's Pathology of the Lung, 3rd Ed. Thieme, 2005.

(2) Chao T-Y, Chien M-T, Lie C-H, Chung Y-H, Wang J-L, Lin M-C. Endobronchial ultrasonography-guided transbronchial needle aspiration increases the diagnostic yield of peripheral pulmonary lesions: a randomized trial. CHEST Journal. 2009;136(1):229-236 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18812446/

(3) Du Rand IA, Blaikley J, Booton R, et al. British Thoracic Society guideline for diagnostic flexible bronchoscopy in adults: accredited by NICE. Thorax 2013;68 Suppl 1:i1-i44. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23860341/

(4) Wolin EM. Advances in the Diagnosis and Management of Well-Differentiated and Intermediate-Differentiated Neuroendocrine Tumors of the Lung. Chest. 2017;151(5):1141‐1146. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27373769/

questions.prompt: You are evaluating a patient with severe COPD for a lung nodule. Patient was recently discharged after the treatment for a COPD exacerbation. CT scan at that time showed 2.5 cm lung nodule with severe emphysema. Review of the CT scan showed that nodule is adjacent to the airway. You perform a bronchoscopy and obtain an eccentric view of the lesion. Based on the available literature, what is the mean diagnostic yield of this nodule by radial EBUS?
questions.explanation: This patient has risk factors for malignancy with severe emphysema, and a suspicious peripheral lung nodule. Radial EBUS has been studied for the biopsy of peripheral nodules and has a diagnostic yield varying from 50-90%. Ali et al. performed meta-analysis on radial EBUS and found an overall diagnostic yield of 70.6%. However, diagnostic yield was higher for lesions greater than 2cm in size and if the lesion had a bronchus sign on CT scan. Diagnostic yield was 78.7% if radial EBUS could be placed within the lesion. However, yield decreased to 52% if radial EBUS probe was adjacent to the lesion. This patient's CT scan is showing that nodule is adjacent to the lesion, consistent with the eccentric view obtained on radial EBUS suggesting the overall diagnostic yield would be around 52%.
references: (1) Ali MS, Trick W, Mba BI, Mohananey D, Sethi J, Musani AI. Radial endobronchial ultrasound for the diagnosis of peripheral pulmonary lesions: A systematic review and meta-analysis. Respirology. 2017;22(3):443‐453. doi:10.1111/resp.12980 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28177181/

(2) Yamada N, Yamazaki K, Kurimoto N, et al. Factors Related to Diagnostic Yield of Transbronchial Biopsy Using Endobronchial Ultrasonography With a Guide Sheath in Small Peripheral Lesions. Chest 2007 Aug;132(2):603-8. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17573504/

(3) Chen A, Chenna P, Loiselle A, et al. Radial Probe Endobronchial Ultrasound for Peripheral Pulmonary Lesions. A 5-Year Institutional Experience. Ann Am Thorac Soc. 2014 May;11(4):578-82 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24635641/

questions.prompt: Which is the correct rank order in regards to depth of penetration of the following techniques (from superficial to deep)?

options.text: Optical Coherent Tomography < Radial Ultrasound < Confocal microscopy
Radial Ultrasound < Optical Coherent Tomography < Confocal microscopy
Confocal microscopy < Optical Coherent Tomography < Radial Ultrasound
Optical Coherent Tomography < Confocal microscopy < Radial Ultrasound
Radial Ultrasound < Confocal microscopy < Optical Coherent Tomography

questions.explanation: Confocal microscopy is an optical imaging technique which increases optical resolution and contrast by adding a spatial pinhole placed at the confocal plane lens to eliminate out-of-focus light and allows reconstruction of three-dimensional structures of cellular and subcellular microstructures at a depth of approximately100-300 μm. Optical coherence tomography utilizes near-infrared light transit time and to provides a macroscopic optical cross-sectional view of hollow organs which provides excellent special resolution with a depth of up to 3mm. Radial ultrasound with a frequency of 20-30 MHz provides 360 degree imaging with less special resolution than confocal microscopy and optical coherence tomography but with a depth of penetration of 4-5 cm.
references: (1) Thiberville L, Moreno-Swirc S, Vercauteren T, Peltier E, Cavé C, Bourg Heckly G. In vivo imaging of the bronchial wall microstructure using fibered confocal fluorescence microscopy. American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine. 2007;175(1):22-31. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17023733/

(2) Fujimoto JG, Pitris C, Boppart SA, Brezinski ME. Optical coherence tomography: an emerging technology for biomedical imaging and optical biopsy. Neoplasia. 2000;2(1):9-25. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1531864/
(3) Herth FJ, Eberhardt R, Ernst A. The future of bronchoscopy in diagnosing, staging and treatment of lung cancer. Respiration. 2006;73(4):399-409. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16775411/

questions.prompt: A 60 years-old Asian female lifetime non-smoker is found to have a right upper lobe lung mass with an enlarged 4R lymph node suspicious for mediastinal metastasis. She undergoes EBUS bronchoscopy for diagnosis and staging. During airway inspection, endobronchial tumor is seen originating from the right upper lobe bronchus. Pass number 1 and 2 from station 4R suggested adenocarcinoma morphology via rapid onsite evaluation. Regarding molecular marker testing on tumor tissue obtained by bronchoscopy, which of the following is correct?

options.text: The presence of malignancy on two needle passes indicates sufficient material for molecular analysis.
The adequacy of endobronchial biopsies from the RUL tumor is superior to EBUS-TBNA for molecular marker analysis.
Material should be immediately fixed in Bouin's solution for mutational testing.
Immunohistochemical staining should be minimized if possible

questions.explanation: Expert consensus suggest at least 4 passes should be obtained from the target when molecular testing is planned and that 10% neutral-buffered formalin is the optimum fixative, whereas Bouin's fluid should not be used. The literature suggests a tissue acquisition adequacy rate for molecular marker testing of greater than 90% in samples obtained by EBUS-TBNA and may result in a higher RNA yield than bronchoscopic mucosal biopsy. When a non-small cell morphology is suspected, it is recommended that histologic subtyping is accomplished with the least amount of immunohistochemical staining necessary to preserve enough tissue for molecular analysis. For example, with the use of only one adenocarcinoma marker and one squamous marker.
references: (1) Van Der Heijden EH, Casal RF, Trisolini R, et al. Guideline for the acquisition and preparation of conventional and endobronchial ultrasound-guided transbronchial needle aspiration specimens for the diagnosis and molecular testing of patients with known or suspected lung cancer. Respiration. 2014;88(6):500-517. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25377908/

(2) Atkins D, Reiffen K-A, Tegtmeier CL, Winther H, Bonato MS, Störkel S. Immunohistochemical Detection of EGFR in Paraffin-embedded Tumor Tissues Variation in Staining Intensity Due to Choice of Fixative and Storage Time of Tissue Sections. Journal of Histochemistry & Cytochemistry. 2004;52(7):893-901. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15208356/

(3) Pirker R, Herth FJ, Kerr KM, et al. Consensus for EGFR mutation testing in non-small cell lung cancer: results from a European workshop. Journal of Thoracic Oncology. 2010;5(10):1706-1713. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20871269/

(4) Travis WD, Brambilla E, Noguchi M, et al. International association for the study of lung cancer/american thoracic society/european respiratory society international multidisciplinary classification of lung adenocarcinoma. Journal of Thoracic Oncology. 2011;6(2):244-285. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21252716/

questions.prompt: A 68-year old current smoker male presented for cough and was found to have a 4 cm right lower lobe lung mass associated with large right hilar and subcarinal lymph nodes. The patient was scheduled for flexible bronchoscopy with EBUS-TBNA for the diagnosis and staging of lung cancer. Which of the following regarding sedation and analgesia for bronchoscopy is correct?

options.text: A higher number of lymph nodes can be sampled using general anesthesia compared to moderate sedation for EBUS-TBNA.
Anticholinergic agents such as atropine or glycopyrrolate decrease bronchial secretions during the procedure and their use is suggested.
Either Lidocaine and Benzocaine are suggested for use for topical anesthesia during bronchoscopy as they have a similar safety profile.
Compared to topical lidocaine, pre-bronchoscopy administration of nebulized lidocaine achieves less cough and discomfort during the procedure.
Patient tolerance and complication risk for EBUS-TBNA are no different between use of general anesthesia or moderate sedation.

questions.explanation: Based on a single randomized controlled trial comparing endobronchial ultrasound-guided transbronchial needle aspiration under moderate sedation versus general anesthesia, patients had comparable diagnostic yield, number of lymph nodes sampled, rate of major complications, and tolerance of the procedure (choice E correct, A incorrect). Based on a 2011 ACCP consensus statement, the use of anticholinergic agents does not produce any clinically meaningful improvement in lung function or decrease in bronchial secretion and their use during bronchoscopy is discouraged (choice B incorrect). Lidocaine is considered the preferred topical anesthetic agent based on its safety profile. Benzocaine should be used with extreme caution because of its higher risk of inducing methemoglobinemia (choice C incorrect). Randomized controlled trials showed that prebronchoscopy administration of nebulized lidocaine did not improve cough severity of patients' discomfort (choice D incorrect).
references: (1) Wahidi MM, Jain P, Jantz M, et al. American College of Chest Physicians Consensus Statement on the Use of Topical Anesthesia, Analgesia, and Sedation During Flexible Bronchoscopy in Adult Patients. Chest. 2011 Nov;140(5):1342-1350. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22045879/

(2) Casal RF, Lazarus DR, Kuhl K, et al. Randomized Trial of Endobronchial Ultrasound-guided Transbronchial Needle Aspiration under General Anesthesia versus Moderate Sedation. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2015 Apr 1;191(7):796-803. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25574801/

(3) Charalampidou, S, Harris, E, Chummun, K et al. Evaluation of the efficacy of nebulised lignocaine as adjunctive local anaesthesia for fibreoptic bronchoscopy: a randomised, placebo-controlled study. Irish Medical Journal [01 Jan 2006, 99(1):8-10] https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16506681/

(4) Stolz, D, Chhajed, PN, Leuppi, J et al. Nebulized lidocaine for flexible bronchoscopy: a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial. Chest. 2005; 128: 1756-1760 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16162784/

questions.prompt: Which of the following methods of upper airway anesthesia has been shown to result in fewest number of coughing episodes during EBUS bronchoscopy?

options.text: Spray as you go technique
Administration of topical anesthetic agent transcricoid or transtracheal route
Administration of topical anesthetic agent with a spray catheter
Administration of topical anesthetic agent via nebulizer

questions.explanation: Lee, et al. randomized 40 patients undergoing EBUS bronchoscopy to lidocaine delivery via the spray catheter and standard 'spray as you go' via working channel techniques. The patients treated with spray catheter had fewer number of significant coughing episodes. All patients received nebulized lidocaine prior the bronchoscopy. Bilateral superior laryngeal nerve blockade associated with topical anesthesia of the trachea by transcricothyroid membrane puncture is safe, technically easy to perform, rapid, and comfortable and satisfying for both patient and endoscopist. However, there are no trials comparing laryngeal nerve block or transtracheal lidocaine administration to other methods of topical airway anesthesia.
references: (1) Respirology. 2011 Jan;16(1):102-6 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20920136/

questions.prompt: Which of the following patients is the most appropriate candidate for a transbronchial cryobiopsy (TBCB)?

options.text: 42 years-old man who is 6 months post lung transplant for cystic fibrosis and presents for surveillance bronchoscopy

65 years-old woman with progressive dyspnea on exertion. HRCT shows subpleural, basal predominance of reticular abnormalities and honeycombing with mild traction bronchiectasis. There are no ground glass opacities, no micronodules, no cysts or consolidations noted on the scan.

70 y/o woman with progressive dyspnea on exertion. HRCT shows mild basilar reticulation and bronchiectasis. Subtle ground glass opacities and mosaic attenuation predominantly in upper and mid lung distribution are also noted. A serologic evaluation has been unremarkable.
All of the above

questions.explanation: In general, the indications for transbronchial cryo-biopsy (TBCB) for diffuse parenchymal lung disease are the same as for surgical lung biopsy. Biopsy is typically not indicated in subjects demonstrating HRCT images typical of a UIP pattern (choice B incorrect). A CT pattern that is indeterminate for UIP and/or suggests an alternative diagnosis would be most appropriate for surgical biopsy or TBCB (choice C). Because TBCB allows the harvesting of larger and more expanded lung tissue samples compared to transbronchial forceps biopsy, it has theoretical potential as an alternative sampling technique for post-lung transplant surveillance. However, data on the practical utility in this population is lacking (choice A incorrect).
references: (1) Chest 2013; 143: 621-626. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23328889/
(2) Respiration 2018;95:188-200 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29316560/

questions.prompt: For which of the following clinical scenarios would convex probe EBUS-TBNA be least helpful as the next step in evaluation?

options.text: A 24 years-old male with no past medical history and no unusual exposures, complaining of fevers and night sweats, found to have isolated bulky multistation mediastinal adenopathy
A 69 years-old male with a heavy smoking history and COPD, presenting with a left upper lobe spiculated lung mass and enlarged nodes at stations 4L and 5.
A 58 years-old woman being treated for breast cancer, with new bilateral lower lobe lung nodules and an enlarged 11Ri lymph node
A 64 years-old male with a history diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, initially responsive to therapy, now with fevers and enlarged lymph nodes at stations 7 and 4R.
An otherwise healthy 48 years-old African-American woman with cough and wheezing, found to have enlarged lymph nodes at stations 11R, 4R and 11L.

questions.explanation: EBUS-TBNA is a well-established tool for evaluating a myriad of thoracic disease, including primary lung malignancy, metastatic extrathoracic cancer, and granulomatous disease (choices B, C, and E, respectively).1-3 Its performance with assessing lymphoma has been less consistent, in particular for establishing a de novo diagnosis.4,5 Those with Hodgkin's Disease may be especially difficult to definitively characterize, in part due to relative paucity of Reed-Sternberg cells in aspirate samples.
references: (1) Agarwal R, Srinivasan A, Aggarwal AN, Gupta D. Efficacy and safety of convex probe EBUS-TBNA in sarcoidosis: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Respir Med 2012;106:883-92. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22417738/

(2) Yang B, Li F, Shi W, et al. Endobronchial ultrasound-guided transbronchial needle biopsy for the diagnosis of intrathoracic lymph node metastases from extrathoracic malignancies: a meta-analysis and systematic review. Respirology 2014;19:834-41. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24935652/

(3) Labarca G, Sierra-Ruiz M, Kheir F, et al. Diagnostic Accuracy of Endobronchial Ultrasound Transbronchial Needle Aspiration in Lymphoma. A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis. Ann Am Thorac Soc 2019;16:1432-9. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31291126/

(4) Wahidi MM, Herth F, Yasufuku K, et al. Technical Aspects of Endobronchial Ultrasound-Guided Transbronchial Needle Aspiration: CHEST Guideline and Expert Panel Report. Chest 2016;149:816-35. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26402427/

(5) Grosu HB, Iliesiu M, Caraway NP, et al. Endobronchial Ultrasound-Guided Transbronchial Needle Aspiration for the Diagnosis and Subtyping of Lymphoma. Ann Am Thorac Soc. 2015;12(9):1336‐1344. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26146788/

questions.prompt: The arrow below denotes the junction of the left brachiocephalic vein (BCV) with the superior vena cava (SVC). Assuming conventional orientation of the EBUS scope and ultrasound monitor screen, which of the following is true about the associated lymph node seen in the image?

options.text: It is a station 10R node
If it is a node involved with tumor metastasis, the primary lung cancer was likely in the right upper lobe
The superior portion of the aortic arch is cephalad to this node
If it measures under 10mm in short axis on CT scan in the setting of a lung mass, it does not need to be sampled
The pulmonary artery is located immediately left lateral and inferior to the lymph node

questions.explanation: The EBUS image depicts the junction of the left BCV and SVC, suggesting the lymph node seen is a right paratracheal node (choices A and E incorrect). Given the information provided, whether it is a 2R or 4R node cannot be definitively determined, as the boundary between the 2R and 4R station is at the level where the inferior aspect of the left BCV as it initially intersects with the trachea.1 The still image denotes a point right lateral to the midline after the intersection, and thus the node would likely be slightly inferior to the 2R/4R transition. Regardless, both 4R and 2R stations drain the right upper lobe and a tumor in this location would be expected to metastasize here (choice B correct). With normal anatomy, the superior portion of the aortic arch is at the level of or caudad to the junction of the left BCV and SVC (choice C incorrect). Mediastinal nodes that are negative by CT or PET/CT criteria (=<10mm by short axis, no FDG avidity) in the setting of a large, central clinically suspected lung cancer or one involving an ipsilateral N1 station node still have a sizeable chance of occult metastases, and thus should be sampled (choice D incorrect).3,4
references: (1) Silvestri GA, Gonzalez AV, Jantz MA, et al. Methods for staging non-small cell lung cancer: Diagnosis and management of lung cancer, 3rd ed: American College of Chest Physicians evidence-based clinical practice guidelines. Chest 2013;143:e211S-e50S. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23649440/

(2) Kim JH, van Beek EJ, Murchison JT, Marin A, Mirsadraee S. The International Association for the Study of Lung Cancer Lymph Node Map: A Radiologic Atlas and Review. Tuberc Respir Dis (Seoul) 2015;78:180-9. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26175770/
(3) Kim AW. Lymph node drainage patterns and micrometastasis in lung cancer. Semin Thorac Cardiovasc Surg. 2009;21(4):298‐308. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20226342/

(4) Gao SJ, Kim AW, Puchalski JT, et al. Indications for invasive mediastinal staging in patients with early non-small cell lung cancer staged with PET-CT. Lung Cancer. 2017;109:36‐41. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28577947/

questions.prompt: A 39 years-old African American male, never smoker, presents with dry cough and occasional night sweats. CT of the chest is obtained and representative images are seen below. You elect to perform EBUS-TBNA. Which one of the following statements is correct?

options.text: If the diagnosis is sarcoidosis, either conventional TBNA or EBUS-TBNA will provide similar yield.
If the diagnosis is sarcoidosis, mucosal and/or transbronchial lung biopsies should be performed in addition to TBNA.
If the diagnosis is lymphoma, EBUS-TBNA will have a higher diagnostic yield than if this was sarcoidosis.
With the aid of flow cytometry, EBUS-TBNA can rule out lymphomas

questions.explanation: EBUS-TBNA has been shown to be superior to conventional TBNA for stages I and II sarcoidosis, providing a fairly consistent yield of about 80% across most studies. Adding endobronchial or transbronchial biopsy to EBUS-TBNA optimizes diagnostic sensitivity for sarcoidosis. The performance of EBUS-TBNA for the evaluation of lymphoma is more variable. Diagnostic yield is significantly higher for recurrence than "de-novo" lymphoma. Even with addition of flow cytometric analysis, the accuracy of EBUS for confirming lymphoma remains sub-optimal when pre-test suspicion is high, especially with Hodgkin's disease. In these cases, a negative EBUS-TBNA should be confirmed by surgical sampling.
references: (1) Grosu HB, Iliesiu M, Caraway NP, et al. Endobronchial Ultrasound-Guided Transbronchial Needle Aspiration for the Diagnosis and Subtyping of Lymphoma. Ann Am Thorac Soc. 2015;12(9):1336-1344. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26146788/

(2) Moonim MT, Breen R, Fields PA, Santis G. Diagnosis and Subtyping of De Novo and Relapsed Mediastinal Lymphomas by Endobronchial Ultrasound Needle Aspiration. American Journal of Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine. 2013;188(10):1216-1223. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24047336/

(3) von Bartheld MB, Dekkers OM, Szlubowski A, et al. Endosonography vs conventional bronchoscopy for the diagnosis of sarcoidosis: The granuloma randomized clinical trial. JAMA. 2013;309(23):2457-2464. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23780458/

(4) Steinfort DP, Conron M, Tsui A, et al. Endobronchial ultrasound-guided transbronchial needle aspiration for the evaluation of suspected lymphoma. Journal of thoracic oncology : official publication of the International Association for the Study of Lung Cancer. 2010;5(6):804-809. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20521347/

(5) Tremblay A, Stather DR, Maceachern P, Khalil M, Field SK. A randomized controlled trial of standard vs endobronchial ultrasonography-guided transbronchial needle aspiration in patients with suspected sarcoidosis. Chest. 2009;136(2):340-346. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19188547/

(6) Gupta D, Dadhwal DS, Agarwal R, Gupta N, Bal A, Aggarwal AN. Endobronchial ultrasound-guided transbronchial needle aspiration vs conventional transbronchial needle aspiration in the diagnosis of sarcoidosis. Chest. 2014;146(3):547-556. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24481031/

(7) Sun J, Yang H, Teng J, et al. Determining factors in diagnosing pulmonary sarcoidosis by endobronchial ultrasound-guided transbronchial needle aspiration. Ann Thorac Surg. 2015;99(2):441-445. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25497069/

questions.prompt: You are performing EBUS bronchoscopy and note the image below. What would be an appropriate next step?

options.text: Increase the image depth to gain better perspective of the target lesion
Ensure adequate scope flexion and inflate the balloon to enhance contact with the bronchial wall
Proceed with TBNA, with needle directed toward the center of the image
Utilize Color Doppler to confirm a suspected vascular lesion
Abort the procedure and prepare for a chest tube to treat a likely pneumothorax

questions.explanation: The image shows a classic reverberation artifact (as with A-lines during thoracic ultrasonography). These artifacts denote the reverberation of ultrasound waves between interface of two media with different ultrasound propagating properties (usually fluid/tissue and air-- in this case the EBUS transducer with overlying fluid-filled balloon and the air-filled airway). The most common reason for this artifact during EBUS is poor contact of the transducer with the airway wall. Ensuring proper flexion of the EBUS bronchoscope is the first step to resolving the issue. Occasionally, inflating the balloon further with saline will be required (choice B correct; choices A, D incorrect). In rare cases, such as at sharp airway angles of bronchial branching, such as at the 4L and 10L locations, proper articulation with the airway wall cannot be achieved, especially with presence of the TBNA needle within the scope. TBNA should not be performed if a reliable image can't be obtained (choice C incorrect). Pneumothorax is extremely rare during convex-probe EBUS bronchoscopy (choice E incorrect).
references: (1) Wahidi MM, Herth F, Yasufuku K, et al. Technical Aspects of Endobronchial Ultrasound-Guided Transbronchial Needle Aspiration: CHEST Guideline and Expert Panel Report. Chest 2016;149:816-35. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26402427/

questions.prompt: You are referred a patient with a right upper lobe lung lesion and elect to perform bronchoscopic lung biopsy. In counseling the patient regarding the procedure, which of the following statements is TRUE?

options.text: Size of the lesion does not affect the yield
There is no significant difference in yield between the use of navigational bronchoscopy or radial-EBUS to guide sampling
Bronchoscopic approaches are as accurate for diagnosis as CT-guided transthoracic sampling
A negative result from navigational bronchoscopy will need no further testing

questions.explanation: Diagnostic yield in peripheral bronchoscopy is strongly influenced by the size of the lesion. A meta-analysis from 2012 found no significant difference in diagnostic yield of peripheral nodules between different advanced bronchoscopic approaches with an overall diagnostic yield of approximately 70%. This is lower than the reported diagnostic yield of CT-guided TTNA which approaches 90%. Newer guided bronchoscopic techniques, such as the use of cone-beam CT or robotics may enhance the yield, but these approaches are not yet widely used and comparisons to established techniques are still evolving. Non-diagnostic peripheral bronchoscopic procedures require further follow-up either with close interval imaging or CT-guided biopsy, depending on the pre-test probability for malignancy.
references: (1) Chen A, Chenna P, Loiselle A, Massoni J, Mayse M, Misselhorn D. Radial probe endobronchial ultrasound for peripheral pulmonary lesions. A 5-year institutional experience. Ann Am Thorac Soc. 2014;11(4):578-582. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24635641/
(2) Wang Memoli JS, Nietert PJ, Silvestri GA. MEta-analysis of guided bronchoscopy for the evaluation of the pulmonary nodule. Chest. 2012;142(2):385-393. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21980059/

questions.prompt: You perform bronchoscopy on a heavy smoking, immunosuppressed patient with a lung mass and low-grade fevers. You find an exophytic endobronchial lesion you suspect could be lung cancer or fungus. You perform both forceps biopsy (FB) and endobronchial needle aspiration (ENA) of the mass with rapid on-site evaluation (ROSE). The on-site cytopathologist remarks, "the cells on the slides look malignant," guiding you to obtain more tissue from the lesion, and allowing prompt discontinuation of Amphotericin. From which of your samples, and utilizing which evaluation technique, was the cytopathologist most likely able to give you this timely information?

options.text: ENA, flow cytometry
ENA, Romanowsky ('Diff-Quik') staining
ENA, Gram staining
FB, Hematoxylin/Eosin (H/E) staining
FB, Periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) staining

questions.explanation: Histocytologic assessment involves the use of various staining methods that either enhance surface, cytoplasmic or nuclear structures of various types of cells. In this case, a malignant diagnosis was made with ROSE. Commonly used processing media for cells obtained by needle aspiration during ROSE are the Romanowsky ('Diff-Quik') and/or PAP stains, which complement one another by providing cytoplasmic and nuclear characterization, respectively (choice B correct). Hematoxylin-Eosin (H/E) staining is used on histologic specimens or cell block, Gram staining for various bacterial organisms (choice C), PAS staining to highlight fungal elements (choice E), and flow cytometry for lymphocyte immunophenotyping when lymphoma is suspected (choice A).
references: (1) Muthu V, Sehgal IS, Dhooria S, et al. Endobronchial Ultrasound-Guided Transbronchial Needle Aspiration: Techniques and Challenges. J Cytol 2019;36:65-70. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30745744/

questions.prompt: A 24 years-old male with history of acute myeloid leukemia underwent autologous hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) 3 months ago and currently under immunosuppressive regimen. Three days ago, he was hospitalized with fever and shortness of breath which began the day before admission. CT chest demonstrates bilateral lung infiltrates. Broad spectrum antibiotics are initiated. After 48 hours of IV antibiotics, he is still febrile. You are consulted for bronchoscopy. Which of the following is true about bronchoscopy in immunocompromised patients with lung infiltrate?

options.text: Diagnostic yield of combined bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL), protected sheath brushing (PSB) and transbronchial lung biopsy (TBLB) is higher than combined BAL and TBLB
Bronchoscopy provides higher yield in allogenic HSCT compared to autologous HSCT
Bleeding rate with TBLB is 20% among recipient of lung transplant.
Diagnostic yield is highest among neutropenic patients

questions.explanation: Infiltrates in immunocompromised patients presents unique challenges. Among the infectious etiology of infiltrates, bacterial infections are the most common cause. Bronchoscopy is regarded as an important tool for differentiation of various etiologies. Jain et al. studied yield of different techniques during bronchoscopy. Diagnostic yield of bronchoscopy in their study was 56.2%. Individual diagnostic yield of BAL was equivalent to that of TBLB- 38%. Diagnostic yield of protected specimen brushing was 13%. Combined yield of BAL and TBLB was 70% and was equivalent to combined yield of BAL, TBLB and PSB (choice A incorrect). Patel et al. studied diagnostic yield of bronchoscopy in bone marrow transplant patients. A specific diagnosis was more commonly obtained in allogenic hematopoetic stem cell transplant (HSCT) patients as compared to autologous HSCT. The yield reported in their study was 50% in allogenic HSCT as compared to 34% in autologous HSCT (choice B correct). However, additional diagnostic yield of TBLB over the BAL was less than 10%. Decision to perform bronchoscopy in an immunocompromised patient requires careful consideration. BAL can be done safely in most of the immunocompromised patients. However, platelet counts and coagulopathy can affect decision about TBLB. Significant bleeding rate in lung transplant recipients after TBLB varies from 3-12%. Among the immunocompromised patients, diagnostic yield may be lowest in critically ill, GVHD or neutropenic patients.
references: (1) Patel NR et al. The influence of diagnostic bronchoscopy on clinical outcomes comparing adult autologous and allogeneic bone marrow transplant patients. Chest. 2005 Apr;127(4):1388-96. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15821221/

(2) Gruson D et al. Utility of fiberoptic bronchoscopy in neutropenic patients admitted to the intensive care unit with pulmonary infiltrates. Crit Care Med. 2000 Jul;28(7):2224-30 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/10921544/
(3) Harris B et al. Diagnostic bronchoscopy in solid-organ and hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.Ann Am Thorac Soc. 2013 Feb;10(1):39-49 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23509331/
(4) Rañó A et al. Pulmonary infections in non-HIV-immunocompromised patients. Curr Opin Pulm Med. 2005 May;11(3):213-7. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15818182/
(5) Jain P et al. Role of flexible bronchoscopy in immunocompromised patients with lung infiltrates. Chest. 2004 Feb;125(2):712-22. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/14769756/

questions.prompt: Which of the following is NOT used as an anatomical landmark during convex probe-EBUS?

options.text: Superior margin of aortic arch
Upper rim of the main pulmonary artery
Lower border of azygous vein
Caudal margin of innominate vein

questions.explanation: Station 4L (left lower paratracheal) is bounded inferiorly by the upper rim of left (not main) pulmonary artery and superiorly by the upper rim of the aortic arch. All others are important anatomical landmarks in distinguishing various lymph node stations.
references: (1) Rusch VW, et al. The IASLC lung cancer staging project: A proposal for a new international lymph node map in the forthcoming seventh edition of the TNM classification for lung cancer. J Thor Onc 2009. 4(5): 568-577. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19357537/

questions.prompt: You are consenting a patient for bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage, convex probe EBUS-TBNA, and radial probe EBUS-guided peripheral TBNA and transbronchial forceps biopsy. Aside from the thoracic findings on chest imaging for which you are performing bronchoscopy, the patient does not have known medical problems and is not taking any medicines. You state that aside from a mild sore throat and cough, other complications are "very rare." But before signing the consent, your patient insists on knowing the chances of all the possible risks. Which of the following LEAST accurately describes the likelihood of the given complication for the planned procedure?

options.text: Pneumothorax, 1-5%
Respiratory infection requiring antibiotics, 1-5%
Bleeding requiring blood transfusion, 1-3%
Mediastinitis, < 1%
Acute respiratory failure, < 1%

questions.explanation: Advanced diagnostic bronchoscopy is overall very safe, though any procedure that involves biopsies is not completely without risks. The more common ones should be shared with the patient when obtaining informed consent. The procedure in the question stem involves central as well as peripheral sampling under EBUS guidance. Of the complications listed, pneumothorax (1-5% in those undergoing peripheral sampling, choice A) and respiratory infection (1-5% overall, choice B) are the most common. All the other complications listed occur in less than 1% of properly selected patients undergoing advanced diagnostic bronchoscopy. While bleeding does occur in a measurable proportion of cases, it is usually mild and/or controlled easily with routine measures. The need for blood transfusion is extremely rare (choice C correct).
references: (1) Hayama M, Izumo T, Matsumoto Y, Chavez C, Tsuchida T, Sasada S. Complications with Endobronchial Ultrasound with a Guide Sheath for the Diagnosis of Peripheral Pulmonary Lesions. Respiration 2015;90:129-35. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26112297/

(2) Eapen GA, Shah AM, Lei X, et al. Complications, consequences, and practice patterns of endobronchial ultrasound-guided transbronchial needle aspiration: Results of the AQuIRE registry. Chest 2013;143:1044-53. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23117878/

(3) Caglayan B, Yilmaz A, Bilaceroglu S, Comert SS, Demirci NY, Salepci B. Complications of Convex-Probe Endobronchial Ultrasound-Guided Transbronchial Needle Aspiration: A Multi-Center Retrospective Study. Respir Care 2016;61:243-8. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26556895/

(4) Asano F, Aoe M, Ohsaki Y, et al. Complications associated with endobronchial ultrasound-guided transbronchial needle aspiration: a nationwide survey by the Japan Society for Respiratory Endoscopy. Respir Res 2013;14:50. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23663438/

questions.prompt: You are seeking informed consent for bronchoscopy with transbronchial biopsies from a 62-year-old obese male with coronary artery disease, obstructive sleep apnea (OSA), and combined pulmonary fibrosis and emphysema (CPFE) who is on 2L/min home oxygen therapy. Which of the following statements would be appropriate?

options.text: Aspirin should be held for 5 days
Sleep apnea does not increase your risk of post-bronchoscopy complications but could potentially make intubation more challenging
The risk of pneumothorax is approximately 18-20%
Obtaining an echocardiogram would be advised.

questions.explanation: This patient has multiple risk factors for secondary pulmonary hypertension, which increases risk of morbidity and mortality with non-cardiac surgery. He also has risk factors for congestive heart failure and as such, assessment of cardiac function via a trans-thoracic echocardiogram would be an appropriate next step. Aspirin monotherapy has not been shown to increase risk of bleeding following transbronchial biopsy. Patients with obstructive sleep apnea are at significantly higher risk of post-anesthesia complications. Most studies report a post-transbronchial biopsy pneumothorax risk ranging from 1-6%. In a randomized trial comparing various approaches to image-guided transbronchial biopsy of peripheral lung lesions, risk of pneumothorax was between 5-8% and not significantly different across the three study arms (radial EBUS, electromagnetic navigational bronchoscopy, and electeromagnetic navigational bronchsocopy followed by radial EBUS).
references: (1) Steppan J, et al. Focused Review of Perioperative Care of Patients with Pulmonary Hypertension and Proposal of a Perioperative Pathway. Cureus. 2018 Jan; 10(1): e2072. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29552434/

(2) Eberhardt R, et al. Multimodality bronchoscopic diagnosis of peripheral lung lesions: a randomized controlled trial. Am J Respir Crit Care Med 2007;176:36-41. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17379850/
(3) den Herder, et al. Risks of general anaesthesia in people with obstructive sleep apnoea. BMJ. 2004 Oct 23; 329(7472): 955-959. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC524108/

(4) Youness HA, et al. Management of oral antiplatelet agents and anticoagulation therapy before bronchoscopy. J Thorac Dis. 2017 Sep; 9(Suppl 10): S1022-S1033. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5696555/
(5) Boskovic T, et al. Pneumothorax after transbronchial needle biopsy. J Thorac Dis. 2014 Oct; 6(Suppl 4): S427-S434. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4203979/

questions.prompt: At your institution's multidisciplinary Interstitial lung disease (ILD) conference, discussion arises about the utility of transbronchial lung cryobiopsy (TBLC). Given the relatively recent introduction of this approach for the evaluation of diffuse parenchymal lung disease, several members of the team are concerned about the reported complications in the medical literature. You explain that the most frequently encountered complication is:

options.text: Pneumothorax
ILD exacerbation
Moderate bleeding
Non-diagnostic yield

questions.explanation: The recommended approach to and safety of TBLC was reviewed in the expert statement by Hetzel, et al (2018).1 Bleeding is a well described complication of TBLC. In a recent meta-analysis of 12 studies, moderate bleeding after cryobiopsy was observed in 16.9% of procedures, with an overall pooled probability of developing a moderate bleed of about 0.12, while the average rate of pneumothorax was 10%.2 During retrieval of specimen, the bronchoscope and the cryoprobe are removed en bloc, leaving substantial blind time during which hemorrhage may occur. Consequently, Hetzel et al. recommend the prophylactic use of a bronchial blocker (Fogarty balloon or other tools) during this blind time to minimize chance of uncontrolled hemorrhage and asphyxiation.1 In comparison, while yields vary across studies, a recent CHEST guideline and expert panel report cited a non-diagnostic yield of around 20%.3 ILD exacerbations after cryobiopsy have been reported but are very rare.
references: (1) Maldonado F, Danoff SK, Wells AU, et al. Transbronchial Cryobiopsy for the Diagnosis of Interstitial Lung Diseases: CHEST Guideline and Expert Panel Report. Chest. 2020;157(4):1030-1042. doi:10.1016/j.chest.2019.10.048 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31783014/

(2) Hetzel J, et al. Transbronchial Cryobiopsies for the Diagnosis of Diffuse Parenchymal Lung Diseases: Expert Statement from the Cryobiopsy Working Group on Safety and Utility and a Call for Standardization of the Procedure. Respiration 2018;95:188-200. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29316560/

(3) Ravaglia C, Bonifazi M, Wells AU, Tomassetti S, Gurioli C, et al: Safety and diagnostic yield of transbronchial lung cryobiopsy in diffuse parenchymal lung diseases: a comparative study versus video-assisted thoracoscopic lung biopsy and a systematic review of the literature. Respiration 2016; 91: 215-227. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26926876/

questions.prompt: You are asked to see a 55 years-old active smoker with a right upper lobe lung mass with associated ipsilateral hilar and paratracheal lymphadenopathy. You have planned for sampling of the mediastinum with rapid on-site cytologic evaluation (ROSE), and of the primary lung mass if needed. On the day of your patient's scheduled procedure, your hospital's cytologist tells you she is unable to assist. Which of the following is most likely to occur as a consequence of NOT having ROSE available for this EBUS-TBNA case?

options.text: The diagnostic yield and/or accuracy will likely suffer
You will likely have to perform a higher number of needle passes
Your procedure will likely be much longer in duration
Your patient is more likely to suffer a major procedure-related complication

questions.explanation: In a systematic review and meta-analysis the authors identified five randomized controlled studies evaluating the yield of both conventional TBNA and EBUS-TBNA with and without ROSE in subjects with mediastinal adenopathy.1 Overall, they found no difference in yield, procedure time or complication rate. The presence of ROSE was, however, associated with fewer needle passes performed during EBUS procedures.
references: (1) Sehgal IS, Dhooria S, Aggarwal AN, Agarwal R. Impact of Rapid On-Site Cytological Evaluation (ROSE) on the Diagnostic Yield of Transbronchial Needle Aspiration During Mediastinal Lymph Node Sampling: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis. Chest. 2018;153(4):929-938. doi:10.1016/j.chest.2017.11.004 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29154972/

questions.prompt: You are assessing a patient for mediastinal staging of a RUL adenocarcinoma of the lung. After systematic evaluation with EBUS, you decided the following mediastinal lymph nodes met the biopsy criteria: 4R, 2R, 7, 4L. Which node should be biopsied first?

options.text: 4R
2R
4L

questions.explanation: When performing proper staging with EBUS-TBNA, lymph node sampling should proceed from N3 station to N1 station. In this patient, the nodule is on the right side; thus, sampling should begin from the left and move progressively to right.
references: (1) Fielding D, Kurimoto N. Endobronchial Ultrasound-Guided Transbronchial Needle Aspiration for Diagnosis and Staging of Lung Cancer. Clin Chest Med. 2018 Mar;39(1):111-123. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29433708/

questions.prompt: Which of the following is a border of the 4R lymph node station?

options.text: Intersection of the cephalad margin of the innominate vein with the trachea.
The caudal aspect of the innominate artery
The inferior aspect of the azygos vein
The right lateral wall of the trachea

questions.explanation: The boundary between the 4R and 2R lymph node station is the intersection of the caudal margin of the innominate vein with the trachea while the boundary between the 4R and 10R lymph node stations is the inferior border of the azygos vein. The left lateral wall of the trachea serves as the boundary between stations 4R and 4L.
references: (1) Sobin L, Gospodarowicz M, Wittekind C. TNM Classification of Malignant Tumors 7th edn Wiley. Blackwell: Oxford, UK; 2009.

(2) Rusch VW, Asamura H, Watanabe H, Giroux DJ, Rami-Porta R, Goldstraw P. The IASLC lung cancer staging project: a proposal for a new international lymph node map in the forthcoming seventh edition of the TNM classification for lung cancer. Journal of Thoracic Oncology. 2009;4(5):568-577. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19357537/

questions.prompt: Which lymph node stations can be commonly accessed by EUS but not by EBUS?

options.text: Station 3A, Station 8, Station 9
Station 3P, station 4L, Station 9
Station 5, Station 8, Station 9
Station 7, Station 4L, Station 3P

questions.explanation: Endobronchial ultrasound guided mediastinal staging has become an important tool in lung cancer staging. EUS has an advantage of reaching station 5, 8 and 9 which are usually not accessible by EBUS. EUS can also sample station 7,3P, 4L, some left upper lobe masses abutting esophagus, left adrenal mass, celiac lymph node and liver masses close to GE junction. EUS has combined sensitivity and specificity of 89% and 100% respectively. Combining EBUS and EUS for mediastinal staging has sensitivity of 91% and Specificity of 100%.
references: (1) Silvestri GA, Gonzalez AV, Jantz MA, et al. Methods for staging non-small cell lung cancer: Diagnosis and management of lung cancer, 3rd ed: American College of Chest Physicians evidence-based clinical practice guidelines. Chest. 2013;143(5 Suppl):e211S‐e250S. doi:10.1378/chest.12-2355 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23649440/

(2) Pravachan V.C. Hegde Endosonographic Lymph Node Staging byCombined Endobronchial Ultrasound (EBUS)and Endoscopic Ultrasound: Technique and Technical Tricks. Operative Techniques in Thoracic and Cardiovascular Surgery. Volume 23, Issue 3, Autumn 2018, Pages 136

questions.prompt: 65 years-old male with 120 pack-year history of smoking, presented with 3.5cm lung mass in his right upper lobe with bilateral enlarged mediastinal lymph nodes. You were asked to perform EBUS for diagnosis and staging. Which one of the following EBUS characteristic of the node is NOT a predictor of malignancy?

options.text: Distinct Margin
Heterogeneous echogenicity
Presence of coagulation necrosis sign
Absence of blood vessels in the lymph node

questions.explanation: Endobronchial ultrasound-guided transbronchial needle aspiration (EBUS-TBNA) is a minimally invasive procedure with a high yield for lymph node staging of lung cancer. The utility of sonographic features of lymph nodes during EBUS-TBNA for the prediction of nodal metastasis in patients with lung cancer was examined by Fujiwara, et. al. The authors used digital images of lymph nodes obtained during EBUS-TBNA in patients with lung cancer and categorized according to the following characteristics: (1) size (short axis) less or more than 1 cm, (2) shape (oval or round), (3) margin (indistinct or distinct), (4) echogenicity (homogeneous or heterogeneous), (5) presence or absence of central hilar structure, and (6) presence or absence of coagulation necrosis sign. The sonographic findings were compared with the final pathologic results. The authors found that round shape, distinct margin, heterogeneous echogenicity, and presence of coagulation necrosis sign were independent predictive factors for nodal metastasis.
references: (1) Fujiwara T, et al. The utility of sonographic features during endobronchial ultrasound-guided transbronchial needle aspiration for lymph node staging in patients with lung cancer: a standard endobronchial ultrasound image classification system. Chest. 2010 Sep;138(3):641-7. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20382710/

questions.prompt: You have elected to perform EBUS-TBNA for both diagnosis and mediastinal staging of suspected lung cancer. Which one of the following statements is correct?

options.text: In experienced hands, mediastinal staging could be performed under moderate sedation without decreasing diagnostic yield
Forceps biopsies from primary tumor would be required if molecular analysis is needed
Rapid on-site evaluation (ROSE) would be required if molecular analysis is needed
Ultrasound characteristics of lymph nodes can obviate the need of biopsy

questions.explanation: A 2015 randomized control trial published in AJRCCM comparing general anesthesia with moderate sedation for EBUS-guided mediastinal staging found no difference in diagnostic yield, sensitivity, sample adequacy or lymph nodes samples while procedural duration was shorter in the moderate sedation group.1 Interestingly, abnormal hemodynamic events occurred more commonly in the moderate sedation group. EBUS-TBNA has been shown to be an excellent tool for molecular analysis with a specimen insufficiency rate of only 4%. In a 2015 study by Trisolini, ROSE was associated with a small non-significant improvement in achieving compete genotyping.3 Although echo features suggestive of malignancy including shape (oval or round), margin (indistinct or distinct), echogenicity (homogeneous or heterogeneous), presence or absence of central hilar structure, and presence or absence of coagulation necrosis sign can increase or decrease the probability of malignancy within a lymph node, no lymph node characteristic can effectively exclude or prove malignancy.7 These sonographic features can be useful in targeting a specific lymph node for biopsy when multiple lymph nodes are present within a station or when a lymph node is below the commonly utilized 5mm cut-off for sampling.
references: (1) Schmid-Bindert G, Wang Y, Jiang H, et al. EBUS-TBNA Provides Highest RNA Yield for Multiple Biomarker Testing from Routinely Obtained Small Biopsies in Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer Patients - A Comparative Study of Three Different Minimal Invasive Sampling Methods. PLoS ONE. 2013;8(10):e77948. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22789110/

(2) Casal RF, Lazarus DR, Kuhl K, et al. Randomized Trial of Endobronchial Ultrasound-guided Transbronchial Needle Aspiration under General Anesthesia versus Moderate Sedation. American Journal of Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine. 2015;191(7):796-803. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25574801/
(3) Billah S, Stewart J, Staerkel G, Chen S, Gong Y, Guo M. EGFR and KRAS mutations in lung carcinoma. Cancer cytopathology. 2011;119(2):111-117. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21400670/

(4) Bugalho A, Martins C, Dias SS, et al. Cytokeratin 19, carcinoembryonic antigen, and epithelial cell adhesion molecule detect lung cancer lymph node metastasis in endobronchial ultrasound-guided transbronchial aspiration samples. Clin Lung Cancer. 2013;14(6):704-712. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23886796/

(5) Trisolini R, Cancellieri A, Tinelli C, et al. Randomized trial of endobronchial ultrasound-guided transbronchial needle aspiration with and without rapid on-site evaluation for lung cancer genotyping. Chest. 2015;148(6):1430-1437. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26158441/

(6) Folch E, Yamaguchi N, VanderLaan PA, et al. Adequacy of lymph node transbronchial needle aspirates using convex probe endobronchial ultrasound for multiple tumor genotyping techniques in non-small-cell lung cancer. Journal of thoracic oncology: official publication of the International Association for the Study of Lung Cancer. 2013;8(11):1438-1444. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24128714/

(7) Schmid-Bindert G, Jiang H, Kahler G, et al. Predicting malignancy in mediastinal lymph nodes by endobronchial ultrasound: a new ultrasound scoring system. Respirology. 2012;17(8):1190-1198. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24205040/

(8) Fujiwara T, Yasufuku K, Nakajima T, et al. The utility of sonographic features during endobronchial ultrasound-guided transbronchial needle aspiration for lymph node staging in patients with lung cancer: a standard endobronchial ultrasound image classification system. Chest. 2010;138(3):641-647. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20382710/

(9) Casal RF. Endobronchial ultrasound images may predict malignant involvement of mediastinal lymph nodes: is tissue still the issue? Respirology. 2012;17(8):1155-1156. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22934609/

questions.prompt: You are referred a middle-aged female patient with intermittent cough and wheezing. A CT scan shows thoracic adenopathy, most prominent in the right hilar and paratracheal locations, and a few scattered small peripheral lung nodules in a peri-lymphatic distribution. The patient has not had fever, night sweats or weight loss, is a social tobacco smoker, and has a family history of lymphoma. There is no predisposition to tuberculosis and no recent sick contacts. The bronchoscopic airway exam is normal except for the area shown above as seen from the right mainstem bronchus. Forceps sampling of the mucosa and EBUS-TBNA of the lymph nodes in question are most likely to reveal which of the following?

options.text: Abundant lymphocytes mixed with occasional Reed-Sternberg cells
Invasive carcinoma with frequent mitotic features and positive synaptophysin stain
Amorphous and lamellar material, Congo-Red stain positive
Non-caseating granuloma, negative Periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) and acid-fast (AFB) stains
The samples are unlikely to yield a specific diagnosis, as mucosal forceps sampling and EBUS-TBNA of lymph nodes both offer a low yield for the likely condition in this patient.

questions.explanation: The case presented is one of typical stage 2 sarcoidosis. The airway mucosal findings of subtle nodularity over the anterolateral aspect of the right mainstem bronchus represent endobronchial granulomatous inflammation. Histologically, the granulomas of sarcoidosis are typically non-necrotizing (non-caseating) and do not react with stains that highlight fungal or mycobacterial organisms, such as PAS and AFB, respectively (choice D correct). EBUS-TBNA of lymph nodes provides over 80% sensitivity in detecting the granulomas of sarcoidosis, and the odds of obtaining a diagnosis are higher with the addition of endobronchial and/or transbronchial forceps sampling in the proper context (choice E incorrect).1,2 Choice A describes Hodgkin's lymphoma, choice B a small cell carcinoma, and choice C amyloid. None of these are more likely than sarcoidosis given the clinical scenario and mucosal findings.
references: (1) Agarwal R, Srinivasan A, Aggarwal AN, Gupta D. Efficacy and safety of convex probe EBUS-TBNA in sarcoidosis: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Respir Med 2012;106:883-92. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22417738/

(2) Hu LX, Chen RX, Huang H, et al. Endobronchial Ultrasound-guided Transbronchial Needle Aspiration versus Standard Bronchoscopic Modalities for Diagnosis of Sarcoidosis: A Meta-analysis. Chin Med J (Engl) 2016;129:1607-15. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27364799/

questions.prompt: Assuming conventional orientation of the EBUS scope and ultrasound monitor screen, what is the structure denoted by the arrow?

options.text: The left pulmonary artery
The aorta
The azygous vein
The 4R lymph node
The left atrium

questions.explanation: The EBUS image depicts the classic view of the 4L station, which is bounded superiorly by the superior aspect of the aorta (vessel on the right of image) and inferiorly by the superior aspect of the left pulmonary artery (choice A correct).
references: (1) Kim JH, van Beek EJ, Murchison JT, Marin A, Mirsadraee S. The International Association for the Study of Lung Cancer Lymph Node Map: A Radiologic Atlas and Review. Tuberc Respir Dis (Seoul) 2015;78:180-9.

questions.prompt: You are performing an advanced diagnostic bronchoscopy for right sided thoracic lesions in a patient with underlying moderate emphysematous COPD. At the end of the procedure you notice the patient's oxygen saturations have dropped to ~88% with moderate oxygen supplementation. This doesn't improve much as the patient awakens from sedation and between coughing spells tells you he feels slightly short of breath. You perform thoracic ultrasonography while the patient is in supine and semi-fowler position, and obtain images seen below. Right anterior. Left anterior. Which of the following bronchoscopic techniques you performed is MOST LIKELY the cause of these findings?

options.text: Forceps biopsy of an emphysematous RB1 segment under fluoroscopic guidance
Bronchial brushings of a mucosal lesion in the bronchus intermedius
EBUS-TBNA of a station 4R lymph node
Bronchoalveolar lavage of a non-emphysematous RB3 segment
Peripheral TBNA of a non-emphysematous RB2 segment without fluoroscopic guidance

questions.explanation: The patient in question has moderate emphysematous COPD which increases the risk for several bronchoscopy-related complications. Most important of these include hypoxemia of various causes,1 pneumothorax, and sedation-related hypoventilation. This patient has mild-moderate hypoxemia at the conclusion of the procedure. The fact that this persists after the patient awakens argues against hypoventilation as the main cause. Further evaluation with a careful physical exam and chest imaging is indicated to investigate other etiologies. Thoracic ultrasound is an effective and efficient tool for evaluating hypoxemia of various causes, including after bronchoscopic sampling.2,3 The images shown are of near-confluent B-lines seen anteriorly on the right and a normal A-profile exam on the left (A-lines with lung sliding). B-lines indicate interstitial thickening and confluence correlates with transition to alveolar filling of some cause.4,5 Presence of B-lines rules out a pneumothorax with 100% certainty at the imaged site. A pneumothorax large enough to cause hypoxemia would be easily detected anteriorly in an individual laying supine or semi-fowler. This patient underwent an advanced diagnostic bronchoscopy with employment of several sampling techniques as listed in the answer choices. Only choice D correctly reconciles the ultrasound findings with the use of these techniques and the associated anatomy listed. B-lines can be seen with lung lavage and the anterior segment of the right upper lobe (RB3) would be imaged by an anterior thoracic ultrasound exam.6 Of the techniques described above, a BAL is also the most likely to cause mild-moderate hypoxemia, especially in a COPD patient.7,8 The main risk with TBLB and TBNA is pneumothorax (about 1-5%, depending on procedure and patient-related factors) and peripheral bleeding.9,10 The ultrasound findings support an asymmetric interstitial and alveolar filling process, not pneumothorax. Furthermore, due to paucity of lung parenchyma, an emphysematous segment is less likely to generate B-line artifacts caused by sampling-related hemorrhage (choice A incorrect). The posterior (RB2) segment is not adequately imaged by an anterior thoracic US exam (choice E incorrect). Brushings of an endobronchial mucosal lesion and TBNA of a 4R node are examples of central sampling, and would not directly lead to any of the complications above or the presence of B-lines (choices B, C incorrect).
references: (1) Viglietta L, Inchingolo R, Pavano C, et al. Ultrasonography for the Diagnosis of Pneumothorax after Transbronchial Lung Cryobiopsy in Diffuse Parenchymal Lung Diseases. Respiration. 2017;94(2):232‐236. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28637050/
(2) Lichtenstein DA. BLUE-protocol and FALLS-protocol: two applications of lung ultrasound in the critically ill. Chest 2015;147:1659-70. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26033127/

(3) Bensted K, McKenzie J, Havryk A, Plit M, Ben-Menachem E. Lung Ultrasound After Transbronchial Biopsy for Pneumothorax Screening in Post-Lung Transplant Patients. J Bronchology Interv Pulmonol. 2018;25(1):42‐47. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29076936/

(4) Lichtenstein DA, Meziere GA, Lagoueyte JF, Biderman P, Goldstein I, Gepner A. A-lines and B-lines: lung ultrasound as a bedside tool for predicting pulmonary artery occlusion pressure in the critically ill. Chest 2009;136:1014-20. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19809049/

(5) Eapen GA, Shah AM, Lei X, et al. Complications, consequences, and practice patterns of endobronchial ultrasound-guided transbronchial needle aspiration: Results of the AQuIRE registry. Chest 2013;143:1044-53. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23117878/

(6) Hayama M, Izumo T, Matsumoto Y, Chavez C, Tsuchida T, Sasada S. Complications with Endobronchial Ultrasound with a Guide Sheath for the Diagnosis of Peripheral Pulmonary Lesions. Respiration 2015;90:129-35. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26112297/

(7) Via G, Lichtenstein D, Mojoli F, et al. Whole lung lavage: a unique model for ultrasound assessment of lung aeration changes. Intensive Care Med 2010;36:999-1007. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20221746/

(8) Fang WF, Chen YC, Chung YH, et al. Predictors of oxygen desaturation in patients undergoing diagnostic bronchoscopy. Chang Gung Med J. 2006;29(3):306‐312. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16924893/

(9) Matsushima Y, Jones RL, King EG, Moysa G, Alton JD. Alterations in pulmonary mechanics and gas exchange during routine fiberoptic bronchoscopy. Chest. 1984;86(2):184‐188. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/6744959/
(10) Jones AM, O'Driscoll R. Do all patients require supplemental oxygen during flexible bronchoscopy?. Chest. 2001;119(6):1906‐1909. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11399722/

questions.prompt: You are referred a patient with lupus on immunomodulator therapy with a right upper lobe lung mass and multifocal right inta-thoracic adenopathy. Her only symptoms are fatigue and intermittent cough. You proceed with advanced diagnostic bronchoscopy, sampling both the primary lesion and station 4R and 7 lymph nodes. Rapid on-site evaluation from the 4R node yields "non-specific cells that don't look malignant." Histologic analysis by Hematoxylin-Eosin (H/E) and Periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) staining is shown below. H/E stain PAS stain Which of the following is the most appropriate management for this patient?

options.text: Refer for mediastinoscopy-guided sampling of the 4R node
Start prednisone at 0.5-1.0 mg/kg daily
Repeat EBUS-TBNA using miniforceps or a 19G needle to sample the 4R node
Start fluconazole 400 mg daily
PET/CT and refer to hematology/oncology

questions.explanation: Immunosuppressed patients presenting with focal thoracic lesions should be considered to have either malignancy or infection. Mimickers of lung cancer include mycobacterial and endemic dimorphic fungal disease (particularly histoplasma and coccidioides), as these can manifest as focal lung lesions with associated thoracic adenopathy. Advanced diagnostic bronchoscopy is an efficient next step in evaluating this patient.1 The hematoxylin-eosin (H/E) stain demonstrates a pattern of immune cell aggregation consistent with a non-caseating granuloma. Presence of granuloma should prompt the search for responsible microorganisms, particularly mycobacteria and fungi. In this case, the organism is visible on the H/E stain and consistent with coccidioidal species (spherical structure near center of the granuloma). This is further highlighted by the PAS stain. Treatment of choice is fluconazole (choice D correct), and further testing to look for additional diagnoses is not necessary.
references: (1) Leslie, K. and Wick, M. Practical Pulmonary Pathology. Churchill Livingstone, 2005.

(2) Shah RA, Vempilly JJ, Noor Ul Husnain SM, Hegde P. Combined Endosonography Reduces Time to Diagnose Pulmonary Coccidioidomycosis. J Bronchology Interv Pulmonol 2018;25:152-5. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29346251/

questions.prompt: When performing post-lung transplant surveillance biopsies, which of the following should the bronchoscopist consider?

options.text: Using fluoroscopy has been proven to increase diagnostic yield
No less than five pieces of alveolated lung tissue are required
There is a relatively higher risk of post-procedural pneumothorax in these patients
Taking biopsies from the right lower lobe has been proven to increase diagnostic yield

questions.explanation: At least five pieces of well-expanded alveolated lung tissue are recommended for adequate assessment of acute cellular rejection per the 2007 International Society for Heart and Lung Transplantation (ISHLT) guidelines. There is no evidence that using fluoroscopy or biopsying the right lower lobe results in a higher yield. According to an analysis of Florida hospital discharge data from 2000 - 2009, COPD patients and females had a higher rate of pneumothorax following transbronchial biopsy in the general population. Lung transplant recipients had a lower rate of both pneumothorax (adjusted odds: 0.40 (95% CI: 0.26-0.62), p <0.0001) and procedure-related hemorrhage (adjusted odds 0.39 (95% CI: 0.21-0.72), p = 0.002).
references: (1) Stewart S, et al. Revision of the 1996 Working Formulation for the Standardization of Nomenclature in the Diagnosis of Lung Rejection. J Heart Lung Transplant. 2007 Dec;26(12):1229-42. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18096473/
(2) Tukey MH, et al. Population-based estimates of transbronchial lung biopsy utilization and complications. Respir Med. 2012 Nov; 106(11): 1559-1565. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22938740/

questions.prompt: A 51 years-old female with past medical history significant for non-small cell lung cancer on chemoradiation is admitted to your hospital with acute respiratory failure and fever. Overnight she deteriorates and is transferred to the medical ICU for further treatment. She is being treated empirically with broad spectrum antibiotics and steroids for bilateral diffuse pulmonary infiltrates on admission CXR. She fails non-invasive positive pressure ventilation and the intensivist emergently intubates her. A comprehensive infectious workup has been ordered, including tracheal aspirate sampling, but so far, it has been non-diagnostic. You have been consulted for possible diagnostic bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage. Her husband asks you what is the role of this procedure for his wife. Based on recent publications in the medical literature, you tell him:

options.text: Bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage is not indicated in her case
Bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage could change management by 20%
Bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage could change management by 40%
Bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage could change management by 60%
Risks for bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage are prohibitive in her case

questions.explanation: This patient fulfills clinical criteria for pneumonia. Active chemoradiation is typically considered an immunosuppressed condition, therefore the workup for a potential treatable infection should not be delayed, and bronchoscopy is one available tool. Bauer et al. found that bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage had a 27% adjusted diagnostic yield (165 out of 618 patients), with a 38% change in management (236 out of 618 patients).1 While not prohibitive, bronchoscopy was associated with higher ICU (40% vs 28%) and hospital mortality (49% vs 41%), likely reflecting illness severity (86.3% of patients that had a bronchoscopy were intubated vs 46% of patients that did not have a bronchoscopy).
references: (1) Bauer PR, Chevret S, Yadav, H, et al. Diagnosis and Outcome of Acute Respiratory Failure in Immunocompromised Patients after Bronchoscopy. Eur Respir J 2019;54:1802442 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31109985/

(2) Azoulay E, Pickkers P, Soares M, et al. Acute Hypoxemic Respiratory Failure in Immunocompromised Patients: the Efraim Multinational Prospective Cohort Study. Intensive Care Med 2017;43:1808-1819. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28948369/

questions.prompt: A 42 years old male of Japanese descent presented with several lung nodules, mediastinal adenopathy and brain metastasis consistent with stage IV lung cancer. Endobronchial ultrasound was used to obtain a specimen from station 4R which was the most accessible site. Rapid onsite cytology (ROSE) was not utilized. The final diagnosis was adenocarcinoma, and genetic analysis is performed on the sample. Which of the following is true regarding this situation?

options.text: ROSE has been shown to significantly improve the diagnostic yield of testing for molecular markers on EBUS-TBNA specimens.
EBUS-TBNA specimens should not be used to test for molecular markers.
The ability to perform molecular analysis on EBUS-TBNA samples directly correlates with the number of genes being tested.
The probability of obtaining a sufficient sample from EBUS-TBNA for identification of EGRF and ALK mutations is roughly 94%.

questions.explanation: EBUS-TBNA is a minimally invasive procedure that has been shown to provide adequate samples for molecular testing for EGFR and ALK mutations. The pooled probability of obtaining sufficient sample to identify EGFR mutations and ALK mutations was reported to be 94.5% (95% CI 93.2%-96.4%) and 94.9% (95% CI 89.4%-98.8%) in a recent meta-analysis published in the Annals ATS.1 As of 2018, the number of studies evaluating the adequacy of EBUS-TBNA samples for ROS1 and PD-L1 testing is too small to make definite conclusions but suggest that it may be possible to test for these markers as well.2-4 A subgroup analysis in the meta-analysis showed that the presence or absence of rapid onsite cytology (ROSE) did not affect the sufficiency of the sample for testing. Additionally, a randomized controlled trial evaluating the role of ROSE for EBUS-TBNA samples in lung cancer genotyping (EGFR and KRAS testing by bidirectional next generation sequencing followed by ALK testing if EGFR and KRAS wild-type) found that complete genotyping could be performed in 90% samples with ROSE and 80% of samples without ROSE (p=0.09).5 As of 2018, there is very little data on the adequacy of EBUS-TBNA specimens for next generation sequencing (testing for multiple gene alleles) to draw further conclusions.
references: (1) Comparison of Programmed Death Ligand-1 Immunohistochemical Staining Between Endobronchial Ultrasound Transbronchial Needle Aspiration and Resected Lung Cancer Specimens. Sakata KK, Midthun DE, Mullon JJ, Kern RM, Nelson DR, Edell ES, Schiavo DN, Jett JR, Aubry MC. Chest. 2018 Jul 27. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30059678/

(2) Randomized Trial of Endobronchial Ultrasound-Guided Transbronchial Needle Aspiration With and Without Rapid On-site Evaluation for Lung Cancer Genotyping. Trisolini R, Cancellieri A, Tinelli C, de Biase D, Valentini I, Casadei G, Paioli D, Ferrari F, Gordini G, Patelli M, Tallini G.Chest. 2015 Dec;148(6):1430-1437. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26158441/

(3) EBUS-TBNA as a Promising Method for the Evaluation of Tumor PD-L1 Expression in Lung Cancer. Sakakibara R, Inamura K, Tambo Y, Ninomiya H, Kitazono S, Yanagitani N, Horiike A, Ohyanagi F, Matsuura Y, Nakao M, Mun M, Okumura S, Inase N, Nishio M, Motoi N, Ishikawa Y. Clin Lung Cancer. 2017 Sep;18(5):527-534 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28111119/

(4) PD-L1 Expression in a Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer Specimen Obtained by EBUS-TBNA. Fernandez-Bussy S, Pires Y, Labarca G, Vial MR. Arch Bronconeumol. 2018 May;54(5):290-292. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29223638/

(5) Feasibility of endobronchial ultrasound transbronchial needle aspiration for massively parallel next-generation sequencing in thoracic cancer patients. Turner SR, Buonocore D, Desmeules P, Rekhtman N, Dogan S, Lin O, Arcila ME, Jones DR, Huang J. Lung Cancer. 2018 May;119:85-90. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29656758/

(6) Adequacy of Samples Obtained by EBUS-TBNA for Molecular Analysis in Patients with Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis. Labarca G, Folch E, Jantz M, Mehta HJ, Majid A, Fernandez-Bussy S. Ann Am Thorac Soc. 2018 Jul 16. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30011388/

questions.prompt: During EBUS-TBNA, you perform five total passes from a mediastinal node, confirming metastatic lung adenocarcinoma on cytologic evaluation. Staging work up reveals cerebral and bone metastases. Your oncologist requests you send tumor tissue for molecular analysis. Given the information available, what is the most appropriate approach?

options.text: Repeat EBUS-TBNA for additional material from the metastatic mediastinal node
Subspecialty consultation for sampling of either cerebral or bone metastases
Thoracic surgery consultation for surgical sampling of the node in question or of the primary lung tumor
Send the originally obtained material for testing after confirming histologically adequate sample with your pathologist.

questions.explanation: EBUS-guided sampling has a success rate of over 90% for tumor genetic analysis. Current recommendations suggest that a minimum of four passes be performed to obtain enough tissue for molecular marker testing. Assuming there was enough material present from the original sample, it can be submitted for molecular testing.
