Therefore, biopsy of the node via mediastinoscopy, sampling of the primary tumor, or of other metastatic sites are not needed at this stage of the evaluation.
references: (1) Santis G, Angell R, Nickless G, et al. Screening for EGFR and KRAS mutations in endobronchial ultrasound derived transbronchial needle aspirates in non-small cell lung cancer using COLD-PCR. PLoS One. 2011;6(9):e25191. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21949883/

(2) Kang YR, Park HY, Jeon K, et al. EGFR and KRAS mutation analyses from specimens obtained by bronchoscopy and EBUS-TBNA. Thoracic Cancer. 2013;4(3):264-272. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28920245/

(3) Krawczyk P, Ramlau R, Chorostowska-Wynimko J, et al. The efficacy of EGFR gene mutation testing in various samples from non-small cell lung cancer patients: a multicenter retrospective study. Journal of cancer research and clinical oncology. 2015;141(1):61-68. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25086987/

(4) Esterbrook G, Anathhanam S, Plant PK. Adequacy of endobronchial ultrasound transbronchial needle aspiration samples in the subtyping of non-small cell lung cancer. Lung Cancer. 2013;80(1):30-34. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23312886/

questions.prompt: A 75 years-old man with 58 pack year history of smoking was found to have an 11 mm PET avid spiculated nodule in right upper lobe. CT scan of chest without contrast showed no enlarged hilar and mediastinal lymph nodes. The patient is not a good surgical candidate and agrees to undergo electromagnetic navigational bronchoscopy and biopsy of the pulmonary nodule. Which of the following likely increases the diagnostic yield of electromagnetic navigation?

options.text: Location of lesion
Concurrent use of fluoroscopy or radial EBUS
CT bronchus sign
All of above

questions.explanation: Recent advances in newer bronchoscopic techniques such as electromagnetic navigational bronchoscopy (ENB) and endobronchial ultrasound (EBUS) have allowed pulmonologists to biopsy small peripheral lesions. Studies have shown several factors may increase the yield of ENB including presence of CT bronchus sign, lesion size >20 mm, location of lesion in upper lobe, and concurrent use of fluoroscopy or radial endobronchial ultrasound.
references: (1) Minezawa T., et al. Bronchus sign on thin-section computed tomography is a powerful predictive factor for successful transbronchial biopsy using endobronchial ultrasound with a guide sheath for small peripheral lung lesions: a retrospective observational study. BMC Med Imaging. 2015 Jun 21;15:21. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26092497/

(2) Eberhardt R., et al. Multimodality bronchoscopic diagnosis of peripheral lung lesions: a randomized controlled trial. American journal of Critical Care Medicine. 2007.1;176(1):36-41. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17379850/

questions.prompt: According to current evidence, which of the following statements is correct for management of peripheral pulmonary nodules?

options.text: Radial EUBS (R-EBUS) has a better safety profile as compared to electromagnetic navigational bronchoscopy (ENB).
ENB is superior to R-EBUS in localization of pulmonary nodules and therefore has higher diagnostic sensitivity for detecting malignancy.
ENB has higher rate of pneumothorax requiring chest tube drainage as compared to R-EBUS.
Current evidence suggests diagnostic yield and accuracy of ENB for malignancy is similar to R-EBUS.
There is no consistent evidence to suggest or recommend against use of R-EBUS with ENB to increase diagnostic yield.

questions.explanation: R-EBUS and ENB both carry minimal risk to patients. The most common complication for both modalities is pneumothorax (1.5% R-EBUS and 4.6% ENB), with a minority in both groups require chest tube drainage. The second most common complication for is minor bleeding (1.5%). Sedation related respiratory complications during the biopsy procedures are comparable and relatively uncommon. A recent meta-analysis demonstrated successful localization of peripheral pulmonary nodules by both modalities. However, ENB had higher success rate compared with R-EBUS on stratification.1 Interestingly, this apparent superior ability of ENB to localize the peripheral lung lesion for biopsy did not translate into evidence of superior pooled diagnostic sensitivity for detecting malignancy on stratification by localization technology. While a randomized trial by Eberhardt et al. in 2007 showed diagnostic yield of ENB plus R-EBUS (87.5%) was significantly higher than ENB alone (59%), in the NAVIGATE study diagnostic yield was higher without R-EBUS (76.4%) than with R-EBUS (70.6%).2,3 The AQuIRE Registry also found a relatively low diagnostic yield with R-EBUS (57%) and ENB (38.5%).4 Since this study incorporated a large number of centers with heterogenous practices, this finding could suggest that ENB and R-EBUS performance is dependent on expertise and volume of practicing centers.
references: (1) Eberhardt R, Anantham D, Ernst A, Feller-Kopman D, Herth F. Multimodality bronchoscopic diagnosis of peripheral lung lesions: a randomized controlled trial. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2007;176(1):36-41. doi:10.1164/rccm.200612-1866OC https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17379850/

(2) McGuire AL, Myers R., Grant K., Lam S., Yee J. The Diagnostic Accuracy and Sensitivity for Malignancy of Radial-Endobronchial Ultrasound and Electromagnetic Navigation Bronchoscopy for Sampling of Peripheral Pulmonary Lesions: Systematic Review and Meta-analysis. Journal of Bronchology and Interventional Pulmonology. 2020 Apr;27(2):106-121. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31985505/

(3) Folch EE., Pritchett MA., et al. Electromagnetic Navigation Bronchoscopy for peripheral pulmonary lesions: One-year Results of the prospective, multicenter NAVIGATE Study. Journal of Thoracic Oncology. 2018; Vol 14 No. 3:445-458. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30476574/

(4) Ost DE, Ernst A, Lei X, et al. Diagnostic Yield and Complications of Bronchoscopy for Peripheral Lung Lesions. Results of the AQuIRE Registry. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2016 Jan 1;193(1):68-77 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26367186/

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about peripheral sampling bronchoscopic techniques is most accurate?

options.text: The yield of electromagnetic navigation bronchoscopy (ENB)-guided sampling is not dependent on a bronchus sign
The use of a guide sheath improves the yield of radial probe EBUS procedures
ENB always provides real-time tracking of lesions during procedures
Biopsies performed with a concentric radial EBUS view have an increased yield compared with eccentric view lesions
The addition of TBNA does not influence the yield of the procedure

questions.explanation: Radial probe ultrasound-guided sampling can be performed through a guidesheath as a conduit for biopsy instruments or with a small-caliber outer diameter bronchoscope without a guide sheath. The diagnostic yield of these two approaches is similar. When a concentric radial EBUS image is seen, the diagnostic yield approaches that of CT-guided biopsy but in eccentric views the yield falls to less than 50%. Electromagnetic navigation utilizes a virtual, three-dimensional reconstruction from a CT. Although some software (Veran Medical) attempt to compensate for respiratory movement, respiratory variation can be up to four centimeters and the location of the lesion on planning CT does not always reflect the actual location at the time of bronchoscopy. The diagnostic yield of electromagnetic bronchoscopy is strongly influenced by the presence of a bronchus sign. In a 2010 study the yield of navigational bronchoscopy was 79% with a bronchus sign compared to only 31% without. The utilization of multiple instruments, including transbronchial needles for peripheral bronchoscopic sampling greatly increases the yield, especially with lesions located eccentric to the airway.
references: (1) Chao T-Y, Chien M-T, Lie C-H, Chung Y-H, Wang J-L, Lin M-C. Endobronchial ultrasonography-guided transbronchial needle aspiration increases the diagnostic yield of peripheral pulmonary lesions: a randomized trial. CHEST Journal. 2009;136(1):229-236. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18812446/

(2) Kurimoto N, Miyazawa T, Okimasa S, et al. Endobronchial ultrasonography using a guide sheath increases the ability to diagnose peripheral pulmonary lesions endoscopically. CHEST Journal. 2004;126(3):959-965. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15364779/

(3) Chen A, Chenna P, Loiselle A, Massoni J, Mayse M, Misselhorn D. Radial probe endobronchial ultrasound for peripheral pulmonary lesions. A 5-year institutional experience. Ann Am Thorac Soc. 2014;11(4):578-582. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24635641/

(4) Yamada N, Yamazaki K, Kurimoto N, et al. Factors related to diagnostic yield of transbronchial biopsy using endobronchial ultrasonography with a guide sheath in small peripheral pulmonary lesions. Chest. 2007;132(2):603-608. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17573504/

questions.prompt: A 65-year-old male prior smoker is evaluated for a right sided lung nodule with a bronchus sign. You want to perform a diagnostic bronchoscopy tomorrow; however, your nurse coordinator mentions that your electromagnetic navigational system is inoperative. Your coordinator notes that there are new available prototype ultrathin and thin bronchoscopes (3 and 4 mm external diameter, respectively) available in the bronchoscopy suite. Please select the correct statement:

options.text: There is no difference in the diagnostic yield between ultrathin and thin bronchoscopes
Procedure duration is generally longer with an ultrathin bronchoscope
Complication rate is higher with the ultrathin bronchoscope
Addition of radial endobronchial ultrasound and fluoroscopy will increase the yield regardless of bronchoscope used

questions.explanation: A recent study led by Oki et al,1 evaluated the diagnostic yield of peripheral lung nodule sampling using a thin (4 mm outer diameter, 2 mm working channel) vs. ultrathin (3mm diameter, 1.7mm working channel) bronchoscopes in 360 patients. The ultrathin group used a virtually created bronchoscopic pathway, radial endobronchial ultrasound (R-EBUS, diameter 1.4mm), fluoroscopy and 1.5mm forceps. The thin bronchoscopy group used a guide sheath with curette in most cases (60%), along with R-EBUS, fluoroscopy, and forceps. A 21-gauge needle was inserted when the lesion was adjacent or invisible to R-EBUS. There was no significant difference between the baseline demographic data or lung nodule characteristics in both groups. The median lesion diameter was 19 mm. This study found a significantly higher diagnostic yield in the ultrathin group when compared to the thin bronchoscope group (70.1% vs 58.7%, respectively; P=0.027), even after using needle aspiration and larger biopsy forceps (choice A incorrect). The procedure duration was significantly shorter in the ultrathin group versus the thin group (median, 24.8 vs 26.8 minutes, respectively; P=0.008) (choice B incorrect). Additionally, there was no statistical difference in the complication rates in both groups (2.8% ultrathin and 4.5 % thin group, P=0.574) (choice C incorrect). The sampling of lung nodules is enhanced by R-EBUS.2 Prior reports have shown that bronchoscopy with fluoroscopic guidance alone has a sensitivity of 34% for the diagnosis of malignant disease in nodules <2 cm and of 63% for nodules >2 cm.3 Prior metanalyses confirmed an improved yield when using R-EBUS as guidance for sampling in malignant disease (up to 73%).4 The diagnostic yield of a multimodal bronchoscopic approach utilizing both endobronchial ultrasound and navigation for peripheral pulmonary lesions is higher than each technique alone (choice D correct).2,5
references: (1) Rivera MP, Mehta AC. Initial diagnosis of lung cancer: ACCP evidence-based clinical practice guidelines (2nd ed). Chest. 2007;132:131S-148S. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17873165/

(2) Oki M, Saka H, Asano F, et al. Use of an Ultrathin vs Thin Bronchoscope for Peripheral Pulmonary Lesions: A Randomized Trial. Chest. 2019 Nov;156(5):954-964. doi: 10.1016/j.chest.2019.06.038. Epub 2019 Jul 26. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31356810/

(3) Oki M, Saka H, Ando M, et al. Ultrathin Bronchoscopy with Multimodal Devices for Peripheral Pulmonary Lesions. A Randomized Trial. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2015 Aug 15;192(4):468-76. doi: 10.1164/rccm.201502-0205OC. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26039792/

(4) Hsia DW, Jensen KW, et al. Diagnosis of lung nodules with peripheral/radial endobronchial ultrasound-guided transbronchial biopsy. J Bronchology Interv Pulmonol. 2012 Jan;19(1):5-11. doi: 10.1097/LBR.0b013e31823fcf11. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23207256/

(5) Steinfort DP, Khor YH, Manser RL, et al. Radial probe endobronchial ultrasound for the diagnosis of peripheral lung cancer: systematic review and meta- analysis. Eur Respir J. 2011;37:902-910. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20693253/

questions.prompt: You are assessing a patient with suspected malignancy and metastases to thoracic lymph nodes with EBUS-TBNA. You are particularly interested in several nodes that on CT scan appeared partially necrotic. The EBUS examination of one of these nodes is shown below.

options.text: Sample the node at its periphery
Sample the node at its center
Move on to the next node
Sample the structure deep to the node instead
Adjust the gain to better characterize the structure at the center of the node

questions.explanation: The EBUS images depict a lymph node with a single large vessel coursing through its center, as seen with the use of the Color Doppler feature. Therefore, to avoid the vessel this node should be sampled at its periphery rather than the center (choice A correct, B incorrect). It is unclear how well the relative magnitude and patterns of nodal vascularity predict malignancy. However, Nakajima and colleagues suggested higher malignant potential with richness of blood flow and increased number of vessels within a node, while those with a single vessel (as in this case) were more likely to be benign.1 Regardless, the presence of this feature (or lymph node characteristics in general) should not alone obviate sampling of an accessible node within the context of systematic mediastinal staging for suspected lung malignancy (choice C incorrect).2 The structure 'deep' to the node in the image is the lung and is not the target (choice D incorrect). The gain function would not add any further information in this circumstance. It can be used to better characterize a hypoechoic area of interest in instances when Color Doppler flow is negative which suggests either central necrosis or cystic fluid (choice E incorrect).
references: (1) Nakajima T, Anayama T, Shingyoji M, Kimura H, Yoshino I, Yasufuku K. Vascular image patterns of lymph nodes for the prediction of metastatic disease during EBUS-TBNA for mediastinal staging of lung cancer. J Thorac Oncol 2012;7:1009-14. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22525556/

(2) Wahidi MM, Herth F, Yasufuku K, et al. Technical Aspects of Endobronchial Ultrasound-Guided Transbronchial Needle Aspiration: CHEST Guideline and Expert Panel Report. Chest 2016;149:816-35. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26402427/

questions.prompt: You are performing radial EBUS-guided sampling of the lung lesion seen below on CT. You are able to localize the lesion via several subsegmental bronchi and obtain EBUS images from each. You choose one of these airways and sample transbronchially using only flexible forceps, which yields a diagnosis. Which of the following EBUS images did you most likely use to guide biopsy during your procedure?
questions.explanation: All the images demonstrate a radial EBUS signal within the airway. The pattern of circumferential echoes about the pinhole center of the image (which represents the rotating probe within the airway) gives clues as to the type of lesion and its location about the bronchiole. To view the lesion, the radial probe must be in contact with the airway wall, as intervening air will degrade the image quality. Lung consolidations and solid nodules/masses will appear heterogeneously hyperechoic (as in choices A, B, and D). If the bronchiole is adjacent to but still in contact with the lesion, the echo will appear eccentric (choices B and D). In contrast, a bronchiole within the target will have lesional echo completely surrounding the airway/probe, and yield a concentric image (choice A). Because concentric images likely indicate localization within the lesion, they predict the highest accuracy with radial EBUS-guided sampling, up to 80%, especially when forceps are used (choice A correct). Eccentric images predict only fair diagnostic yields, about 40-50%. However, the use of a needle, which allows for eccentric transbronchiolar sampling, may play a synergistic role in enhancing yield in these cases (choices B and D incorrect). Choice C shows a radial EBUS image within normally aerated lung. Even if the lesion is nearby, lack of communication with the airway makes sampling with forceps here a likely miss. Choice E shows a radial EBUS image of a central airway, not a peripheral lung lesion as depicted in the question stem.
references: (1) Ali MS, Trick W, Mba BI, Mohananey D, Sethi J, Musani AI. Radial endobronchial ultrasound for the diagnosis of peripheral pulmonary lesions: A systematic review and meta-analysis. Respirology 2017;22:443-53. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28177181/

(2) Chao T-Y, Chien M-T, Lie C-H, Chung Y-H, Wang J-L, Lin M-C. Endobronchial ultrasonography-guided transbronchial needle aspiration increases the diagnostic yield of peripheral pulmonary lesions: a randomized trial. CHEST Journal. 2009;136(1):229-236 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18812446/

questions.prompt: A 50 years-old male without any significant past medical history is referred to you for the evaluation of interstitial lung disease diagnosed on CT chest. Patient complains of dyspnea on exertion limiting his functional capacity to one block. He denies any history of joint pain, rash, dysphagia, or arthralgia. Extensive rheumatologic work up is negative. There is no history of exposure to birds, pets or chemicals. CT scan is suggestive of possible NSIP and very unlikely to be UIP pattern. You are planning for a cryobiopsy. Based on the COLDICE study, what would you explain to the patient?

options.text: Tranbronchial forceps biopsy is equivalent to transbronchial cryobiopsy.
Incidence of mild to moderate bleeding attributed to cryobiopsy is 22%.
Histopathological agreement between cryobiopsy and surgical lung biopsy is 65%.
Incidence of pneumothorax attributed to cryobiopsy is 5%.

questions.explanation: This patient has a CT scan suggestive of NSIP. In the prospective multicenter comparative COLDICE study, patients with Interstitial Lung Disease received transbronchial lung cryobiopsy (TBLC) and surgical lung biopsy (SLB). The study was designed to compare TBLC with SLB only. Histopathological agreement between TBLC and SLB was 70.8%. For multidisciplinary discussion (MDD), agreement between TBLC and SLB was 76.9% with k of 0.62. After MDD, patients with high confidence in TBLC diagnosis, had 95% concordance with SLB. In patients with low confidence or unclassifiable TBLC diagnosis, 23% were reclassified by SLB. 2% of the patients undergoing TBLC had pneumothorax attributable to TBLC. Mild to moderate bleeding was noted in 22% of patients and was attributed to TBLC. There were no cases of severe bleeding attributable to TBLC. 2% of patients had bleeding at VATS port site requiring intervention.
references: (1) Troy LK, Grainge C, Corte TJ, et al. Diagnostic accuracy of transbronchial lung cryobiopsy for interstitial lung disease diagnosis (COLDICE): a prospective, comparative study. Lancet Respir Med. 2020;8(2):171‐181. doi:10.1016/S2213-2600(19)30342-X https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31578168/

questions.prompt: A patient presents with 3 months of worsening hoarseness and dysphonia. His PCP refers him to an ENT physician who finds left vocal cord paralysis on laryngoscopy. Speech training for a few weeks does not help. The patient then develops persistent chest heaviness and mild dyspnea. CT scan of the chest reveals a large paraaortic lung mass arising from the medial aspect of the apical left upper lobe, with associated thoracic adenopathy by CT criteria at stations 4L, 5 and 7. You proceed with EBUS bronchoscopy, during which you are able to adequately sample stations 4L and 7. Your on-site cytopathologist tells you that the cells from 4L "look malignant, with squamoid features." Given the information so far available, which of the following is most accurate about this patient's status?

options.text: Assuming a staging PET/CT does not reveal extrathoracic disease, the tumor should be resected.
The tumor is at least a stage 3A, but may ultimately be resectable after neoadjuvant therapy, depending on the final cytohistology results from the station 7 node.
The tumor is at least a stage 3B, and the patient should be referred for oncologic evaluation.
The tumor is considered stage 4 but has a high chance to harbor a driver EGFR mutation and respond to a tyrosine kinase inhibitor.
The tumor's stage cannot be determined until surgical sampling of the station 5 node is performed.

questions.explanation: The case presented is of a large primary squamous cell lung cancer with clinical involvement of the left recurrent laryngeal nerve (RLN) and multiple ipsilateral mediastinal lymph node stations. Given its anatomic course, the left RLN can be directly affected by tumors or bulky metastatic disease in the para-aortic location. This can result in an initial presentation of hoarseness due to a paralyzed left vocal cord, as in this case. At this point of the evaluation, this patient's status corresponds to a stage cT4N2Mx, which is classified as stage 3B (choice C correct).1 Stage 3B and 4 represent advanced disease in which surgical intervention does not offer benefit, with or without neoadjuvant therapy (choices A and B incorrect).2,3 Further evaluation with sampling of the mass and lymph nodes can be most efficiently accomplished by EBUS-TBNA which would establish N2 disease and obviate the need to sample the 5 node (choice E incorrect).4 Staging can be completed with PET/CT and MRI brain to look for extrathoracic disease and confirm stage 4. Regardless, this patient should be referred for further oncologic evaluation for potential definitive chemotherapy with or without radiation depending on final stage and palliative needs. As the tumor appears to be a squamous cell cancer, it is unlikely to harbor an EGFR mutation (choice D incorrect).
references: (1) Ramnath N, Dilling TJ, Harris LJ, et al. Treatment of stage III non-small cell lung cancer: Diagnosis and management of lung cancer, 3rd ed: American College of Chest Physicians evidence-based clinical practice guidelines. Chest. 2013;143(5 Suppl):e314S‐e340S. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23649445/

(2) Goldstraw P, Chansky K, Crowley J, et al. The IASLC Lung Cancer Staging Project: Proposals for Revision of the TNM Stage Groupings in the Forthcoming (Eighth) Edition of the TNM Classification for Lung Cancer. J Thorac Oncol. 2016;11(1):39‐51. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26762738/

(3) Socinski MA, Evans T, Gettinger S, et al. Treatment of stage IV non-small cell lung cancer: Diagnosis and management of lung cancer, 3rd ed: American College of Chest Physicians evidence-based clinical practice guidelines. Chest. 2013;143(5 Suppl):e341S‐e368S. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23649446/

(4) Silvestri GA, Gonzalez AV, Jantz MA, et al. Methods for staging non-small cell lung cancer: Diagnosis and management of lung cancer, 3rd ed: American College of Chest Physicians evidence-based clinical practice guidelines. Chest 2013;143:e211S-e50S. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23649440/

questions.prompt: A 46 year-old male presented due to progressive shortness of breath over the past 6 months. He first attributed it to his recent weight gain, but his shortness of breath became so severe that he presented to a local ER. He was admitted and a full cardiac work-up including a left heart catheterization revealed normal coronaries. Echocardiogram showed an LVEF of 72% with no wall motion abnormalities, however, his estimated RVSP was elevated at 55 mm Hg. This prompted a CT pulmonary embolism protocol that revealed the following representative image. Pulmonary was thus consulted and interventional pulmonology was consulted for possible transbronchial cryobiopsy to obtain the diagnosis of his ILD. Which of the following statements are true regarding transbronchial cryobiopsy.

options.text: Histopathologic examination is the gold standard for diagnosis of interstitial lung diseases.
The smaller (1.9 mm) cryoprobe is recommended due to better tactile feedback when the cryoprobe reaches the pleura.
Transbronchial cryobiopsy should be performed only with rigid bronchoscopy due to easier bleeding control.
Biopsy should be performed > 1 cm from the pleura due to risk of pneumothorax.

questions.explanation: Transbronchial cryobiopsy has been increasingly adopted as potential alternative to surgical lung biopsy and concerns have been raised over the yield and safety of the procedure. Expert recommendations have been published and calls for standardization of the procedure have been made (1,2). Histopathologic examination is only part of the information needed in a multidisciplinary discussion to arrive at the specific diagnosis of ILD (A is incorrect). The use of a bronchial balloon or blocker is recommended and the procedure can be performed either through an endotracheal tube or rigid bronchoscope (C is incorrect). To balance histological yield with the risk of pneumothorax and bleeding, the biopsy should be taken 1 cm from the pleura (D is incorrect). The smaller cryoprobe (1.9 mm) is recommended over the larger (2.4 mm) probe as it better facilitates maneuverability and tactile feedback when the cryoprobe reaches the pleura (B is correct).
references: (1) Maldonado, F., Danoff, S. K., Wells, A. U., Colby, T. V., Ryu, J. H., Liberman, M. et al. (2020). Transbronchial Cryobiopsy for the Diagnosis of Interstitial Lung Diseases: CHEST Guideline and Expert Panel Report. Chest, 157(4), 1030-1042. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31783014/

(2) Hetzel, J., Maldonado, F., Ravaglia, C., Wells, A. U., Colby, T. V., Tomassetti, S. et al. (2018). Transbronchial Cryobiopsies for the Diagnosis of Diffuse Parenchymal Lung Diseases: Expert Statement from the Cryobiopsy Working Group on Safety and Utility and a Call for Standardization of the Procedure. Respiration, 95(3), 188-200. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29316560/

questions.prompt: A 70 year male with a 30 pack year smoking history presents to interventional pulmonary clinic for evaluation of abnormal CT imaging findings. He underwent CT chest for workup for shortness of breath and cough and was found to have a 3.5 cm right upper lobe spiculated lung mass. Additionally, there are 2 other nodules in the right upper lobe measuring 1.8 cm and 1.0 cm. PET scan shows FDG avidity in the right upper lobe lung mass and nodules; there is no FDG avid mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. Brain MRI is normal. He undergoes robotic bronchoscopy and staging EBUS. TBNA of the RUL lung mass and the lymph node pictured below are both malignant on ROSE. What is the TNM stage of this patient's lung cancer?

options.text: T3N3M0
T3N2M0
T2aN3M0
T2bN2M0
T4N3M0

questions.explanation: Based on the 8th edition of the TNM classification for lung cancer, the patient's T stage would be T3 because of separate tumor nodules in the same lobe as the primary tumor. The lymph node station in the ultrasound image obtained by EBUS is the 4L lymph node station bordered by the superior border of the aortic arch and the upper rim of the left pulmonary artery. Because it is a contralateral mediastinal lymph node, it would be considered an N3 node. The patient has no extrathoracic metastatic disease. Therefore the patient's TNM staging would be T3N3M0 (Choice A) and Choice B-E are incorrect. https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/aabip-app.appspot.com/o/Question_14(b).png?alt=media&token=6eb74cc4-a4cc-4c9c-b951-75d9c4a2a865
references: (1) Detterbeck FC, Boffa DJ, Kim AW, et al. The Eighth Edition Lung Cancer Stage Classification. Chest 2017;151:193-203. https://journal.chestnet.org/article/S0012-3692(16)60780-8/fulltext

(2) Goldstraw P, Chansky K, Crowley J, et al. The IASLC Lung Cancer Staging Project: Proposals for Revision of the TNM Stage Groupings in the Forthcoming (Eighth) Edition of the TNM Classification for Lung Cancer. J Thorac Oncol. 2016 Jan;11(1):39-51. doi: 10.1016/j.jtho.2015.09.009. PMID: 26762738. https://www.jto.org/article/S1556-0864(15)00017-9/pdf

questions.prompt: 60 year-old male patient with h/0 60 pack year smoking is evaluated for lung mass and mediastinal adenopathy. You are planning to do a EBUS FNA. Your colleague suggested doing capillary pull for EBUS. Which of the following is true as compared to suction FNA technique?

options.text: Capillary pull is associated with less blood during rapid on site evaluation.
Capillary pull is associated with similar yield on rapid on site evaluation.
Capillary pull technique is associated with less procedural time.
Capillary pull technique is associated with less adequate sample for biomarker evaluation in patients with NSCLC.

questions.explanation: EBUS FNA is a standard of care for the evaluation of mediastinal adenopathy. In a randomized controlled trial, rapid on-site evaluation (ROSE) has been shown to decrease the number of target lesions needed to sample (1). However, ROSE does not affect final diagnosis. Syringe suction technique can hinder ROSE by blood contamination of sample. Kasirrian et al. compared syringe suction technique to capillary pull technique for adequacy during ROSE. They randomized 118 patients to either syringe suction or capillary pull technique. Adequate sample yield was 79% and 72.4% of patients during first pass in capillary pull and syringe suction technique, respectively (p=0.28). Blood contamination or score was not different between two techniques (p= 0.10). 92% of samples in both the groups had adequate samples for biomarker testing. Mean procedure time was 29.2 and 31.1 minutes in capillary pull and syringe suction technique, respectively (p= 0.38).
references: (1) Oki M, Saka H, Kitagawa C, Kogure Y, Murata N, Adachi T, Ando M. Rapid on-site cytologic evaluation during endobronchial ultrasound-guided transbronchial needle aspiration for diagnosing lung cancer: a randomized study. Respiration. 2013;85(6):486-92. doi: 10.1159/000346987. Epub 2013 Apr 3. PMID: 23571718. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23571718/

(2) Kassirian S, Mitchell MA, McCormack DG, Zeman-Pocrnich C, Dhaliwal I. Rapid On-site Evaluation (ROSE) in Capillary Pull Versus Suction Biopsy Technique With Endobronchial Ultrasound-transbronchial Needle Aspiration (EBUS-TBNA). J Bronchology Interv Pulmonol. 2022 Jan 1;29(1):48-53. doi: 10.1097/LBR.0000000000000776. PMID: 34010221. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34010221/

questions.prompt: Your colleague calls you for advice about a 50-year-old patient with history of lupus in the ICU. The patient is currently being managed for respiratory failure and is receiving mechanical ventilation support. Chest x ray shows diffuse bilateral infiltrate and endotracheal aspirate culture is negative. He is contemplating transbronchial biopsies in ICU. He is asking you advise about the risk and benefits.

options.text: Diagnostic yield of transbronchial biopsy in ICU is less than 50%.
Only in 30% cases, Transbronchial biopsy changes the management in ICU.
Pneumothorax rate is 10% with transbronchial biopsy on mechanically ventilated patients in ICU.
Bleeding risk is 3% in mechanically ventilated patients in ICU undergoing transbronchial biopsy.

questions.explanation: Mechanically ventilated patients in ICU are at higher risk of complications with bronchoscopy procedures as compared to outpatient bronchoscopies. Transbronchial biopsy particularly carries a higher risk of complications. These risks should be weighed against the potential benefits of yield from transbronchial biopsies. Helgeson et al. conducted a meta-analysis of 9 observational trials. 232 patients from 9 studies were included in final analysis. 25% of patients had a complication of the procedure. Risk of pneumothorax and bleeding were 9.5% (CI -4.5-16.2%) and 8.9% (CI 4.1-15.3%) respectively. 62.9% had a diagnosis established as a result of transbronchial biopsy - Most common (42/146) diagnosis with infectious etiology. Out of 33 specific infections reported, PJP was the number one infectious etiology identified (11 cases). Specifically, 28 out of 33 cases (84.8%) had negative BAL. ARDS sequalae was the second most common diagnosis. Out of 8 studies evaluating the change in management,103/210 patients (49%) had change in the management. Data regarding specific changes were available in only 23 patients (9 with steroid introduction,9 with antimicrobial,3 with new chemotherapeutic agent and 2 with new immunosuppressant).
references: (1) Helgeson SA, Imam JS, Burnside RC, Fernandez-Bussy S, Brigham TJ, Patel NM. Transbronchial Forceps Biopsy in the Intensive Care Unit: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis. J Bronchology Interv Pulmonol. 2021 Oct 1;28(4):281-289. doi: 10.1097/LBR.0000000000000767. PMID: 33758151 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33758151/

questions.prompt: A 69 years-old female with unremarkable past medical history comes to your clinic for a second opinion. She underwent a CT chest for chronic cough and minimal weight loss. She is a lifelong non-smoker and currently resides in northern Kentucky. She had significant hilar and mediastinal adenopathy. A PET scan revealed significant FDG uptake on multiple intrathoracic lymph node stations. She subsequently underwent endobronchial ultrasound bronchoscopy with transbronchial needle aspiration (EBUS-TBNA) with inconclusive results. Her pulmonologist recommended a Thoracic Surgery evaluation, but patient wants to avoid a surgical procedure. Based on the most recent literature, you offer:

options.text: Repeat EBUS-TBNA procedure with the Olympus 19G needle
Repeat EBUS-TBNA procedure with endobronchial ultrasound guided miniforceps biopsy
Repeat EBUS-TBNA procedure with transbronchial mediastinal cryobiopsy
Referral to Thoracic Surgery for mediastinoscopy regardless of patient's preference
B and C are correct

questions.explanation: The differential diagnosis on this patient's imaging studies should include malignancy (lymphoma) vs active granulomatous disease (histoplasmosis). Since patient already underwent EBUS-TBNA, repeating it with a bigger gauge EBUS needle might not yield better (option A is incorrect). EBUS guided miniforceps biopsy of mediastinal/hilar lymph nodes [1, 2] and EBUS guided transbronchial cryobiopsy of mediastinal/hilar lymph nodes [3] is safe and effective. Both techniques have better performance for benign disease and lymphoma. Referral to Thoracic Surgery against patient's preference is not a good option (option D is incorrect).
references: (1) Radchenko CC, Cho PK, Kang L, et al. Performance of endobronchial-ultrasound guided miniforceps biopsy of targeted mediastinal and hilar lesions. Respir Med 2019;158:92-96 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31627081/

(2) Agrawal A, Ghori U, and Chaddha U. Response: Endobronchial-ultrasound guided miniforceps biopsy - Need for combined yield with EBUS-TBNA. Respir Med 2020;167:105922 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32421536/

(3) Zhang J, Guo JR, Huang ZS, et al. Transbronchial mediastinal cryobiopsy in the diagnosis of mediastinal lesions: a randomized trial. Eur Respir J 2021;58(6):2100055. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33958432/

questions.prompt: A 66 year-old male with a 40 pack year history of smoking is referred to you for evaluation of a 16mm RLL spiculated pulmonary nodule. Based upon your clinical risk assessment as well as the Mayo Clinic Model calculator, you estimate an approximately 35% probability of malignancy. After discussion with the patient about different diagnostic modalities, a decision is made to proceed with navigation/guided bronchoscopy. Based upon available data, which of the following is true regarding the peripheral nodule sampling?

options.text: The use of an extensive multimodality sampling strategy (use of forceps, brush, and bal/washing plus the use an aspirating needle, triple needle brush, core biopsy tool, or needle tipped cytology brush) is associated with higher complication rates compared to that of a more restricted sampling pattern using forceps, brush, and/or bal/washing.
When using multiple sampling tools, a cytology brush should be used as the first biopsy modality.
Use of an aspirating needle is associated with an increase in diagnostic yield.
The use of rapid on-site evaluation (ROSE) has been shown to definitely increase diagnostic yield.

questions.explanation: There are number of tools available for sampling peripheral nodules. While the recently published NAVIGATE study was not specifically designed to address the ideal sampling algorithm, it does highlight several important concepts regarding practice patterns, diagnostic yield, and complications as well as correlation with earlier studies. For instance, the use of multiple sampling modalities with the inclusion of an aspirating needle or other needle tipped instrument does not increase the risk of complications. In addition, as has been suggested by earlier studies, the use of an aspirating needle increases the diagnostic yield. This is particularly relevant for lesions without a CT-bronchus sign or concentric radial image when R-EBUS is utilized. Also, while many institutions and operators have a defined sampling algorithm, there is no consensus or data to indicated which biopsy tool should be used first. Lastly, while ROSE is frequently utilized and can guide sample processing, it has not been shown to definitely increase diagnostic yields. While a meta-analysis suggests ROSE does increase yields, this study includes older data and suffers from significant heterogeneity within the studies.
references: (1) Gildea TR, Folch EE, Khandhar SJ, Pritchett MA, LeMense GP, Linden PA, Arenberg DA, Rickman OB, Mahajan AK, Singh J, Cicenia J, Mehta AC, Lin H, Mattingley JS; NAVIGATE Study Investigators. The Impact of Biopsy Tool Choice and Rapid On-Site Evaluation on Diagnostic Accuracy for Malignant Lesions in the Prospective: Multicenter NAVIGATE Study. J Bronchology Interv Pulmonol. 2021 Jul 1;28(3):174-183. doi: 10.1097/LBR.0000000000000740. PMID: 33369988; PMCID: PMC8219084. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33369988/

(2) Folch EE, Pritchett MA, Nead MA, Bowling MR, Murgu SD, Krimsky WS, Murillo BA, LeMense GP, Minnich DJ, Bansal S, Ellis BQ, Mahajan AK, Gildea TR, Bechara RI, Sztejman E, Flandes J, Rickman OB, Benzaquen S, Hogarth DK, Linden PA, Wahidi MM, Mattingley JS, Hood KL, Lin H, Wolvers JJ, Khandhar SJ; NAVIGATE Study Investigators. Electromagnetic Navigation Bronchoscopy for Peripheral Pulmonary Lesions: One-Year Results of the Prospective, Multicenter NAVIGATE Study. J Thorac Oncol. 2019 Mar;14(3):445-458. doi: 10.1016/j.jtho.2018.11.013. Epub 2018 Nov 23. PMID: 30476574. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30476574/

(3) Odronic SI, Gildea TR, Chute DJ. Electromagnetic navigation bronchoscopy-guided fine needle aspiration for the diagnosis of lung lesions. Diagn Cytopathol. 2014 Dec;42(12):1045-50. doi: 10.1002/dc.23164. Epub 2014 Apr 1. PMID: 24692403. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24692403/

(4) Yuan M-L, Yang X, Yin W, et al. The effectiveness of rapid on-site cytological evaluation (ROSE) on the diagnostic yield of bronchoscopy in peripheral pulmonary lesions: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Int J Clin Exp Med. 2019;12:3283-3293.

questions.prompt: 63-year-old man, current smoker with 40 pack-year smoking history presents with 2.5 cm right upper lobe nodule and right hilar adenopathy. Patient undergoing bronchoscopy and EBUS guided lymph node sampling is obtained. What is the role of Rapid On-site Evaluation in this setting?

options.text: Increase diagnostic yield of EBUS TBNA
Increased patient and/or proceduralist satisfaction
Decrease the need for further procedures (transbronchial biopsy, endobronchial biopsy, repeat biopsy)
Decreases procedure time

questions.explanation: A randomized controlled trial in 128 patients, highly suspected of having lung cancer who had hilar/mediastinal lymphadenopathy or a tumor adjacent to the central airway were enrolled and randomized to undergo EBUS-TBNA with or without ROSE and a total of 108 patients (55 in the ROSE group, 53 in the non-ROSE group) were analyzed. Additional procedures including EBUS-TBNA for lesions other than the main target lesion and/or transbronchial biopsy in the same setting were performed in 11% of patients in the ROSE group and 57% in the non-ROSE group (p < 0.001). Mean puncture number was significantly lower in the ROSE group (2.2 vs. 3.1 punctures, p < 0.001), and mean bronchoscopy time was similar between both groups (22.3 vs. 22.1 min, p = 0.95). The sensitivity and accuracy for diagnosing lung cancer were 88 and 89% in the ROSE group, and 86 and 89% in the non-ROSE group, respectively. No complications were associated with the procedures.
references: (1) Oki M, Saka H, Kitagawa C, Kogure Y, Murata N, Adachi T, Ando M: Rapid On-Site Cytologic Evaluation during Endobronchial Ultrasound-Guided Transbronchial Needle Aspiration for Diagnosing Lung Cancer: A Randomized Study. Respiration 2013;85:486-492. doi: 10.1159/000346987. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23571718/

(2) Trisolini R, Cancellieri A, Tinelli C, Paioli D, Scudeller L, Casadei GP, Parri SF, Livi V, Bondi A, Boaron M, Patelli M: Rapid on-site evaluation of transbronchial aspirates in the diagnosis of hilar and mediastinal adenopathy: a randomized trial. Chest 2011;139:395-401. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21030491/

questions.prompt: While you are at a pulmonary conference, you run into a medical school friend who currently is an interstitial lung disease (ILD) specialist at an academic medical center in the Midwest. He has heard about the utility of transbronchial lung cryobiopsy (TBLC) in the diagnosis of undifferentiated ILD. He just saw a new clinic patient whom he suspects has fibrotic hypersensitivity pneumonitis (fHP) and is wondering if he should refer him to an IP center of excellence for TBLC or ask a Thoracic Surgeon to evaluate for surgical lung wedge biopsy. The patient has mild restriction on pulmonary function tests and currently is not on oxygen therapy. Based on the most recent literature, you tell him:

options.text: Recommend discussing his case on a multidisciplinary discussion (MDD)
Refer directly to IP center of excellence for consideration of TBLC
Refer directly to Thoracic Surgery for surgical biopsy
Proceed with treatment assuming the diagnosis is fHP

questions.explanation: The decision to submit a patient with undifferentiated ILD for lung biopsy should be made in the setting of a multidisciplinary discussion (MDD) [1]. While the 2020 ATS (American Thoracic Society) guidelines on diagnosis of hypersensitivity pneumonitis [2] suggest TBLC as a diagnostic modality for fHP, the CAN-ICE trial [3] suggests TBLC was insufficient to distinguish between idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) and fHP (post hoc analysis). The authors describe multiple explanations on this finding, to include a small sample size, a smaller number of TBLC samples when compared to COLDICE [4], different comfort levels on interpreting TBLC samples, and the higher prevalence of fHP on this patient population. Until further data is available, the choice of referral for either SLB or TBLC (or if even a biopsy is needed) is individualized, and usually decided upon after MDD.
references: (1) Travis WD, Costabel U, Hansell DM, et al. An Official American Thoracic Society/European Respiratory Society Statement: Update of the International Multidisciplinary Classification of the Idiopathic Interstitial Pneumonias. Am J Respir Crit Care Med 2013;188(6): 733-748.

(2) Raghu G, Remy-Jardin M, Ryerson CJ, et al. Diagnosis of Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis in Adults: An Official ATS/ERS/JRS/ALAT Clinical Practice Guideline. Am J Respir Crit Care Med 2020;202(3): e36-e69.

(3) Fortin M, Liberman M, Delage A, et al. Transbronchial Lung Cryobiopsy and SurgicAl LuNg Biopsy: A Prospective MultI-CEntre Agreement Study (CAN-ICE). Am J Respir Crit Care Med, 2023. In press (https://doi.org/10.1164/rccm.202209-1812OC)

(4) Troy LK, Grainge C, Corte TJ, et al. Diagnostic accuracy of transbronchial lung cryobiopsy for interstitial lung disease diagnosis (COLDICE): a prospective, comparative study. Lancet Respir Med 2020;8(2):171-181.(https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31578168/)

questions.prompt: A 64-year-old male with 50 pack year tobacco history and BMI of 35 kg/m2 was noted to have a 2 cm lung nodule in the right lower lobe during first lung cancer screening. You reviewed CT chest and confirmed that there is an airway leading to the nodule. You are planning for a navigation bronchoscopy for the biopsy. During preparation for the procedure, you are discussing your plan with anesthesia. What is the role of positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) during this type of procedure?

options.text: Minimizing PEEP to below 5 decreases the risk of pneumothorax.
Application of PEEP only improves unilateral atelectasis.
A modest level of PEEP between 8 - 10 cm of H2O should be used.
PEEP is associated with higher risk of hypotension in this setting.

questions.explanation: Lung cancer screening may detect high risk lung nodules, necessitating the use of diagnostic navigational bronchoscopy. The CT scan used to plan a navigational bronchoscopy is performed pre-operatively while patients are spontaneously breathing. However, during induction for anesthesia, there is higher risk of lung atelectasis, which may lead to preoperative CT to intraoperative anatomy (CT to body) divergence. This is thought to affect the accuracy of navigation, and thus diagnostic yield.Sagar et al. studied incidence of atelectasis during bronchoscopy and found that 89% of patients develop atelectasis in at least one segment during bronchoscopy. Higher BMI was associated with atelectasis (OR 1.13). Incidence of atelectasis was higher in lower lobes (50% vs 20% in upper).In a multicenter randomized controlled trial (VESPA), Salahuddin et al. studied the effect of applied intraoperative PEEP in the context of a ventilation protocol on atelectasis and complications during the procedure. They performed intraoperative CT scan 20-30 minutes after the intubation and categorized degree of atelectasis in patients with PEEP and without PEEP. 84% of patients without PEEP developed some atelectasis at 30 minutes as compared to 28.9% of patients with PEEP of 8-10 cm H2O. Risk of atelectasis was reduced below 25% in lower lobes, and the benefit seen bilaterally, in both lungs. There were no cases of pneumothorax in 38 patients randomized to PEEP group. Incidence of hypotension was statistically similar in both groups.
references: (1) Sagar AS, Sabath BF, Eapen GA, Song J, Marcoux M, Sarkiss M, Arain MH, Grosu HB, Ost DE, Jimenez CA, Casal RF. Incidence and Location of Atelectasis Developed During Bronchoscopy Under General Anesthesia: The I-LOCATE Trial. Chest. 2020 Dec;158(6):2658-2666 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32561439/

(2) Salahuddin M, Sarkiss M, Sagar AS, Vlahos I, Chang CH, Shah A, Sabath BF, Lin J, Song J, Moon T, Norman PH, Eapen GA, Grosu HB, Ost DE, Jimenez CA, Chintalapani G, Casal RF. Ventilatory Strategy to Prevent Atelectasis During Bronchoscopy Under General Anesthesia: A Multicenter Randomized Controlled Trial (Ventilatory Strategy to Prevent Atelectasis -VESPA- Trial). Chest. 2022 Dec;162(6):1393-1401. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35803302/

questions.prompt: A 67 years-old female with 35 pack-year smoking history is referred to you for a 1.5cm spiculated, subpleural, right upper lobe pulmonary nodule, suspicious for primary malignancy. You have recently acquired robotic-assisted bronchoscopy (RAB) technology and are considering its use in this case. When advising the patient about the benefits of RAB over other peripheral bronchoscopic approaches such as radial-probe endobronchial ultrasound (rEBUS) or electromagnetic navigation bronchoscopy, which of the following is correct information?

options.text: Peripheral lung access is enhanced with RAB over previous techniques
All available RAB platforms utilize guidance technology other than electromagnetic navigation
The accuracy of RAB obviates the need for lesion confirmation techniques, such as fluoroscopy, rEBUS or intraprocedural computed tomography.
Pneumothorax risk with RAB is similar to higher than with other bronchoscopic approaches.

questions.explanation: Robotic-assisted bronchoscopy is a new approach to guided peripheral navigation bronchoscopy, offering advantages of peripheral access, stability, maneuverability, and precision over previous techniques. In a human cadaver study, Chen and Gillespie (1) demonstrated that the robotic bronchoscope could advance beyond the conventional thin bronchoscope in all lung segments, particularly in bronchi with increased angulation such as the right and left apical areas (mean generation count 8 versus 3.5, and 8 versus 4.5, respectively; answer A correct). This enhanced peripheral access is likely due to a combination of the robotic bronchoscope's stability (which helps minimize kinking, twisting and prolapse) and precise maneuverability (which helps the tip negotiate the subtle turns of smaller and shorter peripheral airways).Several RAB platforms are currently available, and at least one utilizes electromagnetic navigation guidance technology (choice B incorrect). While navigation success is high, the operative conditions during robotic-assisted bronchoscopy may still promote CT-to-body divergence (mismatch of pre-operative planning CT and intraoperative live anatomy), leading to unintended inaccurate navigation. Therefore, the use of modalities such as intraoperative fluoroscopy, rEBUS or CT to confirm lesion localization (or tool-in-lesion) are recommended (choice C incorrect). While data continues to accrue on the safety of RAB, pneumothorax risk appears to be similar to lower than other peripheral bronchoscopy approaches (choice D incorrect). This is likely owing to RAB's precision when sampling even small sub-pleural lesions, such as in this case.
references: (1) Chen AC, Gillespie CT. Robotic Endoscopic Airway Challenge: REACH Assessment. Ann Thorac Surg. 2018 Jul;106(1):293-297. doi: 10.1016/j.athoracsur.2018.01.051. Epub 2018 Feb 24. PMID: 29486178.https://www.annalsthoracicsurgery.org/article/S0003-4975(18)30186-3/fulltext

(2) Chen AC, Pastis NJ Jr, Mahajan AK, Khandhar SJ, Simoff MJ, Machuzak MS, Cicenia J, Gildea TR, Silvestri GA. Robotic Bronchoscopy for Peripheral Pulmonary Lesions: A Multicenter Pilot and Feasibility Study (BENEFIT). Chest. 2021 Feb;159(2):845-852. https://journal.chestnet.org/article/S0012-3692(20)34233-1/fulltext

(3) Lu M, Nath S, Semaan RW. A Review of Robotic-Assisted Bronchoscopy Platforms in the Sampling of Peripheral Pulmonary Lesions. J Clin Med. 2021 Dec 1;10(23):5678. doi: 10.3390/jcm10235678. PMID: 34884380; PMCID: PMC8658555.https://www.mdpi.com/2077-0383/10/23/5678

(4) Lin J, Ost DE. Robotic bronchoscopy for peripheral pulmonary lesions: a convergence of technologies. Curr Opin Pulm Med. 2021 Jul 1;27(4):229-239. doi: 10.1097/MCP.0000000000000782. PMID: 33973554. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33973554/

modules.title: Pathology
modules.description: Pathologic correlates that inform bronchoscopic decision-making.

questions.prompt: A 68-year-old male is referred for evaluation of a left hilar mass and bulky mediastinal lymphadenopathy seen on chest CT obtained for cough. The patient has an 80-pack-year smoking history and is currently using tobacco with no intention to quit. Pulmonary function tests revealed moderate obstruction with no significant bronchodilator response and a moderately reduced DLCO. ECOG performance status is 1. The patient is deemed appropriate for bronchoscopy, and EBUS-guided TBNA of an enlarged mediastinal lymph node is performed. The following slide was seen on cytology evaluation of the TBNA specimen. An 18-FDG PET/CT was then performed and revealed multiple additional FDG-avid extra-thoracic lymph nodes. Head MRI has been ordered and the patient is referred to medical oncology for further evaluation. Which of the following is the correct diagnosis for this patient based on the available information? High power view, original magnification X600, Papanicolaou stain, cytology smear a) Lymphoma b) Small cell carcinoma c) Adenocarcinoma d) Squamous cell carcinoma
questions.explanation: Suspicion for small cell carcinoma should be high based on the chest CT with a centrally located mass and significant lymphadenopathy, along with the patient's history of extensive, current tobacco use. Non-small cell lung cancer may also be considered, however, the slide shown in the question stem is a cytology smear on high power view showing features of a high-grade neuroendocrine tumor (small cell carcinoma). https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/aabip-app.appspot.com/o/pathology_Dis_1.0.png?alt=media&token=5309c5a1-dbe5-4e34-af72-1967d1789eec Tumor cell clusters display dark (hyperchromatic) nuclei, very scant cytoplasm, and lack of a nucleolus. Nuclear molding (nuclei of adjacent cells 'squishing' into each other), apoptotic bodies, and nuclear cellular debris (tumor cell necrosis) are also noted and commonly seen with this tumor. (Papanicolaou stain; original magnification X600). Immunohistochemical staining positive for neuroendocrine markers such as synaptophysin and chromogranin would confirm the diagnosis, though may not be needed when morphology is evident. A mitotic index marker, such as Ki-67 would help classify grade. A, C, D are incorrect. A. https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/aabip-app.appspot.com/o/pathology_Dis_1.1.png?alt=media&token=82dcfd1c-286d-446c-8f6f-b3b9dab75593 High power view, original magnification X400, Diff-Quik stain, cytology smear showing Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma. Tumor clusters display round nuclei and scant 'blue sky' cytoplasm. Note abundant lymphoglandular bodies in the background. C. https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/aabip-app.appspot.com/o/pathology_Dis_1.2.png?alt=media&token=983698a8-9c04-414a-8560-49757fb0f536 High power view, original magnification X400, Hematoxylin and Eosin stain, tissue section, adenocarcinoma with acinar pattern. Tumor cells can be seen forming irregularly-shaped glands. D. https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/aabip-app.appspot.com/o/pathology_Dis_1.3.png?alt=media&token=35f81dd6-446a-4098-a11c-0bc3ac855b92 Low power view, original magnification X200, Hematoxylin and Eosin stain, tissue section. Squamous cell carcinoma (keratinizing) is shown in the upper right part of the slide and depicted on the left is benign bronchial mucosa. https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/aabip-app.appspot.com/o/Pathology_Diss_1.7.png?alt=media&token=f391b637-d5bf-45ab-8a2b-1a343f4b90de High Power view, original magnification X600, Hematoxylin and Eosin stain, tissue section. Squamous cell carcinoma displaying a 'keratin pearl' composed of laminated keratin.
references: (1) Ganti AKP, Loo BW, Bassetti M, Blakely C, Chiang A, D'Amico TA, D'Avella C, Dowlati A, Downey RJ, Edelman M, Florsheim C, Gold KA, Goldman JW, Grecula JC, Hann C, Iams W, Iyengar P, Kelly K, Khalil M, Koczywas M, Merritt RE, Mohindra N, Molina J, Moran C, Pokharel S, Puri S, Qin A, Rusthoven C, Sands J, Santana-Davila R, Shafique M, Waqar SN, Gregory KM, Hughes M. Small Cell Lung Cancer, Version 2.2022, NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology. J Natl Compr Canc Netw. 2021 Dec;19(12):1441-1464. doi: 10.6004/jnccn.2021.0058. PMID: 34902832. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34902832/

questions.prompt: A 71-year-old female active smoker with a 35-pack year smoking history, hyperlipidemia, GERD and mild COPD is referred by a colleague for further evaluation of an abnormal chest CT. A yearly chest CT has been obtained since 2018 for lung cancer screening purposes. Her most recent chest CT shows a 6.6 x 8.5 mm endobronchial lesion in the medial subsegment of the right middle lobe as seen below. There is no mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. Review of prior chest CTs reveals this lesion has been present since at least 2018 without growth until this year. PET/CT is not yet available. The patient notes intermittent wheezing and mild worsening in a chronic cough with occasional inability to fully expectorate mucus, but otherwise has no significant complaints. A bronchoscopy is performed for further evaluation and an endobronchial lesion is seen in the right middle lobe. Endobronchial forceps biopsies are obtained and the base of the tumor is treated with argon plasma coagulation and cryotherapy. A small amount of secretions were cleared from the distal airways after the tumor was removed. Which of the following cytology slides accurately represents the patient's diagnosis? a) b) c) d)
questions.explanation: This patient has a typical carcinoid tumor (low-grade neuroendocrine tumor). Typical carcinoid tumors tend to be slow-growing with a low likelihood of metastases to local lymph nodes or distant sites. This patient's endobronchial lesion has been present for at least five years without substantial growth, thus increasing the likelihood it is either a non-cancerous tumor or slow-growing malignancy such as carcinoid. Additionally, carcinoid tumors often present in the proximal mainstem and subsegmental airways, but may also develop in the lung parenchyma. Signs and symptoms are often related to the location of the tumor and include cough, wheezing, shortness of breath, hemoptysis and post-obstructive pneumonia, particularly when located endobronchially.(1) Squamous cell carcinoma can affect both the lung parenchyma and the central airways, but lung adenocarcinoma less commonly presents as an isolated endobronchial tumor. A papilloma may also be considered, but these most often present as multiple endobronchial nodules rather than a solitary endobronchial lesion as the one seen image. https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/aabip-app.appspot.com/o/pathology_Diss_2.1.png?alt=media&token=5a8d4e28-baa2-4713-adc6-72761ad96e57 High power view, original magnification X600, Hematoxylin and Eosin stain, tissue section, typical carcinoid. Monotonous tumor cells with 'crispy' (salt and pepper) chromatin can be seen. https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/aabip-app.appspot.com/o/pathology_Diss_2.2.png?alt=media&token=acc7e472-efa3-4290-a342-026079023c75 Answers A, B, D are incorrect. A. https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/aabip-app.appspot.com/o/pathology_Diss_2.3.png?alt=media&token=a34f0b13-6179-4b68-a1e0-4a1d8dcc67f0 High power view, original magnification X400, Hematoxylin and Eosin stain, tissue section, adenocarcinoma with acinar pattern. Tumor cells can be seen forming irregularly-shaped glands. B. https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/aabip-app.appspot.com/o/pathology_Diss_2.4.png?alt=media&token=6f17f1b9-045f-400e-9c79-fdf7a1b5f064 High power view, original magnification X400, Hematoxylin and Eosin stain, tissue section, lung adenocarcinoma papillary type. Enlarged tumor cells with no cilia and occasional intranuclear inclusions (blue arrows) are seen. D. https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/aabip-app.appspot.com/o/pathology_Diss_2.5.png?alt=media&token=7282fb5e-d19b-464b-b13c-4b3155a4f8cf Low power view, original magnification X200, Hematoxylin and Eosin stain, tissue section. Squamous cell carcinoma (keratinizing) is shown in the upper right part of the slide and depicted on the left is benign bronchial mucosa. https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/aabip-app.appspot.com/o/Pathology_Diss_2.6.png?alt=media&token=00e3f22f-a82e-4ac9-8c90-124e8bba5803 High Power view, original magnification X600, Hematoxylin and Eosin stain, tissue section. Squamous cell carcinoma displaying a 'keratin pearl' composed of laminated keratin.
references: (1) Caplin ME, Baudin E, Ferolla P, Filosso P, Garcia-Yuste M, Lim E, Oberg K, Pelosi G, Perren A, Rossi RE, Travis WD; ENETS consensus conference participants. Pulmonary neuroendocrine (carcinoid) tumors: European Neuroendocrine Tumor Society expert consensus and recommendations for best practice for typical and atypical pulmonary carcinoids. Ann Oncol. 2015 Aug;26(8):1604-20. doi: 10.1093/annonc/mdv041. Epub 2015 Feb 2. PMID: 25646366. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25646366/

(2) Limaiem F, Tariq MA, Ismail U, Wallen JM. Lung Carcinoid Tumors. 2023 Jan 6. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023 Jan-. PMID: 30725765. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30725765/

questions.prompt: A 37-year-old female with no significant medical history presents to the emergency room for evaluation of persistent left hip pain and dyspnea that she first noticed while completing her daily run the week prior. The hip pain has been mild, but persistent. Her dyspnea has been progressively worsening and she now notes an occasional dry cough and associated right chest pain. Pulmonary physical exam is notable for diminished breath sounds in the right lower to mid hemithorax compared to the left, therefore chest imaging is obtained. Chest CT reveals a spiculated lung nodule measuring 1.6 cm in the right upper lobe and a moderate sized right sided pleural effusion. She is a never smoker and has no known family history of lung cancer. A right sided thoracentesis procedure is performed for diagnostic and therapeutic purposes. One liter of fluid is drained and the patient reports significant improvement in her dyspnea. Cytology from the pleural fluid is positive for metastatic adenocarcinoma of the lung. High power view, original magnification X400, Hematoxylin and Eosin stain, pleural fluid cytology. Abundant tumor cells are arranged singly, some with vacuolated cytoplasm (blue arrows). a) ROS1 b) EGFR c) ALK d) BRAF e) All of the above are recommended
questions.explanation: The NCCN NCSLC Panel recommends testing for specific biomarkers in patients with metastatic NSCLC in order to determine whether these patients are eligible for targeted therapy. Data is available showing improvement in overall survival for patients receiving targeted therapies compared to traditional chemotherapy regimens. Recommended initial molecular biomarker testing includes: ALK rearrangements, BRAF point mutations, EGFR, ERBB2 (HER2), and KRAS mutations, METex14 skipping mutations, NTRK1/2/3 gene fusions, RET and ROS1 gene rearrangements and PD-L1 expression. HER2 mutation testing is a newer recommendation based on clinical trial data and the recent FDA approval of fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki.(1)
references: (1) Ettinger DS, Wood DE, Aisner DL, Akerley W, Bauman JR, Bharat A, Bruno DS, Chang JY, Chirieac LR, DeCamp M, Dilling TJ, Dowell J, Durm GA, Gettinger S, Grotz TE, Gubens MA, Hegde A, Lackner RP, Lanuti M, Lin J, Loo BW, Lovly CM, Maldonado F, Massarelli E, Morgensztern D, Ng T, Otterson GA, Patel SP, Patil T, Polanco PM, Riely GJ, Riess J, Schild SE, Shapiro TA, Singh AP, Stevenson J, Tam A, Tanvetyanon T, Yanagawa J, Yang SC, Yau E, Gregory KM, Hughes M. NCCN Guidelines® Insights: Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer, Version 2.2023. J Natl Compr Canc Netw. 2023 Apr;21(4):340-350. doi: 10.6004/jnccn.2023.0020. PMID: 37015337. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37015337/

questions.prompt: A 71-year-old-male with a history of low-grade appendiceal mucinous adenocarcinoma diagnosed one year ago is referred for evaluation of a left lower lobe lung mass and right hilar lymphadenopathy that was identified on chest imaging obtained as part of his cancer surveillance. 18-FDG PET-CT imaging revealed the left lower lobe mass measured approximately 2.5 cm x 3 cm and was mildly FDG avid with an SUV max of 2.5. He is a never smoker and has no family history of lung cancer. Histopathology from EBUS-guided transbronchial forceps biopsies of the left lower lobe lung mass is shown below. EBUS lymph node staging was also performed and samples from stations 11Rs, 7 and 11L were negative for evidence of malignancy. Low power view, original magnification X400, Hematoxylin and Eosin stain Which of the following is the correct diagnosis for this patient based on the above information? a) Hamartoma b) Carcinoid tumor c) Metastatic mucinous adenocarcinoma d) Primary squamous cell carcinoma of the lung
questions.explanation: This patient was diagnosed with a pulmonary hamartoma based on biopsy of the left lower lobe lung mass and imaging findings. Pulmonary hamartomas are one of the most common non-cancerous lung tumors in adults, although they are still quite rare. They generally consist of various combinations of pulmonary components such as: cartilage, adipose tissue, smooth muscle and rarely bone. They usually have a smooth edge with focal collections of fat and calcifications on imaging. These radiological findings are not always present, therefore further work-up is usually necessary to confirm the diagnosis and rule out more serious conditions, particularly in patients with a history of malignancy. TBNA sampling is often non-diagnostic given the dense structure of some lesions, necessitating larger biopsy specimens or surgical resection. The typical patient is a middle aged male and treatment of the tumor depends on the duration and severity of symptoms and radiological findings.(1,2) https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/aabip-app.appspot.com/o/Pathology_Diss_3.1.png?alt=media&token=21355fda-6c81-4427-bdd1-796a1a6c6b7e Low power view, original magnification X400, Hematoxylin and Eosin stain, lung hamartoma, tissue section. Mature adipose tissue and benign cartilage are seen. Entrapped airway spaces are present in a fibrous septa (blue arrows). Answers B, C and D are incorrect. B. https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/aabip-app.appspot.com/o/Pathology_Diss_3.2.jpeg?alt=media&token=d13d35ca-d44b-4fab-8752-6c2a3a14d5c7 High power view, original magnification X600, Hematoxylin and Eosin stain, tissue section, typical carcinoid. Monotonous tumor cells with 'crispy' (salt and pepper) chromatin can be seen. https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/aabip-app.appspot.com/o/Pathology_Diss_3.3.jpeg?alt=media&token=ecb48486-c5eb-40e9-a9de-acdf3b7f500e High power view, original magnification X600, Papanicolaou stain, cytology smear, typical carcinoid. Monotonous tumor cells with 'crispy'' (salt and pepper) chromatin can be seen. C. https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/aabip-app.appspot.com/o/Pathology_Diss_3.4.jpeg?alt=media&token=cabd723b-4d73-4d5a-8811-b1b57ff0081e Low power view, original magnification x100, Hematoxylin and Eosin stain, tissue section, mucinous adenocarcinoma. Alveolar septae are partially lined by columnar tumor cells and pools of mucin are seen dissecting the stroma. D. https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/aabip-app.appspot.com/o/Pathology_Diss_3.5.jpeg?alt=media&token=f720ea50-0c52-415a-9827-cb808d037071 Low power view, original magnification X200, Hematoxyli and Eeosin stain, tissue section. Squamous cell carcinoma (keratinizing) is shown in the upper right part of the slide and depicted on the left is benign bronchial mucosa. https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/aabip-app.appspot.com/o/Pathology_Diss_3.6.png?alt=media&token=2054cdd6-ffe9-4e96-9b99-7de3e9384fa9 High Power view, original magnification X600, Hematoxylin and Eosin stain, tissue section. Squamous cell carcinoma displaying a 'keratin pearl' composed of laminated keratin.
references: (1) Fiorelli A, D'Andrilli A, Carlucci A, Vicidomini G, Argento G, Trabalza Marinucci B, Ardissone F, Rapanà R, Sobrero S, Carbognani P, Ventura L, Bocchialini G, Ragusa M, Tassi V, Sollitto F, Loizzi D, Ardò NP, Anile M, Puma F, Rendina EA, Venuta F, Serra N, Santini M. Pulmonary Hamartoma Associated With Lung Cancer (PHALC Study): Results of a Multicenter Study. Lung. 2021 Aug;199(4):369-378. doi: 10.1007/s00408-021-00460-8. Epub 2021 Jul 24. PMID: 34302497; PMCID: PMC8416857. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34302497/

(2) Lundeen KS, Raj MS, Rajasurya V, Ludhwani D. Pulmonary Hamartoma. 2022 Jul 4. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023 Jan-. PMID: 30969628. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30969628/

questions.prompt: An 85-year old female never-smoker presents with a solitary lung mass. Imaging shows metastases to mediastinal lymph nodes, liver and bone. She has no prior history of malignancy. FNA shows an adenocarcinoma. Next-generation sequencing testing reveals an EGFR exon 19 mutation, and 90% of tumor cells are positive for PD-L1. What type of therapy is indicated? a) Alectinib b) Pembrolizumab c) Osimertinib d) Lorlatinib
questions.explanation: In patients who have both an EGFR mutation as well as high PD-L1, treatment directed towards the EGFR mutation is indicated because non-small cell lung carcinomas with driver mutations derive greater benefit from targeted therapy than from immunotherapy. Osimertinib (a third-generation tyrosine kinase inhibitor) is currently the drug of choice for metastatic lung adenocarcinoma with a targetable EGFR mutation. Alectinib and lorlatinib are used for ALK-driven lung adenocarcinomas. Pembrolizumab is a checkpoint inhibitor (i.e., immunotherapy), not a targeted therapy.
references: (1) Antonio Calles , Jonathan W Riess, Julie R Brahmer.Checkpoint Blockade in Lung Cancer With Driver Mutation: Choose the Road Wisely. Am Soc Clin Oncol Educ Book. 2020 May;40:372-384. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32421452/

questions.prompt: Match the drug to the most appropriate molecular/immunohistochemical test: a) Alectinib/ALK, Pembrolizumab/PD-L1, Osimertinib/EGFR, Crizotinb/ROS1, Sepercatinib/RET b) Alectinib/RET, Pembrolizumab/ALK, Osimertinib/EGFR, Crizotinib/ROS1, Sepercatinib/ALK c) Alectinib/ALK, Pembrolizumab/PD-L1, Osimertinib/EGFR, Crizotinib/RET, Sepercatinib/ROS1 d) Alectinib/PD-L1, Pembrolizumab/ALK, Osimertinib/EGFR, Crizotinib/ROS1, Sepercatinib/RET
questions.explanation: Alectinib is used for ALK-driven lung adenocarcinomas in the metastatic setting. Pembrolizumab is an immune checkpoint inhibitor used for non-small cell carcinomas; efficacy is highest in tumors with high PD-L1 levels. Osimertinib is currently the drug of choice for metastatic lung adenocarcinoma with an EGFR mutation. Crizotinib is used for treatment for adenocarcinomas with ROS1 rearrangements. Selpercatinib is used in lung adenocarcinomas with RET rearrangements.
references: (1) NCCN Guidelines® Insights: Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer, Version 2.2023. J Natl Compr Canc Netw. 2023 Apr;21(4):340-350 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37015337/

questions.prompt: You are performing an EBUS-TBNA procedure in a 70-year-old female never-smoker with a solitary lung mass clinically suspicious for primary lung cancer, multiple bone lesions and metastasis to a 4R lymph node. During rapid on-site assessment (ROSE), the cytopathologist says that she is seeing an adenocarcinoma (see image). Which ancillary studies are most critical in this case? a) A large panel of immunohistochemical stains for site of origin b) Microsatellite instability (MSI) testing and tumor mutational burden c) EGFR, ALK, ROS-1 and PD-L1 d) PD-L1 only
questions.explanation: EGFR, ALK and ROS-1 are the most important mutations to test for targeted therapy in patients with metastatic lung adenocarcinoma, and PD-L1 testing is also required. A large panel of immunostains is unnecessary in this setting and may deplete tissue needed for molecular testing and PD-L1 immunohistochemistry. MSI and tumor mutational burden are less important than EGFR, ALK and PD-L1 and are currently not considered mandatory in this setting. PD-L1 testing alone is not indicated and could be misleading without concurrent testing for driver mutations.
references: (1) Molecular Testing of Non-Small Cell Lung Carcinoma Diagnosed by Endobronchial Ultrasound-Guided Transbronchial Fine-Needle Aspiration: The Cleveland Clinic Experience. Arch Pathol Lab Med. 2019 Jun;143(6):670-676 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29372844/

questions.prompt: You are performing an EBUS-TBNA on a 72-year old man with a lung mass and mediastinal adenopathy. At ROSE, the cytopathologist tells you that he sees a non-small cell carcinoma in the sample from the 4R lymph node (see image). What is the most appropriate next step during the procedure? a) Send a sample for flow cytometry b) Ensure there is adequate material for molecular testing and PD-L1 c) Stop the procedure since diagnostic tissue has been obtained d) Send material to microbiology for cultures
questions.explanation: Once the pathologist tells you that he is seeing non-small cell carcinoma, the most appropriate next step is to ensure there is enough material for molecular testing and PD-L1 immunohistochemistry. In some centers, additional passes are performed for this purpose. Occasionally, in highly necrotic samples, sampling of other sites may be required to obtain viable tumor for ancillary testing. Flow cytometry is used for suspected lymphoma and would not be indicated in this patient. The procedure should not be stopped until you are sure that material for ancillary testing has been obtained. Material should be sent for cultures if pathology reveals granulomas or organisms.
references: (1) Rapid On-Site Evaluation of Endobronchial Ultrasound-Guided Transbronchial Needle Aspirations for the Diagnosis of Lung Cancer: A Perspective From Members of the Pulmonary Pathology Society. Arch Pathol Lab Med. 2018 Feb;142(2):253-262 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28639854/

questions.prompt: A 63-year old female current smoker with COPD and a 50-pack year history of smoking presents with a large cavitary lung mass and suspected metastasis to mediastinal lymph nodes. She has no prior history of malignancy. EBUS-TBNA shows a keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma (see image). What ancillary testing is indicated? a) PD-L1 immunohistochemistry b) Large-panel next generation sequencing for driver mutations c) Tumor mutation burden d) Microsatellite instability testing
questions.explanation: PD-L1 testing (which is done by immunohistochemistry, and is not a molecular test) is indicated for any non-small cell carcinoma regardless of subtype, especially in advanced stages. Unlike lung adenocarcinoma, targetable driver mutations are very uncommon in squamous cell carcinoma of the lung. Because of the extremely low yield, many centers do not test for driver mutations in this setting except for patients who are very young or never-smokers. Tumor mutation burden and microsatellite instability as not required for current treatment algorithms in squamous cell carcinoma.
references: (1) Clarifying the spectrum of driver oncogene mutations in biomarker-verified squamous carcinoma of lung: lack of EGFR/KRAS and presence of PIK3CA/AKT1 mutations. Clin Cancer Res. 2012 Feb 15;18(4):1167-76 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22228640/

questions.prompt: A 32-year old woman with mild cough is found to have bilateral lung nodules and symmetrical hilar and mediastinal adenopathy. You perform an EBUS-TBNA. In the FNA of station 7 the cytopathologist tells you she sees granulomas (see image). What is the most appropriate next step? a) Stop the procedure b) Send tissue for molecular testing c) Ask the pathologist whether the diagnosis is sarcoidosis d) Send material to the microbiology lab for cultures
questions.explanation: The finding of a granuloma, which is a clustering of epithelioid histiocytes, (white arrows) with a peripheral cuff of lymphocytes and plasma cells, suggests an adaptive Th-1 immune response. It can be seen with a multitude of infectious and non-infectious conditions, including fungal and mycobacterial diseases, and sarcoidosis. Since the diagnosis of sarcoidosis requires exclusion of an infection, submitting material for cultures is essential. A definitive diagnosis of sarcoidosis cannot be rendered at the time of ROSE, as sarcoidosis is a clinical, not cytolologic diagnosis (there are no cytologic features specific for sarcoidosis).
references: (1) Granulomatous lung disease: an approach to the differential diagnosis. Arch Pathol Lab Med. 2010 May;134(5):667-90 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20441499/

questions.prompt: An 85-year old heavy smoker presents with weakness and is found to have SIADH, a large hilar mass and mediastinal adenopathy. At ROSE, during EBUS-TBNA, the cytopathologist calls the cells 'positive for malignancy, favor small cell carcinoma' (see image). Which malignancies most closely mimic the morphology of small cell carcinoma at ROSE? a) Adenocarcinoma, large cell carcinoma b) Lymphoma, poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma c) Sarcoma, melanoma d) Metastatic colonic carcinoma, metastatic breast carcinoma
questions.explanation: Although a diagnosis of small cell carcinoma on ROSE is usually correct, other malignancies can mimic this morphology, especially lymphoma and poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma. These mimics of small cell carcinoma can be differentiated by immunohistochemistry, which cannot be performed during ROSE.
references: (1) Best Practices Recommendations for Diagnostic Immunohistochemistry in Lung Cancer. J Thorac Oncol. 2019 Mar;14(3):377-407 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30572031/

questions.prompt: A 46-year old man presents with fever, weight loss and cough. Imaging shows a lung mass in the left upper lobe. A CT-guided core needle biopsy shows necrotizing granulomas and acid-fast bacteria. No tissue was sent to microbiology. The infectious disease physician wants to know whether the AFB seen by the pathologist are M. tuberculosis or non-tuberculous mycobacteria such as M. avium complex (MAC) because treatment will differ. What can be done to determine what type of organism these AFBs represent? a) Call the pathologist and ask them to send the biopsied tissue for cultures b) Call the pathologist and ask them whether this is TB or not based on the AFB-stain. c) Ask the pathologist to send a tissue block for mycobacterial PCR d) Repeat CT-guided biopsy
questions.explanation: Definitive speciation is essential for proper mycobacterial therapy. While culture-based speciation with antimicrobial sensitivity testing is standard of care, cultures are not available in this scenario-- once a specimen has been placed into formalin (and subsequently processed and embedded in paraffin) it becomes unusable for cultures (choice A incorrect). The preferred alternative would be to perform bronchoscopy and obtain cultures from bronchioalveolar secretions and/or tissue. This sampling approach would minimize risk of pneumothorax and potential consequent contamination of the pleural space with mycobacterial organisms (Choice D incorrect; bronchoscopy option not provided in answer choices). A reasonable non-invasive alternative is to send tissue samples for mycobacterial PCR for accurate speciation (choice C).
