Mycobacterial PCR can be performed on formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded tissue but this is a send out test performed only in a few specialized labs. Choice B is incorrect since there is no way to tell morphologically whether an AFB-positive organism is M. tuberculosis or a non-tuberculous mycobacterium.
references: (1) Pathology of pulmonary tuberculosis and non-tuberculous mycobacterial lung disease: Facts, misconceptions, and practical tips for pathologists. Semin Diagn Pathol. 2017 Nov;34(6):518-529 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28693908/

questions.prompt: A 52-year old woman with a history of breast cancer is found to have multiple bilateral lung nodules ranging from 1 mm to 1.4 cm in size. Metastatic breast cancer is suspected and EBUS is performed. At ROSE, 'spindle cells, possible low-grade neuroendocrine lesion' is reported. What would explain this seemingly discrepant read? a) Most breast cancers have neuroendocrine morphology b) When breast cancers metastasize, they develop neuroendocrine features c) The largest nodule might be a carcinoid tumor and the smaller nodules could possibly be neuroendocrine (carcinoid tumorlets/DIPNECH) d) The pathologic interpretation must be incorrect since there are multiple bilateral nodules, and carcinoid tumor is typically a solitary lung nodule
questions.explanation: Carcinoid tumors of the lung are neuroendocrine neoplasms that are sometimes discovered incidentally during chest imaging for other cancers. Minute neuroendocrine proliferations forming multiple small bilateral lung nodules (known as multiple carcinoid tumorlets or so-called diffuse intrapulmonary neuroendocrine cell hyperplasia/'DIPNECH') are often present in the background lung in patients with carcinoid tumor. Most breast cancers do not have (or develop) neuroendocrine morphologic features.
references: (1) Significance of multiple carcinoid tumors and tumorlets in surgical lung specimens: analysis of 28 patients. Chest. 2007 Jun;131(6):1635-43 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17400673/

modules.title: Pleural Disease
modules.description: Diagnosis and management of malignant and benign pleural processes.

questions.prompt: A 72 years-old man is admitted with left sided pleural effusion. A 14Fr pigtail catheter is placed and 1600ml drained. No air-leak is present. A CT scan is performed to further assess for underlying pathology. The radiologist calls emergently and reports a large pneumothorax on the left side, shown on CT scan below. What is the next step?

options.text: Increase the suction to -40 cmH2O
Place a 32 Fr. surgical chest tube
Placement of endobronchial valves
Stop suction and consider surgical consultation
Repeat imaging in 12 hrs

questions.explanation: This is an "un-expandable" lung, as supported by ipsilateral hemithorax volume loss on the chest CT. Increasing suction will only result in a more negative intrapleural pressure and can cause patient discomfort and injury to the visceral pleura. The use of a large bore chest tube is not indicated as there is no persistent air leak or evidence that the current tube is inadequate.
references: (1) Huggins JT, Sahn SA, Heidecker J, Ravenel JG, Doelken P. Characteristics of trapped lung: pleural fluid analysis, manometry, and air-contrast chest CT. CHEST Journal. 2007;131(1):206-213. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17218577/

(2) Qureshi RA, Collinson SL, Powell RJ, Froeschle PO, Berrisford RG. Management of malignant pleural effusion associated with trapped lung syndrome. Asian Cardiovascular and Thoracic Annals. 2008;16(2):120-123. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18381869/

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements regarding pleural physiology is true?

options.text: Pleural manometry during thoracentesis is not valuable in detecting non-expandable lung and guide pleural interventions
Pleural elastance is defined as change in volume divide by change in pressure. The upper limit of normal is 14.5 L/cm H2O
Higher pleural elastance is associated with non-expandable lung
Measured pleural pressures depend on the location of the catheter tip
In a resting person at functional residual capacity, normal opening pleural pressure is slightly positive

questions.explanation: Although there is debate regarding the routine use of pleural manometry, this simple and inexpensive measurement provides valuable information for the early detection of non-expandable lung, although a recent study did not suggest that measurement of pleural pressure altered procedure-related chest discomfort. (Choice A incorrect).1-4 Pleural elastance is defined as , the upper limit of normal is 14.5 cmH2O/L. Choice B describes the formula for compliance, not elastance. Lan et al. analyzed 65 patients with malignant pleural effusions, performed pleural manometry, and calculated pleural elastance. Elastance was ⩾19 cmH2O/L in 11 out of 14 patients with non-expandable lung, but in only 3 out of 51 patients with fully expandable lung.2 Therefore, choice C is correct. Pleural pressure measurements do not depend on the location of the catheter tip within the effusion, but on the zero-reference level, which is typically the level of catheter insertion (choice D incorrect).1 In clinical practice, the pleural catheter is inserted in the most dependent position to allow maximal drainage. In a resting person at functional residual capacity, the normal pleural pressure is slightly negative (approximately -3 to -5 cmH2O) creating positive transpulmonary pressure and preventing atelectasis at end expiration (choice E incorrect). As fluid accumulates, in the absence of non-expandable lung the pleural pressure becomes positive.
references: (1) Feller-Kopman D, Walkey A, Berkowitz D, et al. The relationship of pleural pressure to symptom development during therapeutic thoracentesis. Chest 2006; 129: 1556-1560. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16778274/

(2) Lester MG, Feller-Kopman D, Maldonado F. Pleural physiology: what do we understand and what should we measure in clinical practice? In: Maskell NA, Laursen CB, Lee YCG, et al., eds. Pleural Disease (ERS Monograph). Sheffield, European Respiratory Society, 2020; pp. 105-119 https://doi.org/10.1183/2312508X.10023419 https://jhu.pure.elsevier.com/en/publications/pleural-physiology-what-do-we-understand-and-what-should-we-measu

(3) Lan RS, Lo SK, Chuang ML, et al. Elastance of the pleural space: a predictor for the outcome of pleurodesis in patients with malignant pleural effusion. Ann Intern Med 1997; 126: 768-774. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/9148649/

(4) Lentz RJ, Lerner AD, Pannu JK, et al. Routine Monitoring With Pleural Manometry During Therapeutic Large-Volume Thoracentesis to Prevent Pleural Pressure-Related Complications: A Multicentre, Single-Blinded Randomised Controlled Trial. Lancet Respir Med. 2019 May;7(5):447-455 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30772283/
(5) Heidecker J, Huggins JT, Sahn SA, et al. Pathophysiology of pneumothorax following ultrasound-guided thoracentesis. Chest 2006; 130: 1173-1184. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17035453/

questions.prompt: Your colleague from the cardiac intensive care unit calls you about a patient. This is a 59-year-old male with non-ischemic cardiomyopathy with ongoing alcohol abuse that has been admitted for the third time this month with acutely decompensated heart failure. He has refractory ascites and a right pleural effusion. He remains on a dobutamine drip and efforts to increase his diuretic regimen have failed during this admission. An initial ultrasound-guided right thoracentesis removed over 3,000 mL of transudative pleural fluid a few days ago. His CXR today reveals a complete "white out" in the right hemithorax. Patient has decided to enroll into hospice care and go home. He is concerned that his effusion will reaccumulate and is fearful to "drown" while at home. Based on the best available evidence, what would you recommend:

options.text: Repeat ultrasound-guided thoracentesis
Chest tube placement with talc slurry
Indwelling pleural catheter placement
Medical thoracoscopy with pleural biopsies
Initiate inpatient hospice for comfort care

questions.explanation: This patient has a recurrent non-malignant pleural effusion (end-stage cardiomyopathy). Choice A is not appropriate since he had a thoracentesis a few days ago with rapid fluid reaccumulation. Choice E is not appropriate as his dyspnea will not be relieved without further interventions. Medical thoracoscopy with pleural biopsies is not needed as the etiology of his effusion is known (cardiomyopathy), choice D incorrect. Chest tube placement with talc slurry will probably fail due to rapid fluid reaccumulation. An indwelling pleural catheter for the management of benign pleural effusion is an effective, reasonable, and viable option for this patient. Major complications are uncommon, and for this particular patient with a short life expectancy, benefits outweigh any risks.
references: (1) Patil M et al. Management of Benign Pleural Effusions Using Indwelling Pleural Catheters. A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis. Chest 2017;151(3):626-635 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27845052/
(2) Bintcliffe OJ et al. The Management of Benign Non-Infective Pleural Effusions. Eur Respir Rev 2016;25:303-316. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27581830/

questions.prompt: A small-bore chest tube (SBCT) placed for a complicated parapneumonic effusion drained 650ml of purulent fluid in the initial 12 hours, and has slowed down to 10ml over the last 12 hours. Clinically the patient has not worsened. Chest imaging shows moderate persistent effusion, without evidence of organization. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Ensure flushing of tube with 30ml Q6hrs
Replace small bore chest tube with a large-bore tube
Consult thoracic surgery for VATS decortication
Remove chest tube and monitor
Continue antibiotics and instill alteplase through the SBCT

questions.explanation: Small-bore chest tubes require continuous flushing to maintain patency. The use of a large-bore chest tube is not indicated until other options have been exhausted. Removal of the chest tube before the cavity is obliterated can result in development of a fibrothorax and should not be attempted with more than a minimal amount of residual fluid present. Intrapleural lysis can be performed for a complex parapneumonic effusion in lieu of surgical intervention if indicated.1,2 Given good initial drainage, lack of pleural space organization by imaging, and clinical stability intrapleural lytics may be deferred at present. Regardless, flushing the tube to assess its patency should be attempted first if this option is pursued in this case. In addition, intrapleural fibrinolysis regimen should include a combination of alteplase and DNase, which offers a better outcome compared to each individually.
references: (1) Rahman NM, Maskell NA, West A, et al. Intrapleural use of tissue plasminogen activator and DNase in pleural infection. N Engl J Med. 2011;365(6):518‐526. doi:10.1056/NEJMoa1012740 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21830966/
(2) Davies CW, Gleeson FV, Davies RJ. BTS guidelines for the management of pleural infection. Thorax. 2003;58 Suppl 2:ii18-28. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20696693/

questions.prompt: A 70-year-old male patient with end-stage liver disease and cirrhosis is admitted for severe dyspnea secondary to right sided hepatic hydrothorax. Therapeutic paracentesis is performed, his diuretic management optimized during his hospital stay, and he is scheduled for his tenth thoracentesis in the last 3 weeks despite transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt (TIPS). The liver transplant team notifies your team that he is not a candidate for transplant and would like you to consider a more definitive palliative approach for his hepatic hydrothorax. Select the correct statement regarding the role of indwelling pleural catheters (IPC) in this population:

options.text: Indwelling pleural catheters are contraindicated in patients with hepatic hydrothorax
The infection rate of IPC in this population is similar to the rate in the malignant pleural effusion cohort
The rate of spontaneous pleurodesis is lower (10%) when compared to other chronic pleural effusion populations
The most common complications of IPC are clogging, renal failure, and catheter fluid leakage
The use of an IPC in patients with hepatic hydrothorax may be an overall safe procedure with an infection rate of ~10%

questions.explanation: The management of refractory hepatic hydrothorax in cirrhotic patients is challenging due to the non-malignant nature of the pleural fluid, as well as concurrent comorbidities. Ascitic fluid control - achieved by restriction of sodium intake, aggressive diuretic therapy, and paracentesis - and simple serial pleural drainages triggered by symptoms are the mainstays of treatment of hepatic hydrothorax (HH).1 Up to 10% of patients with HH will develop refractory hepatic hydrothorax,2 potentially requiring transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt (TIPS) and/or liver transplant.1,3 Unfortunately, not all patients are candidates for transplant and many have previously failed TIPS (20%).3 Therefore, IPC may be considered as a viable symptomatic palliative alternative for these patients, either as a definitive long-term treatment or bridge to TIPS or liver transplantation (choice A incorrect).2 Shojaee et al. performed a retrospective multicenter study that included 79 patients with hepatic hydrothorax who underwent IPC placement.4 They found that the most common complication was parapneumonic effusion and/or empyema which occurred in 10% (N=8) (choice E correct, D incorrect). Sixty-two percent (N=5) of these patients also had cellulitis at the site of IPC insertion, while 25% (N=2) died of sepsis secondary to the IPC-related infection. The rate of pleural infection in this population is higher than the aggregated infection rate of 4.6% reported in patients with malignant pleural effusions with IPC (choice B incorrect).5 Spontaneous pleurodesis occurred in 28% of patients (N=22) and was the second most common indication for IPC removal (after patient death) (choice C incorrect).
references: (1) Bozkurt S, Stein J, Teuber G. Der hepatische Hydrothorax [Hepatic hydrothorax]. Z Gastroenterol. 2005;43(12):1319‐1328. doi:10.1055/s-2005-858742 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16315128/
(2) Porcel JM. Management of refractory hepatic hydrothorax. Curr Opin Pulm Med. 2014;20(4):352‐357. doi:10.1097/MCP.0000000000000058 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24811830/

(3) Shojaee S, Rahman N, Haas K, et al. Indwelling Tunneled Pleural Catheters for Refractory Hepatic Hydrothorax in Patients With Cirrhosis: A Multicenter Study. Chest. 2019;155(3):546‐553. doi:10.1016/j.chest.2018.08.1034 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30171863/

(4) Feller-Kopman DJ, Reddy CB, DeCamp MM, et al. Management of Malignant Pleural Effusions. An Official ATS/STS/STR Clinical Practice Guideline. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2018;198(7):839‐849. doi:10.1164/rccm.201807-1415ST https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30272503/

(5) Badillo R, Rockey DC. Hepatic hydrothorax: clinical features, management, and outcomes in 77 patients and review of the literature. Medicine (Baltimore). 2014;93(3):135‐142. doi:10.1097/MD.0000000000000025 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24797168/

questions.prompt: A 52 years-old woman is admitted to the hospital with clinical dx of community acquired pneumonia After 3 days of antibiotics and persistent fever. Her CT chest is shown. Which of the following profiles is the pleural fluid most likely to have?

options.text: pH 7.23; glucose 40; LDH 1100; 60% lymphocytes
pH 7.19; glucose 90; LDH 110; 45% lymphocytes
pH 7.34; glucose 70; LDH 500; 65% neutrophils
pH 7.14; glucose 45; LDH 1100; 65% neutrophils
Ph7.35; glucose 90; LDH 220; 60% macrophages

questions.explanation: The question stem represents a complicated parapneumonic effusion or empyema. The pleural fluid chemistry profile most consistent with this is choice D: acidic, inflammatory (high LDH), low glucose and neutrophil-predominant.
references: (1) Davies CW, Gleeson FV, Davies RJ. BTS guidelines for the management of pleural infection. Thorax. 2003;58 Suppl 2:ii18-28. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20696693/

(2) Colice GL, Curtis A, Deslauriers J, et al. Medical and surgical treatment of parapneumonic effusions: an evidence-based guideline. CHEST Journal. 2000;118(4):1158-1171 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11035692/
(3) Heffner JE, Klein JS, Hampson C. CHEST Postgraduate Education Corner. Chest. 2009;136:1149.

questions.prompt: You are evaluating a 55-year-old patient with lung cancer and malignant pleural effusion. He is otherwise asymptomatic and can walk without any shortness of breath. He underwent thoracentesis a week ago. Approximately 200 ml of fluid was drained from the right pleural space. Results of thoracentesis showed, LDH of 800 U/L, pH 7.35 and protein of 3.6 gm/dl. CBC revealed WBC count of 9,000/mm3 with 60% neutrophils and 20% lymphocytes. Based on the LENT score, what is the approximate survival of this patient?

options.text: 400 days
220 days
130 days
44 days

questions.explanation: Median survival for patients with malignant effusion ranges between 3-12 months. Clive et al. developed and validated a scoring system - the LENT score - based on LDH level, ECOG, serum neutrophil to lymphocyte ratio (NLR) and tumor type. Patients were divided into low, moderate and high risk based on the composite LENT score of 0-1, 2-4 and 5-7, respectively. Those with LDH >1,500 U/L, NLR >9, poor ECOG, and lung cancer have the worst prognosis. This patient has LENT score of 2 (lung cancer:2 points, LDH <1,500 U/L: 0 points, ECOG 0: 0 points and NLR=3: 0 points). Based on this score, the patient has an approximate survival of 130 days.
references: (1) Clive AO, Kahan BC, Hooper CE, et al. Predicting survival in malignant pleural effusion: development and validation of the LENT prognostic score. Thorax. 2014;69(12):1098‐1104. doi:10.1136/thoraxjnl-2014-205285 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25100651/

questions.prompt: You are at a bronchoscopy symposium and run into a colleague of yours from medical school. He is interested in Interventional Pulmonology and is considering applying for fellowship training. His area of interest is pleural disease. He is trying to narrow his options and asks you about semirigid and rigid medical thoracoscopy. He intuitively tells you that rigid medical thoracoscopy provides bigger biopsies, hence has a higher diagnostic yield. Based on the available evidence in the medical literature, you answer:

options.text: Rigid medical thoracoscopy has higher diagnostic yield
Semirigid medical thoracoscopy has higher diagnostic yield
If biopsy procurement is adequate, yield is similar
Cryobiopsies have a higher diagnostic yield than semirigid forceps biopsies
There is no prospective evidence in regards of diagnostic yield

questions.explanation: Larger biopsies intuitively may suggest better diagnostic yield (favoring rigid forceps). However, Dhooria et al.1 found that if successfully performed, rigid and semirigid (flexible) forceps biopsies had a similar diagnostic yield, similar to findings from studies by Khan et al. and Rozman et al 2,3. However, Wurps et al. 4 described that cryoprobe semirigid parietal pleural biopsy procurement was non-inferior to semirigid forceps biopsies, but still inferior to rigid forceps biopsies. Overall, as demonstrated in a systematic review and metaanalysis of 7 observational studies involving 586 pleural biopsies, pleural cryobiospies do not have a superior diagnostic yield over semirigid forceps biopsies 5.
references: (1) Wurps H et al. Intra-Patient Comparison of Parietal Pleural Biopsies by Rigid Forceps, Flexible Forceps and Cryoprobe Obtained During Medical Thoracoscopy: A Prospective Series of 80 cases with Pleural Effusion. BMC Pulmonary Medicine 2016;16:98 https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4937596
(2) Khan MA, Ambalavanan S, Thomson D, Miles J, Munavvar M. A comparison of the diagnostic yield of rigid and semirigid thoracoscopes. J Bronchology Interv Pulmonol. 2012;19(2):98-101.

(3) Dhooria S et al. A Randomized Trial Comparing the Diagnostic Yield of Rigid and Semirigid Thoracoscopy in Undiagnosed Pleural Effusions. Respir Care 2014;59(5):756-764. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24106326/
(4) Shafiq M, Sethi J, Ali MS, Ghori UK, Saghaie T, Folch E. Pleural Cryobiopsy: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis. Chest. 2020;157(1):223-230. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31610161/

(5) Rozman A, Camlek L, Marc-malovrh M, Triller N, Kern I. Rigid versus semi-rigid thoracoscopy for the diagnosis of pleural disease: a randomized pilot study. Respirology. 2013;18(4):704-10. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23418922/

questions.prompt: A 63 years-old male with end-stage pulmonary fibrosis on 4L oxygen presents with sudden onset pleuritic chest pain and respiratory distress. Chest radiograph reveals a moderate right sided pneumothorax. A 16F chest tube is placed and the patient's clinical status improves quickly. A post-chest tube placement chest radiograph demonstrates complete lung re-expansion and he is admitted to the general medicine wards. After five days, he has a persistent single chamber, near continuous air leak and he does not tolerate water seal. Which of the following represents the most reasonable approach for managing his pneumothorax?

options.text: Talc pleurodesis
VATS wedge resection and pleurodesis
Bronchoscopy with atrial septal device placement
Bronchoscopy with balloon occlusion and endobronchial valve placement

questions.explanation: The management options in a patient with a prolonged alveolo-pleural fistula following chest tube placement include surgical repair, chemical autologous blood pleurodesis and bronchoscopic placement of endobronchial valves. In this patient, pleurodesis using blood or sclerosants through the chest tube would not be ideal as the patient cannot tolerate water-seal or chest-tube clamping. VATS wedge resection would be a preferred option in a healthier patient with apical blebs but in this patient with end-stage pulmonary fibrosis this would offer excessive risk. Placement of atrial septal occlusion devices has been utilized for proximal broncho-pleural fistulas but this patient likely has a distal alveolar fistula which would not be amenable to this approach. The best option in this patient is bronchoscopic balloon occlusion for leak localization and selective endobronchial valve placement (choice D). The specific indications for endobronchial valve placement in the setting of post-surgical air-leaks are: 1- a leak lasting at least 7 days, or 2- a leak lasting 5 days if continuous, present with unforced inspiration, present with unforced expiration, or when associated with subcutaneous emphysema or respiratory compromise. Although this is a non-surgical air-leak there are no other viable options and endobronchial valves should be considered.
references: (1) Dugan KC, Laxmanan B, Murgu S, Hogarth K. Management of Persistent Air Leaks. Chest 2017; 152(2):417-423 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28267436/

questions.prompt: You are asked to evaluate 60 y/o woman with recurrent left pleural effusion in your clinic. She has history of heart failure (EF 30%). Her medical regimen is maximized per her Cardiologist. She has been hospitalized for CHF exacerbation four times in the last three months. She underwent bilateral thoracentesis twice in the last month, and six times in as many months. Pleural effusion results from last two thoracentesis are listed below. On physical exam she has no edema in his lower extremities. Chest ultrasound shows moderate effusion on the left and small effusion on the right. She admits exertional dyspnea that worsened over past 2 weeks. She always reports relief when the effusion is drained. (LDH Upper limit of normal - 225 U/L). You have a discussion about the long-term management of her recurrent effusion. Which of the statements is accurate in regards to chemical pleurodesis for recurrent benign pleural effusions.

options.text: Rate of successful chemical pleurodesis is comparable to malignant pleural effusions when Talc is used as the pleurodesis agent.
Thoracoscopic chemical pleurodesis has less operative morbidity and shorter hospitalization when compared to TPC placement
Rate of successful pleurodesis is higher with use of doxycycline when compared to Talc
Autologous blood patch has similar efficacy as talc for achieving pleurodesis in patients with benign pleural effusions.
None of the above

questions.explanation: Benign pleural effusions have lower success rate of chemical pleurodesis when compared to malignant pleural effusion. The 2004 Cochrane review suggest that talc is the most efficacious sclerosants when compared to other agents. TPC placement has less operative morbidity and shorter hospitalization when compared to thoracoscopic pleurodesis. Autologous blood patch-pleurodesis has been studied for preventing secondary pneumothorax, not non-malignant pleural effusions.
references: (1) Patil M, Dhillon SS, et al. Management of Benign Pleural Effusions Using Indwelling Pleural Catheters: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis. Chest. 2017 Mar;151(3):626-635.https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27845052/

questions.prompt: You see a patient with stage 4 lung adenocarcinoma, on 4th line chemotherapy, and recurring moderate-sized malignant pleural effusion after several thoracenteses done by her private pulmonologist over the last year. She states that the last two have had little symptomatic benefit. She now has mild dyspnea with exertion, but is comfortable at rest, and has no orthopnea. You proceed with US-guided thoracentesis with pleural manometry. Initial pleural pressure is -12cm H2O, and drops to -40cm H2O with removal of 500mL light yellow fluid. Around this time, the patient starts complaining of persisting deep central chest pressure despite short breaks. She feels little difference in her breathing, though she now appears uncomfortable. HR elevates to 116, but vitals are otherwise stable, and three-lead heart monitoring is not much different in morphology. You abort the procedure. During post-thoracentesis upright thoracic ultrasound, you notice seashore sign in the anterior and upper lateral exam, but 'stratosphere sign' posterolaterally mid-lower thorax. You identify a fixed lung point postero-laterally, at about the 6th intercostal space. There is residual small pleural effusion in the posterior costophrenic sulcus.What is most appropriate next step in managing this patient?

options.text: Observation
STAT 12-lead ECG; cardiac enzymes if abnormal
Chest CT with PE-protocol angiogram
Tube thoracostomy
Resume thoracentesis

questions.explanation: The clinical scenario described is typical of a thoracentesis in the setting of a poorly expandable lung. Patients at risk for trapped lung include those with malignant effusions, long-standing or inadequately treated effusions, remote inflammatory effusions, and/or those who have undergone multiple previous procedures.1 In this case, the pleural manometry values (a high elastance), the patient's symptoms of central chest pressure with fluid removal, and the findings on pleural ultrasonography (a posterolateral pneumothorax 'ex-vacuo') are also all consistent with unexpandable lung.2-4 The patient's clinical response is fairly typical as the excessive negative pressure on the visceral pleura can be stimulating to autonomic nerves which may contribute to the signs and symptoms seen in this scenario. Continued drainage of fluid would further decrease the intrapleural pressure and exacerbate the situation (choices D and E incorrect). Given a likely explanation of the symptoms, a CT chest specifically to assess for PE, or work up for an acute coronary syndrome are not indicated at the present time (choices B, C incorrect). Additional work up is reserved for if symptoms worsen or do not abate over 30-60 minutes. Therefore, simple observation is most prudent (choice A correct). For severe cases, some air or fluid can be introduced into the pleural space to help normalize pressures and hasten resolution of symptoms.
references: (1) Huggins JT, Maldonado F, Chopra A, Rahman N, Light R. Unexpandable lung from pleural disease. Respirology. 2018;23(2):160‐167. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29064169/
(2) DeBiasi EM, Feller-Kopman D. Physiologic Basis of Symptoms in Pleural Disease. Semin Respir Crit Care Med. 2019;40(3):305‐313. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31525806/

(3) Feller-Kopman D, Walkey A, Berkowitz D, Ernst A. The relationship of pleural pressure to symptom development during therapeutic thoracentesis. Chest. 2006;129(6):1556‐1560. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16778274/
(4) Akulian J, Yarmus L, Feller-Kopman D. The evaluation and clinical application of pleural physiology. Clin Chest Med. 2013;34(1):11‐19. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23411052/

questions.prompt: You are called for an inpatient consultation for a cytology-proven malignant pleural effusion (MPE) in a patient with now stage 4 (T3N1M1a) squamous cell lung cancer. The effusion has reaccumulated rapidly after several previous thoracenteses, with a median of 4 days between sessions. He is slated to begin second line chemotherapy next week after discharge from the hospital. Aside from mild dyspnea and orthopnea, the patient has no new symptoms. Vital signs are stable, and exam is remarkable only for decreased breath sounds and tactile fremitus over the right lower hemithorax. Routine labs are normal. Bedside pleural/lung ultrasonography shows a large, anechoic pleural effusion at the PLAPS point. You proceed with thoracentesis with pleural manometry. Initial pleural pressure is +2cm H2O. After a little over one liter of dark amber-colored fluid is removed, it is -5cm H2O. At about the same time, the patient starts coughing and complains of ipsilateral shoulder pain. Soon after, you notice cessation of fluid flow that does not respond to catheter and patient positioning maneuvers. Post-thoracentesis ipsilateral chest ultrasound reveals 'seashore' sign in M-mode over all usual exam points. You discuss the options available for managing the MPE with the patient. He is leaning toward opting for chemical pleurodesis to avoid the presence of a catheter for multiple reasons. Which of the following features of this clinical scenario is likely the least important to the success of chemical pleurodesis?

options.text: Timing of the pleurodesis before chemotherapy
The patient's pleural elastance
Presence of seashore sign throughout the right hemithorax on post-thoracentesis chest US
The patient's final ('closing') pressure on pleural manometry

questions.explanation: One option for definitive management of a MPE is chemical pleurodesis.1 This is achieved by the administration of a sclerosing agent into the pleural space to induce inflammation and fibrosis of the pleural layers, thereby effectively eliminating the space and preventing MPE recurrence. The most important factor for chemical pleurodesis candidacy is the ability to achieve and maintain visceral-parietal pleural apposition. In patients with poorly expandable lung, chemical pleurodesis is very likely to fail. Choices B, C, and D are all consistent with an expandable lung.2 However, even with an expandable lung, a rapidly reaccumulating effusion may prevent enough time of pleural apposition needed for pleural sclerosis (choice E). While theoretically active chemotherapy may blunt the immune response needed to induce pleurodesis, there is insufficient evidence to suggest the timing of pleurodesis in relation to chemotherapy administration has an impact on success (Choice A correct).
references: (1) Feller-Kopman DJ, Reddy CB, DeCamp MM, et al. Management of Malignant Pleural Effusions. An Official ATS/STS/STR Clinical Practice Guideline. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2018;198(7):839‐849. doi:10.1164/rccm.201807-1415ST https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30272503/

(2) Akulian J, Yarmus L, Feller-Kopman D. The evaluation and clinical application of pleural physiology. Clin Chest Med. 2013;34(1):11‐19. doi:10.1016/j.ccm.2012.11.001 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23411052/

questions.prompt: 56 years-old woman is admitted with pneumonia, sepsis and respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation. The CXR is shown. A thoracentesis is performed: Neutrophils of 85%, LDH 1250, total protein 4.5, gram stain positive for cocci in clusters.

options.text: Start Ceftriaxone and Vancomycin and perform therapeutic thoracentesis
Start broad spectrum antibiotics (BSA) including anaerobic coverage and await for extubation before attempting thoracentesis
Start BSA and consult surgery for VATS-debridement
Start levofloxacin and vancomycin and place a large-bore chest tube
Start BSA including anaerobic coverage and place a small-bore chest tube

questions.explanation: Empyema or complicated parapneumonic effusion requires anaerobic coverage unless Streptococcus pneumoniae is isolated, and should be drained with a chest tube. The organism in this scenario is suggestive of staphylococcus species. There is no difference in outcomes for empyema between small-bore or large-bore chest tubes. Clinical deterioration or poor drainage not responding to conservative management requires surgical evaluation.
references: (1) Davies CW, Gleeson FV, Davies RJ. BTS guidelines for the management of pleural infection. Thorax. 2003;58 Suppl 2:ii18-28 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/12728147/

(2) Mayo PH, Goltz HR, Tafreshi M, Doelken P. Safety of ultrasound-guided thoracentesis in patients receiving mechanical ventilation. Chest. 2004;125(3):1059-1062. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15006969/

questions.prompt: A 42-year-old female with treatment-naïve breast cancer comes to your office for follow-up of dyspnea one week after a left- sided thoracentesis. The pleural fluid analysis revealed a lymphocytic exudate with malignant cells on cytology. Her CXR post-procedure confirmed fully re-expandable lung. She is an avid marathon runner and states her dyspnea improved significantly after the initial drainage. Today, she describes recurrence of her symptoms, but feels fine at rest. Her vitals are normal and there are no signs or symptoms suggesting orthostasis. Bedside ultrasonography of the left hemithorax done in clinic is shown below. What is the most efficient approach to this patient's management? Figure 1. Pleural ultrasound of left hemithorax.

options.text: Repeat thoracentesis in clinic
Indwelling pleural catheter (IPC), recommend daily drainage
Indwelling pleural catheter, recommend drainage every other day
Chest tube, then bedside chemical pleurodesis
Echocardiogram

questions.explanation: This patient has metastatic breast cancer with a malignant pleural effusion (MPE) and apparent large pericardial effusion. After initial diagnostic and therapeutic thoracentesis, confirmation of both malignant etiology and improvement of symptoms has already been achieved. Next steps in intervention should incorporate overall clinical picture, prognosis, and patient goals and p
references: , while offering the most efficient approach.1 Performing another thoracentesis is not a long-term solution for this rapidly recurring MPE in a patient with a likely survival over three months. Even factoring in a treatment-naïve situation, it is unlikely the effusion will respond rapidly enough to obviate further drainage procedures (choice 'a' incorrect). Bedside chemical pleurodesis via chest tube can achieve long term palliation, and is a viable option for those with a good long-term prognosis. However, none of these pleural approaches will address the pericardial effusion as seen on the lung ultrasound image, which may be contributing to the patient's symptoms. While there are no convincing signs of tamponade, a formal echocardiogram should be obtained to better characterize the hemodynamic impact of the pericardial effusion (choice 'e'). This would dictate the urgency and order of next steps in management. Presence of tamponade may require urgent bedside pericardiocentesis, while absence of such features allows a more regimented approach. In a stable patient without tamponade or hemodynamic compromise, video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) is an efficient next step, during which a pericardial fenestration or "window" can be made, allowing for both pericardial drainage and biopsy. Simultaneously, IPC placement with or without concomitant chemical pleurodesis can be performed under direct visualization, and then safely removed in the future once pleurodesis is achieved.1,5 References

(1) Fortin M, Tremblay A. Pleural controversies: indwelling pleural catheter vs. pleurodesis for malignant pleural effusions. J Thorac Dis. 2015 Jun;7(6):1052-7. doi: 10.3978/j.issn.2072-1439.2015.01.51. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26150918/

(2) Reddy C, Ernst A, Lamb C, et al. Rapid pleurodesis for malignant pleural effusions: a pilot study. Chest. 2011 Jun;139(6):1419-1423. doi: 10.1378/chest.10-1868. Epub 2010 Oct 7. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20930006/

(3) Bhatnagar R, Piotrowska HEG, Laskawiec-Szkonter M et al. Effect of Thoracoscpic Talc Poudrage vs Talc Slurry via Chest Tube on Pleurodesis Failure Rate Among Patients with Malignant Pleural Effusions: A Randomized Clinical Trial. JAMA 2019 Dec;5. Doi: 10.1001 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31804680/

(4) Porcel JM, Lui MM, Lerner AD et al Com.paring approaches to the management of malignant pleural effusions. Expert Rev Respir Med. 2017 Apr;11(4):273-284. doi: 10.1080/17476348.2017.1300532. Epub 2017 Mar 8. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28271728/

(5) Wahidi MM, Reddy C, Yarmus L, et al. Randomized Trial of Pleural Fluid Drainage Frequency in Patients with Malignant Pleural Effusions. The ASAP Trial. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2017 Apr 15;195(8):1050-1057. doi: 10.1164/rccm.201607-1404OC. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27898215/

questions.prompt: A 45 years-old woman with no significant PMH presents with fever, chills and pleuritic chest pain on the right side for the last 12 hrs. Which pleural fluid laboratory value is the most likely to predict the need for chest tube drainage?

options.text: WBC
pH
Glucose
LDH
WBC differential

questions.explanation: The pH is a most important marker of need for drainage of a parapneumonic effusion (PPE), as it is the first change to occur as the PPE becomes complex. Guidelines vary on the exact pH which would necessitate chest tube drainage with recommendations ranging between 7.20 and 7.29. The next best marker of need for drainage is glucose less than 60 followed by LDH greater than 1000.
references: (1) M Porcel J. The diagnostic utility of pleural fluid tests in clinical practice. Current Respiratory Medicine Reviews. 2012;8(5):383-390.

(2) Porcel JM, Vives M, Cao G, et al. Biomarkers of infection for the differential diagnosis of pleural effusions. The European respiratory journal. 2009;34(6):1383-1389 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19541708/

(3) Colice GL, Curtis A, Deslauriers J, et al. Medical and surgical treatment of parapneumonic effusions: an evidence-based guideline. CHEST Journal. 2000;118(4):1158-1171. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11035692/

(4) Davies HE, Davies RJ, Davies CW. Management of pleural infection in adults: British Thoracic Society pleural disease guideline 2010. Thorax. 2010;65(Suppl 2):ii41-ii53. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20696693/

(5) Heffner JE, Brown LK, Barbieri C, DeLeo J. Pleural fluid chemical analysis in parapneumonic effusions. A meta-analysis. American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine. 1995;151(6):1700-1708. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/7767510/

questions.prompt: A 62-year-old male patient with diffuse large B-cell lymphoma receiving treatment with R-CHOP presents for management of a recurrent left sided malignant pleural effusion. The patient has undergone 3 thoracenteses in the last 2 months at an outside hospital for symptomatic relief. You begin to discuss management options with the patient, and he expresses a reluctance to undergo any invasive surgical procedure. As you present the option of an indwelling pleural catheter to the patient, you realize that his absolute neutrophil count is only 200 and he will continue to get chemotherapy once his counts recover. You therefore recommend

options.text: Repeat thoracentesis: he may respond to treatment
Push for talc pleurodesis despite the patient's reservations
Place an indwelling catheter.
Place an indwelling catheter but put the patient on prophylactic antibiotics.

questions.explanation: Existing data supports the use of indwelling pleural catheters (IPC) to manage symptomatic malignant effusions from hematological malignancies. A large single center retrospective study showed that the cumulative incidence of all complications was 13.6% and the cumulative incidence of all significant catheter-related complications was 9.5%. 71% of patients received chemotherapy within 3 month of IPC placement.1 Infectious complications occurred in 5% of patients. Chemotherapy does not seem to increase the rates of infections.1,2 In addition, both univariate and multivariate analysis did not reveal a significant impact of absolute neutrophil count with respect to complications. The role of prophylactic antibiotics has not been studied in this patient population.3
references: (1) Faiz SA, Pathania P, Song J, et al. Indwelling pleural catheters for patients with hematologic malignancies. a 14-year, single-center experience. Ann Am Thorac Soc 2017; 14:976-985. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28326798/

(2) Morel A, Mishra E, Medley L, et al. Chemotherapy should not be withheld from patients with an indwelling pleural catheter for malignant pleural effusion. Thorax 2011; 66:448 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20880866/
(3) Lui MM, Thomas R, Lee YC. Complications of indwelling pleural catheter use and their management. BMJ Open Respir Res 2016; 3:e000123 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26870384/

(4) Pleural effusions in hematologic malignancies and their management with indwelling pleural catheters. Vakil E, Jimenez CA, Faiz SA. Curr Opin Pulm Med. 2018 Jul;24(4):384-391. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29629920/

questions.prompt: A 27-year-old male with no significant past medical history is admitted with fevers, productive cough and right-sided chest pain. His CXR is shown below. Bedside ultrasound shows an echogenic pleural effusion with extensive septations. Thoracentesis reveals cloudy fluid with a pH of 7.1, glucose of 20 mg/dL, LDH of 1,200 IU/L and gram stain positive for gram positive cocci in pairs and chains. In addition to starting antibiotics, which of the following statements regarding the management of this patient's condition is true?

options.text: This patient will be more likely to require thoracic surgery for management of his empyema if a small bore chest tube (<20 F) is placed compared to a large bore chest tube (>20F)
In addition to chest tube placement, instillation of intrapleural streptokinase twice a day for three days will reduce the likelihood that this patient will die or require thoracic surgery

In addition to chest tube placement, instillation of TPA and DNAse twice a day for three days would not be expected to improve fluid drainage or reduce the liklihood that this patient will require thoracic surgery
This patient is less likely to experience pain related to chest tube placement and maintenance if a small bore (<20 F) chest tube is placed compared to a large bore (>20 F) chest tube.

questions.explanation: The MIST 1 trial was a randomized, double-blind placebo-controlled trial in patients with complicated parapneumonic effusion or empyema (defined as purulent pleural fluid, pleural fluid pH < 7.2, or positive gram stain or culture) who were randomized to receive either streptokinase or placebo instilled into the pleural space twice daily for 3 days 1. The primary outcome was death or need for surgical referral. No benefit was found. A secondary analysis of the MIST1 trial was performed looking at whether size of the chest tube placed affected clinical outcome 2. The authors found no difference in clinical outcomes including death or need for surgical referral between patients who received large bore chest tubes (>20 F) or small bore chest tubes (< 20 F) (Answers A and B are incorrect). However, pain scores were significantly lower in patients who received small bore chest tubes (choice D correct). The MIST 2 trial was a 2 by 2 factorial trial in which patients with pleural infection were randomized to receive one of four study treatments instilled into the pleural space twice daily for 3 days (placebo/placebo, placebo/ DNAse, placebo/TPA and TPA/DNAse) 3. In this trial the primary outcome was change in pleural fluid opacity measured by CXR and secondary outcomes included duration of hospital stay and need for surgery. In the MIST 2 trial patients randomized to receive both TPA and DNAse were found to have improved change in pleural fluid opacity compared to placebo (-29.5% vs -17.2%, p=0.005) and were less likely to require surgery (4% vs 16%, p = 0.03) (choice C incorrect)
references: (1) Maskell NA, Davies CW, Nunn AJ, Hedley EL, Gleeson FV, Miller R, Gabe R, Rees GL, Peto TE, Woodhead MA, Lane DJ, Darbyshire JH, Davies RJ; First Multicenter Intrapleural Sepsis Trial (MIST1) Group. U.K. Controlled trial of intrapleural streptokinase for pleural infection. N Engl J Med. 2005 Mar 3;352(9):865-74. doi: 10.1056/NEJMoa042473. Erratum in: N Engl J Med. 2005 May 19;352(20):2146. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15745977/

(2) Rahman NM, Maskell NA, Davies CW, Hedley EL, Nunn AJ, Gleeson FV, Davies RJ. The relationship between chest tube size and clinical outcome in pleural infection. Chest. 2010 Mar;137(3):536-43. doi: 10.1378/chest.09-1044. Epub 2009 Oct 9. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19820073/

(3) Rahman NM, Maskell NA, West A, et al. Intrapleural use of tissue plasminogen activator and DNase in pleural infection. N Engl J Med. 2011;365(6):518‐526. doi:10.1056/NEJMoa1012740https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21830966/

questions.prompt: 40-year-old Asian female non-smoker with minimal past medical history presented with cough and shortness of breath for 2-month duration. CXR showed a large pleural effusion on the left. Pleural effusion was drained with good relief of her symptoms. CT chest post drainage showed full expansion of her lung without an obvious mass. Pleural fluid was serosanguinous, analysis showed lymphocytic predominant exudative fluid with positive cytology for adenocarcinoma. However, there was not sufficient material for genetic testing. Patient wants the most definitive diagnostic and therapeutic treatment for her malignant pleural effusion. What is the most appropriate approach?

options.text: The patient has stage IV disease; initiate EGFR TKI therapy
Repeated thoracentesis if the fluid recurs.
Placement of tunneled pleural catheter
Pleuroscopy with pleural biopsy and pleurodesis
Chest tube placement with talc pleurodesis

questions.explanation: Patient wants the most definitive diagnostic and therapeutic treatment for her malignant pleural effusion. In this case, repeat thoracentesis or placement of tunneled pleural catheter are not the most definitive treatment options. Pleuroscopy allows pleural biopsy; hence, providing larger tissue for genetic and molecular marker studies. Pleurodesis can be done at the same setting as the pleuroscopy procedure. While talc slurry through a chest tube and pleuroscopy with talc insufflation achieve equals rates of pleurodesis, the former does not allow for tissue acquisition
references: (1) Desai NR, Lee HJ. Diagnosis and management of malignant pleural effusions: state of the art in 2017. J Thorac Dis. 2017 Sep;9(Suppl 10):S1111-S1122. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29214068/
(2) Michaud G, Berkowitz DM, Ernst A. Pleuroscopy for diagnosis and therapy for pleural effusions. Chest. 2010 Nov;138(5):1242-6. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21051401/

(3) Feller-kopman DJ, Reddy CB, Decamp MM, et al. Management of Malignant Pleural Effusions. An Official ATS/STS/STR Clinical Practice Guideline. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2018;198(7):839-849. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30272503/

questions.prompt: A 78-year-old Asian female is admitted for worsening shortness of breath and chest pain. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis revealed a right upper lobe mass, metastatic lesions to the spine and liver and a large right pleural effusion. An ultrasound-guided right thoracentesis is performed, removing 2.5 liters of yellowish fluid. Labs revealed a lymphocytic-predominant exudate, with negative cytology. She cannot tell you whether she felt relief with her thoracentesis. Her performance status is excellent despite her likely metastatic disease. The next step in management should be:

options.text: Repeat thoracentesis with pleural manometry
Thoracic Surgery consult for VATS
Chest tube insertion with talc slurry
Medical thoracoscopy with pleural biopsies
EBUS and navigational bronchoscopy

questions.explanation: Medical thoracoscopy with pleural biopsies will serve three purposes: diagnosis, staging and definite management of the effusion. Diagnosis and staging through parietal pleural biopsies, and during the procedure, this patient can be evaluated for talc poudrage if the lung does appear to be trapped (prior thoracentesis does not describe whether there is good lung expansion or not). This patient already underwent a thoracentesis, with negative cytology. While it holds true that repeating a thoracentesis might marginally increase the diagnostic yield, repeating it will only delay her diagnosis, so option (a) is not appropriate. Option (b) could be considered, but medical thoracoscopy is less invasive. Option (c) without tissue diagnosis of cancer is not appropriate either, while option (e) will not aid in staging (stage IV) and will not address the effusion itself.
references: (1) Murthy V and Bessich JL. Medical Thoracoscopy and Its Evolving Role in the Diagnosis and Treatment of Pleural Disease. J Thorac Dis 2017;9(Suppl. 10):S1011-S1021.https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29214061/
(2) Valsecchi A et al. Medical Thoracoscopy: Analysis on Diagnostic Yield through 30 years of Experience. Ann Thorac Med 2016;11(3):177-182.https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27512506/

(3) Miyoshi S et al. Diagnostic Utility of Pleural Fluid Cell Block versus Pleural Biopsy Collected by Flex-Rigid Pleuroscopy for Malignant Pleural Disease: A Single Center Retrospective Analysis. PLoS One 2016;11(11):e0167186. doi: 10.1371/journal.pone.0167186. eCollection 2016 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27880851/#:~:text=Conclusion%3A%20Pleural%20biopsy%20using%20flex,pleural%20diseases%20presenting%20with%20effusion.

questions.prompt: You are asked to perform a right-side thoracentesis for a moderate size pleural effusion on a 65 years-old woman with known heart failure (EF 20%), cryptogenic liver cirrhosis, recent weight loss, and multiple lung nodules. On physical exam she is on 4 L of O2 with saturation 90%, speaks in incomplete sentences due to dyspnea, and has diminished breath sounds on the right. Upon review of the chart, you notice INR of 2.2, Plt 100,000 and BUN 35. Which of the statements is the most accurate regarding thoracentesis and coagulopathy in this case?

options.text: Patient's coagulopathy should be reversed with infusion of fresh frozen plasma in dose 6 ml/kg and 1 unit of platelets prior performing a thoracentesis for INR goal < 1.5 and platelets goal of > 150,000
Patient's coagulopathy does not need to be reversed since risk of administration of the blood products outweighs the risk of thoracentesis
Patient's coagulopathy should be reversed with infusion of fresh frozen plasma in dose 6 ml/kg for goal of INR < 1.5, no platelets transfusion is necessary
Patient's coagulopathy should be reversed with infusion of 2 units of fresh frozen plasma and procedure can start as soon as the transfusion of the fresh frozen plasma starts.

questions.explanation: Pleural effusions are diagnosed annually in more than 1.5 million people in the USA resulting in over 170,000 thoracenteses per year. Ultrasound guidance during thoracentesis is associated with lower complication rates. Specific complications and their risk factors have been extensively documented. Recent literature suggests that thoracentesis can be safely performed in patient with increased risk of bleeding without the need to correct INR, platelet count or withhold anticoagulation or antiplatelets agents. As the thoracentesis is urgent in this case given how symptomatic the patient is and as giving him FFPs may potentially worsen his respiratory symptoms due to volume overload, the benefit of proceeding with the thoracentesis outweighs the risks.
references: (1) Puchalski J. Thoracentesis and the risks for bleeding: a new era. Curr Opin Pulm Med. 2014 Jul;20(4):377-84. doi: 10.1097/MCP.0000000000000062.https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24852328/
(2) Puchalski J, Argento C, et al. The Safety of Thoracentesis in Patients with Uncorrected Bleeding Risk. Ann Am Thorac Soc. 2013 Aug; 10(4): 336-341. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23952852/

questions.prompt: A 40 years-old patient with history of with recently diagnosed Non-Hodgkin's lymphoma is receiving chemotherapy. You are consulted for evaluation of left sided pleural effusion. Patient is short of breath while walking to the restroom and is requiring 4 liters of oxygen. Chest X ray showed moderate size pleural effusion. Laboratory data showed hemoglobin of 8, WBC of 1 and platelet count of 40,000. Coagulation studies are within normal limits. You performed bedside ultrasound which confirmed moderate size free flowing, anechoic pleural effusion. What would you do next?

options.text: Transfuse platelets with the target platelet count of more than 50,000.
Order CT chest to evaluate pleural effusion.
Perform ultrasound guided Thoracentesis.
Start Lasix and reevaluate symptoms.

questions.explanation: Thoracentesis is a relatively safe procedure with minimal complications when done under ultrasound guidance. Usual recommendation is to correct platelet counts to more than 50,000. However, Hibbert et al. studied 1,009 thoracentesis done in the radiology department under Ultrasound guidance in a University Hospital. 248 patients had platelet count less than 50,000. 113 patients underwent thoracentesis without platelet transfusion. In their series, 3 patients with platelet count < 50,000 prior to procedure developed bleeding complications. However, all of them had received 1-4 units of platelets before the procedure. None of the patients without platelet transfusion had any bleeding complications. Orlandi et al. studied 41 patients with severe thrombocytopenia (<30,000) who underwent thoracentesis with 32 thoracentesis performed under ultrasound guidance. None of the 32 patients had any hemorrhagic complications. However, 3 out of 9 patients who underwent thoracentesis without USG guidance developed mild hemorrhagic complications.
references: (1) Orlandi E, Citterio C, Seghini P, Di Nunzio C, Mordenti P, Cavanna L. Thoracentesis in advanced cancer patients with severe thrombocytopenia: Ultrasound guide improves safety and reduces bleeding risk. Clin Respir J. 2018;12(4):1747‐1752. doi:10.1111/crj.12739 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29115028/

(2) Hibbert RM, Atwell TD, Lekah A, et al. Safety of ultrasound-guided thoracentesis in patients with abnormal preprocedural coagulation parameters. Chest. 2013;144(2):456‐463. doi:10.1378/chest.12-2374 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23493971/

questions.prompt: A 29-year-old male with past medical history of alcoholic end-stage liver disease status post transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt (TIPS) for esophageal variceal bleeding, currently on the liver transplantation list at your institution, is admitted for the second time this year for refractory ascites and hepatic hydrothorax, despite maximal medical therapy. On a prior admission, up titration of his diuretics caused acute kidney injury. The hospitalist attending calls a consult for an indwelling pleural catheter placement. He is hemodynamically stable, appears euvolemic, remains on ambient air and is in no respiratory distress. His INR is 1.7 and his platelet count is 99,000/mm3. Based on the most recent evidence, you recommend the following:

options.text: Increase diuretic dose to attempt better control of ascitic fluid production
No further invasive procedures are warranted in this particular patient
Ultrasound guided thoracentesis based on respiratory symptoms
Proceed with indwelling tunneled pleural catheter (IPC) placement
Repeat TIPS procedure

questions.explanation: Choice A is incorrect based on prior intolerance to increased doses of diuretic therapy. Choice B is incorrect as this patient is on the liver transplantation list so further diagnostic/therapeutic procedures will probably be ordered as needed in the near future. There is no role for repeat TIPS procedure (choice E incorrect). As the patient is active on the transplant list, thoracentesis (choice C) is the best option, with the understanding that a higher complication rate is observed in patients with hepatic hydrothorax (when compared to patients without liver disease related pleural effusion). If the patient continues to have recurrent hospitalizations without obtaining a liver transplant, IPC placement (choice D) can be considered for temporizing palliation. However, this procedure carries risks of empyema in 10-20% of cases and mortality is described to be around 2.5%, and thus should be pursued only after careful multidisciplinary discussion.
references: (1) Shojaee S, Khalid M, Kallingal G, et al. Repeat Thoracentesis in Hepatic Hydrothorax and Non-Hepatic Hydrothorax Effusions: A Case-Control Study. Respiration 2018;96:330-337. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29991046/
(2) Aboudara M and Maldonado F. Indwelling Pleural Catheters for Benign Pleural Effusions: What is the Evidence. Curr Opin Pulm Med 2019;25(4):369-373. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30865036/

(3) Shojaee S, Rahman N, Haas K, et al. Indwelling Tunneled Pleural Catheters for Refractory Hepatic Hydrothorax in Patients with Cirrhosis: A Multicenter Study. Chest 2019;155(3):546-553. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30171863/

(4) Kniese C, Diab K, Ghabril M, et al. Indwelling Pleural Catheters in Hepatic Hydrothorax: A Single-Center Series of Outcomes and Complications. Chest 2019;155(2):307-314. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29990479/

questions.prompt: 75 years-old man is admitted with signs of sepsis. After a left internal jugular vein central line placement attempt, the CXR shows the following. The patient is symptomatic. Which of the following approaches to managing this complication is most appropriate?

options.text: 8 Fr pigtail posterior axillary line
14 Fr 5th intercostal space (ICS) mid-clavicular line
32Fr 5th ICS mid-clavicular line
32 Fr 2nd ICS mid-clavicular line
32Fr within the "triangle of safety"

questions.explanation: This is a case of a large iatrogenic pneumothorax requiring intervention. The choice of chest tube size is less important than the location. Large-bore chest tubes should be placed in the triangle of safety which is bordered by the latissimus dorsi posteriorly, pectoralis major anteriorly, superior to the horizontal level of the nipple and below the axilla. Small bore chest tubes for pneumothorax should be placed in the second intercostal space at the midclavicular line.
references: (1) Laws D, Neville E, Duffy J. BTS guidelines for the insertion of a chest drain. Thorax. 2003;58(Suppl 2):ii53-ii59.

(2) Davies HE, Davies RJ, Davies CW. Management of pleural infection in adults: British Thoracic Society pleural disease guideline 2010. Thorax. 2010;65(Suppl 2):ii41-ii53. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20696693/
(3) Henry M, Arnold T, Harvey J. BTS guidelines for the management of spontaneous pneumothorax. Thorax. 2003;58(Suppl 2):ii39. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/12728149/

questions.prompt: You are consulted to evaluate a 30-year-old African American man with moderate-sized right spontaneous pneumothorax. This was diagnosed during evaluation of cough and dyspnea on exertion. His vitals are stable, and he is not short of breath at rest. He had a similar episode 4 months prior where he was noted to have a small right apical pneumothorax which was managed conservatively. He smokes about 1 pack per day since the age of 16. A CT of the chest has been ordered. What is the next best step in management of this patient?

options.text: Place a small-bore chest tube and connect to -40 cmH2O wall suction
Place a small-bore chest tube and connect to water seal
Place a small-bore chest tube followed by chemical Pleurodesis once the lung re expands.
Consult thoracic surgery for VATS Pleurodesis
Conservative management with serial chest x-ray only

questions.explanation: This patient has a second episode of primary spontaneous pneumothorax. The initial presentation was managed conservatively. He is at risk for recurrence of pneumothorax especially given the smoking history, hence steps should be taken to prevent recurrence. Surgical approach to pleurodesis offers a higher success rate (lower risk of recurrence) when compared to less invasive approaches, such as via bedside chest tube. Since the patient is a good surgical candidate, he should be evaluated by thoracic surgery for surgical pleurodesis (choice D correct, B and C incorrect). He is hemodynamically stable and asymptomatic, so, there is no urgent need for a chest tube. He can be managed conservatively pending evaluation by thoracic surgery. In addition, aggressive suction is rarely necessary for a spontaneously breathing patient with pneumothorax and may even be detrimental (choice A incorrect). Conservative management with serial chest x-ray would have been an option if this was the first episode of primary spontaneous pneumothorax. However, this patient is at risk for recurrence of pneumothorax and needs an intervention to prevent recurrence (choice E incorrect). However, a repeat CXR in a few hours to document that the pneumothorax isn't enlarging to warrant a chest tube is reasonable.
references: (1) ERS task force statement: diagnosis and treatment of primary spontaneous pneumothorax. Eur Respir J. 2015 Aug;46(2):321-35. Epub 2015 Jun 25. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26113675/

(2) MacDuff A, Arnold A, Harvey J; BTS Pleural Disease Guideline Group. Management of spontaneous pneumothorax: British Thoracic Society pleural disease guideline 2010. Thorax 2010;65(Suppl 2):ii18eii31https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20696690/
(3) Brown SGA, Ball EL, Perrin K, et al. Conservative versus Interventional Treatment for Spontaneous Pneumothorax. N Engl J Med. 2020;382(5):405-415. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31995686/

questions.prompt: 83-year-old male with a history of heart failure, DMII, hypertension, paraplegia due to spinal cord injury 10 years ago, neurogenic bladder managed with chronic indwelling Foley catheter, and recurrent urinary tract infections who presents for evaluation of the left perinephric fluid collection that appears to have extended above the diaphragm into the left pleural cavity. You performed an ultrasound that showed significant loculations. The pleural fluid analysis reveals pH 6.8, Glucose 8 mg/dL, LDH >7500 IU/L, protein 2.8 g/dL, Nucleated cells: 274,200/mm3 (Neutrophils 66%, Eosinophils 3%, degenerated cells 31%). You discuss the treatment options. Which following treatment approach is most appropriate:

options.text: Surgical referral for decortication
Place a surgical (32F) chest tube to drain the space
Place a pigtail (14F) chest tube to drain the space
Place a pigtail (14F) chest tube and initiate alteplase (10mg) and DNase (5mg) twice daily dosing for three days
Continue to observe for now

questions.explanation: The patient has a history of urinary tract infection and now has a perinepheric abscess with possible extension of the infection into the pleural cavity with a complex pleural effusion noted by the ultrasound. Patient's co-morbid conditions makes surgical options less desirable. Chest tube sizes does not effectively change the ability to drain the infected pleural space. The second Multi-center Intra-pleural Sepsis Trial (MIST2) trial demonstrated intrapleural therapy with tissue plasminogen activator (tPA 10mg) and deoxyribonuclease (DNase 5mg) twice a day for three days improved chest radiographic appearance at day 7 in infected pleural effusion. Using small bore chest tubes with tPA-DNase has become the treatment of choice for complex pleural effusions.
references: (1) Rahman NM, Maskell NA, West A, et al. Intrapleural use of tissue plasminogen activator and DNase in pleural infection. N Engl J Med. 2011;365(6):518-526 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21830966/

questions.prompt: Which of the following is not an indication for definitive management of primary spontaneous pneumothorax (PSP)?

options.text: Second episode of PSP
Persisting air leak >3-5 days
Presence of apical blebs on CT
Bilateral pneumothorax
Professions at risk (aircraft personnel, divers)

questions.explanation: The estimated recurrence rate after the first primary spontaneous pneumothorax (PSP) approaches 50% at five years with the highest risk occurring in the first 30 days. For PSP, the ACCP guidelines recommend definitive thoracoscopic treatment approach should be reserved for patients with persistent air leaks, a second occurrence of pneumothorax, or for individuals with high risk occupations. Additionally, the BTS recommends surgical advice should be obtained in patients with synchronous bilateral spontaneous pneumothoraces. Although recurrence rates are higher in patients who have multiple or large apical blebs or bullae on CT scan and might argue for surgical intervention, the mere presence of blebs has not been shown to significantly alter the rates of recurrence and there is currently insufficient data to result in alteration of guidelines.
references: (1) Baumann MH, Strange C, Heffner JE, et al. Management of spontaneous pneumothorax: an American College of Chest Physicians Delphi consensus statement. CHEST Journal. 2001;119(2):590-602. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11171742/

(2) MacDuff A, Arnold A, Harvey J. Management of spontaneous pneumothorax: British Thoracic Society pleural disease guideline 2010. Thorax. 2010;65(Suppl 2):ii18-ii31. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20696690/

questions.prompt: An ER resident asks you which size chest tube you would recommend for a large pneumothorax, 20 Fr or 28 Fr. While she is eagerly getting supplies ready, you review the case and notice the patient is a 22 years-old man who presented with acute onset of dyspnea while he was helping a friend to move a couch from his college apartment. He never experienced a similar event before. He smokes 1⁄2 pack a day and plays college basketball. CXR is shown below. You should advice the ER resident following:

options.text: 28 Fr chest tube is better because there are fewer clogging events when future pleurodesis is attempted
Pneumothorax is large and therefore the size of the chest tube does not matter as long as it is a large bore chest tube and not a pigtail catheter.

Large bore chest tubes when compared to pigtail catheters have similar success rate of lung re-expansion and are associated with longer drainage duration and length of stay in patients with spontaneous pneumothorax.

Large bore chest tubes when compared to pigtail catheters have higher success rate of re-expansion, and are associated with shorter length of stay, but higher complication rate regardless to etiology of the pneumothorax

questions.explanation: Metaanalysis of 11 studies comparing pigtail catheter (PC) and large bore chest tube (LBCT) showed that the success rate of lung re-expansion was similar in the PC (79.84%) and LBCT (82.87%) groups. PC drainage was associated with a significantly shorter drainage duration (mean difference, -1.51 [95% CI, -2.93 to -0.09]) and hospital stay (mean difference: -2.54 [95% CI, -3.16 to -1.92]; P < .001) than the LBCT group. There are no studies comparing clogging events following pleurodesis with different chest tube sizes.
references: (1) Chang SH, Kang YN, et al. A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis Comparing Pigtail Catheter and Chest Tubes as the Initial Treatment for Pneumothorax. Chest 2018; 153(5):1201-1212 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29452099/

questions.prompt: Which of the following ultrasonographic signs is most specific of pneumothorax?

options.text: Absence of lung sliding
Absence of B-lines
Presence of barcode sign on M-mode ultrasound
Presence of lung point
Presence of A-lines

questions.explanation: Lung sliding is the presence of respirophasic movement of the visceral pleura against the parietal pleura seen as a shimmering pleural line. Alveolo-interstitial abnormalities at the visceral pleural surface cause B-lines ('comet tail artifacts'). On M-mode imaging, normally a seashore pattern seen. Although all of the options (A-lines, absence of lung sliding, absence of B-lines and presence of a barcode/stratosphere sign) of ultrasonographic findings may be seen with a pneumothorax, the presence of a lung point (the point where normal lung sliding meets absence of the same) has the highest specificity for confirming a pneumothorax. While the detection of a lung point has a near 100% specificity for a pneumothorax, it is only ~66% sensitive, as in large pneumothoraces there may not be any portion of the lung in contact with the parietal pleura. Markers of absent lung sliding can also be seen with any condition in which visceral movement is diminished or abolished (inflammatory pneumonitis, pleurodesis, apnea, etc.). A-lines are seen when there is air immediately deep to the parietal pleura, and can be seen with both pneumothorax and normally aerated lung.
references: (1) Lichtenstein D, Mezière G, Biderman P, Gepner A. The "lung point": an ultrasound sign specific to pneumothorax. Intensive Care Med. 2000;26(10):1434-40 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11126253/
(2) Chen L, et al. Bedside ultrasonography for diagnosis of pneumothorax. Quant Imaging Med Surg. 2015 Aug; 5(4): 618-623 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26435925/

questions.prompt: You place an indwelling pleural catheter (IPC) in one of your patients with a malignant pleural effusion. She calls you two weeks after because the IPC output has significantly decreased (less than 50cc of fluid in the last three consecutive cycles). She denies fevers or chills but complains of dyspnea. Images of bedside ultrasonography in clinic are shown in the Figure. There is no clinical sign of infection and the tunnel tract on physical exam and ultrasound is normal. Serum white blood cell count and vital signs are normal. What is the most appropriate next step? Left sided pleural ultrasound

options.text: Flush IPC with normal saline
Start fibrinolytic therapy via the IPC
Consult thoracic surgery for debridement of loculations
Perform pleuroscopy, debridement of loculations
Placement of a small-bore chest tube and administration of fibrinolytic therapy

questions.explanation: Complications of IPC are not uncommon (12.5%).1,2 The most common and concerning complication is catheter malfunction (9.1%) and empyema (2.8%), respectively.2 Although the rate of infection complications related to IPC is overall low, the majority of research has focused on this.3,4 Infection related to IPC include exit-site infections, tunnel tract infections, and empyema. While infections are usually diagnosed clinically and using pleural fluid analysis, ultrasonography may be useful to detect IPC-related infections. A classification of indwelling pleural catheter infections was proposed based on both clinical, ultrasonographic criteria and pleural fluid analysis. 5 These findings and classification system need to be validated in a larger population. This patient presents with a catheter that is unable to drain due to multiple loculations observed on ultrasound. Flushing the catheter with normal saline will not beneficial as the catheter itself is not occluded (choice A incorrect). Choices C and D describe more aggressive surgical approaches that could be considered only if medical therapy with fibrinolytics fails. While the ideal method of sampling a possibly infected pleural space in the setting of IPC is not known, 44% of pleural cultures from previously placed catheters are inaccurate and might mislead the management.7 In this patient, there are no clinical or laboratory symptoms or signs suggestive of empyema, thus placing a new small-bore tube and administering fibrinolytic therapy, when an IPC is already in place would not be the best option (choice D incorrect). Symptomatic loculations of pleural fluid should be treated with fibrinolytics through the IPC, if no contraindications exist. (choice B correct) In a retrospective study aimed to evaluate intrapleural fibrinolytic therapy for symptomatic loculations in MPE patients with IPC, the majority of the patients had symptomatic response and improvement in pleural fluid drainage (83% and 93%, respectively) with minimal complications (3% bleeding).
references: (1) Van Meter ME, McKee KY, Kohlwes RJ. Efficacy and safety of tunneled pleural catheters in adults with malignant pleural effusions: a systematic review. J Gen Intern Med 2011;26:70-6. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20697963/

(2) Fysh ETH, Tremblay A, Feller-Kopman D, et al. Clinical outcomes of indwelling pleural catheter-related pleural infections: an international multicenter study. Chest. 2013;144(5):1597-1602. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23828305/

(3) Fortin M, Tremblay A. Pleural controversies: indwelling pleural catheter vs. pleurodesis for malignant pleural effusions. J Thorac Dis. 2015 Jun;7(6):1052-7. doi: 10.3978/j.issn.2072-1439.2015.01.51. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26150918/
(4) Lui MM, Thomas R, Lee YC. Complications of indwelling pleural catheter use and their management. BMJ Open Respir Res. 2016;3(1):e000123. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26870384/

(5) Cardenas-Garcia J, Fitzpatrick N, Cheng GZ.The Utility of Ultrasound to Diagnose Tunnel-Tract Infection Related to Indwelling Pleural Catheters. J Bronchology Interv Pulmonol. 2018 Jul;25(3):248-252. doi: 10.1097/LBR.0000000000000474. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29346248/

(6) Shen KR, Bribriesco A, Crabtree T, et al. The American Association for Thoracic Surgery consensus guidelines for the management of empyema. J Thorac CardiovascSurg. 2017 Jun;153(6):e129-e146. doi: 10.1016/j.jtcvs.2017.01.030. Epub 2017Feb 4.https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28274565/

(7) Everts RJ, Heneghan JP, Adholla PO, et all. Validity of cultures of fluid collected through drainage catheters versus those obtained by direct aspiration. J Clin Microbiol. 2001;39:66-8. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11136750/

(8) Thomas R, Piccolo F, Miller D, et al. Intrapleural fibrinolysis for the treatment of indwelling pleural catheter-related symptomatic loculations: a multicenter observational study. Chest 2015;148:746-51.https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25742001/

questions.prompt: A 22-year-old male patient presented to the Emergency Department for acute onset right side chest pain. On physical examination, the patient was tachypneic and tachycardic with decreased breath sound on auscultation of the right chest. Vital signs were notable for heart rate of 120 per minute and respiratory rate of 28 per minute. Ultrasound of the chest showed no sliding signs and no evidence of B lines and a +stratosphere sign. A portable chest film was obtained and showed large right pneumothorax occupying about 75% of the pleural cavity. Based on these findings, what would be the next most appropriate next step?

options.text: Place a large-bore chest tube.
Place a small-bore right chest tube.
Endotracheal intubation
Place the patient on 100% non-rebreather face mask and repeat chest X-ray in 24 hours. If the pneumothorax does not improve, then a small-bore chest tube should be placed.

questions.explanation: This patient has primary spontaneous pneumothorax (PSP). In patients with symptomatic pneumothorax, a small-bore right chest tube is the treatment of choice. The American College of Chest Physicians (ACCP) recommends chest tube placement over needle aspiration in symptomatic PSP. Although not adopted by American thoracic societies, needle aspiration for PSP was suggested by the British Thoracic Society guidelines for patients with large or symptomatic PSP. In a recent meta-analysis, the small-bore pleural catheters were associated with lower complication rate, drainage time and hospital stay compared to large-bore chest tubes in patients who were treated for pneumothorax. Choice D is incorrect because the patient is symptomatic and the pneumothorax is large. Both the British Thoracic Society and the ACCP recommend intervention for patients with primary spontaneous pneumothoraces that are large or for symptomatic patients. Endotracheal intubation is not a definitive treatment for the PSP and positive pressure ventilation will likely lead to rapid tension physiology (choice C incorrect).
references: (1) MacDuff A, Arnold A, Harvey J et al. Management of spontaneous pneumothorax: British Thoracic Society Pleural Disease Guideline 2010. Thorax. 2010 Aug;65 Suppl 2:ii18-31. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20696690/

(2) Baumann MH, Strange C, Heffner JE, et al. Management of spontaneous pneumothorax: an American College of Chest Physicians Delphi consensus statement. Chest. 2001 Feb;119(2):590-602. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11171742/

(3) Chang SH, Kang YN, Chiu HY et al. A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis Comparing Pigtail Catheter and Chest Tube as the Initial Treatment for Pneumothorax. Chest. 2018 May;153(5):1201-1212 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29452099/

questions.prompt: A 75-year-old female with recurrent malignant pleural effusion from lung cancer underwent a right indwelling pleural catheter (IPC) for symptomatic relief. The patient presented two weeks later complaining of a new right chest pain and fever. On physical examination, the patient had a temperature of 101.3 and heart rate of 120 per min and respiratory rate of 24 per minute. The site of the indwelling pleural catheter placement was slightly edematous but there was no erythema or purulent discharge. The blood work showed leukocytosis and the patient was admitted to the hospital for sepsis and management of infected IPC. The patient was started on IV fluid and antibiotics, and the IPC was accessed, and cultures and fluid analysis were requested. The IPC was then connected to a continuous drainage system. Ultrasound of the chest showed moderate pleural fluid with septations. The LDH was 850 and the glucose was 30 and the gram stain was positive for gram positive cocci in clusters. What would be the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.text: Start the patient on twice daily doses of alteplase 20 mg and dornase alpha 10 mg through the IPC and for three days.
Consult thoracic surgery for decortication.
Start the patient on twice daily doses of Alteplase 10 mg and dornase alpha 5 mg through the IPC for three days.
Replace the IPC, then start the patient on Alteplase 10 mg and dornase alpha 5 mg through the IPC twice daily for three days.
