questions.explanation: The patient has an infected IPC resulting in empyema and sepsis. The most important step in the management of this condition is to drain the infected pleural fluid. Ultrasound examination of the chest showed septations of the moderate pleural effusion despite continuous drainage of the IPC. Based on the study by Rahman et al., the use of intrapleural tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) 10 mg and DNase 5 mg twice daily for three days improved fluid drainage and reduced the frequency of surgical referral as well as shortening the length of hospital stay. Choice (a) is incorrect as there are no data to support the usage of higher dosage of alteplase and DNase. Choice (b) is incorrect because the patient may benefit from fluid drainage and tPA and DNase intrapleural instillation which may prevent surgical intervention. Choice (d) is incorrect because there is no data to support the immediate removal of IPC in patients who develop empyema. The majority of patients are treated successfully with antibiotics and drainage without the need for catheter removal or surgical intervention.
references: (1) Lui MM, Thomas R, Lee YC. Complications of indwelling pleural catheter use and their management. BMJ Open Respir Res. 2016 Feb 5;3(1):e000123. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26870384/

(2) Fysh ETH, Tremblay A, Feller-Kopman D, et al. Clinical outcomes of indwelling pleural catheter-related pleural infections: an international multicenter study. Chest. 2013 Nov;144(5):1597-1602. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23828305/

(3) Rahman NM, Maskell NA, West A, et al. Intrapleural use of tissue plasminogen activator and DNase in pleural infection. N Engl J Med. 2011 Aug 11;365(6):518-26. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21830966/

questions.prompt: 65 -year-old male with a medical history of mild COPD secondary to a long-standing history of smoking presented with left sided chest pain and progressive dyspnea. Chest x-ray shows a large left-sided pleural effusion. Left thoracentesis revealed a lymphocytic predominant exudative effusion with negative cytology. Repeated thoracentesis is performed and cytology is again negative. What is the best next step?

options.text: Repeating thoracentesis a third time and send fluid for cytology
Chest tube placement to drain pleural space completely and send fluid for cytology
Tunneled indwelling pleural catheter placement
Pleuroscopy with parietal pleural biopsies.
Repeat CXR in six weeks

questions.explanation: The diagnostic yield from pleural fluid cytology is only ~60% in the setting of an unexplained pleural effusion, especially when lymphocytic in nature. Medical thoracoscopy (pleuroscopy) should be considered following two negative pleural fluid cytologic analyses as the diagnostic yield for malignancy using pleuroscopy approaches 95%.
references: (1) Casal RF, Eapen GA, Morice RC, Jimenez CA. Medical thoracoscopy. Curr Opin Pulm Med. 2009;15(4):313-320 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19387349/

questions.prompt: A 90 years-old man, life-long smoker presents with shortness of breath and cough. No recent trauma. CXR shows a unilateral pleural effusion. A thoracentesis done by IR at the 7th ICS paravertebral space has the following results: pH 7.34, glucose 70, LDH 124, >1million RBC, hematocrit 24. The most likely cause of this pleural fluid is?

options.text: Disruption of the thoracic duct
Pancreatitis
Pulmonary embolism
Paramalignant effusion
Traumatic thoracentesis

questions.explanation: Traumatic thoracentesis is the most likely diagnosis given the high hematocrit with the lack of associated trauma. As individuals age the classic distribution of intercostal vessels becomes less reliable and they can develop into a sinusoidal pattern, particularly in the paravertebral region. Performing thoracentesis in the lateral aspects of the chest or the triangle of safety is recommended to avoid this complication. Disruption of the thoracic duct would lead to a chylothorax which will be a lymphocytic predominant exudate with high triglycerides. Pancreatitis associated pleural effusions result from trans-diaphragmatic transfer of exudative effusion and due to inflammation of pleural lymphatics. Typically, this would result in an effusion with a significantly elevated amylase. Pulmonary embolism associated pleural effusions are typically exudative with variable RBC count, but rarely, if ever approach hematocrit levels suggestive of a hemothorax. Parapneumonic effusions are most likely nonhemorrhagic and would not be the most likely cause in this particular patient.
references: (1) Da Rocha RP, Vengjer A, Blanco A, de Carvalho PT, Mongon ML, Fernandes GJ. Size of the collateral intercostal artery in adults: anatomical considerations in relation to thoracocentesis and thoracoscopy. Surgical and radiologic anatomy : SRA. 2002;24(1):23-26.https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/12197006/

(2) Wrightson JM, Fysh E, Maskell NA, Lee YC. Risk reduction in pleural procedures: sonography, simulation and supervision. Curr Opin Pulm Med. 2010;16(4):340-350.https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20531083/

(3) Dewhurst C, O'Neill S, O'Regan K, Maher M. Demonstration of the course of the posterior intercostal artery on CT angiography: relevance to interventional radiology procedures in the chest. Diagnostic and interventional radiology (Ankara, Turkey). 2012;18(2):221-224.

questions.prompt: Despite flushing, a small-bore chest tube placed for a complex parapneumonic pleural effusion yielded minimal fluid. An ultrasound image of the affected pleural space shows a complex septated effusion. What is the best next step?

options.text: Place a large-bore chest tube
Administer alteplase-DNAse through the chest tube
Administer alteplase through the chest tube
Administer DNAse through the chest tube
Consult thoracic surgery for open decortication

questions.explanation: The MIST-2 trial in 2011 evaluated intrapleural TPA and dornase as a treatment for loculated pleural infections found a significant reduction in area of hemithorax occupied by effusion, surgical referrals and hospital stay in patients receiving a combination of tPA and DNase twice daily for three days when compared to placebo. This benefit exceeded that seen when single agent alteplase or DNase was utilized.
references: (1) Bhatnagar R, Maskell NA. Treatment of complicated pleural effusions in 2013. Clinics in chest medicine. 2013;34(1):47-62.https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23411056/

(2) Rahman NM, Maskell NA, West A, et al. Intrapleural use of tissue plasminogen activator and DNase in pleural infection. N Engl J Med. 2011;365(6):518‐526. doi:10.1056/NEJMoa1012740https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21830966/

questions.prompt: You are planning to do pleuroscopy with pleural biopsy to diagnose an exudative pleural effusion of unknown etiology. Which of the following is correct?

options.text: Pleural Cryobiopsy has higher diagnostic yield as compared to forceps biopsy using the semirigid pleuroscope.
Pleural cryobiopsy is associated with higher bleeding risk as compared to forceps biopsy with semirigid pleuroscope.
In the COFFEE trial, median size of biopsy specimen in cryobiopsy arm was larger than semirigid arm
In the MINT trial, semirigid pleuroscopy was associated with similar patient rated pain as rigid mini pleuroscopy.

questions.explanation: Medical Pleuroscopy with semirigid pleuroscope is an important tool for interventional pulmonologist to diagnose and treat various pleural diseases. The MINT trial compared the use of semirigid thoraco-scope (7.0 diameter with 2.8 mm working channel) to rigid mini thoracoscopy (5.5 mm diameter with 3.5 mm working channel). Diagnostic yield of semirigid thoracoscopy was 81.1% and mini thoracoscopy was 69.4% (P=0.25). However, Semirigid thoracoscopy was associated with less operator and patient rated pain as compared to mini thoracoscopy (VAS 32.1 vs. 41.9 P =0.02). Mini thoracoscopy had larger biopsy sample size as compared to semi rigid thoracoscopy (16 mm vs 8 mm, p<0.001). Pleural Cryobiopsy (CB) was thought to have advantage over forceps biopsy (FB) by providing larger sample size, increased depth of biopsy and reduce the crush artifact. The COFFEE Trial showed that the sample size by CB was significantly larger than FB (7 mm vs. 4 mm, p < 0.001) with greater depth of specimens. However, diagnostic yield was similar in both arms (78% and 76% for CB and FB, respectively, p=1.00). A 2020 meta-analysis also showed similar diagnostic yield (96.5% vs. 93.1% for CB and FB, respectively). In the meta-analysis, CB was not associated with any instance of moderate to severe bleeding.
references: (1) Dhooria S, Bal A, Sehgal IS, et al. Pleural Cryobiopsy versus Flexible Forceps Biopsy in Subjects with Undiagnosed Exudative Pleural Effusions Undergoing Semirigid Thoracoscopy: A Crossover Randomized Trial (COFFEE Trial). Respiration. 2019;98(2):133‐141. doi:10.1159/000497212 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31018210/

(2) Shafiq M, Sethi J, Ali MS, Ghori UK, Saghaie T, Folch E. Pleural Cryobiopsy: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis. Chest. 2020;157(1):223‐230. doi:10.1016/j.chest.2019.09.023 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31610161/

(3) Bansal S, Mittal S, Tiwari P, et al. Rigid Mini-Thoracoscopy Versus Semirigid Thoracoscopy in Undiagnosed Exudative Pleural Effusion: The MINT Randomized Controlled Trial [published online ahead of print, 2019 Aug 30]. J Bronchology Interv Pulmonol. 2019;10.1097/LBR.0000000000000620. doi:10.1097/LBR.0000000000000620 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31478939/

questions.prompt: You are seeing a 73-year-old gentleman with advanced bullous COPD who has developed a spontaneous right-sided pneumothorax 4 days ago requiring a small-bore chest tube placement. His pneumothorax has now resolved with no signs of reappearance following a trial of chest tube clamping. Which of the following would be the most appropriate intervention for secondary prophylaxis in this patient?

options.text: Instillation of talc through the chest tube
Instillation of venous blood through the chest tube (blood patch)
Instillation of recombinant DNAse through the chest tube
Flexible bronchoscopy with placement of bronchial valves

questions.explanation: Up to 50% of patients with secondary spontaneous pneumothorax may have a recurrence after the index event. Options for recurrence prevention include chest tube-guided instillation of a chemical sclerosant (such as sterile talc) or thoracoscopic procedures (including chemical poudrage, mechanical pleurodesis, and pleurectomy). A "blood patch" can be used for treatment of an air leak with the idea of "sealing" an active alveolo-pleural fistula (simultaneous pleurodesis may also be achieved). In this case, the defect has healed already as there is no air leak or pneumothorax at this time. Bronchial valve placement is not indicated for the same reason; it works by reducing air flow across an active fistula and thereby promoting healing (choice B and D incorrect). Recombinant DNAse has no role in this case (choice C incorrect).
references: (1) Baumann MH, Strange C, et al. Management of spontaneous pneumothorax: an American College of Chest Physicians Delphi consensus statement. Chest. 2001;119(2):590. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11171742/

(2) MacDuff A, Arnold A, Harvey J. Management of spontaneous pneumothorax: British Thoracic Society Pleural Disease Guideline 2010. Thorax. 2010;65 Suppl 2:ii18. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20696690/

(3) Hallifax RJ, et al. Effectiveness of chemical pleurodesis in spontaneous pneumothorax recurrence prevention: a systematic review. Thorax Published Online First: 01 November 2016. doi: 10.1136/thoraxjnl-2015-207967. https://thorax.bmj.com/content/72/12/1121

questions.prompt: A 30-year-old female marathon runner with breast cancer presents to outpatient clinic for dyspnea. Initial thoracentesis yields 1.5 L of straw color pleural fluid. The pleural fluid analysis is consistent with a lymphocytic exudative pleural effusion. Cytology confirms metastatic origin. The patient has good performance status and symptomatic improvement after the initial drainage. One week after, the fluid reaccumulates. The patient and her oncologist would like to discuss a more definitive approach to this malignant pleural effusion. She would like to avoid an indwelling pleural catheter (IPC). Which of the following is correct in the management of malignant pleural effusion (MPE)?

options.text: Pleurodesis is superior to IPC in providing palliation for MPE.
Non-steroid anti-inflammatory agents do not decrease the success rate of pleurodesis
Talc pleurodesis is more effective when administered as a slurry compared to poudrage
Talc pleurodesis would be recommended if non-expandable lung is confirmed
For chemical pleurodesis, small bore chest tubes showed inferior success rates but were better tolerated

questions.explanation: Current British Thoracic Society guidelines for pleural diseases state that "talc pleurodesis is equally effective when administered as a slurry or by insufflation."1 This statement was supported by a more recent study from Bhatnagar,2 which compared thoracoscopic talc insufflation (poudrage) and talc slurry delivered via chest tube in a randomized controlled trial. This study found no significant difference in the rate of pleurodesis failure at 90 days (Incorrect Answer C). Visceral and parietal pleural apposition is required in order for pleurodesis to be successful.3 This criteria is not met when there is presence of non-expandable lung. The latter, which can be confirmed by pleural manometry, is seen in patients with visceral pleural thickening, endobronchial obstruction/parenchymal consolidation, or any disease that increases the elastic recoil properties of the lung (i.e. lymphangitic carcinomatosis or interstitial lung disease) (5). As there is no contact between pleural surfaces, chemical pleurodesis would not be recommended in cases of non-expandable lung (Incorrect Answer D). However, other palliative methods for symptoms caused by the re-accumulation of pleural effusion should be considered, such as indwelling pleural catheter. The questions of chest tube size and use of non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) in chemical pleurodesis were addressed in the TIME-1 trial.5 This randomized clinical study enrolled 320 patients requiring pleurodesis. NSAID use resulted in noninferior rates of pleurodesis efficacy at 3 months (Correct Answer B). The placement of 12F chest tubes (vs 24F) was associated with a statistically significant, but clinically modest, reduction in pain. However, 12F chest tubes failed to meet noninferiority criteria for pleurodesis efficacy (Incorrect Answer E).
references: (1) Arnold DT, Roberts M, Wahidi M, et al. Optimal diagnosis and treatment of malignant disease: challenging the guidelines. In: Maskell NA, Laursen CB, Lee YCG, et al., eds. Pleural Diseases (ERS Monograph). Sheffield, European Respiratory Society, 2020; pp. 138

(2) Bhatnagar R, Piotrowska HEG, Laskawiec-Szkonter M, et al. Effect of thoracoscopic talc poudrage vs talc slurry via chest tube on pleurodesis failure rate among patients with malignant pleural effusions: a randomized clinical trial. JAMA 2019; in press [DOI 10.1016/j.chest.2019.08.1920]. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31804680/

(3) Roberts ME, Neville E, Berrisford RG, et al. Management of a malignant pleural effusion: British Thoracic Society Pleural Disease Guideline 2010. Thorax 2010; 65: Suppl. 2, ii32-ii40. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20696691/
(4) Feller-Kopman D, Parker MJ, Schwartzstein RM. Assessment of pleural pressure in the evaluation of pleural effusions. Chest 2009; 135: 201-209. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19136407/

(5) Rahman NM, Pepperell J, Rehal S et al. Effect of Opioids vs NSAIDs and Larger vs Smaller Chest Tube Size on Pain Control and Pleurodesis Efficacy Among Patients With Malignant Pleural Effusion: The TIME1 Randomized Clinical Trial. JAMA. 2015 Dec 22-29;314(24):2641-53. doi: 10.1001/jama.2015.16840. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26720026/

questions.prompt: You are asked to evaluate a 55-year-old man with recurrent bilateral pleural effusions in your clinic. He has history of heart failure (EF 25%). His medical regimen is maximized per his Cardiologist. He has been hospitalized for CHF exacerbation 5 times in the last 6 months. He underwent bilateral thoracentesis 4 times in the last 2 months. Pleural effusion results from last two thoracentesis are listed below. On physical exam he has minimal pitting edema in his lower extremities. Chest ultrasound shows a moderate effusion on the right and a small effusion on the left. He admits to exertional dyspnea that worsened over the past 2 weeks. He always gets relief when the effusion is drained. (LDH Upper limit of normal - 225 U/L, serum protein 5.3 g/dL, serum LDH 356 U/L). You decide to have a discussion with him about long term management of his recurrent effusion. Which of the statements is the most accurate statement regarding Tunneled Pleural Catheter (TPC) placement for recurrent non-malignant pleural effusions (NMPE):

options.text: TPC placed for NMPE decreases rate of hospital re-admissions and approximately 50% of patients achieve spontaneous pleurodesis.
TPC is contraindicated for non-malignant pleural effusions, repeat thoracentesis should be offered
TPC placed for NMPE decreases rate of hospital re-admission, because rate of spontaneous pleurodesis approaches 80 %
Rate of TPC related spontaneous pleurodesis is higher as is the incidence of TPC related complication when compared to TPC placed for malignant pleural effusions (MPE)

questions.explanation: TPCs are an effective and safe option in the management of patients with recurrent non-malignant pleural effusion refractory to medical management. The quality of evidence to support its routine use remains low. Small studies suggest that TPCs placed for NMPE decrease rate of hospital re-admissions. Rate of spontaneous pleurodesis and incidence of catheter related complication are comparable for TPCs placed for malignant or non-malignant pleural effusions.
references: (1) Patil M, Dhillon SS, et al. Management of Benign Pleural Effusions Using Indwelling Pleural Catheters: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis. Chest. 2017 Mar;151(3):626-635. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27845052/

(2) Freeman RK, Ascioti AJ, et al. A propensity-matched comparison of pleurodesis or tunneled pleural catheter for heart failure patients with recurrent pleural effusion. Ann Thorac Surg. 2014 Jun;97(6):1872-6 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24726601/

questions.prompt: You receive a consult from the MICU for a 23 years-old female patient with no significant past medical history, who was found down at her place. She was found to have multiple lung lesions compatible with septic emboli in a CT chest. A 2D echo revealed vegetations in both the tricuspid and mitral valve. Her condition deteriorated and she needed to be intubated. While on the ventilator, she experienced bilateral pneumothoraces with subcutaneous emphysema. Currently, she has 2 large bore chest tubes in her right chest and 4 large bore chest tubes in her left chest. She has a significant leak on the ventilator (around 40 L/min). It has been a week since her last chest tube placement in the left chest. The patient cannot be extubated due to her mental status. Based on your knowledge about air leak management, you tell the team that you will perform the following, based on the available evidence:

options.text: Urgent consult to Thoracic Surgery
Palliative Care consult and hospice referral
Bilateral spigot placement
Bilateral bronchoscopic delivery of glue
Bilateral endobronchial valve placement

questions.explanation: This is a young woman with no past medical history, currently with acute respiratory failure and a significant air leak due to septic emboli. Option (a) is not viable given her bilateral defects and availability of less invasive options. Option (b) is not standard of care for air leak management. Options (c), (d) and (e) have been described for persistent air leak.1 However, endobronchial valve placement has the advantage of allowing secretion drainage into the main airways while limiting air flow into the lung parenchyma. Treating septic emboli consists of adequate antibiotic treatment, but also secretion drainage. Using options (c) and (d) could potentially cause post obstructive pneumonia in an already infected lung.
references: (1) Sakata KK et al. Persistent Air Leak - Review. Respir Med 2018;137:213-218. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29605207/

questions.prompt: You are asked to see a 54-year-old female with metastatic breast cancer for shortness of breath and abnormal chest imaging. Prior to one week ago she was exercising daily and working as a preschool teacher. She is now short of breath while climbing the stairs in her home. You obtain the following CXR. You perform a diagnostic and therapeutic thoracentesis with removal of 1.8 L serosanguinous fluid and repeat CXR shows complete re-expansion of her lung. One week later the patient returns to the clinic and reports that she felt well for three days after the procedure but has had progressive shortness of breath over the past 4 days. Repeat CXR shows re-accumulation of the pleural effusion. The patient asks you what are her options for palliation of her symptoms. Which of the following statements regarding palliation of recurrent symptomatic malignant pleural effusions is true?

options.text: Based on the above information, she is a candidate for tunneled pleural catheter placement but not for chest tube pleurodesis with talc
Tunneled pleural catheter placement would be more likely to improve her shortness of breath compared to chest tube pleurodesis with talc
Tunneled pleural catheter would be more cost effective than talc pleurodesis in this patient, given her excellent functional status and anticipated survival of > 1 year
Tunneled pleural catheter placement would be expected to result in fewer days spent in the hospital compared to chest tube pleurodesis with talc

questions.explanation: Given that this patient has evidence of complete re-expansion of the underlying lung after therapeutic thoracentesis she would be a candidate for either talc pleurodesis or tunneled pleural catheter placement for palliation of her malignant pleural effusion (Answer A is incorrect). The AMPLE and TIME 2 randomized control trials both showed no difference in dyspnea or quality of life in patients randomized to receive a tunneled pleural catheter versus bedside talc pleurodesis 1,2 (Answer B is incorrect). Both trials, however, showed a reduction in hospital days in patients randomized to receive a tunneled pleural catheter (Answer D is correct). Puri et al evaluated the cost-effectiveness of repeated thoracentesis, bedside pleurodesis, thoracoscopic pleurodesis and tunneled pleural catheter placement. For patients with an anticipated survival > 12 months bedside pleurodesis was the most cost-effective treatment
references: (1) Thomas R, Fysh ETH, Smith NA, Lee P, Kwan BCH, Yap E, Horwood FC, Piccolo F, Lam DCL, Garske LA, Shrestha R, Kosky C, Read CA, Murray K, Lee YCG. Effect of an Indwelling Pleural Catheter vs Talc Pleurodesis on Hospitalization Days in Patients With Malignant Pleural Effusion: The AMPLE Randomized Clinical Trial. JAMA. 2017 Nov 21;318(19):1903-1912. doi: 10.1001/jama.2017.17426 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29164255/

(2) Davies HE, Mishra EK, Kahan BC, et al. Effect of an indwelling pleural catheter vs chest tube and talc pleurodesis for relieving dyspnea in patients with malignant pleural effusion: the TIME2 randomized controlled trial. Jama. 2012;307(22):2383-2389.https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22610520/

(3) Puri V, Pyrdeck TL, Crabtree TD, Kreisel D, Krupnick AS, Colditz GA, Patterson GA, Meyers BF. Treatment of malignant pleural effusion: a cost-effectiveness analysis. Ann Thorac Surg. 2012 Aug;94(2):374-9; discussion 379-80. doi: 10.1016/j.athoracsur.2012.02.100. Epub 2012 May 10. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22579398/

questions.prompt: A 70 years-old male with history of occupational asbestos exposure presents with dyspnea and a dull right chest pain for several months. He underwent several non-diagnostic thoracenteses. Results from most recent thoracentesis were as follows: LDH 612 (plasma=125) protein 4.9, glucose 20mg/dl, Lymph (64%), rheumatoid factor 5 (same as serum), Cytology negative. Which statement is TRUE?

options.text: The low fluid glucose suggests that the effusion is due to rheumatoid arthritis rather than malignancy
The dull chest pain is suggestive of malignancy
The multiple negative fluid cytology results exclude malignancy from consideration
Bronchoscopy should be performed as the next diagnostic procedure

questions.explanation: Mild dull chest pain is only present in approximately one third of malignant pleural effusions. However, this finding is rare in benign effusions and should raise suspicion for the presence of malignancy. Although rheumatoid effusions typically have low glucose levels, multiple other causes of exudative effusions can also be associated with low glucose to include empyema/parapneumonic, tuberculous, and malignant effusions. Pleural fluid cytology from a single specimen has a sensitivity of approximately 60% for the detection of malignancy. This can be increased by 20-25% with a second specimen but there is very little benefit with further samplings and hence cytology alone from thoracentesis cannot effectively rule out malignancy. In the absence of hemoptysis or a focal lung lesion bronchoscopy has a diagnostic yield of only approximately 2% for undiagnosed pleural effusions and would not be the next diagnostic step. More appropriate at this stage in the diagnostic algorithm would be to proceed with either medical or surgical thoracoscopy for direct tissue diagnosis.
references: (1) Marel M, Štastny B, Melínová Le, Svandová E, Light RW. Diagnosis of pleural effusions : Experience with clinical studies, 1986 to 1990. Chest. 1995;107(6):1598-1603 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/7781353/

questions.prompt: An 88-year-old male patient arrives to the trauma bay at a local emergency department after motor vehicle accident. He is found to have multiple rib fractures in his left hemithorax leading to a suspected hemothorax. A large bore chest tube is placed with drainage of 1 liter of blood. His prior medical history includes four-vessel coronary bypass and very severe emphysema. Three days after his admission, a CT chest reveals a loculated retained hemothorax. Due to other multiple prior comorbidities, he is not a surgical candidate. Figure 1. Chest radiograph showing a large opacification of the left hemithorax with displacement of mediastinal structures towards the contralateral side. Pleural ultrasonography revealed a large iso-echoeic pleural effusion. Chest tube drainage confirmed hemothorax. Please select the correct statement regarding management for this clinical situation:

options.text: Retained traumatic hemothorax does not impact the increase the risk of development of empyema or non-expandable lung
The Eastern Association for the Surgery of Trauma practice management guidelines for retained hemothorax suggest early video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) as second line treatment
Lytic therapy could reduce the need for operative intervention in trauma patients with retained hemothorax
Intrapleural Tissue Plasminogen Activator (TPA) therapy is associated to risk of systemic bleeding in trauma patients with retained hemothorax

questions.explanation: Hemothorax is defined as a bloody pleural effusion in which the pleural fluid hematocrit is at least 50% of serum1. The management includes large bore chest tube drainage except in aortic dissection or rupture, as in both cases, this intervention can lead to rapid exsanguination.1,2 Surgical approach in acute traumatic hemothorax such in this clinical vignette, include an initial drainage greater than 1500 mL following thoracostomy, or drainage of more than 200 mL per hour for 4 hours.2 Clotted blood may not be fully evacuated by single or multiple chest tubes, leading to retained hemothorax, a risk factor for the development of both empyema and non-expandable lung (choice A incorrect).2 The Eastern Association for the Surgery of Trauma practice management guidelines for retained hemothorax recommend the use of early video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) as first line treatment (Level I Evidence) (choice B incorrect). Fibrinolytic therapy could be considered in non-surgical candidates (Level of Evidence III).3 A recent metanalysis by Hendriksen,4 suggested that the use intrapleural lytic therapy in cases of retained hemothorax is associated with an operative avoidance rate ranging from 81% to 92% (choice C correct). The same study found no bleeding complications related to the use of TPA in this population, which might be due to the low systemic absorption when administered into the pleural space, and the drug's short half-life (3-5 min) (choice D incorrect).
references: (1) Yeam I, Sassoon C. Hemothorax and chylothorax. Curr Opin Pulm Med. 1997;3(4):310‐314. doi:10.1097/00063198-199707000-00013 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/9262119/

(2) Hendriksen BS, Kuroki MT, Armen SB, Reed MF, Taylor MD, Hollenbeak CS. Lytic Therapy for Retained Traumatic Hemothorax: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis. Chest. 2019;155(4):805‐815. doi:10.1016/j.chest.2019.01.007 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30664856/
(3) Broderick SR. Hemothorax: Etiology, diagnosis, and management. Thorac Surg Clin. 2013;23(1):89‐vii. doi:10.1016/j.thorsurg.2012.10.003 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23206720/

(4) Mowery NT, Gunter OL, Collier BR, et al. Practice management guidelines for management of hemothorax and occult pneumothorax. J Trauma. 2011;70(2):510‐518. doi:10.1097/TA.0b013e31820b5c31 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21307755/

questions.prompt: A previously healthy patient presents with several days of cough, fever and right-sided pleuritic chest pain. CXR reveals opacification of the right lower lung field. You perform point-of-care lung and pleural ultrasound and find a moderate-sized pleural effusion with several fibrin strands and 'plankton' sign on dynamic imaging. In addition to appropriate fluid drainage, which of the following empiric antibiotic regimens should be initiated?

options.text: Ceftriaxone + Azithromycin
Levofloxacin + Azithromycin
Moxifloxacin + Vancomycin
Doxycycline
Ceftriaxone + Azithromycin +Metronidazole

questions.explanation: This patient has a complicated parapneumonic effusion related to a community acquired pneumonia. Antibiotics should be initiated promptly and not be delayed until after fluid drainage. Appropriate antimicrobial coverage for community-acquired organisms associated with complicated parapneumonic effusions include standard community-acquired pneumonia coverage with the addition of an agent targeting anaerobic organisms that colonize the oropharynx. The most prevalent bacteria isolated from community-acquired parapneumonic effusions include fusobacterium, prevotella, peptostreptococcus, bacteroides, streptococcus milleri, staphylococcus aureus, enterobacteriaceae, kebsiella and streptococcus pneumoniae. Up to 40% of community-acquired parapneumonic effusions are culture negative.
references: (1) Davies HE, Davies RJ, Davies CW. Management of pleural infection in adults: British Thoracic Society pleural disease guideline 2010. Thorax. 2010;65(Suppl 2):ii41-ii53 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20696693/

questions.prompt: A 68-year-old man with history of stage IV non-small cell lung cancer and right malignant pleural effusion presented with worsening dyspnea and decreased output from his left indwelling pleural catheter (IPC). Chest x-ray showed right IPC in good position and moderate right pleural effusion. IPC drainage was attempted with no success. What is the next step in the management of this patient's symptoms?

options.text: Thoracentesis
Flush the IPC with sterile saline and try pleural drainage again
Trial of fibrinolytic instillation in the IPC
Replace existing IPC with new one.

questions.explanation: IPC has been increasingly used for management of malignant pleural effusions. One of the complications associated with IPC is complete or partial blockage of the lumen by clot or dense fibrinous tissue, which occurs in about 5% of patients with IPCs. In this patient, decreased output from the IPC along with the presence of a pleural effusion on x-ray can be due to an obstructed catheter or a poorly draining pleural space. As the information available does not allow for further delineation between these two possibilities, a saline flush is a reasonable next step. If saline flush was ineffective in restoring catheter flow then Alteplase mixed with saline can be instilled into the pleural space. Replacement of non-draining blocked IPC should only be considered when saline flush and trial of Alteplase are unsuccessful.
references: (1) Lui MMS., et al. Complications of indwelling pleural catheter use and their management. BMJ Open Respiratory Research. 2016;3: e000123.https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26870384/

(2) Wahidi MW., et al. Randomized trial of pleural fluid drainage frequency in patients with malignant pleural effusions: The ASAP Trial. American Journal of Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine. 2017. Vol 195. pp1050-1057 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27898215/

questions.prompt: A 63 years-old former smoker with T4N1M0 adenocarcinoma underwent uneventful pneumonectomy and returned to emergency department four weeks following discharge with increasing cough and fevers. A CXR is obtained and shown below. Which of the following is the first step regarding the management of this patient?

options.text: Perform thoracentesis as out-patient and send patient home
Give patient oral antibiotics and have him return in 2 weeks
Perform bronchoscopy to obtain BAL culture samples
Place right-sided chest tube, IV antibiotics, and perform bronchoscopic airway exam

questions.explanation: Post-pneumonectomy empyema is an uncommon but potentially life-threatening complication. It has a strong association with bronchopleural fistula, which acts as a continued source of infection into the thoracic cavity. Chest radiograph may reveal air in the hemithorax that was previously filled with pleural fluid, or a drop in an air-fluid level compared with previous radiographs. If post-pneumonectomy empyema is suspected by biochemical and radiological evidence, definitive drainage with a chest tube should be performed as the first intervention. Additionally, control of sepsis is of critical importance and bronchoscopy can be useful to identify possible bronchopleural fistula. Although surgical intervention might prove necessary, drainage and infection control should occur first.
references: (1) Boudaya MS, Smadhi H, Zribi H, et al. Conservative management of postoperative bronchopleural fistulas. The Journal of thoracic and cardiovascular surgery. 2013;146(3):575-579. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23810114/

(2) Cardillo G, Carbone L, Carleo F, et al. The Rationale for Treatment of Postresectional Bronchopleural Fistula: Analysis of 52 Patients. Ann Thorac Surg. 2015;100(1):251-257. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26024752/

questions.prompt: A 74-year-old male with a heavy tobacco smoking history presents with cough and minimal hemoptysis. CXR shows a large right hilar mass and moderate ipsilateral pleural effusion. The patient has an extensive history of cardiac disease including ischemia-related systolic heart failure and atrial fibrillation. The patient was complaining of chest pain 3 months prior and his echocardiographic stress test showed reversible myocardial wall defect, which has been managed medically with dual antiplatelet therapy. The patient underwent an ultrasound-guided thoracentesis, which revealed an exudative pleural fluid and was negative for malignant cells. You consult his cardiologist, who is unwilling to take him off clopidogrel for 5 days. What would be the next step in the diagnosis and management of this patient?

options.text: Perform medical thoracoscopy and pleural biopsy under local anesthesia and place an indwelling tunneled pleural catheter during the same procedure
Schedule the patient for endobronchial ultrasound procedure under moderate sedation for the diagnosis and staging of the right hilar mass.
Repeat right thoracentesis and obtain PET/CT thereafter
Refer for computed tomography-guided percutaneous needle biopsy of the right hilar mass.

questions.explanation: The clinical scenario is suspicious for stage 4 lung cancer and an efficient approach, maximizing yield while minimizing risk, is indicated. Based on the most recent American College of Chest Physicians (ACCP) guidelines, in patients suspected of having lung cancer with an accessible pleural effusion, the fluid should be sampled first. While this patient already underwent one thoracentesis, initial pleural fluid cytology has a sensitivity of 50-60%, which can increase to ~70-75% with a second thoracentesis. However, the yield depends on histology and is best with adenocarcinomas and poor for squamous cell cancers. Given his active coronary disease and the associated risks of anesthesia, a second thoracentesis under local anesthesia, rather than a medical thoracoscopy could be considered at this time to reassess pleural fluid cytology (choice C correct). If the cytology is negative, clinical staging with a PET/CT can also be considered, but he will still need a tissue diagnosis. PET/CT also allows for better evaluation of the ipsilateral hemithorax and pleura, while simultaneously assessing for mediastinal and other distant metastases that might be additional targets for future sampling. While EBUS-TBNA can be considered in patients on clopidogrel, in this patient with active cardiac disease, obtaining as much information prior to invasive procedures that require moderate sedation or general anesthesia is prudent (choice B incorrect). Sampling of the hilar mass would not stage the patient (choice D incorrect).
references: (1) Detterbeck FC, Lewis SZ, Diekemper R et al. Executive Summary: Diagnosis and Management of Lung Cancer, 3rd ed: American College of Chest Physicians Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guidelines. Chest. 2013 May;143(5 Suppl):7S-37S. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23649434/

(2) Porcel JM, Hernandez P, Martinez-Alonso M et al. Accuracy of Fluorodeoxyglucose-PET Imaging for Differentiating Benign From Malignant Pleural Effusions. Chest. 2015 Feb;147(2):502-512 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25188411/

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements is true in regards to palliation of malignant pleural effusions?

options.text: Pleural catheters are superior to pleurodesis in relieving dyspnea
The overall cost of treatment with indwelling pleural catheters is significantly lower than pleurodesis.
Length of hospital stay is similar between pleural catheter and pleurodesis therapy
The rate of spontaneous pleurodesis in patients with pleural catheters approaches 50%

questions.explanation: Indwelling pleural catheters and pleurodesis are both effective tools for relieving dyspnea associated with malignant pleural effusions. The TIME-2 study was a randomized trial of IPC vs. talc pleurodesis for malignant pleural effusions. In this study there was no difference in the primary outcome measure of dyspnea improvement at 42 days, although improved symptoms became significant at the 6 months in favor of the IPC group. Length of initial hospitalization was significantly shorter in the IPC group with a median of 0 days compared to 4 days for the talc group. A 2014 study based on the TIME-2 trial demonstrated overall mean costs over a 1 year follow up of $4,993 for IPC vs. $4,581 for pleurodesis. If patients survived less than 14 weeks IPC became significantly less costly. In a 2010 systematic review of 19 studies evaluating the efficacy and safety of tunneled pleural catheters in malignant pleural effusions the rate of spontaneous pleurodesis was 45.6%, with an average time to pleurodesis of 52 days.
references: (1) Penz ED, Mishra EK, Davies HE, Manns BJ, Miller RF, Rahman NM. COmparing cost of indwelling pleural catheter vs talc pleurodesis for malignant pleural effusion. Chest. 2014;146(4):991-1000. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24832000/

(2) Davies HE, Mishra EK, Kahan BC, et al. Effect of an indwelling pleural catheter vs chest tube and talc pleurodesis for relieving dyspnea in patients with malignant pleural effusion: the TIME2 randomized controlled trial. Jama. 2012;307(22):2383-2389. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22610520/

questions.prompt: 43-year-old African American woman, never smoker, with past medical history significant for recurrent pneumothorax. She has had 3 episodes of small right-sided pneumothorax with none requiring chest tube placement. Her episodes of pneumothorax correspond with her menstrual cycles. Her past medical history is significant for a right sided ovarian cyst requiring oophorectomy. She is currently on leuprolide. She reports dyspnea on exertion for the last 1 week. Her chest x-ray done in clinic is as shown below. Her vitals are stable, and she is asymptomatic at rest. What would be the best treatment option for her pneumothorax?

options.text: Inpatient admission with administration of supplemental oxygen via 100% nonrebreather mask and chest x-ray follow-up.
Place a small-bore chest tube and perform chemical Pleurodesis once lung re-expands.
Consult thoracic surgery for VATS and Pleurodesis.
Conservative management with outpatient follow-up with repeat chest x-ray in 1 week and intervene if pneumothorax persists.

questions.explanation: This is a case of secondary spontaneous pneumothorax from endometriosis also known as catamenial pneumothorax, given her history of oophorectomy and the association of pneumothorax with her menstrual cycle. Since she has been having recurrent spontaneous pneumothorax, pleurodesis should be considered. Since the patient is young and has no significant comorbidities, she should be evaluated for VATS with pleural biopsy and pleurodesis which would be both diagnostic as well as therapeutic with low recurrence rate when compared to chemical pleurodesis. In patients with catamenial pneumothorax, the recurrence rate is lower when surgery is combined with hormone therapy (choice C correct). She is asymptomatic at rest, hemodynamically stable and is on room air. There is no indication for an inpatient admission as she can be managed as an outpatient with referral to thoracic surgery (choice A incorrect). She has a small right sided pneumothorax and she is asymptomatic at rest on room air. She does not require a chest tube and she should be evaluated for surgical pleurodesis before considering chemical pleurodesis (choice B incorrect). While her pneumothorax can be managed conservatively with outpatient follow up, she does require an intervention to prevent recurrence. (Choice D incorrect).
references: (1) Videothoracoscopic repair of diaphragm and pleurectomy/abrasion in patients with catamenial pneumothorax: a 9-year experience. Chest. 2013;143(4):1066. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23117231/
(2) Clinical characteristics of catamenial and non-catamenial thoracic endometriosis-related pneumothorax. Respirology (2015) 20, 1272-1276 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26306694/

(3) Thoracic endometriosis syndrome: Comparison between catamenial pneumothorax or endometriosis-related pneumothorax and catamenial hemoptysis. Eur J Obstet Gynecol Reprod Biol. 2018;225:118. Epub 2018 Apr 20. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29704815/

questions.prompt: An 83-year-old male with symptomatic recurrent right sided pleural effusion from chronic systolic heart failure. His LVEF is 15%, he has a LV thrombus for which he is anticoagulated. Pertinent medical history includes COPD, renal insufficiency, diabetes. He has undergone a therapeutic thoracentesis 5 times in the last 3 months with symptomatic improvement in his symptoms following each drainage. He has been medically optimized and is considered a high risk case for invasive surgery. His most recent CXR after a therapeutic thoracentesis: The best next step in management is:

options.text: Tube thoracostomy
Indwelling pleural catheter
Talc pleurodesis
Pleurectomy

questions.explanation: The optimal management for recurrent non-malignant pleural effusions is to treat the underlying disease process. In this case, the pleural effusion recurs despite maximal medical therapy for chronic systolic heart failure. Surgical pleurectomy/thoracoscopic pleurodesis would have been a viable option if the patient was a surgical candidate. However, patients with effusions caused by heart failure who undergo placement of an indwelling pleural catheter have shorter hospitalization and less operative complications than those who undergo a thoracoscopic pleurodesis.1 Chemical pleurodesis may not be effective in treating his effusion since there is suggestion of a trapped lung based on radiographic findings of thickened visceral pleura, loculations and incomplete expansion after a therapeutic thoracentesis. Data supporting the use of indwelling pleural catheters, initially developed for the management of malignant pleural effusions, is mostly observational but have been shown to be successful in palliating symptoms with low complication rates (empyema 5.2% and drain site infection 2.9%).2
references: (1) Freeman RK, Ascioti AJ, Dake M, Mahidhara RS. A propensity-matched comparison of pleurodesis or tunneled pleural catheter for heart failure patients with recurrent pleural effusion. Ann Thorac Surg. 2014 Jun;97(6):1872-6.https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24726601/
(2) Bintcliffe O, Arnold D, Maskell N. Indwelling pleural catheters for benign pleural effusions. Curr Respir Care Rep 2014; 3: 61-70.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statement is correct about complications of indwelling pleural catheters (IPCs)?

options.text: The incidence of IPC-related infection is 0% to 12%.
IPC-related infection typically occurs within first 6-8 weeks after insertion.
The incidence of IPC related infection is higher in patients with ongoing chemotherapy.
Catheter blockage with fibrinous tissue within the lumen occurs in 10-20% of patients.
All of the above

questions.explanation: IPCs are increasingly utilized for management of pleural effusions since their approval by the FDA in 1997. Infection is one of the potential complications associated with IPCs. The incidence of IPC-related infection varies from 0% to 12% in different studies. A large multi-center review by Fysh et al. reported an infection rate of 4.9%. Skin flora such as staphylococcus species account for most of the cases, followed by Pseudomonas and Enterobacteriaceae. Infections usually occur at least 6-8 weeks after IPC insertion (therefore, speaking against a procedure-related infection). Infection usually is not severe and responds to antibiotics. Removal of catheter is rarely required, and can be considered when the infection does not respond to antibiotics or is complicated with worsening clinical status. Available data have shown the incidence of IPC-related infections are not significantly higher in immunocompromised patients (including those receiving chemotherapy). Catheter blockage is uncommon and occurs in <5% of patients with IPCs.
references: (1) Gilbert CR, et al. The use of indwelling tunneled pleural catheters for recurrent pleural effusions in patients with hematologic malignancies: a multicenter study. Chest 2015;148:752-8. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25789576/

(2) Mekhaiel E, et al. Infections associated with tunneled indwelling pleural catheters in patients undergoing chemotherapy. Journal of Bronchology Interventional Pulmonology. 2013;20:299-303. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24162111/

(3) Wahidi MW., et al. Randomized trial of pleural fluid drainage frequency in patients with malignant pleural effusions: The ASAP Trial. American Journal of Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine. 2017. Vol 195. pp1050-1057.https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27898215/
(4) Lui MMS., et al. Complications of indwelling pleural catheter use and their management. BMJ Open Respiratory Research. 2016;3: e000123. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26870384/

questions.prompt: 23-year-old man had thoracic spinal cord surgery for gunshot injury three months ago presented with progressive dyspnea. Thoracentesis was performed with chemistries consistent with a transudate. What is the next best test in establishing the diagnosis?

options.text: Echocardiogram
Chest tube placement
Ultrasound of the liver
Intrathecal contrast-enhanced CT scan
Beta-2 microglobulin test

questions.explanation: Duropleural fistula is a result of a connection between the dural membrane and the parietal pleura. Typically this develops following spinal cord injury or neurosurgery. When duropleural fistula is considered in the differential diagnosis CT myelogram, MRI or pleural fluid beta-2 transferrin testing can be used to establish a diagnosis. Treatment is usually surgical.
references: (1) Light RW. The undiagnosed pleural effusion. Clinics in chest medicine. 2006;27(2):309-319 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16716820/

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements is correct regarding the management of patients with refractory non-malignant pleural effusion (NMPE)?

options.text: Thoracoscopic talc pleurodesis with indwelling pleural catheter (IPC) can be used in selected patients with recurrent benign pleural effusion.
Estimated rate of spontaneous pleurodesis using IPC is similar to MPE.
IPC carries a significant risk of infectious complications and mortality in the patients with hepatic hydrothorax.
All of the above

questions.explanation: IPC have been effective in management of recurrent benign pleural effusions refractory to treatment of underlying disease such as heart failure, ESRD, hepatic hydrothorax and chylothorax. IPC also palliates patient's symptoms. A retrospective study by Freeman et al. indicated reduced hospital length of stay, readmission rate and morbidity in the IPC group as compared to patients who underwent thoracoscopic talc poudrage. Majid et al. retrospectively showed higher pleurodesis rates and shorter time to IPC removal in those who underwent thoracoscopic IPC placement + talc versus just IPC placement alone. In a systematic review and meta-analysis of 13 studies of IPC use in NMPE, including 325 patients, the pleurodesis rate was 51%. Infectious complications were noted in 5%. IPC has been used for palliation or a bridge to liver transplantation in patients with hepatic hydrothorax. A retrospective study by Shojaee et al. revealed that the rate of infections was higher in these patients (10%) comparable to reported rates in malignant pleural effusions.
references: (1) Shojaee S, Rahman N, Haas K, et al. Indwelling Tunneled Pleural Catheters for Refractory Hepatic Hydrothorax in Patients With Cirrhosis: A Multicenter Study. Chest. 2019;155(3):546‐553. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30171863/
(2) Patil M., Dhillon SS., et al. Management of Benign Pleural Effusions Using Indwelling pleural catheters. CHEST 2017; 151(3):626-635. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27845052/

(3) Aboudara A., Maldonado F. Indwelling pleural catheters for benign pleural effusions: what is the evidence? Current Opinion in Pulmonary Medicine. 2019 Jul;25(4):369-373. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30865036/

(4) Majid A, Kheir F, Fashjian M, et al. Tunneled Pleural Catheter Placement with and without Talc Poudrage for Treatment of Pleural Effusions Due to Congestive Heart Failure. Ann Am Thorac Soc. 2016;13(2):212-6. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26598967/

questions.prompt: You are seeing a patient in the hospital for the first time for a rapidly recurrent malignant pleural effusion related to lung adenocarcinoma. You believe the patient may be an appropriate candidate for an indwelling pleural catheter (IPC). However, the patient has multiple concerns and questions about this approach in comparison with alternatives. Which of the following is correct about managing this patient's malignant pleural effusion with an IPC?

options.text: The procedure requires a surgical approach to maximize chance of placement success.
Managing the effusion with an IPC provides comparable palliation to alternatives such as chemical pleurodesis
A symptom-driven drainage schedule maximizes the chance of catheter removal in 6-12 weeks.
Infection risk is about 10-15% during the life of the catheter, includes cellulitis and empyema, and usually requires catheter removal.
IPC placement does not reduce the need for future pleural interventions such as thoracentesis, compared to management with chemical pleurodesis.

questions.explanation: Indwelling pleural catheters have emerged as an efficient tool for palliating malignant pleural effusions. They offer the convenience of easy placement in the outpatient setting (choice A incorrect), similar palliation outcomes, and reduced need for future pleural interventions compared to chemical pleurodesis (choice B correct, choice E incorrect).1,2 IPCs also have a favorable safety profile, with an infection risk of 5% or less that usually does not require removal of the catheter (choice D incorrect). A more aggressive drainage strategy may be more effective in achieving spontaneous pleurodesis (and hence catheter removal) compared to a more relaxed drainage schedule such as one driven by symptoms alone (choice C incorrect).3,4
references: (1) Muruganandan S, Azzopardi M, Fitzgerald DB, et al. Aggressive versus symptom-guided drainage of malignant pleural effusion via indwelling pleural catheters (AMPLE-2): an open-label randomised trial. Lancet Respir Med. 2018;6(9):671‐680. doi:10.1016/S2213-2600(18)30288

(2) Wahidi MM, Reddy C, Yarmus L, et al. Randomized Trial of Pleural Fluid Drainage Frequency in Patients with Malignant Pleural Effusions. The ASAP Trial. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2017;195(8):1050‐1057. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27898215/

(3) Davies HE, Mishra EK, Kahan BC, et al. Effect of an indwelling pleural catheter vs chest tube and talc pleurodesis for relieving dyspnea in patients with malignant pleural effusion: the TIME2 randomized controlled trial. JAMA. 2012;307(22):2383‐2389. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22610520/

(4) Feller-Kopman DJ, Reddy CB, DeCamp MM, et al. Management of Malignant Pleural Effusions. An Official ATS/STS/STR Clinical Practice Guideline. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2018;198(7):839‐849. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30272503/

questions.prompt: A 57-year-old female with a history of metastatic melanoma on immunotherapy presents to the hospital for evaluation of worsening dyspnea and right sided back pain for three days. She had a right-sided indwelling pleural catheter placed one month ago for a recurrent, symptomatic malignant pleural effusion and was draining one liter of pleural fluid daily until four days ago when the drainage decreased to approximately 200mL each time. She is febrile to 101.4F, tachycardic to 121 and has a normal BP. She has diminished breath sounds in the right lung region and examination of the skin surrounding the IPC is without erythema, induration or exudates. Labs are significant for a WBC 14, moderate anemia, a sodium of 120 mmol/L and a lactate of 11.3 mmol/L. CXR reveals an enlarging right sided pleural effusion compared to prior (seen below) and bedside US confirms a large multiloculated effusion. You are only able to drain 115 mL of cloudy, red fluid from the IPC and pleural fluid analysis reveals an LDH of 4371 U/L, glucose of 27 mg/dL, 40,000 RBCs and 89% neutrophils. Gram stain of the pleural fluid shows many gram-positive cocci in pairs and clusters. Which of the following is the most likely etiology of the patient's mal-functioning IPC and what is the best management plan?

options.text: Fibrin clot in the IPC🡪Administer intrapleural fibrinolytics and allow to dwell for 1-2 hours prior to drainage
Pleural space infection🡪Remove the pleural catheter, place a large bore chest tube and administer IV antibiotics
Catheter mal-positioning🡪Remove the pleural catheter and replace with a new catheter
Pleural space infection🡪Attach the catheter to continuous drainage, administer IV antibiotics and intrapleural fibrinolytics
Pleural space infection🡪Continue to drain the catheter once daily, prescribe a 7-day course of oral antibiotics and have the patient follow-up in clinic within one week

questions.explanation: This patient has a non-functioning catheter with an enlarging pleural effusion and positive pleural fluid gram stain. She has developed a pleural space infection in the setting of an indwelling pleural catheter (IPC). Current expert recommendations for management of an IPC-related pleural space infection are to attempt continuous drainage through the IPC rather than remove the catheter.(1) This recommendation is based on several studies evaluating patient outcomes for IPC-related pleural infections showing a low mortality rate, even in those without catheter removal.(3-6) No studies are currently available specifically evaluating the role of continuous versus intermittent IPC drainage in the setting of infection, therefore this recommendation is made based on the standard of care for other complicated parapneumonic effusions.(1,2) This patient's pleural catheter is not draining properly and therefore the recommendation is to administer intrapleural fibrinolytics to aid in catheter drainage and clearance of the infection.(1, 6) This recommendation is based on data from the MIST2 trial for management of complicated parapneumonic effusions in patient's without IPC.(7) Surgical intervention may be an option for this patient, but less invasive interventions are often pursued first. Removal of the pleural catheter can be considered if there is inadequate drainage or no clinical improvement after the above-mentioned maneuvers.
references: (1) Miller RJ, Chrissian AA, Lee YCG, Rahman NM, Wahidi MM, Tremblay A, Hsia DW, Almeida FA, Shojaee S, Mudambi L, Belanger AR, Bedi H, Gesthalter YB, Gaynor M, MacKenney KL, Lewis SZ, Casal RF. questions.prompt: A 63-year-old female with a history of obesity, OSA on BiPAP, HFpEF and diabetes presents to the hospital with worsening dyspnea and right upper quadrant abdominal pain. She is afebrile, hypertensive and in atrial fibrillation with a rate of 125. She is tachypneic requiring high flow nasal cannula at 40L 50%. She is tender to palpation in the RUQ and epigastric region and has diminished breath sounds on the right when compared to the left. You have no prior records as she is new to your facility. CT abdomen was unremarkable for intra-abdominal abnormalities but revealed a right sided pleural effusion. A dedicated chest CT was then obtained that revealed a multi-loculated right-sided pleural effusion with mediastinal lymphadenopathy as seen below. A 5.5cm posterior mediastinal mass is also seen near the esophagus. Upon questioning, she admits to undergoing an EUS procedure with biopsy of the mediastinal mass about 2 weeks prior. Cytology was negative for malignancy per her report and showed "scarring and dead cells." Labs are relatively unremarkable except for mild anemia and a WBC of 24. Cultures are obtained and she is admitted to the hospital for IV antibiotic administration. CT esophagram to evaluate for possible esophageal fistula is unrevealing. A 14F pigtail catheter is placed for right sided pleural fluid drainage and a total of 70mL of bloody fluid is drained. Pleural fluid is exudative with a predominance of neutrophils. Pleural fluid LDH is 5268 U/L with a glucose of 30 mg/dL. Gram stain and culture are pending. What is the next best step in the management of this patient's pleural infection based on available expert recommendations?

options.text: Remove the 14F pigtail catheter and place a 32F surgical chest tube
Begin instillation of 5mg DNase and 10mg tPA into the right pleural space twice daily to assist with pleural fluid drainage given minimal output after chest tube placement
Begin instillation of 5mg DNase into the right pleural space twice daily to assist with pleural fluid drainage while decreasing risk of hemorrhage
Consult the thoracic surgery team for immediate VATS decortication

questions.explanation: This patient developed an empyema as a possible complication of mediastinal mass biopsy via EUS. She now has sepsis and acute hypoxic respiratory failure requiring hospital admission. The primary treatment of empyema consists of antibiotic therapy and urgent pleural drainage. Chest tube placement may be all that is necessary for pleural fluid drainage in some patients with an early stage empyema, but many will require further interventions, such as this patient. The administration of 10mg tPA and 5mg DNase twice daily into the pleural space was shown to enhance pleural fluid drainage, improve radiographs, reduce surgical referrals and decrease hospital lengths of stay in the MIST2 trial.(1) Other dosing and administration regimens for tPA and DNase may be effective, but further studies are needed, therefore the dosing regimen used in the MIST2 trial is currently recommended. These medications were administered sequentially in the MIST2 trial, but some smaller studies have shown that concurrent dosing may also be effective. (3,4) The MIST2 trial also found that DNase monotherapy was ineffective at improving pleural fluid drainage and was associated with increased surgical referrals( answer C incorrect). No other large studies are currently available. (1,2) There was also no difference in outcomes with large bore versus small bore chest tubes in the MIST1 trial, thus answer A is incorrect.(5) Surgical decortication may be necessary, but pursuing less invasive management options is reasonable, especially considering her comorbidities and current need for significant supplemental oxygen support. A recently published consensus statement regarding the use of fibrinolytics and DNase in adult patients with pleural empyema is available for review.(2)
references: (1) Rahman NM, Maskell NA, West A, Teoh R, Arnold A, Mackinlay C, Peckham D, Davies CW, Ali N, Kinnear W, Bentley A, Kahan BC, Wrightson JM, Davies HE, Hooper CE, Lee YC, Hedley EL, Crosthwaite N, Choo L, Helm EJ, Gleeson FV, Nunn AJ, Davies RJ. Intrapleural use of tissue plasminogen activator and DNase in pleural infection. N Engl J Med. 2011 Aug 11;365(6):518-26. doi: 10.1056/NEJMoa1012740. PMID: 21830966. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21830966/

(2) Chaddha U, Agrawal A, Feller-Kopman D, Kaul V, Shojaee S, Maldonado F, Ferguson MK, Blyth KG, Grosu HB, Corcoran JP, Sachdeva A, West A, Bedawi EO, Majid A, Mehta RM, Folch E, Liberman M, Wahidi MM, Gangadharan SP, Roberts ME, DeCamp MM, Rahman NM. Epub 2021 Feb 2. PMID: 33545086. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33545086/

(3) Majid A, Kheir F, Folch A, Fernandez-Bussy S, Chatterji S, Maskey A, Fashjian M, Cheng G, Ochoa S, Alape D, Folch E. (4) Kheir F, Cheng G, Rivera E, Folch A, Folch E, Fernandez-Bussy S, Keyes C, Parikh M, Channick C, Chee A, Majid A. (5) Rahman NM, Maskell NA, Davies CW, Hedley EL, Nunn AJ, Gleeson FV, Davies RJ. The relationship between chest tube size and clinical outcome in pleural infection. Chest. 2010 Mar;137(3):536-43. doi: 10.1378/chest.09-1044. Epub 2009 Oct 9. PMID: 19820073. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19820073/

questions.prompt: A 52F with ER+ breast adenocarcinoma who underwent a right indwelling pleural catheter insertion due to malignant pleural effusion 6 weeks ago presented to the emergency department with fever and chills. She is currently draining about 400 ml of serosanguineous pleural fluid every other day, with no change in color of the fluid. She presented to the ER, and a chest radiograph reveals indwelling pleural catheter in place, with residual right pleural effusion. Blood, sputum, and urine cultures were obtained, and your service was consulted to manage the pleural catheter. Which of the following should be the next step to ensure appropriate work-up and management of a possible catheter related infection is performed?

options.text: Aspirate pleural fluid from the catheter. A positive bacterial culture of the sample ensures a diagnosis of empyema.
Current society consensus guidelines recommend removal of the pleural catheter, and placement of a chest tube in case empyema is suspected.
A thoracentesis is sometimes needed from a separate site to obtain a diagnosis of empyema.
Intrapleural fibrinolytics are usually not required for management of pleural space infections from indwelling pleural catheters.

questions.explanation: The appropriate method for obtaining pleural fluid for culture is unknown, and the rate of bacterial colonization of IPC has not been studied, but studies exploring this in post-surgical patients showed 83% prevalence of colonization of chest tubes within 4 days of placement. Due to this concern, a positive sample from the catheter does not completely ensure the diagnosis of empyema (A is incorrect). A thoracentesis from a different site may be needed if this is a concern (C is correct). Current consensus guidelines favor using the existing IPC while treating the empyema and using intrapleural fibrinolytics as per other empyemas (C and D are both incorrect).
references: (1) Miller, R. J., Chrissian, A. A., Lee, Y. C. G., Rahman, N. M., Wahidi, M. M., Tremblay, A. et al. (2020). AABIP Evidence-informed Guidelines and Expert Panel Report for the Management of Indwelling Pleural Catheters. J Bronchology Interv Pulmonol, 27(4), 229-245. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32804745/

questions.prompt: An 83 years-old male underwent bronchoscopy with transbronchial biopsy from the right lower lobe for workup of interstitial lung disease. He developed a symptomatic pneumothorax prior to discharge, and a 14 French pigtail chest tube was inserted on the right, and his symptoms resolved. A large air leak was noted in his chest tube chamber, which persisted with no signs of improvement after 4 days. Physical examination shows signs of crepitus in the right chest and neck, and mild crepitus in the right face and periorbital area. Your colleagues consult you to see if the patient could benefit from any endobronchial strategies for his air leak. Which of the following are the best statements about the management of air leaks?

options.text: The agent that has the strongest evidence for pleurodesis in the setting of persistent air leak is talc rather than bleomycin.
There is a clear cost benefit with valve placement in hospitalized patients compared to the cost of usual post-operative hospital care.
Autologous blood pleurodesis appears to be an acceptable alternative to managing PALs, though supporting data is limited.
As of year 2021, all FDA approved valve systems for treating emphysema currently on the market are also FDA approved for treatment of persistent air leaks.

questions.explanation: The definition of a "persistent air leak" varies among different publications, but generally accepted as an air leak persisting after 5-7 days. Endobronchial valve therapies have been growing in popularity as an option for management of this challenging problem, but strong evidence with the use of these devices is still lacking. A small study exploring the cost benefit ratio of IBV placement showed comparable costs to those of usual hospital care in hospitalized patients up to 8 days, but predicting which air leaks persist after 8 days is a challenge (B is incorrect). As of year 2021, only one valve system has a humanitarian use device (HUD) exemption from the FDA (D is incorrect). The statement regarding the relative efficacy of talc and bleomycin is true for malignant effusions (A is incorrect), but has not been shown in persistent air leak. Autologous blood pleurodesis (ABP) is a reasonable alternative to managing PALs in various settings, including those resulting from spontaneous pneumothorax and lung surgery (1-6). The procedure utilizes 50-150 ml of the patient's venous blood which is drawn at the bedside, instilled into the pleural space under sterile conditions and dwelled for 30-60 minutes. The tube is then unclamped and placed to suction. The mechanism of efficacy of the autologous blood is thought to be multifactorial, including the physical sealing of the leak leading to adequate lung re-expansion, and triggering of pleural inflammation which ultimately results in pleurodesis (7). A meta-analysis of 10 studies showed that the overall success rate of ABP for resolving an air leak was 93% in post-operative patients and 92% in those with spontaneous pneumothorax (8). Three studies within this analysis also found ABP to be superior in time to resolution of leak compared to the control arm (4-6). A larger retrospective propensity-matched analysis of 109 patients with PAL after lobectomy found ABP significantly reduced the number of days before chest tube removal, length of hospital stay, perioperative complications, and need for additional invasive procedures or reoperation (9). Complications of ABP appear to be rare, though empyema is possible (9% risk in one study [2]). Side effects to consider include those expected with other types of chemical pleurodesis such as mild fever and pleurisy. Therefore, while the data supporting the use of ABP for PALs is promising, it derives mostly from studies incorporating few patients (range 6-32 in the meta-analysis discussed above) or those of retrospective design. Large, prospective, and/or randomized investigations are needed to better characterize the efficacy and role of this approach (choice C correct).
