questions.prompt: A 65 year-old male with a history of alcohol abuse presents to the emergency room with fevers, chills, shortness of breath and right sided pleuritic chest pain for 3 days. His medications include apixaban for a history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. On exam he is febrile to 39.0 C and is tachycardic to 110 bpm. He has diminished breath sounds on the right lung base. CT chest shows a moderate loculated right sided pleural effusion without pleural thickening. An ultrasound shows a hypoechoic right pleural effusion with septations. A thoracentesis is performed with extraction of frank, green pus, and subsequently a 14Fr chest tube is placed to facilitate drainage. Broad spectrum antibiotics including anaerobic coverage are started. Which of the following is a correct statement regarding use of intrapleural fibrinolytics in this patient?

options.text: Intrapleural fibrinolytic or DNase monotherapy should be used since it has been shown to reduce surgery referral rates.
The dose of DNase and alteplase (tPA) is 10 mg and 5 mg, respectively, for twice daily concurrent administration.
The number of doses should be individualized based on clinical and radiographic response to treatment.
Surgery should be first line management in patients with fibrinopurulent empyema.

questions.explanation: Management of empyema is largely based on the landmark MIST2 trial which was a multicenter, two by two factorial trial in which patients were randomized to receive tPA and DNase, alteplase (tPA) alone, DNase alone, or placebo. The group that received combination intrapleural fibrinolytic and DNase had improved radiographic changes (primary outcome), decrease in surgery referral rates, and shorter hospital length of stays. In a consensus statement published by Chaddha et al, an expert panel provided recommendations for use of fibrinolytics and deoxyribonuclease in adult patients with empyema. One of the recommendations addressed the duration and number of doses for fibrinolytic and DNase therapy. Choice C is correct in that the number of doses should be individualized base on clinical and radiographic response to treatment. The rationale behind this is that in the MIST2 trial, only 67% of patients completed the full treatment course (6 doses). Choice A is incorrect because in the MIST1 trial, streptokinase monotherapy did not lead to a significant reduction in death and surgical drainage at 3 months compared with placebo; there was no difference in radiographic outcomes or hospital length of stay. In the MIST2 trial, tPA monotherapy did not reduce surgical referral rates, hospital length of stay or radiographic outcomes. Choice B is incorrect because the recommended dose is 5 mg of DNAse and 10 mg tPA twice daily which are the doses that the landmark MIST2 trial used. There have been other dosing regimens that have been studied in smaller studies however without significant impact on outcomes or adverse events. Choice D is incorrect because in the MIST-2 trial, administration of intrapleural fibrinolytics showed a decrease in surgery referral rates. In the consensus statement from the expert panel published by Chaddha et al, the recommendation is to trial a fibrinolytic agent and DNAase before considering surgery especially for fibrinopurulent empyema.
references: (1) Maskell NA, Davies CW, Nunn AJ, et al. U.K. Controlled trial of intrapleural streptokinase for pleural infection. N Engl J Med 2005; 352: 865-74. https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/nejmoa042473

(2) Rahman NM, Maskell NA, West A, et al. Intrapleural use of tissue plasminogen activator and DNase in pleural infection. N Engl J Med 2011; 365: 518-26. https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/nejmoa1012740

(3) Chaddha U, Agrawal A, Feller-Kopman D, Kaul V, Shojaee S, Maldonado F, Ferguson MK, Blyth KG, Grosu HB, Corcoran JP, Sachdeva A, West A, Bedawi EO, Majid A, Mehta RM, Folch E, Liberman M, Wahidi MM, Gangadharan SP, Roberts ME, DeCamp MM, Rahman NM. Use of fibrinolytics and deoxyribonuclease in adult patients with pleural empyema: a consensus statement. Lancet Respir Med. 2021 Sep;9(9):1050-1064. doi: 10.1016/S2213-2600(20)30533-6. Epub 2021 Feb 2. PMID: 33545086. https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lanres/article/PIIS2213-2600(20)30533-6/fulltext

questions.prompt: A 62 y/o male w/ hx of cirrhosis secondary to hepatitis C is on the waiting list for a liver transplant. He has a hepatic hydrothorax that has been drained three times in the last month. The liver transplant team is inquiring about further management of the pleural effusion with a indwelling pleural catheter (IPC). Which of the following statements is false regarding pleural interventions in patients with hepatic hydrothorax?

options.text: Serial thoracentesis is associated with increased cumulative risk in patients with hepatic hydrothorax.
The decision for placing tunneled indwelling pleural catheters (IPC) should be discussed as a multidisciplinary team involving the hepatology and transplant team.
Chest tube placement is associated with increased risk of death due to sepsis and empyema.
The infection rate for indwelling pleural catheters in hepatic hydrothorax is approximately 5%.

questions.explanation: In recent years, more data has emerged regarding pleural interventions in hepatic hydrothorax. Namely, use of indwelling pleural catheters (IPC) is becoming a more accepted management strategy for refractory hepatic hydrothorax. In a retrospective study by Shojaee et. al, patients with hepatic hydrothorax who underwent serial thoracentesis had an increased cumulative risk of complications (Choice A is true). The cumulative complication risk for patients with hepatic hydrothorax was 12.2% in the study. Small retrospective studies have highlighted increased morbidity and mortality with chest tube thoractostomy in hepatic hydrothorax (Choice C is true). The increased morbidity and mortality are due to the risk of sepsis and empyema. For example, a study showed that hospitalized patients with hepatic hydrothorax who underwent chest tube thoracostomy had two times greater mortality than those that underwent thoracentesis (2). The data for using IPCs in hepatic hydrothorax is limited but is becoming an acceptable management strategy. While infection risk with IPC placed for malignant pleural effusions is around 5%, it is higher, between 10-16%, with IPCs placed for hepatic hydrothorax (Choice D is false, and thus the correct answer choice). IPC-related mortality is reported to be 2-3%. In patients who are liver transplant candidates or are awaiting transplant, the decision to place an indwelling pleural catheter should be made after discussion with the hepatology and transplant team, since the risks and benefits need to be carefully assessed (Choice B is true).
references: (1) Gilbert CR, Shojaee S, Maldonado F, et al. Pleural Interventions in the Management of Hepatic Hydrothorax. Chest. 2022 Jan;161(1):276-283. doi: 10.1016/j.chest.2021.08.043. Epub 2021 Aug 12. https://journal.chestnet.org/article/S0012-3692(21)03672-2/fulltext

(2) Shojaee S, Khalid M, Kallingal G, Kang L, Rahman N. Repeat Thoracentesis in Hepatic Hydrothorax and Non-Hepatic Hydrothorax Effusions: A Case-Control Study. Respiration. 2018;96(4):330-337. doi: 10.1159/000490001. Epub 2018 Jul 10. PMID: 29991046. https://www.karger.com/Article/FullText/490001

(3) Ridha A, Al-Abboodi Y, Fasullo M. The outcome of thoracentesis versus chest tube placement for hepatic hydrothorax in patients with cirrhosis: a nationwide analysis of the national inpatient sample. Gastroenterol Res Pract. 2017; 20175872068 https://www.hindawi.com/journals/grp/2017/5872068/

(4) Kniese C. Diab K. Ghabril M. Bosslet G. Indwelling pleural catheters in hepatic hydrothorax: a single-center series of outcomes and complications. Chest. 2019; 155: 307-314 https://journal.chestnet.org/article/S0012-3692(18)31053-5/fulltext

questions.prompt: A 66-year-old woman with IDDM, hypertension, dyslipidemia is referred from her routine dialysis session for an overall being unwell and increasing dyspnea. She has known mild cardiac diastolic dysfunction and a stable chronic right sided pleural effusion (figure 1). In the emergency room she is noted to be acutely hypoxemic (SpO2 80s on room air); with relative hypotension (blood pressure 93/62) and a new leukocytosis (WBC - 14.4 x109/L from a baseline of 6 x109/L); prior serum liver enzymes have been normal beyond a low baseline albumin level. Chest X-ray is unchanged from prior. She is admitted to the ICU and undergoes a bedside ultrasound which shows a simple appearing pleural effusion (figure 2). Given her concerning overall appearance, what additional considerations are warranted with regards to pleural effusion?

options.text: The effusion is stable, we should continue to monitor it for changes
Discuss with renal the option of additional hemofiltration
Small volume diagnostic thoracentesis for labs and culture, act accordingly once studies are finalized
Large bore chest tube (>20 french)
Small bore chest tube (<20 french)

questions.explanation: Though the effusion is chronic and guidelines only recommend pigtail placement for cases where infection or biologically complex fluid is confirmed these guidelines are relatively outdated. Given the gravity of her condition further delays in source control of a potential pleural infection may increase her mortality by up to 7 fold. The patient's RAPID score (renal function, age, infection source and albumin level) is 5 stratifying her as likely high risk for mortality at 3 months (47.8% for high risk, 17.8% for medium risk). Given the advances in pleural procedures, ultrasound, pigtail drainage systems since the publication of formal guidelines, the risk to benefit ratio has shifted management towards earlier intervention when delay in treating empyema can potentially carry significant mortality risk. In this case the patient initially underwent thoracentesis which demonstrated a simple transudate, likely from concomitant underlying transudate. However, both blood and pleural cultures were positive for E faecalis after 24 hours suggesting a superimposed empyema. A pigtail was later placed; however, this was only done after 48 hours from presentation at which point her effusion became loculated (figure 3). The RAPID score is a prognostic tool that was generated using post study data from the MIST trials and can help stratify a patient's risk of three-month death from pleural sepsis. It is comprised of Renal function, Age, Purulence, Infection source (hospital vs community acquired), Dietary (albumin state). It is important to note that of the five components of the RAPID, four do not rely on actual fluid sampling and relate to the patient's underlying co-morbidities. A pre-drainage calculation can be done to determine risk prior to sampling and used to guide treatment. In this case, given her multiple medical co-morbidities and septic picture requiring ICU level care definitive drainage upfront is warranted. Observation in this critically ill patient is not reasonable as the effusion may be harboring a potential nidus of infection. Her hemodynamic instability makes additional hemofiltration inappropriate at present. A diagnostic thoracentesis is reasonable though this may further delay her care while awaiting labs to finalize. There is no benefit to larger bore tubes in the management of pleural sepsis. https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/aabip-app.appspot.com/o/Question_17(c).png?alt=media&token=58298e34-00ff-44b4-8a2e-7091de6d5db8
references: (1) Colice et al Chest 2000 - Chest. 2000 Oct;118(4):1158-71. doi: 10.1378/chest.118.4.1158. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11035692/
(2) Davies et al Thorax 2010. Aug;65 Suppl 2:ii41-53. doi: 10.1136/thx.2010.137000. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20696693/
(3) Rahman et al Chest 2014 Chest . 2014 Apr;145(4):848-855. doi: 10.1378/chest.13-1558. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24264558/

questions.prompt: A 65-year-old woman with mild obesity, diabetes and no smoking history is referred to you after presenting with sub-acute decline in her functional status and increasing dyspnea at rest. Initial work up revealed a large unilateral right sided pleural effusion on chest x-ray (figure 1) which you drained in clinic, post drainage image in figure 2. You call her the following week for review of the results, which showed pleural involvement of metastatic breast cancer on pleural cytology. She endorses relief of her symptoms after the drainage, but notes that she feels as if her dyspnea has started to recur over the last few days. She would like to discuss potential interventions and you raise the option of an indwelling tunneled pleural catheter. She is reluctant and states that she is confident that she won't need it because she has not yet received any systemic therapy which will address her effusion related dyspnea. What could you advise her during your discussion?

options.text: Agree, advise waiting for chemotherapy before any further interventions.
Discuss the option of pleurodesis if she doesn't want an indwelling pleural catheter.
She has a high chance of spontaneous pleurodesis with an indwelling pleural catheter, and that the IPC would likely only be needed temporarily.
She should opt for repeat thoracentesis until effusion recurrence ceases.
The impact of chemotherapy on malignant pleural effusion recurrence is uncertain.

questions.explanation: Breast cancer is a common cause of malignant pleural effusions, second to lung cancer. Malignant pleural effusions (MPE) are a major source of morbidity. While treating the underlying malignancy should be a priority, effects of systemic therapy on MPE are not predictable (choice A correct). Additionally, the patient should be advised on the time interval that may pass until therapy might be initiated rendering the patient symptomatic in that interval. One single academic center report of 10 year follow up for 207 breast cancer patients, half of whom were given chemotherapy and half were not, who underwent placement of indwelling pleural catheters (IPC) showed no difference in time to IPC removal (HR 0.73, 95% CI 0.50-1.07, p = 0.10), rate of IPC removal (OR 1.16, 95% CI 0.68-1.98, p = 0.59) or risk of IPC infection (RR 0.57, 95% CI 0.06-5.39, p = 0.48) (figure 3).Current guidelines recommend discussing definitive intervention upon first recurrence of an MPE if symptomatic relief was obtained on an initial large volume thoracentesis (figure 4). Guideline concordant care using definitive procedures compared with repeat thoracentesis has been associated with fewer subsequent procedures and complications. Serial thoracentesis carries the advantage of simplicity, however therapeutic effect may be short lived and exposes the patient to cumulative procedural risk as well as concerns of increasing effusion complexity (choice D incorrect). Pleurodesis would likely fail given her likely trapped lung as evident on post drainage chest x-ray (choice B incorrect). IPCs have been shown to have spontaneous pleurodesis rates of roughly 40% at three months, however the impact of trapped lung on the ability to pleurodese with IPC is not clear (choice C incorrect). https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/aabip-app.appspot.com/o/Question_18(c).png?alt=media&token=267ca766-84e2-4400-a339-1c763881a397 https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/aabip-app.appspot.com/o/Question_18(d).png?alt=media&token=0278bb7d-f282-45c3-8e0b-a074962e6494
references: (1) Mitchell MA et al Respiration 2018;96:552-559 https://doi.org/10.1159/000491097. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30205402/
(2) Feller Kopman D et al AJRCCM 2018 Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2018 Oct 1;198(7):839-849. doi: 10.1164/rccm.201807-1415ST. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30272503/

(3) Ost DE, Niu J, Zhao H, Grosu HB, Giordano SH. Quality Gaps and Comparative Effectiveness of Management Strategies for Recurrent Malignant Pleural Effusions. Chest. 2018;153(2):438-452. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28864054/

questions.prompt: What is true regarding the results of TAPPS trial (Thoracosocopy and Talc Poudrage compared with intercostal drainage and talc slurry for malignant pleural effusion)?

options.text: Talc slurry and intercostal drainage and thoracoscpy and talc poudrage are equally cost effective.
Talc poudrage was more effective in achieving pleurodesis at 90 days.
Hospital stay was higher in Talc poudrage group as compared to talc slurry group.
Talc Slurry group had higher VAS pain scale as compared to Poudrage group.

questions.explanation: TAPPS was a randomized multicenter trial conducted in UK for patients with malignant pleural effusion. Primary outcome of the trial was proportion of patients with pleurodesis failure at 3 months post procedure. 330 adults were randomized with 166 in poudrgage group and 164 in slurry group. 97% of patients in poudrage group received the intended treatment as compared to 88% in the slurry group. One of the main reason, patients in slurry group did not receive talc was to poor lung expansion (9/18 patients who did not receive talc). 22% of patients in poudrage group had pleurodesis failure at 90 days as compared to 24% in talc group. 14/36 and 17/38 patients had IPC placed in first 90 days in the poudrage and talc group. Mean hospital length of stay was 12 days and 11 days in poudrage and slurry group respectively. Mean change in VAS scale from baseline was 1 and 2.3 days in poudrage and slurry group at day 7. At day 90, mean change in VAS scale was -2.5 and -5.6 poudrage and slurry group, respectively ( p=0.61). Adverse events were similar in both groups. At 6 months, per participant cost of the talc poudrage and slurry were £10,687 and £10,146 respectively. Mean QALY gained were not statistically significant (Choice A correct).
references: (1) Bhatnagar R, Luengo-Fernandez R, Kahan BC, Rahman NM, Miller RF, Maskell NA. Thoracoscopy and talc poudrage compared with intercostal drainage and talc slurry infusion to manage malignant pleural effusion: the TAPPS RCT. Health Technol Assess. 2020 Jun;24(26):1-90. doi: 10.3310/hta24260. PMID: 32525474; PMCID: PMC7307272. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32525474/

questions.prompt: A 65-year-old patient came for the evaluation of exudative pleural effusion. Patient has 60 pack year smoking history and worked in shipyard for 40 years. Patient underwent medical pleuroscopy. Results of biopsy showed that patient has malignant mesothelioma. Based on Bayman et al. trial, which of the following is true regarding the role of prophylactic radiation of the surgical tract?

options.text: In the absence of radiation, risk of chest wall metastasis is 3.2% at 6 months.
At 12 months, chest wall metastasis incidence for radiation group was 8.1%.
RCT showed that prophylactic radiation reduced the incidence of chest wall metastasis.
Patients with Epithelioid histology are more likely to have chest wall metastasis.
Intention to give chemotherapy was associated with reduce likelihood for chest wall tract metastasis.

questions.explanation: Malignant mesothelioma has very poor 2-year survival rate. Chest wall metastasis at the site of biopsy is concern because of tumor seeding along the tract. Reported incidence of chest wall metastasis varies from 2 to 50%. Bayman et al. randomized 375 patients in 54 centers to either received prophylactic radiation or standard of care- 186 to prophylactic radiation and 189 to no radiation. Patients were eligible to be included if they were: 18 years or older, confirmed diagnosis of MPM, inoperable disease, ECOG 0 or 2. Included chest wall procedure were: Open surgical biopsy, VATS, local anesthetic pleuroscopy, insertion of chest drain. Radiation was given within 42 days of the chest wall procedure. They were excluded if patient underwent open thoracotomy, needle biopsy, IPC in situ or were on chemotherapy. Post radiation, chemotherapy was initiated within 1 week of completion based on discretion of treating physician . 3.2% and 5.3% of patients in prophylactic radiotherapy and control group developed chest wall metastasis at 6 months. (p=0.44). At 12 months, rate of chest was metastasis was 8.1% in prophylactic radiation as compared to 10.1% in no radiation (p=0.59). Epithelioid histology or intention to get chemotherapy did not affect the rate of chest wall metastasis at 6 moths. Another trial, SMART trial, had similar rate of chest wall metastasis.
references: (1) Bayman N, Appel W, Ashcroft L, Baldwin DR, Bates A, Darlison L, Edwards JG, Ezhil V, Gilligan D, Hatton M, Jegannathen A, Mansy T, Peake MD, Pemberton L, Rintoul RC, Snee M, Ryder WD, Taylor P, Faivre-Finn C. Prophylactic Irradiation of Tracts in Patients With Malignant Pleural Mesothelioma: An Open-Label, Multicenter, Phase III Randomized Trial. J Clin Oncol. 2019 May 10;37(14):1200-1208. doi: 10.1200/JCO.18.01678. Epub 2019 Mar 28. PMID: 30920878. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30920878/

(2) Clive AO, Taylor H, Dobson L, Wilson P, de Winton E, Panakis N, Pepperell J, Howell T, Stewart SA, Penz E, Jordan N, Morley AJ, Zahan-Evans N, Smith S, Batchelor TJP, Marchbank A, Bishop L, Ionescu AA, Bayne M, Cooper S, Kerry A, Jenkins P, Toy E, Vigneswaran V, Gildersleve J, Ahmed M, McDonald F, Button M, Lewanski C, Comins C, Dakshinamoorthy M, Lee YCG, Rahman NM, Maskell NA. Prophylactic radiotherapy for the prevention of procedure-tract metastases after surgical and large-bore pleural procedures in malignant pleural mesothelioma (SMART): a multicentre, open-label, phase 3, randomised controlled trial. Lancet Oncol. 2016 Aug;17(8):1094-1104. PMID: 27345639 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27345639/

questions.prompt: An 87 years-old female patient with metastatic lung adenocarcinoma in whom you placed an indwelling pleural catheter (IPC) about 2 months ago for refractory malignant pleural effusion, is admitted to your institution with fever, chest pain and very diminished drainage through her IPC for the past week. On admission to the medical stepdown, she is febrile (103 F), has pleuritic chest pain (ipsilateral to her IPC) and relatively hypotensive. Her WBC is 24k and she tells you she was told by her home health nurse the fluid might have been purulent. She is on broad spectrum antibiotics. You suspect an IPC related pleural effusion. Thoracic Surgery already evaluated her and deemed her not a surgical candidate. Based on the most recent literature, you recommend:

options.text: Remove the IPC, place a small bore chest tube and add antifungal coverage
Remove the IPC, place a small bore chest tube and initiate MIST-2 trial fibrinolytic and DNAse treatment
Place a small bore chest tube and initiate MIST-2 trial fibrinolytic and DNAse treatment, hoping to "salvage" the IPC
Use the pre-existing IPC to initiate MIST-2 trial fibrinolytic and DNAse treatment
Suggest palliative care consultation for hospice evaluation

questions.explanation: Fitzgerald et al [1] published the largest retrospective cohort suggesting that intrapleural tPA/DNAse therapy may be used safely and successfully for management of IPC-related pleural infection. Option (a) is incorrect as antifungal coverage is rarely used for pleural infection (unless you isolate fungus in the fluid). Options (b) and (c) are incorrect as the IPC could be used as the "delivery" catheter. Option (e) is incorrect as patient could potentially be treated for her pleural infection.
references: (1) Fitzgerald DB, Muruganandan S, Tsim S, et al. Intrapleural Fibrinolytics and Deoxyribonuclease for Treatment of Indwelling Pleural Catheter-Related Pleural Infection: A Multi-Center Observational Study. Respiration 2021;100:452-460 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33784710/
(2) Rahman NM, Maskell NA, West, A, et al. Intrapleural use of tissue plasminogen activator and DNase in pleural infection N Engl J Med 2011;365(6):518-26 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21830966/

questions.prompt: A 56 years-old female with a history of cholangiocarcinoma 5 years earlier, s/p right hepatectomy complicated by post-operative leak and right pleural effusion, subsequently treated with adjuvant chemoradiaton, is sent to you for further management of a recurrent left pleural effusion. Her course has been complicated by numerous intra-abdominal sequalae including recurrent biliary stricture requiring biliary stents and frequent revision with interventional radiology. There is also evidence of elevated portal pressure and cirrhosis. She has also undergone 2 separate ERCP procedures in the last year for cholangitis with subsequent biliary dilatation. Recent imaging shows no evidence of recurrence, and her laboratory studies reveal no elevated tumor markers. In addition, she underwent an ultrasound-guided left thoracentesis 3 months previously. This revealed a lymphocytic predominant exudative effusion with negative cytology. On evaluation, she is moderately dyspneic with a large, free flowing, complex, non-septate effusion. You offer a repeat thoracentesis and 1300ml of serous fluid is removed. Analysis shows a lymphocytic exudative effusion with TGL of 61 and no evidence of malignancy on cytology. What is the next best step for diagnostic purposes?

options.text: Repeat thoracentesis
Medical thoracoscopy
Closed pleural biopsy
Chylomicron analysis

questions.explanation: Chylothorax is defined as either pleural triglyceride levels of greater than 110 mg/dL and/or the presence of chylomicrons. It is often suspected when milky fluid is aspirated during a pleural intervention. However, this classical appearance occurs in less than 50% of patients ultimately diagnosed with chylothorax. Traumatic causes account for approximately 50% of chylous effusions and can be further differentiated into surgical and non-surgical. In non-traumatic causes, while a malignant etiology is the most common with lymphomas comprising the bulk of cases, elevated portal venous pressure has been implicated as etiology. In the current case, the patient has a history of multiple intrabdominal surgeries and interventions whereby a non-traumatic etiology is likely and suspicion for chylothorax should at least be moderate. Sending the fluid from the most recent thoracentesis for chylomicron analysis would be the least invasive and most appropriate step. In the above case, while malignancy could be the cause of her recurrent pleural effusion, a third thoracentesis would not be the correct answer as the diagnostic yield does not increase significantly after the second sample. Medical thoracoscopy would be a reasonable approach as it has a high sensitivity (>90%) for diagnosing malignancy in cytology-negative exudative pleural effusions. In addition, it has been shown to have higher sensitivity than close needle biopsy, whether image guided or not. However, medial thoracoscopy would only be the next best step if chylothorax was not identified through chylomicron analysis.
references: (1) Riley LE, Ataya A. Clinical approach and review of causes of a chylothorax. Respir Med. 2019 Oct;157:7-13. doi: 10.1016/j.rmed.2019.08.014. Epub 2019 Aug 22. PMID: 31454675. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31454675/

(2) Maldonado F, Hawkins FJ, Daniels CE, Doerr CH, Decker PA, Ryu JH. Pleural fluid characteristics of chylothorax. Mayo Clin Proc. 2009 Feb;84(2):129-33. doi: 10.1016/S0025-6196(11)60820-3. PMID: 19181646; PMCID: PMC2664583. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19181646/

(3) Porcel JM, Esquerda A, Vives M, Bielsa S. Etiology of pleural effusions: analysis of more than 3,000 consecutive thoracenteses. Arch Bronconeumol 2014;50:161-165. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24360987/

(4) Haridas N, K P S, T P R, P T J, Chetambath R. Medical Thoracoscopy vs Closed Pleural Biopsy in Pleural Effusions: A Randomized Controlled Study. J Clin Diagn Res. 2014 May;8(5):MC01-4. doi: 10.7860/JCDR/2014/7476.4310. Epub 2014 May 15. PMID: 24995201; PMCID: PMC4080022. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24995201/

(5) Metintas M, Ak G, Dundar E, Yildirim H, Ozkan R, Kurt E, Erginel S, Alatas F, Metintas S. Medical thoracoscopy vs CT scan-guided Abrams pleural needle biopsy for diagnosis of patients with pleural effusions: a randomized, controlled trial. Chest. 2010 Jun;137(6):1362-8. doi: 10.1378/chest.09-0884. Epub 2010 Feb 12. PMID: 20154079. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20154079/

(6) Kaul V, McCracken DJ, Rahman NM, Epelbaum O. Contemporary Approach to the Diagnosis of Malignant Pleural Effusion. Ann Am Thorac Soc. 2019 Sep;16(9):1099-1106. doi: 10.1513/AnnalsATS.201902-189CME. PMID: 31216176. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31216176/

questions.prompt: 73-year old man, active smoker, former construction worker with chronic cough, comes in with right-sided chest discomfort, fatigue, shortness of breath over last 3-4 weeks. He reports tripping and falling after drinking too much 1 month ago. Most likely diagnosis based on signs showing separation visceral and parietal pleura on contrast-enhanced CT chest is?

options.text: Hemothorax
Empyema
Malignant pleural effusion
Hepatic Hydrothorax

questions.explanation: The split pleura sign (↑) is seen with pleural empyemas and is considered the most reliable CT sign helping to distinguish empyemas from noninfectious pleural effusions. The sign results from fibrin coating of both the parietal and visceral surface of the pleura with resulting in growth of blood vessels. Following IV contrast injection CT shows a loculated fluid collection and thickened pleural layers with accompanying enhancement.
references: (1) Yudin, Andrey. Split Pleura Sign. Metaphorical Signs in Computed Tomography of Chest and Abdomen. Springer, Cham, 2014. 5-5.

(2) Stark DD, Federle MP, Goodman PC et-al. Differentiating lung abscess and empyema: radiography and computed tomography. AJR Am J Roentgenol. 1983;141 (1): 163-7. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/6602513/
